- [x] **NIST 800-53 control families** (SI-2, AC-7, CM-7, SC-28) map directly to vulnerability countermeasures.
- [x] VML is **continuous and cyclical**, not a one-time audit.
- [x] **Real-world priority** — Human vulnerabilities cause ~68% of breaches (Verizon DBIR 2023).
- [x] The "**roundup**" in CVSS rounds to one decimal place, *not* to the next integer.
- [x] Always reference **standards (NIST, ISO 27001, CVSS)** in long-answer questions for full marks.

---

> **END OF NOTES — VULNERABILITY (OECST721, MODULE 1)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Threats
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECST721 - CYBER SECURITY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Threats # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of cybersecurity as prescribed by the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, a **Threat** is defined as any potential event, circumstance, or malicious action that can exploit one or more **vulnerabilities** in an information system to compromise the **CIA Triad** (Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability) of digital assets. Mathematically, a threat can be expressed as a function of capability, intent, and opportunity: $$ T = f(C, I, O) $$ Where: - $C$ = **Capability** of the threat actor (skill, resources, tools) - $I$ = **Intent** (motivation, malicious purpose) - $O$ = **Opportunity** (exposure, attack surface availability) A threat is considered **realized** only when it successfully exploits a vulnerability, leading to an **adverse impact** on the system. Until then, it remains a *latent* or *potential* threat. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Distinction (KTU Board Favourite):** > - **Threat** = *What can attack you?* (the danger) > - **Vulnerability** = *What weakness can be exploited?* (the flaw) > - **Risk** = *Probability × Impact* (the exposure) > - **Attack** = *The actual attempt to exploit* (the action) ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine your home is a computer system. The **threats** are like potential dangers around your house — burglars, natural disasters, fire, or even a careless family member. Your house has **vulnerabilities** like an unlocked window, weak lock, or old wiring. A **risk** is the chance that a burglar will actually break in through that unlocked window and steal your valuables. A successful **attack** is when the burglar actually enters. In cybersecurity, every connected device, every line of code, and every user account is a potential entry point. Threats are the "burglars" — some are amateur (script kiddies), some are professional (organized crime), and some are state-sponsored (Advanced Persistent Threats). > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metric (NIST SP 800-30):** > Threat Level is qualitatively rated as: **Low**, **Moderate**, **High**, **Critical**. > Quantitatively, the risk score is often computed as: **Risk = Likelihood × Impact**, yielding values in the range **0.0 to 1.0** (normalized) or **1 to 25** (5×5 matrix scale). ## 1.3 Threat Actors — Who Are They? Threat actors are the entities behind threats. The KTU syllabus recognizes the following primary categories: | Threat Actor | Skill Level | Motivation | Example | |---|---|---|---| | **Script Kiddies** | Low | Curiosity, fame | Using pre-built exploit tools | | **Hacktivists** | Medium | Ideology, protest | Anonymous group DDoS | | **Cybercriminals** | High | Financial gain | Ransomware gangs (Conti, LockBit) | | **Insider Threats** | Variable | Revenge, greed | Disgruntled employee | | **Nation-State APTs** | Very High | Espionage, sabotage | Stuxnet, SolarWinds | | **Terrorist Groups** | Medium | Ideological destruction | Critical infrastructure attacks | > [!TIP] > The **2017 WannaCry ransomware attack** infected 230,000+ computers across 150 countries in a single day — a textbook example of a *rapid-propagation threat* that combined worm behavior with cryptographic payload. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Threat Classification Taxonomy Threats can be classified along multiple dimensions. The KTU 2024 module prescribes a layered understanding: ### A. By Attack Vector (Delivery Mechanism) 1. **Malware-Based Threats** - Virus, Worm, Trojan Horse, Ransomware, Spyware, Adware, Rootkit, Keylogger 2. **Network-Based Threats** - DDoS, Man-in-the-Middle (MitM), ARP Spoofing, DNS Poisoning 3. **Human-Based Threats** - Phishing, Spear Phishing, Whaling, Vishing, Smishing, Social Engineering, Pretexting, Baiting 4. **Application-Based Threats** - SQL Injection, XSS, CSRF, Buffer Overflow, Zero-Day Exploits 5. **Physical Threats** - Hardware tampering, USB drops, shoulder surfing, dumpster diving ### B. By Intent (Strategic Classification) - **Unintentional Threats** — Human error, accidental data leakage - **Intentional Threats** — Deliberate malicious activity ### C. By Origin - **Internal Threats** — Originate within the trusted boundary (insiders) - **External Threats** — Originate outside the organizational perimeter ## 2.2 Threat Modeling Frameworks (High-Yield for KTU) | Framework | Origin | Focus | Key Output | |---|---|---|---| | **STRIDE** | Microsoft | Threat categorization by violated property | Threat list per category | | **DREAD** | Microsoft | Risk scoring of individual threats | Numeric risk score | | **PASTA** | Veracode | Process-driven, business-aligned | Attack scenarios | | **VAST** | ThreatModeler | Agile/scalable threat modeling | Visual threat models | | **ATT&CK** | MITRE | Adversary tactics, techniques, procedures | TTP matrix | ### DREAD Risk Scoring Formula (Critical for KTU Problems) The **DREAD** model quantifies threat severity on a scale of **1 to 10** for each dimension: $$ R_{DREAD} = \frac{D + R + E + A + D_2}{5} $$ Where: - $D$ (Damage Potential): 1–10 — how bad the damage is - $R$ (Reproducibility): 1–10 — how easily the attack can be reproduced - $E$ (Exploitability): 1–10 — how easy it is to launch - $A$ (Affected Users): 1–10 — what percentage of users are impacted - $D_2$ (Discoverability): 1–10 — how easy the flaw is to discover > [!IMPORTANT] > **DREAD Severity Bands (KTU standard):** > - **0–3** → Low severity > - **4–7** → Medium severity > - **8–10** → High severity (immediate action) ### STRIDE Model (Memorize This — Asked Almost Every Year) | Letter | Threat | Violated Security Property | |---|---|---| | **S** | **S**poofing | Authentication | | **T** | **T**ampering | Integrity | | **R** | **R**epudiation | Non-repudiation | | **I** | **I**nformation Disclosure | Confidentiality | | **D** | **D**enial of Service | Availability | | **E** | **E**levation of Privilege | Authorization | ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility Threat modeling is not academic — it directly feeds into: - **Secure SDLC** (Software Development Life Cycle) - **Penetration Testing scope definition** - **SIEM rule tuning** (Security Information & Event Management) - **Insurance underwriting** for cyber policies - **Compliance mapping** (ISO 27001, NIST CSF, PCI-DSS, GDPR) > [!NOTE] > **CWE/CVE Metrics (Real-Time Threat Intelligence):** > - **CVE** (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures) — unique ID per disclosed vulnerability > - **CVSS** (Common Vulnerability Scoring System) — severity score **0.0 to 10.0** > - **CWE** (Common Weakness Enumeration) — category of weakness > As of 2024, the NVD lists over **240,000+ CVEs** with an average CVSS of **7.5** (High). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example: DREAD Risk Score Computation **Problem Statement (Model KTU-style):** > A university portal suffers from a SQL Injection vulnerability in its login form. The vulnerability allows full database dump. Evaluate the threat using the DREAD model and classify its severity. ### Step 1 — Assign Individual DREAD Scores | Dimension | Justification | Score (1–10) | |---|---|---| | **D**amage Potential | Full DB exposure, PII leak, possible admin access | **9** | | **R**eproducibility | Easily reproducible with simple input strings | **10** | | **E**xploitability | Publicly available SQLi tools (sqlmap) — minimal skill | **9** | | **A**ffected Users | All registered users (~50,000) impacted | **10** | | **D**iscoverability | Discoverable via basic input fuzzing | **9** | ### Step 2 — Apply the DREAD Formula $$ R_{DREAD} = \frac{D + R + E + A + D_2}{5} $$ $$ R_{DREAD} = \frac{9 + 10 + 9 + 10 + 9}{5} $$ $$ R_{DREAD} = \frac{47}{5} $$ $$ R_{DREAD} = 9.4 $$ ### Step 3 — Classify Severity Since $9.4 \geq 8.0$, the threat is classified as **HIGH SEVERITY**. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often forget to add the **Discoverability** dimension or divide by the wrong denominator. Always divide by **5** (the number of DREAD dimensions), not 4 or 6. Also, integer scores must be assigned **before** averaging. ## 3.2 Worked Example: CVSS v3.1 Base Score Derivation The **CVSS Base Score** is computed from three metric groups. A simplified derivation for a *critical remote code execution* flaw: ### Input Metrics - **Attack Vector (AV):** Network = **0.85** - **Attack Complexity (AC):** Low = **0.77** - **Privileges Required (PR):** None = **0.85** - **User Interaction (UI):** None = **0.85** - **Scope (S):** Changed = reference value - **Confidentiality (C):** High = **0.56** - **Integrity (I):** High = **0.56** - **Availability (A):** High = **0.56** ### Step-by-Step Calculation **Step 1 — Compute Impact Sub-Score (ISS):** $$ ISS = 1 - \left[(1 - C) \times (1 - I) \times (1 - A)\right] $$ $$ ISS = 1 - \left[(1 - 0.56) \times (1 - 0.56) \times (1 - 0.56)\right] $$ $$ ISS = 1 - \left[0.44 \times 0.44 \times 0.44\right] $$ $$ ISS = 1 - \left[0.085184\right] $$ $$ ISS = 0.914816 $$ **Step 2 — Compute Impact Score (Impact):** Since Scope is **Changed**: $$ Impact = 7.52 \times (ISS - 0.029) - 3.25 \times \left[(ISS - 0.02)^{15}\right] $$ $$ Impact = 7.52 \times (0.914816 - 0.029) - 3.25 \times (0.894816)^{15} $$ $$ Impact = 7.52 \times 0.885816 - 3.25 \times 0.1829 $$ $$ Impact = 6.6613 - 0.5944 $$ $$ Impact \approx 6.07 $$ **Step 3 — Compute Exploitability Score:** $$ Exploit = 8.22 \times AV \times AC \times PR \times UI $$ $$ Exploit = 8.22 \times 0.85 \times 0.77 \times 0.85 \times 0.85 $$ $$ Exploit = 8.22 \times 0.4720 $$ $$ Exploit \approx 3.88 $$ **Step 4 — Compute Base Score:** Since Impact > 0 and Scope = Changed: $$ Base = \min\left[Impact + Exploit, 10\right] $$ $$ Base = \min[6.07 + 3.88, 10] = \min[9.95, 10] $$ $$ \boxed{Base \approx 9.95 \text{ (Critical)} } $$ ## 3.3 Threat × Vulnerability × Risk Analytical Matrix The following 3×3 analytical matrix maps how **threat capability** interacts with **vulnerability severity** to produce **residual risk**: | | **Vuln: Low** | **Vuln: Medium** | **Vuln: High** | |---|---|---|---| | **Threat: Low** | Negligible | Low | Medium | | **Threat: Medium** | Low | **Medium** | **High** | | **Threat: High** | Medium | **High** | **Critical** | The risk level is computed as: $$ Risk = Threat\_Level \times Vulnerability\_Severity $$ A **High × High = Critical** cell demands immediate remediation under the **NIST 800-40 patch management** window (typically ≤ 15 days for critical). # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Threat Classification Hierarchy The following Mermaid block-diagram provides a hierarchical taxonomy of cybersecurity threats as prescribed in Module 1 of the KTU 2024 syllabus: ```mermaid graph TD A[CYBER THREATS]:::root A --> B[Intentional]:::branch A --> C[Unintentional]:::branch B --> D[Passive Attacks]:::sub B --> E[Active Attacks]:::sub D --> D1[Eavesdropping] D --> D2[Traffic Analysis] E --> E1[Malware Based] E --> E2[Network Based] E --> E3[Web Based] E --> E4[Social Engineering] E1 --> E1a[Virus] E1 --> E1b[Worm] E1 --> E1c[Trojan] E1 --> E1d[Ransomware] E1 --> E1e[Spyware] E2 --> E2a[DDoS] E2 --> E2b[Man in Middle] E2 --> E2c[DNS Poisoning] E2 --> E2d[ARP Spoofing] E3 --> E3a[SQL Injection] E3 --> E3b[Cross Site Scripting] E3 --> E3c[CSRF] E3 --> E3d[Buffer Overflow] E4 --> E4a[Phishing] E4 --> E4b[Spear Phishing] E4 --> E4c[Vishing] E4 --> E4d[Pretexting] C --> F1[Human Error] C --> F2[Misconfiguration] C --> F3[Accidental Data Leak] classDef root fill:#1a1a2e,stroke:#e94560,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff classDef branch fill:#16213e,stroke:#0f3460,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef sub fill:#0f3460,stroke:#533483,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.2 Threat-to-Mitigation Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture) The following diagram shows how an identified threat flows through the **Identify → Protect → Detect → Respond → Recover** (NIST CSF) pipeline: ```mermaid graph LR A[Threat Actor]:::actor --> B[Attack Vector]:::vector B --> C{Vulnerability Exists?}:::decision C -->|Yes| D[Attack Succeeds]:::attack C -->|No| E[Attack Blocked]:::safe D --> F[Compromise CIA]:::impact F --> G[SIEM Detection]:::detect G --> H[Incident Response]:::respond H --> I[Recovery and Patch]:::recover I --> J[Threat Intelligence Update]:::learn J --> A E --> K[Log and Monitor]:::safe K --> A classDef actor fill:#2d132c,stroke:#801336,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef vector fill:#801336,stroke:#c72c41,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef decision fill:#ee9b00,stroke:#ca6702,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef attack fill:#bb3e03,stroke:#ae2012,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff classDef impact fill:#9b2226,stroke:#ae2012,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef detect fill:#005f73,stroke:#0a9396,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef respond fill:#0a9396,stroke:#94d2bd,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef recover fill:#94d2bd,stroke:#e9d8a6,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef learn fill:#e9d8a6,stroke:#ca6702,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef safe fill:#386641,stroke:#6a994e,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.3 STRIDE Threat-to-Defense Mapping Diagram ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "STRIDE Threat Model" S1[S Spoofing]:::s T1[T Tampering]:::t R1[R Repudiation]:::r I1[I Information Disclosure]:::i D1[D Denial of Service]:::d E1[E Elevation of Privilege]:::e end subgraph "Defensive Controls" S1 --> AUTH[Authentication
MFA Certificates] T1 --> INTG[Integrity Checks
Hashing Digital Signatures] R1 --> LOG[Audit Logs
Digital Signatures] I1 --> ENC[Encryption
Access Control] D1 --> AVAIL[Rate Limiting
Redundancy CDNs] E1 --> AUTHZ[Authorization
Least Privilege] end classDef s fill:#d62828,stroke:#003049,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef t fill:#f77f00,stroke:#d62828,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef r fill:#fcbf49,stroke:#f77f00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef i fill:#eae2b7,stroke:#fcbf49,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef d fill:#003049,stroke:#d62828,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef e fill:#669bbc,stroke:#003049,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define a cyber threat. Differentiate between threat, vulnerability, and risk with a suitable example. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** A **cyber threat** is any potential cause of an unwanted incident that may result in harm to an information system or network. It represents the *possibility* of a malicious event, not the event itself. | Term | Definition | Example | |---|---|---| | **Threat** | Potential danger that can exploit a weakness | A malware author creating ransomware | | **Vulnerability** | A weakness in the system | Unpatched SMBv1 protocol | | **Risk** | Probability × Impact of a threat exploiting a vulnerability | 70% chance of WannaCry-style infection due to SMBv1 flaw | In the **WannaCry attack (2017)**, the *threat* was the Shadow Brokers' leaked NSA exploit (EternalBlue), the *vulnerability* was SMBv1 in older Windows, and the *risk* was the high probability of global infection — which materialized, infecting 230,000+ machines. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for differentiation table, 1 for example)* --- ### Question 2: List and briefly explain any FIVE categories of malware-based threats. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** 1. **Virus** — A malicious program attached to a legitimate file that spreads when the host file is executed. Requires user action. 2. **Worm** — Self-replicating malware that propagates across networks without user intervention (e.g., Code Red, Stuxnet). 3. **Trojan Horse** — Disguised as legitimate software; performs hidden malicious actions such as backdoor creation. 4. **Ransomware** — Encrypts victim data and demands payment for decryption key (e.g., WannaCry, Ryuk, LockBit). 5. **Spyware** — Secretly monitors user activity and transmits data to the attacker (e.g., keyloggers, screen capture malware). 6. **Rootkit** — Gains privileged system access and hides its presence from OS and antivirus tools. *(3 marks: 0.5 each for correct identification, 0.1 for example; mention 5+ to ensure full credit)* --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Internal Choice Provided — Answer ANY ONE of the two** --- ### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(a)** Explain the **STRIDE** threat model in detail. List each threat category and the security property it violates. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A web application has a vulnerability where attackers can inject JavaScript into comment fields that execute in other users' browsers, stealing session cookies. Apply the **DREAD** model to assess this threat. *(7 marks)* --- #### Model Solution for Q.A (a) — STRIDE Explanation (7 marks) **STRIDE** is a threat classification framework developed by **Microsoft** in 1999. It categorizes threats into six types, each corresponding to a violated security property. | # | STRIDE Category | Violated Property | Description | Example | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **S** — **Spoofing** | **Authentication** | Impersonating another user/system | IP spoofing, email spoofing | | 2 | **T** — **Tampering** | **Integrity** | Modifying data in transit or at rest | Modifying cookies, SQL injection | | 3 | **R** — **Repudiation** | **Non-repudiation** | Denying an action without proof | User denies a transaction; no logs | | 4 | **I** — **Info Disclosure** | **Confidentiality** | Exposing data to unauthorized parties | Directory traversal, info leak | | 5 | **D** — **DoS** | **Availability** | Disrupting service for legitimate users | SYN flood, DDoS, logic bombs | | 6 | **E** — **EoP** | **Authorization** | Gaining higher privileges than intended | Buffer overflow → root, privilege escalation | **Application in Threat Modeling:** 1. Decompose the system into components (DFD). 2. For each component, iterate through STRIDE. 3. Document threats, mitigations, and residual risk. 4. Map to security controls (authentication, encryption, etc.). **STRIDE is widely used in Microsoft's SDL (Security Development Lifecycle)** and is the most exam-frequent threat model in KTU. *(7 marks: 1 for introduction, 3 for 6 categories with properties, 1 for examples, 1 for application steps, 1 for professional context)* --- #### Model Solution for Q.A (b) — DREAD for XSS Vulnerability (7 marks) **Step 1 — Identify the Threat:** The vulnerability is a **Stored XSS (Cross-Site Scripting)** in comment fields, leading to session cookie theft. **Step 2 — Assign DREAD Scores (with justification):** | Dimension | Score | Justification | |---|---|---| | **D**amage Potential | **7** | Session hijack allows account takeover, but not direct system compromise | | **R**eproducibility | **10** | XSS payloads are highly reproducible | | **E**xploitability | **8** | Easy with basic JavaScript knowledge; tools like BeEF simplify it | | **A**ffected Users | **9** | All users who view the infected comment page are affected | | **D**iscoverability | **7** | Discoverable through manual testing, but not obvious from UI | **Step 3 — Apply DREAD Formula:** $$ R_{DREAD} = \frac{D + R + E + A + D_2}{5} $$ $$ R_{DREAD} = \frac{7 + 10 + 8 + 9 + 7}{5} $$ $$ R_{DREAD} = \frac{41}{5} = 8.2 $$ **Step 4 — Classification:** Score of **8.2** falls in the **HIGH SEVERITY** band (8–10). **Immediate mitigation required.** **Recommended Mitigations:** - Input sanitization and output encoding - Content Security Policy (CSP) headers - HttpOnly and Secure flags on session cookies - Web Application Firewall (WAF) deployment *(7 marks: 1 for threat identification, 2 for DREAD scoring table, 2 for formula and arithmetic, 1 for classification, 1 for mitigation)* --- ### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(a)** What is a **threat actor**? Classify threat actors based on skill, motivation, and origin with at least **six categories**. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Explain the **CVSS v3.1** framework. Compute the base score for a vulnerability with the following metrics: AV=Network, AC=Low, PR=None, UI=None, S=Changed, C=High, I=High, A=High. *(7 marks)* --- #### Model Solution for Q.B (a) — Threat Actor Classification (7 marks) A **threat actor** is any individual, group, or entity responsible for a cyber attack or unauthorized event. They are characterized by their **capability, intent, and origin**. | # | Threat Actor | Skill | Motivation | Origin | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Script Kiddies** | Low | Curiosity, fame | External | | 2 | **Hacktivists** | Medium | Ideology, protest | External | | 3 | **Cybercriminals** | High | Financial gain | External (mostly) | | 4 | **Insider Threats** | Variable | Revenge, financial, accidental | **Internal** | | 5 | **Nation-State APTs** | Very High | Espionage, warfare | External (state-backed) | | 6 | **Terrorist Groups** | Medium-High | Ideological destruction | External | | 7 | **Competitors** | Medium | Industrial espionage | External | | 8 | **Botnet Operators** | Medium | Rent-out, DDoS-for-hire | External | **Key Observations:** - **Internal threats** (insiders) cause **60% of data breaches** in studies (IBM Cost of a Data Breach Report). - **APTs** are the most dangerous — patient, persistent, well-funded (e.g., APT29/Cozy Bear, Lazarus Group). - **Insider threats** are hardest to detect because they have legitimate access. *(7 marks: 1 for definition, 5 for classification table covering 6+ actors, 1 for key observations)* --- #### Model Solution for Q.B (b) — CVSS v3.1 Base Score (7 marks) The **Common Vulnerability Scoring System v3.1** is an industry-standard for rating vulnerability severity from **0.0 to 10.0**. **Given Metrics:** AV = Network (0.85), AC = Low (0.77), PR = None (0.85), UI = None (0.85), S = Changed, C = High (0.56), I = High (0.56), A = High (0.56). **Step 1 — Impact Sub-Score (ISS):** $$ ISS = 1 - \left[(1 - 0.56)(1 - 0.56)(1 - 0.56)\right] $$ $$ ISS = 1 - (0.44)^3 = 1 - 0.085184 = 0.914816 $$ **Step 2 — Impact Score (Scope = Changed):** $$ Impact = 7.52 \times (ISS - 0.029) - 3.25 \times (ISS - 0.02)^{15} $$ $$ Impact = 7.52 \times 0.885816 - 3.25 \times (0.894816)^{15} $$ $$ Impact = 6.6613 - 3.25 \times 0.1829 $$ $$ Impact = 6.6613 - 0.5944 = 6.067 \approx 6.07 $$ **Step 3 — Exploitability Score:** $$ Exploit = 8.22 \times 0.85 \times 0.77 \times 0.85 \times 0.85 $$ $$ Exploit = 8.22 \times 0.4720 = 3.880 \approx 3.88 $$ **Step 4 — Base Score:** $$ Base = \min(Impact + Exploit, 10) = \min(6.07 + 3.88, 10) = 9.95 $$ $$ \boxed{Base = 9.95 \rightarrow \text{CRITICAL Severity}} $$ **Severity Rating Scale (CVSS v3.1):** - 0.1 – 3.9 → **Low** - 4.0 – 6.9 → **Medium** - 7.0 – 8.9 → **High** - **9.0 – 10.0 → Critical** *(7 marks: 1 for framework intro, 2 for ISS+Impact calculation, 2 for Exploit calculation, 1 for Base score and rounding, 1 for severity interpretation)* --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Traps in "Threats" Questions:** > 1. **Conflating Threat with Attack** — Threat is *potential*; Attack is *realized*. Many students use them interchangeably and lose 1–2 marks. > 2. **DREAD Formula Mistakes** — Forgetting to include **Discoverability (D₂)** or dividing by 4 instead of 5. Always divide by **5**. > 3. **STRIDE vs CIA Confusion** — STRIDE categorizes *threats*; CIA is the *security goal*. They are different frameworks, not alternatives. > 4. **CVSS Arithmetic Errors** — The term $(ISS - 0.02)^{15}$ decays very fast. Use a calculator. Approximation is fine if reasoned; random guesses are not. > 5. **Missing Severity Bands** — KTU expects the *interpretation* of the score, not just the number. Always state the severity band (Low/Medium/High/Critical). > 6. **No Examples in STRIDE** — Memorize one real-world example per STRIDE category. Examiners reward applied knowledge. > 7. **Insider vs External Origin** — When asked "origin", write *Internal* or *External* explicitly. Don't say "local" or "far" — those are not KTU-acceptable terms. --- ## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Threats (Module 1)** - 🔑 **Threat = Capability + Intent + Opportunity** ($T = f(C, I, O)$). A threat is *potential*, not *actual*. - 🔑 **Risk = Threat × Vulnerability × Impact** — a triple product, not a sum. - 🔑 **STRIDE** has **6 letters** mapping to **6 security properties**: Spoofing→AuthN, Tampering→Integrity, Repudiation→Non-repudiation, Info Disclosure→Confidentiality, DoS→Availability, EoP→AuthZ. - 🔑 **DREAD** has **5 dimensions**, each scored **1–10**, averaged to give severity. **Divide by 5**, not 4. - 🔑 **DREAD Severity Bands**: 0–3 (Low), 4–7 (Medium), 8–10 (High). - 🔑 **CVSS v3.1** range: **0.0 – 10.0**. Severity bands: 0.1–3.9 (Low), 4.0–6.9 (Medium), 7.0–8.9 (High), 9.0–10.0 (Critical). - 🔑 **Six major threat actor categories**: Script Kiddies, Hacktivists, Cybercriminals, Insider Threats, Nation-State APTs, Terrorist Groups. - 🔑 **Malware taxonomy**: Virus (needs host), Worm (self-propagates), Trojan (disguised), Ransomware (encrypts + extorts), Spyware (monitors), Rootkit (hides). - 🔑 **Web threats**: **SQLi** (database breach), **XSS** (browser-side attack), **CSRF** (forged requests), **Buffer Overflow** (memory corruption). - 🔑 **Social Engineering**: Phishing (broad), Spear Phishing (targeted), Whaling (executives), Vishing (voice), Smishing (SMS), Pretexting (false scenario). - 🔑 **Insider threats** account for ~60% of breaches and are hardest to detect due to legitimate access. - 🔑 **APT** = Advanced Persistent Threat; characterized by **stealth, persistence, and state-level resources**. - 🔑 **MITRE ATT&CK** is the current industry-standard TTP (Tactics, Techniques, Procedures) knowledge base. - 🔑 **Zero-day threats** exploit unknown vulnerabilities — no patch exists at the time of attack. - 🔑 **Defense-in-depth** principle: never rely on a single control; layer **Identify → Protect → Detect → Respond → Recover** (NIST CSF). --- **End of Module 1 — Topic: Threats | OECST721 — Cyber Security | KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: Computer Criminals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST721 - CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Computer Criminals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Computer Criminal (Cybercriminal):** A computer criminal is an individual or an organized group of individuals who deliberately and unlawfully engage in malicious digital activities using computer systems, networks, or the internet, with the intent to cause financial loss, data theft, identity fraud, reputational damage, intellectual property violation, espionage, or disruption of critical services.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, **Computer Criminals** are classified as the **human threat actors** who initiate, orchestrate, or execute cyber-attacks. The syllabus treats them as the *origin* of the threat model — before any technical defense (firewalls, cryptography, IDS) is discussed, the student must first **profile the adversary**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bank Vault and the Burglar"

Imagine a bank vault protected by the most advanced locks, CCTV cameras, and motion sensors. But before designing the vault, the bank's security chief first asks: **"Who is the burglar? Is it a curious teenager, a professional thief, or a foreign spy?"**

- The **vault** = your computer system / network
- The **locks & alarms** = cyber security tools (firewalls, encryption, antivirus)
- The **burglar** = the **Computer Criminal**

Just as different burglars require different counter-measures (a teenager is stopped by a simple lock, a spy needs biometrics and armed guards), different categories of computer criminals require different layers of defense. This is the foundation of the famous **"Know Your Enemy"** principle in cyber security.

### 1.3 Key Terminology — Highlight Box

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Glossary Terms (Must Memorize for Exams):**
> - **Threat Actor** — Any person or entity responsible for an event that impacts the security of an information system.
> - **Attack Vector** — The path or means by which a criminal gains unauthorized access to a system.
> - **Insider Threat** — A threat originating from within the organization (employee, contractor).
> - **Outsider Threat** — A threat originating from outside the organization.
> - **Script Kiddie** — An unskilled individual who uses pre-written scripts or tools to attack systems.
> - **State-Sponsored Actor** — A criminal operating on behalf of a national government.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics of Cybercrime (Board-Important Data)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Global Cybercrime Statistics (Industry Standard References):**
> - **Average cost of a data breach:** **USD 4.45 million** (per IBM Cost of a Data Breach Report).
> - **Cybercrime growth rate:** Approximately **15% year-over-year**.
> - **Human element involvement in breaches:** **~74%** of breaches involve a human element (phishing, error, misuse).
> - **Ransomware detection rate:** A new ransomware attack occurs roughly **every 2 seconds** globally.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Computer Criminals — Structured Logic

The KTU 2024 syllabus categorizes computer criminals along **three primary dimensions** (this is a **favourite 7-mark theory question**):

#### Dimension 1: By **Identity / Origin**

| Type | Description | Example |
|------|-------------|---------|
| **Insiders** | Employees, contractors, or partners with legitimate access. | Disgruntled employee stealing customer data. |
| **Outsiders** | External attackers with no authorized access. | A hacker breaking into a bank's server. |
| **Insider-Outsider Collusion** | Combined attack by internal + external actors. | Employee sells VPN credentials to a hacker group. |
| **State-Sponsored Actors** | Operate on behalf of a nation-state. | APT (Advanced Persistent Threat) groups. |
| **Organized Cybercrime Syndicates** | Structured criminal enterprises. | Ransomware-as-a-Service (RaaS) gangs. |

#### Dimension 2: By **Skill Level / Technical Capability**

The KTU syllabus follows the classic **hierarchy of hackers** (William Stallings, *Cryptography and Network Security* canonical reference, used universally in KTU approved texts):

1. **Novices / Script Kiddies** — Use pre-compiled tools, no real coding knowledge. **Lowest skill, highest volume of attacks.**
2. **Cyberpunks** — Moderately skilled, motivated by thrill and reputation.
3. **Hackers (White Hat)** — Ethical, security professionals. **Defenders.**
4. **Crackers (Black Hat)** — Malicious, break systems for personal gain. **Offenders.**
5. **Hacktivists** — Politically or socially motivated (e.g., Anonymous).
6. **Phreakers** — Specialize in telephone system abuse.
7. **Professional / Elite Hackers** — Highest skill, often state-sponsored. **APT operators.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hacker vs. Cracker — Board Distinction (Frequently Asked):**
> - **Hacker:** Originally meant a *curious, skilled programmer* who explores systems. In modern ethical usage, a *White Hat* — works to *defend* systems.
> - **Cracker:** Specifically refers to a *Black Hat* — breaks into systems with *malicious intent*.
> - **Media misuse:** Popular media wrongly calls *all* malicious intruders "hackers." KTU answers should use **cracker** for malicious actors.

#### Dimension 3: By **Motivation / Intent**

- **Financial Gain** — Banking fraud, credit card theft, ransomware. *(Most common motivator.)*
- **Revenge / Personal Vendetta** — Disgruntled employees.
- **Political / Ideological** — Hacktivism, cyberterrorism.
- **Intellectual Challenge / Curiosity** — Script kiddies, novices.
- **Espionage** — Nation-state actors stealing state secrets.
- **Fame / Reputation** — Wanting to be recognized in underground forums.
- **Fun / Thrill** — Recreational attackers.

### 2.2 The Insider Threat — Deep Dive

> [!NOTE]
> **The Insider Threat is statistically the most dangerous.** According to industry reports, **insiders cause ~60% of data breaches**, because they already have **legitimate access**, **trust**, and **knowledge of system weaknesses**.

**Sub-categories of Insiders:**
- **Pure Insider** — Employee with full access.
- **Affiliated Insider** — Contractor, vendor, ex-employee.
- **Unaffiliated Insider** — A non-employee who has been granted temporary access (e.g., auditor, consultant).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula Sheet — Threat Profiling Metrics:**
>
> While there are no direct "formulas" in computer criminals topic, examiners expect students to **know and apply** these conceptual equations for attack analysis:
>
> | Conceptual Equation | Meaning | KTU Board Use |
> |---------------------|---------|---------------|
> | $\text{Threat} = f(\text{Actor}, \text{Capability}, \text{Opportunity}, \text{Motive})$ | A threat exists when all four converge. | 3-mark definition question. |
> | $\text{Risk} = \text{Threat} \times \text{Vulnerability} \times \text{Impact}$ | Standard risk model. | 7-mark question on risk. |
> | $\text{Attack Surface} = \sum (\text{Entry Points} \times \text{Access Level})$ | Aggregate exposure of a system. | Frequently asked. |
> | $P(\text{Breach}) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | Poisson model of breach probability over time. | Higher-order analytical question. |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Topic Matters for Every KTU Engineer:**
> 1. **Software Engineers** — Must design code resistant to insider logic-bombs.
> 2. **Network Engineers** — Must identify external threat patterns (DDoS, scans).
> 3. **Civil Engineers (Smart Infrastructure)** — SCADA systems are prime targets for *state-sponsored* actors.
> 4. **Electrical Engineers (IoT)** — Botnet herders recruit millions of IoT devices; understanding criminal motives is the first defense.
> 5. **Management / MBA-track students** — Insider threat modeling is a *governance* and *audit* concern.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Derivation

### 3.1 The Threat Modeling Framework (KTU Analytical Tool)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a Framework?** When KTU asks a 14-mark question like *"Analyze the different types of computer criminals and their impact on organizations,"* a structured analytical matrix is the only way to secure full marks.

**Step 1 — Establish the Threat Equation**

A computer criminal's *successful attack* is mathematically conceptualized as a function of four variables. We will now derive this formally.

$$
T = f(A, C, M, O)
$$

where:
- $T$ = Threat severity
- $A$ = Actor's identity & affiliation
- $C$ = Capability (skill + resources)
- $M$ = Motive (intent, payoff)
- $O$ = Opportunity (vulnerability + access)

$$
T = A \times C \times M \times O
$$

> If **any one** of the four variables equals **zero**, the threat is *neutralized*. This is the **core defensive principle**: deny the criminal *opportunity* (patch systems, enforce least-privilege), and even the most skilled attacker is blocked.

**Step 2 — Probability of Insider Breach (Analytical Derivation)**

We model insider breaches using a **Poisson process**, which is the standard statistical model for rare, independent security events.

$$
P(\text{at least one breach in time } t) = 1 - P(\text{no breach in time } t)
$$

By the Poisson distribution property:

$$
P(N(t) = k) = \frac{(\lambda t)^k \cdot e^{-\lambda t}}{k!}
$$

For $k = 0$ (zero breaches):

$$
P(N(t) = 0) = e^{-\lambda t}
$$

Therefore:

$$
P(\text{Breach within time } t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}
$$

> where $\lambda$ is the **breach rate** (breaches per year, calibrated from historical incident data). This formula is **directly applicable** if a KTU numerical question gives you $\lambda$ and $t$.

**Step 3 — Worked Example (For KTU Problem-Solving Practice)**

**Problem:** An organization has a historical insider breach rate of $\lambda = 0.3$ breaches per year. Calculate the probability of **at least one breach** in the next **2 years**.

**Solution:**

$$
P(\text{breach in 2 years}) = 1 - e^{-(0.3)(2)}
$$

$$
P = 1 - e^{-0.6}
$$

$$
P = 1 - 0.5488
$$

$$
P \approx 0.4512 \approx 45.12\%
$$

> **Answer:** There is a **45.12% probability** of an insider breach within 2 years. This single calculation can win a full 7-mark analytical question in KTU.

**Step 4 — Multi-Category Risk Aggregation**

For an organization facing *multiple categories* of criminals (insider + outsider + hacktivist), the **aggregate risk** is the sum of individual risks:

$$
R_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (P_i \times I_i)
$$

where:
- $P_i$ = Probability of attack by criminal category $i$
- $I_i$ = Impact (financial, reputational) of that attack

**Step 5 — Decision Logic for Defensive Allocation**

$$
\text{Defense Budget Allocation} \propto R_i = P_i \times I_i
$$

This means the organization must allocate **more budget** to defending against the *high-probability, high-impact* criminal categories. For example, if insiders have $P = 0.45$ and impact $I = \$1M$, while outsiders have $P = 0.20$ and $I = \$5M$, then:

$$
R_{\text{insider}} = 0.45 \times 1 = 0.45
$$

$$
R_{\text{outsider}} = 0.20 \times 5 = 1.00
$$

> Even though insiders are *more probable*, the **financial impact** of outsider attacks dominates, justifying a larger budget for perimeter defenses (firewalls, IDS).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart of Computer Criminals

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Computer Criminals] --> B[By Identity]
    A --> C[By Skill Level]
    A --> D[By Motivation]

    B --> B1[Insider]
    B --> B2[Outsider]
    B --> B3[State-Sponsored]
    B --> B4[Organized Syndicate]

    C --> C1[Script Kiddie]
    C --> C2[Cyberpunk]
    C --> C3[White Hat Hacker]
    C --> C4[Black Hat Cracker]
    C --> C5[Elite APT Actor]

    D --> D1[Financial Gain]
    D --> D2[Revenge]
    D --> D3[Political Hacktivism]
    D --> D4[Espionage]
    D --> D5[Thrill / Curiosity]

    B1 --> E1[Employee]
    B1 --> E2[Contractor]
    B1 --> E3[Ex-Employee]

    B2 --> F1[Individual Hacker]
    B2 --> F2[Hacktivist Group]
    B2 --> F3[Ransomware Gang]

    B3 --> G1[Military Unit]
    B3 --> G2[Intelligence Agency]

    style A fill:#1f77b4,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#2ca02c,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#ff7f0e,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#d62728,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.2 Threat Convergence Model — How an Attack Happens

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[Actor] -->|Who| T[Successful Attack]
    A2[Capability] -->|Skill| T
    A3[Motive] -->|Why| T
    A4[Opportunity] -->|Vulnerability| T

    T --> OUT1[Data Theft]
    T --> OUT2[Financial Fraud]
    T --> OUT3[Service Disruption]
    T --> OUT4[Reputation Damage]

    style T fill:#d62728,color:#ffffff
    style A1 fill:#2ca02c
    style A2 fill:#2ca02c
    style A3 fill:#2ca02c
    style A4 fill:#2ca02c
```

### 4.3 Insider vs. Outsider — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph OrganizationPerimeter
        FW[Firewall]
        IDS[Intrusion Detection System]
        DB[(Internal Database)]
        SRV[Application Server]
    end

    EXT[External Outsider] -->|Attempts Entry| FW
    FW -->|Filtered| IDS
    IDS -->|Alerts| SOC[Security Operations Center]

    INS[Malicious Insider] -->|Already Inside| SRV
    INS -->|Authorized Access| DB
    SRV --> DB

    SOC -->|Investigate| FW
    SOC -->|Monitor| INS

    style EXT fill:#ff7f0e
    style INS fill:#d62728
    style FW fill:#2ca02c
    style IDS fill:#2ca02c
    style SOC fill:#1f77b4,color:#ffffff
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Notice that the *Outsider* must defeat **two layers** (Firewall + IDS) to reach internal data, but the *Insider* is *already* past the perimeter. This visually demonstrates why **insider threat defense** requires **behavioral analytics, least-privilege access, and audit logs** — not just perimeter security.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**
**Define the term "Computer Criminal." List any four categories of computer criminals based on motivation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A *computer criminal* is an individual or organized group who deliberately engages in unauthorized and malicious activities using computer systems and networks, intending to cause financial loss, data theft, fraud, or service disruption. *(1 mark for definition)*

Four categories based on motivation: *(2 marks)*
1. **Financial Gain** — e.g., banking fraud, ransomware.
2. **Revenge / Personal Vendetta** — e.g., disgruntled employees.
3. **Political / Ideological** — e.g., hacktivists.
4. **Intellectual Challenge** — e.g., script kiddies and curious novices.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**
**Differentiate between a Hacker and a Cracker. Why is this distinction important in Cyber Security?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Hacker (White Hat) | Cracker (Black Hat) |
|--------|--------------------|---------------------|
| Intent | Ethical, defensive | Malicious, offensive |
| Authorization | Operates with permission | Operates without permission |
| Outcome | Strengthens security | Compromises security |

*(2 marks for table)*

**Importance:** The distinction is important because the legal, ethical, and operational responses differ entirely. A hacker is *protected* under Responsible Disclosure frameworks, while a cracker is *prosecuted* under the IT Act 2000 (India) / equivalent cyber laws. Misclassifying one as the other can lead to legal ambiguity. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**

**(a)** Classify computer criminals based on **(i) Identity** and **(ii) Skill Level**. Provide at least three examples for each category. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Explain the concept of **Insider Threat** in detail. Why is it considered the most dangerous type of computer criminal? Justify with two real-world case scenarios. *(7 Marks)*

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification of Computer Criminals (7 Marks):**

**(i) By Identity — Three Examples:**
- **Insider** — A current employee of the organization who misuses legitimate access. *Example: A bank teller copying customer credit card data for sale.* *(1 mark)*
- **Outsider** — An external attacker with no authorized access. *Example: A hacker in another country launching a phishing campaign on an Indian e-commerce site.* *(1 mark)*
- **State-Sponsored Actor** — A criminal operating on behalf of a national government. *Example: APT groups targeting defense research organizations.* *(1 mark)*

**(ii) By Skill Level — Three Examples:**
- **Script Kiddie** — Uses pre-built tools like *Loic* or *Metasploit* without understanding the code. *(1 mark)*
- **Cracker (Black Hat)** — Skilled programmer who writes custom malware. *(1 mark)*
- **Elite / APT Operator** — Highest skill, often with government resources, runs multi-year campaigns. *(1 mark)*
- *Plus a labelled diagram showing the skill hierarchy.* *(1 mark — see Section 4.1)*

**(b) Insider Threat — Detailed Explanation (7 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** An *insider threat* is a security risk that originates from individuals within the organization — such as employees, former employees, contractors, or business partners — who have inside knowledge concerning the organization's security practices, data, and computer systems.

**Why Most Dangerous (3 marks):**
1. **Pre-existing trust** — Insiders are not blocked by firewalls.
2. **Authorized access** — They do not need to "break in."
3. **Knowledge of weaknesses** — They know which servers hold sensitive data.
4. **Detection difficulty** — Their actions look legitimate in logs.

**Real-World Case Scenarios (3 marks):**
- *Case 1: Twitter Bitcoin Scam (2020) —* A Twitter employee was bribed by attackers to provide internal access, leading to the hijacking of high-profile accounts (Obama, Musk, Gates) for a Bitcoin scam. Loss: ~$120,000 in hours.
- *Case 2: Capital One Data Breach (2019) —* A former AWS employee exploited a misconfigured web application firewall (WAF) to access over 100 million customer records. Loss: $190 million in settlements.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**

**(a)** With the help of a **threat equation**, explain how the convergence of *Actor, Capability, Motive, and Opportunity* leads to a successful cyber-attack. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A mid-sized IT firm in Kerala has a historical insider breach rate of $\lambda = 0.25$ per year. Using the Poisson model, calculate the probability of at least one breach in the next **3 years**. Also, suggest two technical controls to mitigate insider threats. *(7 Marks)*

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Threat Equation (7 Marks):**

**The Equation (2 marks):**

$$
T = A \times C \times M \times O
$$

- $A$ = Actor (insider / outsider / state-sponsored)
- $C$ = Capability (script kiddie / cracker / elite)
- $M$ = Motive (financial / revenge / political)
- $O$ = Opportunity (vulnerability + access)

**Explanation (3 marks):** A threat materializes only when **all four** factors align. A skilled actor with no motive will not attack. A motivated actor with no opportunity cannot attack. The defensive principle is to **set at least one factor to zero** — usually *Opportunity* (by patching, segmentation, access control).

**Real-World Example (2 marks):** The 2017 *WannaCry* ransomware attack: **Actor** = North Korean Lazarus Group, **Capability** = Exploit of *EternalBlue* vulnerability, **Motive** = Financial ransom, **Opportunity** = Unpatched Windows SMB port. The convergence of all four caused global damage of $4 billion.

---

**(b) Poisson Probability Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $\lambda = 0.25$ breaches/year, $t = 3$ years. *(1 mark)*

**Formula:** *(1 mark)*

$$
P(\text{breach}) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}
$$

**Substitution:** *(1 mark)*

$$
P = 1 - e^{-(0.25)(3)} = 1 - e^{-0.75}
$$

**Computation:** *(2 marks)*

$$
P = 1 - 0.4724 = 0.5276
$$

**Answer:** *(1 mark)*
$$
P \approx 52.76\%
$$

> There is a **52.76% probability** of an insider breach in the next 3 years — meaning the firm is at *higher than coin-flip* risk.

**Two Technical Controls to Mitigate Insider Threats (1 mark):**
1. **Principle of Least Privilege (PoLP)** — Grant employees access *only* to the data strictly required for their job.
2. **User Behavior Analytics (UBA)** — Deploy AI-driven monitoring that flags anomalous file access (e.g., downloading 10,000 customer records at 2 AM).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Calling all attackers "hackers."** — Examiners will *deduct marks* if you do not distinguish White Hat (hacker) from Black Hat (cracker).
> 2. **Confusing Insider with Outsider.** — Many students describe an *employee stealing data* as an "external" attack. It is *insider* — read the definition carefully.
> 3. **Forgetting Motive.** — A complete threat profile lists *all four* A-C-M-O elements. Skipping *Motive* is a frequent 2-mark loss.
> 4. **Skipping real-world examples.** — KTU 2024 scheme rewards *applied* answers. A case-study reference (WannaCry, Capital One) elevates a 7-mark answer to full marks.
> 5. **No diagram in 7-mark questions.** — A labelled flowchart in Module 1 theory answers is worth **at least 1–2 extra marks**. Always include one.
> 6. **Forgetting the Indian IT Act 2000 / DPDP Act 2023 reference** — Examiners appreciate statutory awareness. Mentioning the relevant law adds 1 mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Computer Criminals (Module 1)**
> - **Definition:** A computer criminal is a *person or group* who maliciously exploits systems for gain, revenge, politics, or thrill.
> - **3 Classification Axes:** Identity, Skill Level, Motivation.
> - **Identity Categories:** Insider, Outsider, State-Sponsored, Organized Syndicate.
> - **Skill Categories (Low to High):** Script Kiddie → Cyberpunk → Cracker → Hacktivist → Elite APT.
> - **Hacker ≠ Cracker:** Hacker is *defensive*; Cracker is *offensive*. (Memorize the table.)
> - **Insider Threat is most dangerous** — due to trust, access, and detection difficulty.
> - **Threat Equation:** $T = A \times C \times M \times O$. Set one factor to zero = threat neutralized.
> - **Poisson Breach Model:** $P(\text{breach in } t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$. Used in KTU numericals.
> - **Aggregate Risk:** $R_{\text{total}} = \sum (P_i \times I_i)$. Allocate defense budget proportionally.
> - **Real Cases to Quote:** WannaCry (2017), Capital One (2019), Twitter Bitcoin Scam (2020).
> - **Statutory Awareness:** IT Act 2000 (India) §§ 43, 66, 66F — and the Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023.
> - **Golden Rule for 14-mark answers:** Always include a **labelled diagram** + **case study** + **statute reference**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CIA Triad
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST721 - CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** CIA Triad

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **CIA Triad** is the cornerstone conceptual model of information security that defines the three fundamental security objectives any robust system must guarantee to protect its information assets. The acronym **CIA** stands for:

- **C — Confidentiality**
- **I — Integrity**
- **A — Availability**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The CIA Triad forms the **evaluative foundation** against which *every* security control, threat, and countermeasure in subsequent modules is measured. It is universally accepted by standards bodies such as **ISO/IEC 27001**, **NIST SP 800-53**, and **ISACA's COBIT** framework.

The triad was first formalized in the late 1970s within early computing security literature and remains the *de facto* reference architecture for designing secure systems. Together, these three properties define what it means for information to be "secure."

| Pillar | Core Security Goal | Core Question Answered |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Confidentiality** | Prevent unauthorized disclosure | *"Who is allowed to see this data?"* |
| **Integrity** | Prevent unauthorized modification | *"Has the data been tampered with?"* |
| **Availability** | Ensure timely reliable access | *"Can authorized users access it when needed?"* |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **highly secure bank vault** containing your most valuable documents:

- **Confidentiality** is the **lock and biometric access control** on the vault door — only the account holder and authorized bank officials may enter. A stranger peering through the vent would violate confidentiality.
- **Integrity** is the **tamper-evident seal** on the documents inside. If anyone slips a forged paper into the stack or alters a figure, the seal breaks and we instantly know the data has been compromised.
- **Availability** is the **24×7 power backup, redundant vault doors, and fire-resistant construction**. Even during a power outage, flood, or earthquake, the customer can still reach their documents when needed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** If *any one* of the three pillars fails, the system is no longer considered secure — even if the other two are perfectly maintained. A database that is confidential and tamper-proof but offline 90% of the time is a **failed** secure system.

## 1.3 The Triad in the KTU Context

For OECST721 (Cyber Security), understanding the CIA Triad is a **prerequisite cognitive anchor** for topics like cryptography (Module 2), access control (Module 3), network security (Module 4), and risk management (Module 5). Examiners routinely frame questions such as *"Classify the following attack under the CIA pillar it violates"* — making this topic **high-yield** for both **Part A (3-mark)** and **Part B (12/14-mark)** questions.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars — Detailed Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Confidentiality

**Definition:** Confidentiality ensures that information is accessible *only* to those entities (people, processes, systems) that have been explicitly authorized to view it.

**Why it matters:** Unauthorized disclosure of data can lead to identity theft, corporate espionage, breach of privacy laws (e.g., India's DPDP Act 2023, EU's GDPR), and reputational collapse.

**How it is enforced:**
- **Encryption** (AES-256, RSA, ECC) at rest and in transit
- **Access Control Lists (ACLs)** and Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)
- **Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)**
- **Data classification and labeling** (Public, Internal, Confidential, Restricted)
- **Steganography** for hiding existence of data
- **Non-Disclosure Agreements (NDAs)** in legal/HR contexts

**Common threats violating confidentiality:**
- Eavesdropping, sniffing, shoulder surfing, social engineering, man-in-the-middle attacks, insider threats.

### 2.1.2 Integrity

**Definition:** Integrity guarantees that information remains *accurate, complete, and unaltered* except through authorized and traceable modification.

**Why it matters:** Corrupted or tampered data can lead to catastrophic decisions — wrong medical prescriptions, fraudulent financial transfers, falsified audit logs, or manipulated sensor readings in industrial control systems (ICS).

**How it is enforced:**
- **Hashing algorithms** (SHA-256, SHA-3) for fixed-length fingerprints
- **Message Authentication Codes (HMAC)**
- **Digital Signatures** (RSA-PSS, ECDSA)
- **Checksums, CRCs, and version control systems**
- **Database constraints, transaction logs, and audit trails**
- **Blockchain / Merkle trees** for tamper-evident records

**Common threats violating integrity:**
- Man-in-the-middle, SQL injection, log tampering, malware modification, replay attacks, bit-flipping attacks.

### 2.1.3 Availability

**Definition:** Availability ensures that information and computing services are accessible to authorized users *whenever required*, without unreasonable delay.

**Why it matters:** Downtime translates directly to lost revenue, productivity, and in safety-critical systems (e.g., aviation, healthcare), potential loss of life. Industry Service Level Agreements (SLAs) routinely target **99.999% ("Five Nines")** uptime.

**How it is enforced:**
- **Redundancy** (RAID, clustered servers, geo-replication)
- **Load balancers and failover clusters**
- **Backup and disaster recovery (DR) plans**
- **DDoS mitigation** (rate limiting, scrubbing centers, CDNs, Anycast)
- **Uninterruptible Power Supplies (UPS)** and generator backups
- **Patch management and preventive maintenance**

**Common threats violating availability:**
- Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS), ransomware, hardware failure, natural disasters, sabotage, misconfiguration outages.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Concept Matrix

| Pillar | Core Property | Primary Cryptographic Tool | Typical Attack | KTU 2024 Frequency |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Confidentiality** | Secrecy | Symmetric/Asymmetric Encryption | Sniffing, Eavesdropping | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Integrity** | Trustworthiness | Hash Functions, Digital Signatures | Tampering, MITM | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Availability** | Reliability | Redundancy, Replication | DDoS, Ransomware | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Healthcare (HIPAA compliance):** Patient Electronic Health Records (EHR) demand strong CIA — confidentiality to protect privacy, integrity to prevent prescription errors, availability for emergency room access.
- **Banking & Finance (PCI-DSS):** Online transactions must be confidential (TLS), integrity-checked (digital signatures), and available 24×7.
- **Industrial IoT & SCADA:** Integrity of sensor data is critical; an attacker altering temperature readings in a nuclear plant violates integrity with potentially catastrophic outcomes.
- **Cloud Computing (AWS, Azure, GCP):** The shared responsibility model explicitly maps CIA responsibilities between cloud provider and customer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Extended Triad Note:** Modern security frameworks (notably the **Parkerian Hexad**) extend CIA with *Possession, Authenticity,* and *Utility*. However, **KTU 2024 syllabus strictly limits the scope to the original CIA Triad** in Module 1.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Mathematical Modeling of CIA in Risk

While the CIA Triad is largely conceptual, KTU examinations often frame it through a **quantitative risk model**. Let us derive the relationship.

### 3.1.1 Defining Risk per CIA Pillar

The fundamental risk equation in information security is:

$$R = T \times V \times I$$

Where:
- $R$ = Risk (the potential impact of a security breach)
- $T$ = Threat (probability that an attack will occur)
- $V$ = Vulnerability (probability the threat succeeds)
- $I$ = Impact (the business damage caused)

### 3.1.2 Extending Risk to the CIA Triad

Each pillar contributes an independent risk vector. The **total CIA risk** is the weighted sum of pillar-specific risks:

$$R_{\text{CIA}} = w_C \cdot R_C + w_I \cdot R_I + w_A \cdot R_A$$

Subject to the constraint:

$$w_C + w_I + w_A = 1, \quad w_C, w_I, w_A \in \mathbb{R}^+$$

Where the weights $w_C, w_I, w_A$ reflect organizational priority (e.g., a bank may set $w_C = 0.5, w_I = 0.4, w_A = 0.1$).

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Substitution

Let us substitute the base risk equation into each pillar:

$$R_C = T_C \cdot V_C \cdot I_C \quad \text{(Confidentiality risk)}$$

$$R_I = T_I \cdot V_I \cdot I_I \quad \text{(Integrity risk)}$$

$$R_A = T_A \cdot V_A \cdot I_A \quad \text{(Availability risk)}$$

Plugging into the total:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{CIA}} &= w_C \cdot (T_C \cdot V_C \cdot I_C) \\
&\quad + w_I \cdot (T_I \cdot V_I \cdot I_I) \\
&\quad + w_A \cdot (T_A \cdot V_A \cdot I_A)
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.1.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** An e-commerce system has the following estimates:
- Confidentiality: $T_C = 0.4, V_C = 0.6, I_C = 50$ units
- Integrity: $T_I = 0.3, V_I = 0.7, I_I = 80$ units
- Availability: $T_A = 0.5, V_A = 0.5, I_A = 40$ units
- Weights: $w_C = 0.4, w_I = 0.3, w_A = 0.3$

**Step 1: Calculate $R_C$**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_C &= T_C \cdot V_C \cdot I_C \\
&= 0.4 \times 0.6 \times 50 \\
&= 12.0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Calculate $R_I$**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_I &= T_I \cdot V_I \cdot I_I \\
&= 0.3 \times 0.7 \times 80 \\
&= 16.8
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Calculate $R_A$**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_A &= T_A \cdot V_A \cdot I_A \\
&= 0.5 \times 0.5 \times 40 \\
&= 10.0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Weighted Total Risk**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{CIA}} &= (0.4 \times 12.0) + (0.3 \times 16.8) + (0.3 \times 10.0) \\
&= 4.8 + 5.04 + 3.0 \\
&= 12.84
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** Integrity is the highest individual risk contributor ($R_I = 16.8$), so the organization should prioritize integrity controls first.

## 3.2 Availability Uptime Calculation

Availability is often expressed as a percentage:

$$A_{\%} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100$$

Where:
- $\text{MTBF}$ = Mean Time Between Failures
- $\text{MTTR}$ = Mean Time To Repair

**Example:** If $\text{MTBF} = 500$ hours and $\text{MTTR} = 1$ hour:

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{\%} &= \frac{500}{500 + 1} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{500}{501} \times 100 \\
&\approx 99.80\%
\end{aligned}$$

This corresponds to roughly **"Three Nines"** of availability.

| Availability Tier | Uptime % | Downtime/Year |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Two Nines | 99% | 3.65 days |
| Three Nines | 99.9% | 8.77 hours |
| Four Nines | 99.99% | 52.6 minutes |
| **Five Nines** | **99.999%** | **5.26 minutes** |

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The CIA Triad Core Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Information Security] --> B[CIA Triad Foundation]
    B --> C[Confidentiality]
    B --> D[Integrity]
    B --> E[Availability]
    C --> C1[Encryption AES RSA]
    C --> C2[Access Control RBAC]
    C --> C3[MFA Authentication]
    D --> D1[Hashing SHA-256]
    D --> D2[Digital Signatures]
    D --> D3[Audit Trails]
    E --> E1[Redundancy RAID]
    E --> E2[DDoS Mitigation]
    E --> E3[Backup DR Plans]
```

## 4.2 Attack-to-Pillar Threat Mapping

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Sniffing Attack] --> C
    P2[SQL Injection] --> I
    P3[DDoS Attack] --> A
    P4[Phishing] --> C
    P5[Log Tampering] --> I
    P6[Ransomware] --> A
    P7[MITM] --> I
    C[Confidentiality]
    I[Integrity]
    A[Availability]
```

## 4.3 Defense-in-Depth Layered Model

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1[Layer 1 Perimeter Security Firewalls IDS] --> L2[Layer 2 Network Security VPN TLS]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3 Host Security Antivirus Patching]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4 Application Security Input Validation]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5 Data Security Encryption Hashing]
    L5 --> CIA[CIA Triad Achieved]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The Defense-in-Depth model demonstrates that *all* three CIA pillars are protected only when **multiple independent layers** operate correctly. Failure of any single layer increases the risk of the corresponding CIA pillar being violated.

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the CIA Triad. List its three components. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

The **CIA Triad** is the foundational model of information security that defines the three primary security objectives that any secure system must satisfy.

The three components are:
1. **Confidentiality** — Ensuring that information is accessible only to authorized entities.
2. **Integrity** — Ensuring that information is accurate and has not been tampered with by unauthorized parties.
3. **Availability** — Ensuring that information and services are accessible to authorized users whenever required.

**[3 Marks: 1 for definition, 2 for components]**

### Question 2: Identify which CIA pillar is violated in each of the following scenarios: (i) A hacker intercepts credit card data over an unencrypted Wi-Fi network, (ii) An attacker modifies a patient's blood test report in a hospital database, (iii) A DDoS attack renders an e-commerce website unreachable. **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

(i) **Confidentiality** — Unauthorized disclosure of credit card data via interception violates secrecy.
(ii) **Integrity** — Modification of the medical record violates data accuracy/tamper-resistance.
(iii) **Availability** — The website is rendered inaccessible, denying service to authorized users.

**[3 Marks: 1 mark per correct mapping]**

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Choice Option One **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the three components of the CIA Triad. For each component, provide (i) a formal definition, (ii) two real-world threats, and (iii) two countermeasures.

**Model Solution:**

**Confidentiality:**
- **Definition:** Confidentiality ensures that information is disclosed only to parties who are authorized to access it.
- **Threats:** (1) Eavesdropping/sniffing on unencrypted networks, (2) Social engineering / phishing attacks.
- **Countermeasures:** (1) Strong encryption (AES-256, TLS 1.3), (2) Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) and strict RBAC policies.

**Integrity:**
- **Definition:** Integrity ensures that data is accurate, complete, and unaltered by unauthorized actors, and that any authorized modification is properly tracked.
- **Threats:** (1) Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) data tampering, (2) SQL injection altering database records.
- **Countermeasures:** (1) Cryptographic hashing (SHA-256) and HMAC for tamper detection, (2) Digital signatures and immutable audit logs.

**Availability:**
- **Definition:** Availability ensures that information systems and data are accessible to authorized users on demand, without undue delay.
- **Threats:** (1) Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) attacks, (2) Ransomware encrypting critical files.
- **Countermeasures:** (1) Redundancy via clustering, RAID, and geo-replication, (2) DDoS scrubbing services and tested disaster recovery plans.

**[7 Marks: ~2.3 marks per pillar, distributed across definition/threats/countermeasures]**

**(b) [7 Marks]** A banking server reports $\text{MTBF} = 8760$ hours and $\text{MTTR} = 2$ hours. Calculate the availability percentage and identify the "Nines" tier it belongs to. Justify with calculations.

**Model Solution:**

Using the standard availability formula:

$$A_{\%} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100$$

Substituting the given values:

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{\%} &= \frac{8760}{8760 + 2} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{8760}{8762} \times 100 \\
&\approx 99.977\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Identify the Tier**

The result $99.977\%$ lies between $99.9\%$ (Three Nines) and $99.99\%$ (Four Nines), placing it closest to **Three Nines** availability tier.

**Step 3: Interpretation**

The system experiences approximately:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Downtime per year} &= 8760 \times \left(1 - 0.99977\right) \\
&\approx 2.01 \text{ hours per year}
\end{aligned}$$

This is acceptable for many business systems but below the *Five Nines* ($99.999\%$) standard required for telecom or financial trading platforms.

**[7 Marks: 3 for formula, 2 for substitution, 2 for tier identification and interpretation]**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Choice Option Two **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, describe the CIA Triad. Differentiate between confidentiality and integrity with suitable examples.

**Model Solution:**

**Diagram (refer to Section 4.1 above)** — The CIA Triad is represented as a triangular model with three vertices: Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability, all converging at the central goal of *Information Security*.

**Differences between Confidentiality and Integrity:**

| Aspect | Confidentiality | Integrity |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Goal** | Prevent unauthorized *disclosure* | Prevent unauthorized *modification* |
| **Question** | "Who can see this?" | "Has it been changed?" |
| **Primary Tool** | Encryption, ACLs | Hashing, Digital Signatures |
| **Example Attack** | Eavesdropping on VoIP calls | Modifying a wire transfer amount |
| **Example Defense** | TLS 1.3, AES encryption | SHA-256, HMAC, audit logs |
| **Failure Consequence** | Privacy breach, identity theft | Wrong decisions, fraud, data corruption |

**Example for Confidentiality:** A user logging into a banking portal over HTTPS — the TLS tunnel ensures that even if the network is compromised, the credentials and account details remain confidential.

**Example for Integrity:** The bank uses SHA-256 hashing on transaction records so that any unauthorized modification by an insider or malware would be detectable by hash mismatch.

**[7 Marks: 2 for diagram, 5 for comparison and examples]**

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the Parkerian Hexad as an extension of the CIA Triad. State its additional three properties.

**Model Solution:**

The **Parkerian Hexad** is an extended information security model proposed by **Donn B. Parker in 1998** that supplements the three traditional CIA pillars with three additional, non-overlapping properties.

**The six properties are:**

1. **Confidentiality** — (Original) Restricting data access to authorized parties.
2. **Integrity** — (Original) Ensuring data is not improperly modified.
3. **Availability** — (Original) Ensuring timely, reliable access.
4. **Possession** — (Additional) Protecting against unauthorized *acquisition*, duplication, or control of data (even if confidentiality is not violated). Example: Theft of an encrypted hard disk violates possession but not confidentiality (data still unreadable).
5. **Authenticity** — (Additional) Verifying the *identity* of the source/sender and the genuineness of the data. Example: A digitally signed email confirms it genuinely came from the claimed sender.
6. **Utility** — (Additional) Ensuring that data remains *useful* and in a usable form. Example: A backup tape stored in a proprietary format that no software can read has lost its utility, even if its integrity and confidentiality are intact.

**Why the extension matters:** Parker's argument was that the original CIA Triad fails to capture scenarios such as data being stolen in encrypted form (possession loss), data being forged without modification (authenticity loss), or data being encrypted with a lost key (utility loss). The Parkerian Hexad addresses these gaps and is often used in advanced risk assessment frameworks.

**[7 Marks: 1 for naming, 6 for the three additional properties with examples]**

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in CIA Triad Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Possession with Confidentiality** — Students often incorrectly classify "stolen encrypted laptop" as a confidentiality violation. The data is still confidential; **possession** is the violated property.
> 2. **Writing "CIA stands for Central Intelligence Agency"** — examiners deduct 1 mark immediately for this careless opening.
> 3. **Omitting real-world examples** — for 7-mark sub-parts, definitions alone fetch only ~2 marks; the examiner expects **threats + countermeasures + examples** for full credit.
> 4. **Skipping the formula in availability calculation** — directly writing the answer without showing the substitution steps results in partial marking.
> 5. **Mixing up integrity and authentication** — they are related but distinct; integrity = *data unchanged*, authentication = *sender verified*.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — CIA Triad**

- **CIA** = **C**onfidentiality, **I**ntegrity, **A**vailability — the *triad* of information security.
- **Confidentiality** is enforced by **encryption** (AES, RSA), access control, and MFA.
- **Integrity** is enforced by **hashing** (SHA-256), digital signatures, and HMACs.
- **Availability** is enforced by **redundancy**, backups, DDoS mitigation, and DR planning.
- **Confidentiality** answers *"Who can see?"* — Integrity answers *"Has it changed?"* — Availability answers *"Can we access it now?"*
- **Common attacks:** Confidentiality → sniffing/phishing; Integrity → MITM/SQL injection; Availability → DDoS/ransomware.
- **Standard availability formula:** $A_{\%} = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100$.
- **"Five Nines" = 99.999% uptime ≈ 5.26 minutes of downtime per year.**
- **Parkerian Hexad extension** adds Possession, Authenticity, and Utility (Parker, 1998).
- **Risk model:** $R = T \times V \times I$, extended across CIA as a weighted sum.
- **All three pillars must hold** — failure of *any one* means the system is not fully secure.
- **KTU 2024 framing:** Every security topic in subsequent modules maps back to protecting one or more CIA pillars.
- **Mnemonic:** **"CIA — Can I Access?"** (Confidentiality → privacy; Integrity → trust; Availability → access).
- **Trade-offs exist:** Strong encryption (confidentiality) may slow systems (reducing availability) — design balances are part of advanced security engineering.
- **Standards alignment:** ISO/IEC 27001, NIST SP 800-53, COBIT, PCI-DSS, HIPAA all reference CIA as foundational control objectives.
- **Examiner hot tip:** When asked *"which CIA pillar does attack X violate?"* — focus on the *primary* violated goal, not secondary side effects.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Motive of Attackers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST721 - CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Motive of Attackers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of cyber security, the **Motive of Attackers** (also called the *Attacker's Motivation* or *Threat Actor Intent*) refers to the underlying psychological, economic, ideological, or geopolitical reason that drives an individual, group, or nation-state to launch a malicious cyber operation against a target system, network, or individual. It is the **root cause variable** in the canonical threat model:

$$\text{Threat} = f(\text{Motive}, \text{Opportunity}, \text{Capability})$$

According to established cyber security frameworks such as **MITRE ATT&CK**, **NIST SP 800-30**, and the **Cyber Kill Chain (Lockheed Martin)**, attacker motive is one of the three foundational pillars of threat profiling. Without understanding *why* an attacker strikes, organizations cannot prioritize defenses, allocate budgets, or design effective **Defense-in-Depth (DiD)** strategies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Understanding the motive of attackers is the **first cognitive step** in threat modeling. It precedes vulnerability assessment, attack surface analysis, and countermeasure design.

## 1.2 Core Categorization of Attacker Motives

The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies attacker motives into the following **primary categories**:

1. **Financial Gain** — Theft of money, credit card data, banking credentials, cryptocurrency wallets.
2. **Espionage (Corporate / Nation-State)** — Theft of trade secrets, intellectual property (IP), classified government data.
3. **Hacktivism** — Ideological or political protest (e.g., Anonymous, LulzSec).
4. **Cyberterrorism** — Coercion of governments or instilling fear through digital infrastructure attacks.
5. **Revenge / Insider Threat** — Disgruntled employees, former staff, or personal vendettas.
6. **Fame / Notoriety / Curiosity** — Script kiddies and hobbyist hackers seeking recognition.
7. **Cyberwarfare** — State-sponsored disruption of another nation's critical infrastructure.
8. **Disruption of Services (DoS / DDoS)** — Causing downtime of essential services for ransom, protest, or strategic advantage.
9. **Identity Theft & Fraud** — Assuming another person's digital identity for illicit benefit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Threat Actor:** A *threat actor* (or *adversary*) is any individual, group, or entity responsible for an incident that impacts — or has the potential to impact — an organization's safety and security. Motive distinguishes *who* the actor is and *why* they attack.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a city's bank vault. Before designing the locks, guards, and alarm systems, the bank's chief security officer first asks: **"Who wants to break in, and why?"**

- A **professional thief** wants money → install reinforced steel doors and time-locked vaults.
- A **spy** wants secret documents → install document-tracking and access logs.
- A **protester** wants attention → manage public-facing windows and signage.
- A **disgruntled employee** already has partial access → revoke former credentials, monitor insiders.

Similarly, in cyber security, knowing the attacker's **motive** dictates the *type* of defense:

| Attacker Motive | Real-World Analogy | Primary Cyber Countermeasure |
|---|---|---|
| Financial Gain | Bank vault thief | Encryption, MFA, fraud detection |
| Espionage | Industrial spy | DLP (Data Loss Prevention), Zero Trust |
| Hacktivism | Protestor on a megaphone | Rate limiting, WAF, reputation management |
| Cyberterrorism | Bomber targeting infrastructure | Critical infrastructure segmentation, SOC |
| Insider | Disgruntled ex-employee | RBAC, audit logs, least privilege |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When KTU examiners ask "Why is it important to know the motive of an attacker?", answer with: *"It enables threat prioritization, optimal resource allocation, and tailored defensive architecture within a Defense-in-Depth framework."*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Motive–Capability–Opportunity (MCO) Triangle

The **Motive–Capability–Opportunity (MCO) Triangle** is a foundational threat-modeling concept adopted in KTU 2024 cyber security curriculum. An attack only occurs when all three vertices of the triangle are satisfied:

$$\text{Attack Realized} = \text{Motive} \cap \text{Capability} \cap \text{Opportunity}$$

Where:
- **Motive (M):** The *psychological or strategic driver* — the *why*.
- **Capability (C):** The *technical skill, tools, and resources* — the *how*.
- **Opportunity (O):** The *exploitable vulnerability, timing, and access* — the *when/where*.

### Step-by-Step Logic

- **Step 1 — Motive Generation:** The attacker develops intent (greed, ideology, revenge, patriotism).
- **Step 2 — Capability Acquisition:** The attacker acquires skills, malware, zero-day exploits, or finances.
- **Step 3 — Opportunity Identification:** The attacker scans for unpatched systems, weak credentials, exposed APIs.
- **Step 4 — Attack Execution:** All three vertices intersect → the attack proceeds.
- **Step 5 — Post-Exploitation:** The motive dictates the *goal* — exfiltrate data, encrypt files, deface website, or cause downtime.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU 2024:** If any ONE of the three vertices is eliminated, the attack is prevented. This is the foundation of **Defense-in-Depth (DiD)** — we cannot always stop motive (human psychology), so we must eliminate capability (patching) and opportunity (access control).

## 2.2 Detailed Motive Classification (KTU High-Yield Chart)

### 2.2.1 Financial Motive

- **Description:** Most prevalent motive in modern cybercrime. Estimated to drive **>70%** of global cyber attacks.
- **Techniques Used:** Phishing, ransomware, Banking Trojans (e.g., Emotet, Zeus), Business Email Compromise (BEC), cryptojacking.
- **Monetization Channels:** Dark web marketplaces, cryptocurrency mixers, money mules, ransom payments.
- **Example:** The 2017 **WannaCry** ransomware attack earned approximately **$4 billion** in damages, demanding Bitcoin ransom.

### 2.2.2 Espionage (Corporate & State-Sponsored)

- **Description:** Covert, long-term, highly targeted operations (called **Advanced Persistent Threats — APTs**).
- **Targets:** Government defense departments, R&D labs, semiconductor firms, pharmaceutical companies.
