> - **Course:** OECST721 - CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Passive Attacks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1: Passive Attacks — Core Technical Foundation

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of computer and network security (as per KTU 2024 OECST721 syllabus terminology), a **Passive Attack** is defined as a category of security attack in which an unauthorized third party silently monitors, observes, intercepts, or eavesdrops on a communication channel between two or more legitimate entities **without altering, modifying, injecting, or disrupting** the original data transmission.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Definition (Direct from Stallings/Stallings-Brown Standard):**
> A *passive attack* attempts to learn or make use of information from the system but **does not affect system resources**. Passive attacks are broadly classified into two sub-categories: **(a) Release of message contents**, and **(b) Traffic analysis**.

The adversary (often termed the **passive eavesdropper** or **sniffer**) aims to achieve one of two intelligence-gathering objectives:
1. **Disclosure of message content** — reading the actual payload (e.g., emails, files, login credentials, voice calls).
2. **Traffic analysis** — inferring communication patterns, frequency, identities of communicating parties, message length, and timing — even when message content is encrypted.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Silent Mailroom Thief"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine two executives, **Alice** and **Bob**, exchanging sealed, registered letters through a central mailroom. A **passive attacker** (let us call her **Eve**) is a mailroom clerk who secretly opens the letters, photocopies every page, reseals the envelope perfectly, and puts it back in transit. Alice and Bob receive their letters normally — they have **no idea** anything was tampered with (because nothing *was* tampered with — the letters were merely *read*). Eve now possesses perfect copies of every confidential document. This is precisely how a passive network attack works: the **integrity** and **availability** of the communication remain intact, but the **confidentiality** is silently violated.

## 1.3 Core Security Triad Impact

Passive attacks exclusively target the **CONFIDENTIALITY** pillar of the **CIA Triad** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability).

| Pillar | Affected by Passive Attack? | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Confidentiality | **YES — Severely Violated** | The attacker reads or infers private data. |
| Integrity | **NO** | Data is not modified during transit. |
| Availability | **NO** | System resources remain fully operational. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Why Passive Attacks are Extremely Dangerous:**
> Because the victim network shows **zero abnormal traffic**, **zero log anomalies**, and **zero system disruption**, passive attacks are the **most difficult to detect** in real time. Their detection almost always requires *out-of-band* mechanisms such as encryption, traffic pattern analysis, or forensic post-mortem examination.

## 1.4 Primary Operational Categories

Per KTU 2024 syllabus (aligned with William Stallings, *Cryptography and Network Security*):

- **Category 1: Release of Message Contents** — Direct interception of readable content (e.g., unencrypted email, HTTP traffic, plaintext VoIP).
- **Category 2: Traffic Analysis** — Even when payload is encrypted (ciphertext), observing *who*, *when*, *how often*, and *how much* data flows reveals critical intelligence.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# MODULE 1: Passive Attacks — Deep Theoretical Analysis

## 2.1 Operational Anatomy of a Passive Attack

A passive attack is not a single action but a **multi-stage intelligence operation**. Each stage has a distinct "Why" (the adversary's goal) and "How" (the technique employed).

### Stage 1: Reconnaissance and Channel Identification
- **Why:** The attacker must first locate a vulnerable segment of the communication path where traffic flows in plaintext or weakly-encrypted form.
- **How:** Tools used include `traceroute`, `pathchar`, network mapping utilities, ARP sweeps, and wireless SSID scanners.

### Stage 2: Positioning on the Wire (Tap Insertion)
- **Why:** To observe packets, the attacker must be physically or logically on the data path.
- **How:** Methods include:
  - *Wired networks:* Hub-based sniffing, port mirroring (SPAN port on switches), inline network taps.
  - *Wireless networks:* Promiscuous-mode Wi-Fi adapters (e.g., monitor mode in Kali Linux).
  - *Internet paths:* Compromised routers, BGP hijacking, rogue VPN endpoints.

### Stage 3: Packet Capture and Logging
- **Why:** Raw data must be stored for offline analysis.
- **How:** Tools include **Wireshark**, **tcpdump**, **TShark**, and commercial DLP (Data Loss Prevention) systems. Capture files are stored in `.pcap` or `.pcapng` format.

### Stage 4: Content Decoding (Release of Message Contents)
- **Why:** Raw frames are useless without reassembly.
- **How:** Protocol stack reassembly — Ethernet → IP → TCP/UDP → Application (HTTP, FTP, SMTP, SIP, etc.).

### Stage 5: Traffic Pattern Inference (Traffic Analysis)
- **Why:** Even encrypted flows leak metadata.
- **How:** Statistical analysis of packet sizes, inter-arrival times, burst patterns, and flow durations.

## 2.2 Real-World Engineering Examples

- **Example A (Release of Contents):** An employee connects to office Wi-Fi using HTTP-based login. A passive sniffer captures the POST request containing the username and password in plaintext.
- **Example B (Traffic Analysis):** A government intelligence agency monitors encrypted VPN traffic between two embassies. Although the message body is unreadable, the *daily 9:00 AM spike in traffic* indicates a scheduled briefing session, revealing operational patterns.
- **Example C (Wired Equivalent Privacy — WEP crack):** Capturing IV (Initialization Vector) collisions passively before launching an offline WEP key recovery — a classic passive-then-active hybrid pattern.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Reference Matrix (Formula/Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> Passive attacks are **conceptual**, not heavily formula-driven. However, certain mathematical relationships govern *traffic analysis metrics* and *entropy of leaked information*. The following table summarizes the high-yield quantitative and qualitative concepts you must memorize for KTU ESE.

| **Parameter / Concept** | **Formal Expression / Description** | **Unit / Type** | **Engineering Significance** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Shannon Entropy of Message | $H(M) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(m_i) \log_2 p(m_i)$ | bits | Measures information content leaked if intercepted. |
| Conditional Entropy (after observation) | $H(M \mid O) = -\sum p(m \mid o) \log_2 p(m \mid o)$ | bits | Residual uncertainty adversary faces. |
| Information Leakage | $I(M; O) = H(M) - H(M \mid O)$ | bits | Direct measure of breach severity. |
| Ciphertext-Only Attack (COA) | Adversary has access only to ciphertext. | Attack Model | Strongest defense model — passive foundation. |
| Known-Plaintext Attack (KPA) | Adversary has plaintext–ciphertext pairs. | Attack Model | Higher capability passive attack. |
| Packet Capture Rate | $R_{cap} = N_{pkts} / T_{window}$ | packets/sec | Quantifies sniffer throughput. |
| Mean Inter-Arrival Time | $\bar{\Delta t} = \frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N-1}(t_{i+1} - t_i)$ | seconds | Used in traffic-analysis timing inference. |
| Detection Difficulty Index (qualitative) | **Very High** | Categorical | Reason: Zero system perturbation. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to "list and explain types of passive attacks," you **must** mention both **Release of Message Contents** AND **Traffic Analysis**. Many students incorrectly mention only one and lose **2–3 marks** out of 14.

## 2.4 Countermeasures (Why Cryptography is the Answer)

The standard, syllabus-mandated countermeasure is **encryption** of the data stream at the appropriate OSI layer. However, KTU 2024 emphasizes that encryption alone **does not defeat traffic analysis** — for that, additional techniques such as **traffic padding**, **onion routing (Tor)**, and **constant-rate links** are required.

- **Symmetric encryption** (e.g., AES-256-GCM) — defeats release of contents.
- **Public-key encryption** (e.g., RSA, ECC) — establishes secure key exchange prior to symmetric use.
- **Traffic padding** — inserts dummy packets to obscure real flow patterns.
- **Onion routing** — multi-layered encapsulation hides source-destination pairs.
- **Steganography (defensive use)** — hides the *existence* of communication altogether.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# MODULE 1: Passive Attacks — Analytical Walkthrough & Exhaustive Derivation

## 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Analysis: How a Passive Sniffing Operation Works

Since passive attacks are process-oriented, this section presents the **full operational pipeline** of a typical release-of-message-contents attack on a switched Ethernet network using ARP poisoning as a positioning vector (note: the *positioning* may be active, but the *capture and reading* itself remains passive).

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Treatment, Not Code:** The KTU OECST721 syllabus does NOT mandate programming snippets. This walkthrough is presented as a **structured engineering procedure** aligned with how examiners expect you to *describe* a passive attack in a 14-mark essay.

### Pipeline Step 1 — Network Interface Configuration

The attacker's wireless or Ethernet adapter is placed into **promiscuous mode**.

$$
\text{Mode}_{\text{NIC}} = 
\begin{cases}
\text{Normal}, & \text{card ignores frames not destined to its MAC.} \\
\text{Promiscuous}, & \text{card accepts ALL frames on the segment.}
\end{cases}
$$

**Logic:** In normal mode, the NIC's hardware filter discards frames whose destination MAC address does not match its own. In promiscuous mode, this filter is bypassed, allowing the OS kernel to receive every frame traversing the broadcast domain.

### Pipeline Step 2 — Channel Attachment and Frame Acquisition

The attacker attaches to a shared broadcast medium. For modern switched networks (where each port is its own collision domain), the attacker must **logically** join the target's broadcast domain. The most common technique is **ARP Cache Poisoning** (often called ARP spoofing).

The attacker sends forged ARP replies:

$$
\text{ARP}_{\text{reply}} = \left\{ \text{Sender\_IP} \to \text{Target\_IP}, \; \text{Sender\_MAC} \to \text{Attacker\_MAC} \right\}
$$

**Logic:** The attacker tells the victim, "I am the gateway." The victim, trusting the unsolicited ARP reply, updates its ARP cache and subsequently forwards all outbound traffic to the attacker's MAC address. The attacker then forwards packets to the real gateway, acting as a transparent **man-in-the-middle (MITM)** — but the *sniffing portion* of this operation remains passive (read-only).

### Pipeline Step 3 — Protocol Stack Reassembly

Captured raw frames must be reassembled through the OSI/TCP-IP stack to recover the application-layer payload.

$$
\text{Frame}_{\text{Ethernet}} \xrightarrow{\text{Strip LLC header}} \text{IP Datagram} \xrightarrow{\text{Strip IP header}} \text{TCP/UDP Segment} \xrightarrow{\text{Strip Transport header}} \text{Application Payload}
$$

**Logic:** Each layer encapsulates the next. To recover the original email body, the attacker must reverse the encapsulation process. This is precisely what tools like Wireshark do automatically.

### Pipeline Step 4 — Application-Layer Content Extraction

For unencrypted protocols (HTTP, FTP, Telnet, SMTP, POP3, IMAP, SNMPv1), the payload is **readable plaintext**.

**Example extraction — HTTP POST containing credentials:**

$$
\text{HTTP}_{\text{POST}} = \text{"POST /login HTTP/1.1\r\nHost: bank.com\r\n..."} \; \| \; \text{user=alice\&password=secret123}
$$

**Logic:** The username and password appear as ASCII strings within the URL-encoded body — instantly readable, no cryptanalysis required.

### Pipeline Step 5 — Traffic Analysis Deduction

Even if the application uses TLS (e.g., HTTPS), the attacker can still infer *metadata*:

$$
\text{Observation Vector} \; O = \left\{ t_{\text{start}}, t_{\text{end}}, N_{pkts}, \bar{S}_{pkt}, \text{IP}_{src}, \text{IP}_{dst}, \text{Port}_{dst} \right\}
$$

**Logic:** A flow to `IP_dst = X, Port_dst = 443` lasting 30 minutes with sustained packet sizes of ~1500 bytes suggests a video conference. A burst of small packets at 03:00 UTC to a known command-and-control IP suggests malware beaconing. This is **traffic analysis** — the hallmark second sub-category of passive attacks.

## 3.2 Mathematical Expression of Information Leakage

The *quantity* of information leaked during a successful passive attack can be quantified using Shannon's mutual information framework.

**Step A:** Total entropy of the message space $M$:

$$
H(M) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(m_i) \log_2 p(m_i)
$$

**Step B:** Entropy of the message conditioned on the adversary's observation $O$:

$$
H(M \mid O) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{m} p(m_i, o_j) \log_2 p(m_i \mid o_j)
$$

**Step C:** Information leakage (mutual information):

$$
I(M; O) = H(M) - H(M \mid O)
$$

**Logic interpretation:**
- If $I(M; O) = 0$, the observation reveals **nothing** about the message (perfect encryption).
- If $I(M; O) = H(M)$, the observation reveals the **entire message** (plaintext interception).
- Real-world passive attacks typically fall between these extremes, with traffic analysis leaking partial information.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** When explaining why encryption is *not* a complete defense against passive attacks, cite that even under perfect encryption, $H(M \mid O) \approx 0$ for content, but traffic-analysis information leakage $I_{\text{traffic}} = H(\text{pattern}) - H(\text{pattern} \mid O)$ may remain **non-zero**.

## 3.3 Passive vs. Active Attack — Distinguishing Analytical Matrix

| **Attribute** | **Passive Attack** | **Active Attack** |
|---|---|---|
| Modifies data? | **No** | **Yes** |
| Disrupts service? | **No** | **Yes (DoS, modification, fabrication)}** |
| Detectable by victim? | **Extremely difficult** | **Moderate to easy (IDS, logs)** |
| Primary goal | Confidentiality breach | Integrity / availability breach |
| Pillar violated | Confidentiality | Integrity, Authenticity, Availability |
| Example | Eavesdropping on Wi-Fi | SQL injection, DoS, MITM modification |
| CIA Triad | C only | I and/or A |
| Countermeasure | Encryption, traffic padding | Authentication, integrity checks, firewalls |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# MODULE 1: Passive Attacks — Structural Schematics & Architecture

## 4.1 High-Level Passive Attack Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following diagram maps the **functional flow** of a passive attack operation, from initial channel identification through to information extraction.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Alice - Legitimate Sender] -->|Plaintext or Encrypted Traffic| B(Shared Transmission Channel)
    B -->|Frame Stream| C[Bob - Legitimate Receiver]
    D[Eve - Passive Attacker] -.->|Promiscuous-mode Tap| B
    B -.->|Intercepted Frames| D
    D --> E{Attack Objective}
    E -->|Objective 1| F[Release of Message Contents]
    E -->|Objective 2| G[Traffic Analysis]
    F --> F1[Protocol Stack Reassembly]
    F1 --> F2[Plaintext Credential Extraction]
    F1 --> F3[Email and File Interception]
    G --> G1[Packet Size Pattern Study]
    G --> G2[Inter-Arrival Timing Analysis]
    G --> G3[Endpoint Identity Inference]
    F2 --> H[Intelligence Gathered - Silent Breach]
    F3 --> H
    G1 --> H
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
```

**Reading the diagram:** The dashed arrows represent the **non-intrusive observation path** of Eve. The solid arrows represent the **legitimate communication path** between Alice and Bob. Eve never touches the solid path — she merely mirrors the channel into her analysis pipeline.

## 4.2 Passive Attack Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PA[Passive Attacks]
    PA --> RM[Release of Message Contents]
    PA --> TA[Traffic Analysis]
    RM --> RM1[Email and Chat Interception]
    RM --> RM2[File Transfer Sniffing]
    RM --> RM3[Credential Capture - HTTP, FTP, Telnet]
    RM --> RM4[VoIP Call Interception]
    TA --> TA1[Connection Pattern Mapping]
    TA --> TA2[Frequency and Duration Analysis]
    TA --> TA3[Encrypted Flow Metadata Inference]
    TA --> TA4[Social Network Reconstruction]
```

## 4.3 Defense-in-Depth Countermeasure Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1[Layer 1 - Link Encryption] --> D2[Layer 2 - Network-layer VPN]
    D2 --> D3[Layer 3 - Application-layer TLS]
    D3 --> D4[Layer 4 - Traffic Padding and Onion Routing]
    D4 --> D5[Layer 5 - Steganographic Covert Channels]
    D5 --> E[Maximum Confidentiality Preservation]
    D1 -.-> E
    D2 -.-> E
    D3 -.-> E
```

**Engineering note:** No single layer is sufficient. KTU 2024 emphasizes a **defense-in-depth** posture: combining encryption (defeats release of contents) with traffic padding and onion routing (defeats traffic analysis).

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs in the diagrams above are alphanumeric (e.g., `A`, `B`, `RM1`, `TA3`) and avoid reserved keywords like `end`, `subgraph`, or `graph`. Labels contain only raw uppercase alphanumeric text, ensuring clean rendering in GitHub, Obsidian, and VS Code preview.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# MODULE 1: Passive Attacks — KTU 2024 Examination Question Bank

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Each Part A question carries **3 marks**. Answers must be concise, definition-oriented, and reference the KTU syllabus taxonomy.

### **Question 1: Define a passive attack. List its two main sub-categories.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **passive attack** is a security breach in which an unauthorized entity monitors, intercepts, or eavesdrops on a communication channel **without modifying or disrupting** the data or system resources. The attacker aims solely to violate the **confidentiality** pillar of the CIA triad.
The two main sub-categories are:
1. **Release of message contents** — direct reading of intercepted plaintext.
2. **Traffic analysis** — inference of communication patterns, identities, and timing, even when content is encrypted.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for sub-category 1, 1 mark for sub-category 2.)*

---

### **Question 2: Why are passive attacks difficult to detect?** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Passive attacks are difficult to detect because the attacker **does not modify, inject, or delete** any data, and **does not disrupt** any system service. As a result:
1. The victim network exhibits **no abnormal traffic patterns** or log anomalies.
2. **Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS)** that rely on signature-based or anomaly-based active-attack signatures cannot trigger.
3. Detection is possible only through **out-of-band mechanisms** such as encryption enforcement, traffic pattern statistical analysis, or post-incident forensic examination.

*(1 mark per valid point.)*

---

## PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE Part B questions carry **14 marks** with **module-level internal choice**. Two sub-parts `(a)` and `(b)` of **7 marks each** are standard. Both alternatives below are independent and complete.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the two main categories of passive attacks with suitable real-world examples. Discuss why passive attacks exclusively target confidentiality and not integrity or availability.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the layered countermeasures used to defend against passive attacks. Why is encryption alone insufficient to defeat traffic analysis? Illustrate with the concept of traffic padding and onion routing.

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** A passive attack is one in which the adversary silently monitors or intercepts communications without altering system resources, with the sole objective of breaching confidentiality.

2. **Category 1 — Release of Message Contents (3 marks):**
   - The attacker intercepts and reads the actual content of messages.
   - **Real-world example 1:** Capturing unencrypted HTTP traffic on a public Wi-Fi network to steal session cookies and login credentials.
   - **Real-world example 2:** Eavesdropping on a plaintext SMTP email relay to read confidential corporate communications.
   - **Real-world example 3:** Sniffing VoIP calls transmitted over RTP without SRTP encryption.

3. **Category 2 — Traffic Analysis (2 marks):**
   - The attacker observes metadata — who communicates with whom, when, how often, and how much data flows.
   - **Real-world example:** A nation-state adversary monitoring encrypted diplomatic cables to deduce meeting schedules and operational tempo from packet timing.

4. **Why only confidentiality is violated (1 mark):** The attacker never modifies packets (integrity preserved), never injects or drops packets (availability preserved), and never forges identities (authenticity preserved). The CIA pillars affected are solely **C** and partially **A** of the *Authenticity* sub-property (since identity may be inferred).

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

1. **Layered Countermeasure Architecture (4 marks):**
   - **Layer 1 — Link-layer encryption:** MACsec, WPA3 encrypt frames on a single hop.
   - **Layer 2 — Network-layer encryption:** IPsec ESP tunnel mode encrypts entire IP payloads.
   - **Layer 3 — Application-layer encryption:** TLS 1.3, SSH, S/MIME protect end-to-end application data.
   - **Layer 4 — Traffic padding:** Insertion of dummy packets at constant intervals to obscure real flow patterns.
   - **Layer 5 — Onion routing:** Multi-hop encryption (e.g., Tor) where each relay decrypts only one layer, hiding the source-destination linkage.

2. **Why Encryption Alone is Insufficient Against Traffic Analysis (2 marks):**
   - Encryption protects **content**, not **patterns**.
   - An observer can still see packet sizes, timing, and endpoints, which may leak critical intelligence even when ciphertext is opaque.
   - For example, a constant 1.5 kB packet stream every 60 seconds from a workstation to an external server strongly suggests beaconing malware, regardless of encryption.

3. **Role of Traffic Padding and Onion Routing (1 mark):**
   - **Traffic padding** equalizes flow characteristics, making real and dummy traffic statistically indistinguishable.
   - **Onion routing** ensures no single relay knows both the source and destination, defeating end-to-end correlation by a passive observer.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the operational pipeline of a passive release-of-message-contents attack on a switched Ethernet network. Highlight the role of promiscuous mode and protocol stack reassembly.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Differentiate clearly between passive and active attacks. Using the information-theoretic expression $I(M; O) = H(M) - H(M \mid O)$, explain how the *quantity of information leaked* during a passive attack can be quantified.

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

1. **Block Diagram Description (2 marks):** The student must include a block diagram showing: Attacker NIC → Promiscuous Mode → Channel Tap → Frame Capture Buffer → Protocol Stack Reassembly Engine → Application Payload Extractor → Plaintext Output.

2. **Promiscuous Mode (2 marks):**
   - In normal mode, a NIC discards frames not addressed to its own MAC.
   - In promiscuous mode, the NIC accepts **all** frames on the segment, passing them up to the kernel.
   - This is the foundational enabler of passive sniffing on shared media.

3. **Protocol Stack Reassembly (2 marks):**
   - Captured Ethernet frames are stripped of their MAC headers.
   - The inner IP datagram is extracted, and its header is parsed to recover the transport-layer segment (TCP or UDP).
   - The transport segment is reassembled into the application-layer stream (e.g., HTTP request, SMTP email).
   - Tools like Wireshark automate this entire reassembly process.

4. **Final Plaintext Extraction (1 mark):** For unencrypted protocols, the application payload (e.g., `username=alice&password=secret`) is now readable as ASCII text.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

1. **Passive vs. Active Attack — Tabular Comparison (3 marks):** The student must present a clear comparison covering: definition, system modification, service disruption, detectability, CIA pillar affected, examples, and countermeasures.

2. **Information-Theoretic Quantification (4 marks):**
   - **Step 1:** Define message entropy $H(M) = -\sum p(m_i) \log_2 p(m_i)$, representing total information content.
   - **Step 2:** Define conditional entropy $H(M \mid O)$, representing residual uncertainty after the adversary's observation $O$.
   - **Step 3:** Define mutual information (leakage) $I(M; O) = H(M) - H(M \mid O)$.
   - **Step 4:** Interpret the boundary cases: $I = 0$ means perfect secrecy (no leakage), $I = H(M)$ means total disclosure.
   - **Conclusion (1 mark):** This formalism allows security engineers to measure *how much* a passive attack has learned, enabling quantitative risk assessment.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing passive with active attacks:** Many students incorrectly describe passive attacks as "hacking into the system" or "modifying files." The defining feature is **non-interference with data or system state**.
> 2. **Mentioning only one sub-category:** Forgetting **traffic analysis** in a Part B answer typically costs **2–3 marks** out of 14.
> 3. **Omitting the CIA Triad linkage:** Every passive attack answer **must** explicitly state that only **confidentiality** is violated.
> 4. **Confusing *positioning* with the attack itself:** ARP poisoning is technically *active* (it injects packets), but the *subsequent sniffing* is passive. Examiners may deduct 1 mark if this distinction is not made clear.
> 5. **Skipping real-world examples:** KTU 2024 rewards context. An answer without a concrete example (e.g., public Wi-Fi credential capture) loses 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Ignoring traffic analysis countermeasures:** Stating only "use encryption" without discussing traffic padding or onion routing is **incomplete** and will be penalized.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- ✅ **Definition:** A passive attack monitors or intercepts communications **without modifying or disrupting** them.
- ✅ **Two Sub-categories:** (1) Release of Message Contents, (2) Traffic Analysis.
- ✅ **CIA Pillar Violated:** **Confidentiality ONLY** — integrity and availability are untouched.
- ✅ **Detectability:** **Extremely difficult** in real time because no system anomaly is generated.
- ✅ **Primary Countermeasure:** **Encryption** (AES, RSA, TLS) — but **not sufficient** against traffic analysis.
- ✅ **Additional Defenses:** **Traffic padding**, **onion routing (Tor)**, **steganography**, **link-layer encryption (MACsec, WPA3)**.
- ✅ **Common Tools (mention only as examples):** Wireshark, tcpdump, TShark, Ettercap (positioning).
- ✅ **Quantitative Metric:** Information leakage $I(M; O) = H(M) - H(M \mid O)$ in bits.
- ✅ **Positioning techniques** (e.g., ARP spoofing) may be *active*, but the **sniffing itself** remains passive — a subtle distinction examiners test.
- ✅ **Key Real-World Cases:** Public Wi-Fi credential theft, encrypted VPN metadata leakage, VoIP interception over unencrypted RTP, WEP IV collection.
- ✅ **Defense-in-Depth:** KTU 2024 emphasizes **multi-layer** countermeasures, not a single solution.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Software Attacks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST721 - CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Software Attacks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):** A **Software Attack** is a deliberate, malicious manipulation of software systems, their underlying logic, or associated data structures executed by an adversary (threat actor) to compromise the **CIA Triad** — *Confidentiality*, *Integrity*, and *Availability* — of computing resources. Software attacks exploit vulnerabilities residing in operating systems, application code, firmware, or supply-chain dependencies to gain unauthorized privilege, exfiltrate data, disrupt services, or seize control of digital assets.

In the formal **threat modeling vocabulary** adopted by KTU (aligned with **NIST SP 800-30** and **ISO/IEC 27005**), a software attack is described by the canonical tuple:

$$ \mathcal{A} = \langle T,\ V,\ E,\ I,\ C \rangle $$

where each component is defined as:

| Component | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Threat Actor | $T$ | Entity launching the attack (script kiddie, insider, APT) |
| Vulnerability | $V$ | Software flaw being exploited (e.g., CVE entry) |
| Exploit Mechanism | $E$ | Code/payload/technique used (buffer overflow, malware) |
| Intended Impact | $I$ | Confidentiality / Integrity / Availability breach |
| Target Context | $C$ | Endpoint, network, cloud, or IoT environment |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 scheme for *OECST721 – Cyber Security* explicitly groups Software Attacks under **Module 1** along with network and cryptographic attacks. Students must master the **classification taxonomy**, **propagation vectors**, **indicators of compromise (IoCs)**, and **defensive countermeasures** for every major malware category.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Digital Burglar

Imagine your computer is a **fortified house**:
- **Walls & locks** = Operating system protections, antivirus, firewalls.
- **Windows & doors** = Software interfaces (ports, APIs, user input fields).
- **Keys** = Authentication credentials, encryption keys.
- **Valuables inside** = Personal data, financial information, intellectual property.

A **software attack** is exactly like a burglar studying the house, finding an *unlocked window* (vulnerability), crawling inside (exploitation), and either stealing things (data exfiltration) or trashing the place (denial of service). Sometimes the burglar leaves a **sleeper agent** (backdoor/rootkit) so they can return later.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters:** Software attacks are the *primary delivery mechanism* for almost every modern cyber-incident. The 2024 *Verizon DBIR* confirms that **~68% of breaches** involve a human or software element that an attacker manipulated — making this topic a high-weight area in KTU examinations.

## 1.3 Core Terminology at a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Vocabulary You Must Memorize:**
> - **Malware** — umbrella term for all malicious software.
> - **Payload** — the destructive/functional component delivered by an exploit.
> - **Zero-Day** — vulnerability unknown to the software vendor (no patch available).
> - **CVE** — *Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures* identifier for a publicly known flaw.
> - **IOC (Indicator of Compromise)** — forensic artifact proving a system has been attacked.
> - **APT (Advanced Persistent Threat)** — long-term, well-resourced attack campaign.
> - **C2 (Command and Control)** — remote server an attacker uses to manage compromised hosts.
> - **Living-off-the-Land (LotL)** — attack using legitimate system tools (PowerShell, WMI) to evade detection.

**The fundamental security metrics** used in quantifying software attack severity are:

$$ \text{CVSS Base Score} \in [0,\ 10] $$
$$ \text{Risk} = f(\text{Threat},\ \text{Vulnerability},\ \text{Consequence}) $$

**Bolded constants/metrics to remember:**
- **CVE** format: `CVE-YYYY-NNNNN`
- **CVSS** range: **0.0 (None) → 10.0 (Critical)**
- **OWASP Top 10** — refreshed every **3–4 years** as the web-app attack baseline.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Master Taxonomy of Software Attacks

The KTU syllabus expects a hierarchical, systematic understanding. Below is the canonical classification tree used by examiners.

### 2.1.1 Taxonomy Tree (Hierarchical)

```
Software Attacks
├── 1. Malware-Based Attacks
│   ├── A. Viruses
│   ├── B. Worms
│   ├── C. Trojans (RATs, Banking, DDoS)
│   ├── D. Ransomware
│   ├── E. Spyware / Keyloggers
│   ├── F. Adware
│   ├── G. Rootkits
│   └── H. Logic Bombs / Time Bombs
│
├── 2. Code-Injection Attacks
│   ├── A. Buffer Overflow (Stack, Heap)
│   ├── B. SQL Injection (SQLi)
│   ├── C. Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)
│   ├── D. Command Injection
│   └── E. Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)
│
├── 3. Software-Supply-Chain Attacks
│   ├── A. Backdoored Dependencies
│   ├── B. Compromised Updates (e.g., SolarWinds-style)
│   └── C. Compiler / Build-Pipeline Injection
│
├── 4. Authentication & Privilege Attacks
│   ├── A. Password Spraying / Brute Force
│   ├── B. Credential Stuffing
│   ├── C. Privilege Escalation (Vertical / Horizontal)
│   └── D. Backdoors
│
└── 5. Document & Macro Attacks
    ├── A. Macro Viruses (Office)
    ├── B. PDF Exploits
    └── C. Malicious LNK / HTA Files
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** In any 14-mark question, examiners award marks for *systematic classification*. Always start an answer with a **taxonomy diagram** before explaining each branch.

## 2.2 Operational Breakdown of Major Attack Classes

### 2.2.1 Viruses

A **computer virus** is a self-replicating program that **requires a host file** (executable, document, boot sector) to propagate. The virus attaches itself to a legitimate program and spreads only when the host is executed or shared.

**Replication life-cycle (4 phases):**

1. **Dormant phase** — virus lies idle, waiting for a trigger.
2. **Propagation phase** — virus copies itself into other host programs/files.
3. **Triggering phase** — a logical condition fires the payload (date, user action, file count).
4. **Execution phase** — the malicious payload activates (file deletion, encryption, display, etc.).

**File-system infection targets:**
- **Executable (.exe, .com)** — appender/prepender infection.
- **Boot sector (MBR/VBR)** — spreads via USB and diskettes.
- **Macro-enabled documents (.docm, .xlsm)** — spreads via email attachments.
- **Companion viruses** — create a same-named file that runs first in the path.

### 2.2.2 Worms

A **worm** is a *self-contained, self-propagating* malicious program that spreads **over a network without user intervention** and without needing a host file. Worms consume bandwidth, scan ports, and frequently carry a *secondary payload* (ransomware, banking trojan).

**Famous historical worms (often asked in KTU papers):**
- **Morris Worm (1988)** — first Internet worm, exploited `rsh` and `finger` vulnerabilities.
- **Code Red (2001)** — exploited Microsoft IIS buffer overflow.
- **Nimda (2001)** — multi-vector worm (email, web, file-share).
- **WannaCry (2017)** — exploited the **EternalBlue** SMBv1 vulnerability.
- **Conficker** — DNS-flux and dictionary-attack enabled worm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Virus vs Worm — KTU Classic Comparison:**
> | Property | Virus | Worm |
> |---|---|---|
> | Needs host file? | Yes | No |
> | Human action needed? | Yes (run file) | No (auto) |
> | Primary vector | USB, email | Network |
> | Speed of spread | Slow | Very fast |
> | Example | Melissa | WannaCry |

### 2.2.3 Trojans

A **Trojan Horse** is a malicious program that *disguises itself as legitimate software*. Unlike viruses/worms, it does **not self-replicate**. The user is the unwitting delivery vehicle.

**Common Trojan sub-classes:**

- **Backdoor Trojan** — opens a covert C2 channel.
- **Banking Trojan (e.g., Zeus, Emotet)** — intercepts browser/web-form credentials.
- **DDoS Trojan** — turns host into a bot.
- **RAT (Remote Access Trojan)** — gives attacker GUI-level remote control.
- **Downloader/Dropper** — fetches and installs additional malware.

### 2.2.4 Ransomware

A **crypto-ransomware** encrypts the victim's files (often with **AES-256** for speed, and the key is wrapped with **RSA-2048/4096**) and demands payment (usually cryptocurrency) for the decryption key.

**Attack chain (modern double-extortion model):**

1. Initial access (phishing, RDP brute force, exploit).
2. Defense evasion + lateral movement.
3. Privilege escalation to Domain Admin.
4. Data exfiltration **BEFORE** encryption.
5. Volume Shadow Copy deletion (anti-recovery).
6. Encryption of all reachable file shares.
7. Ransom note + leak-site threat (double extortion).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why KTU asks this:** Ransomware is a *boardroom topic* in 2024 and appears in nearly every cybersecurity news cycle. The KTU examiner rewards answers that mention the **double-extortion / triple-extortion** evolution.

### 2.2.5 Spyware, Adware, Rootkits, Logic Bombs

| Sub-class | Function | Persistence | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Spyware / Keylogger** | Covertly logs user activity & keystrokes | Browser-level | FinFisher |
| **Adware** | Floods user with unsolicited advertisements | Often bundled with freeware | Fireball |
| **Rootkit** | Hides itself & other malware from OS | Kernel / Boot / User-mode | Stuxnet (2010) |
| **Logic Bomb** | Triggers payload on condition (date/event) | Stays dormant until trigger | Insider threat tool |

**Rootkit categories (memorize for KTU viva):**
- **User-mode** — hooks Win32 API in user space.
- **Kernel-mode** — modifies OS kernel structures (drivers).
- **Bootkit** — infects MBR/EFI, survives OS reinstall.
- **Firmware/UEFI** — lives in motherboard flash.
- **Hypervisor** — runs *below* the OS in a hardware-virtualization layer.

### 2.2.6 Code-Injection Attacks (Application Layer)

- **Buffer Overflow** — attacker writes beyond array bounds to overwrite the **return address** on the stack and redirect execution to attacker-supplied shellcode.
- **SQL Injection (SQLi)** — inserts malicious SQL fragments into input fields to manipulate the database (e.g., `' OR '1'='1' --`).
- **XSS (Cross-Site Scripting)** — injects JavaScript into a trusted webpage viewed by other users (Reflected, Stored, DOM-based).
- **CSRF** — tricks a logged-in user's browser into submitting a forged state-changing request.
- **Command Injection** — injects OS shell commands via unsanitized input (e.g., `; rm -rf /`).

### 2.2.7 Software Supply-Chain Attacks

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A **supply-chain attack** compromises a *trusted third-party* (library, vendor, build tool) to reach the ultimate target indirectly — making it stealthy, scalable, and devastating.

**Landmark cases:**
- **SolarWinds (2020)** — malicious code injected into Orion build pipeline.
- **Log4Shell (2021)** — CVE-2021-44228 in Apache Log4j allowed RCE.
- **3CX DesktopApp (2023)** — trojanized legitimate VoIP installer.
- **XZ Utils backdoor (CVE-2024-3094)** — near-miss in a core Linux compression library.

## 2.3 Attack Lifecycle — The Cyber Kill Chain

The **Lockheed Martin Cyber Kill Chain** is the *canonical 7-stage model* used in KTU question banks.

1. **Reconnaissance** — gather target info (OSINT, Shodan, social media).
2. **Weaponization** — pair exploit with a deliverable payload (e.g., PDF + CVE).
3. **Delivery** — transmit to victim (email, web, USB).
4. **Exploitation** — trigger the vulnerability.
5. **Installation** — establish foothold (dropper, persistence).
6. **Command & Control (C2)** — open channel to attacker's server.
7. **Actions on Objectives** — exfiltration, destruction, ransom.

**MITRE ATT&CK** is the modern matrix-based successor: 14 tactic columns × hundreds of technique rows.

## 2.4 Risk and Severity Formulas — KTU Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL: Avoid using `|` inside table cells (breaks markdown). Use `\vert` instead.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Meaning | KTU Exam Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Risk} = P(\text{Threat}) \times P(\text{Vulnerability}) \times \text{Impact}$ | Composite risk score (NIST) | 7-mark derivation Q |
| 2 | $\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$ | Annual Loss Expectancy | 7-mark SLE/ARO Q |
| 3 | $\text{SLE} = \text{Asset Value} \times \text{Exposure Factor}$ | Single Loss Expectancy | Risk-management problems |
| 4 | $\text{CVSS Base} = f(AV,AC,PR,UI,S,C,I,A)$ | 8 metric subscores combined | Severity interpretation |
| 5 | $\text{Detection Rate} = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | Recall / True Positive Rate | IDS/IPS evaluation |
| 6 | $\text{FAR} = \frac{FP}{FP+TN}$ | False Alarm Rate | Anti-virus testing |
| 7 | $P(\text{breach}) = 1 - (1-p)^n$ | Birthday-style prob. of finding 1 vuln in $n$ probes | Probability derivation |
| 8 | $\text{MTTR}, \text{MTTD}, \text{MTTF}$ | Mean Time To Respond/Detect/Fail | Incident-response Q |

### 2.4.1 Worked Risk Example

If a laptop is worth **₹80,000**, the **Exposure Factor (EF)** for a ransomware attack is **0.75**, and the attack is expected **3 times per year (ARO = 3)**:

$$ \text{SLE} = 80{,}000 \times 0.75 = \text{₹}60{,}000 $$

$$ \text{ALE} = 60{,}000 \times 3 = \text{₹}1{,}80{,}000 \text{ per year} $$

If a security control (EDR + backups) costs **₹50,000/year** and reduces ARO to **0.5**:

$$ \text{ALE}_{\text{new}} = 60{,}000 \times 0.5 = \text{₹}30{,}000 $$

$$ \text{Net Savings} = (1{,}80{,}000 - 30{,}000) - 50{,}000 = \text{₹}1{,}00{,}000 $$

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** These formulas are used by CISOs and insurance underwriters to justify security investments, compute cyber-insurance premiums, and run quantitative *FAIR*-based risk analyses.

## 2.5 Defense-in-Depth Architecture

Software attack defense is **layered**, never a single silver bullet:

| Layer | Control | Defeats |
|---|---|---|
| **1. People** | Awareness training | Phishing, social engineering |
| **2. Perimeter** | Firewall, IDS/IPS | Network worms, port scans |
| **3. Endpoint** | EDR, Anti-virus, App-whitelisting | Trojans, ransomware |
| **4. Application** | Input validation, WAF, SAST/DAST | SQLi, XSS, buffer overflow |
| **5. Data** | Encryption, DLP, backups | Exfiltration, ransomware |
| **6. Identity** | MFA, PAM, least-privilege | Credential stuffing, priv-esc |
| **7. Monitoring** | SIEM, SOAR, threat hunting | APT, LotL attacks |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Pattern Note:** KTU board examiners in Module 1 (Cyber Security) frequently test **numerical risk-calculation** and **probability-derivation** sub-parts. Master the derivations below — they are *board-exam favourites*.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE) from First Principles

The **Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE)** model decomposes monetary cyber-risk into three measurable sub-factors. The derivation is required whenever a question provides asset value, exposure factor, and annual rate of occurrence.

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Define Asset Value (AV).**

$$ \text{AV} = \text{quantitative replacement cost of the asset (₹)} $$

**Step 2: Define Exposure Factor (EF).**

The exposure factor is the *fraction of asset value destroyed* in a single successful attack. It is dimensionless and lies in $[0,1]$.

$$ \text{EF} \in [0, 1] $$

**Step 3: Compute Single Loss Expectancy (SLE).**

The SLE is the monetary damage of *one* occurrence. It is the product of the asset's value and the fraction damaged:

$$ \text{SLE} = \text{AV} \times \text{EF} $$

**Step 4: Define Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO).**

The ARO is the *expected number of times* the threat will materialize in a year. It is a frequency, not a probability:

$$ \text{ARO} \in [0, \infty) $$

**Step 5: Compute Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE).**

Multiplying the loss per event by the number of events per year yields the expected annual monetary damage:

$$ \text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO} $$

$$ \text{ALE} = (\text{AV} \times \text{EF}) \times \text{ARO} $$

**Step 6: Compute Cost-Benefit of a Safeguard.**

Let $C$ be the annual cost of a control. The **Net Annual Savings (NAS)** is:

$$ \text{NAS} = \text{ALE}_{\text{before}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{after}} - C $$

A control is justified if and only if $\text{NAS} > 0$.

### 3.1.2 Numerical Worked Example (Board-Exam Style)

A small clinic holds a patient database worth **₹5,00,000**. A ransomware attack has an **EF of 0.8** and is expected **0.4 times per year**. The proposed EDR control costs **₹60,000/year** and reduces ARO to **0.05**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SLE} &= 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.8 = \text{₹}4{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} &= 4{,}00{,}000 \times 0.4 = \text{₹}1{,}60{,}000 \\
\text{ALE}_{\text{after}} &= 4{,}00{,}000 \times 0.05 = \text{₹}20{,}000 \\
\text{NAS} &= 1{,}60{,}000 - 20{,}000 - 60{,}000 = \text{₹}80{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Since $\text{NAS} = +₹80{,}000 > 0$, the EDR control is **financially justified**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Probability of Successful Exploitation

When an attacker probes a target with $n$ independent vulnerability-checks, each with per-attempt success probability $p$, the probability that **at least one** probe succeeds follows the *complement rule*.

### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Probability of failure of one probe.**

$$ P(\text{1 probe fails}) = 1 - p $$

**Step 2: Probability that all $n$ probes fail (independent).**

$$ P(\text{all } n \text{ fail}) = (1-p)^n $$

**Step 3: Probability of at least one success (complement).**

$$ P(\text{at least one success}) = 1 - (1-p)^n $$

**Step 4: Limiting behaviour as $n \to \infty$.**

$$ \lim_{n \to \infty} \left[1 - (1-p)^n\right] = 1 \quad \text{if } 0 < p \le 1 $$

This shows that given *enough* attempts, an attacker will almost certainly succeed.

### 3.2.2 Numerical Illustration

Let $p = 0.02$ (2% per probe) and $n = 50$ probes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{success}) &= 1 - (1-0.02)^{50} \\
&= 1 - (0.98)^{50} \\
&= 1 - 0.3642 \\
&\approx 0.6358 \;\;\; \text{or} \;\; 63.58\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight for KTU viva:** Doubling the number of probes or doubling the per-attempt probability increases risk non-linearly. This is why **rate-limiting** and **account lockout** controls are so effective — they directly shrink $n$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Buffer Overflow Return-Address Overwrite

For a classic **stack-based buffer overflow**, the attacker overwrites the saved return address with the address of injected shellcode. The KTU question pattern asks you to compute the offset.

### 3.3.1 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Memory layout (descending addresses, low to high).**

$$ \text{Stack frame} = [\text{local buffer} \vert \text{saved EBP} \vert \text{return address} \vert \text{arg}] $$

**Step 2: Buffer size in bytes.**

$$ B = \text{sizeof}(\text{buffer in C code}) $$

**Step 3: Offset to overwrite the return address.**

The attacker must fill $B$ bytes of buffer plus 4 bytes (32-bit) of saved EBP:

$$ \text{Offset} = B + 4 \quad \text{(32-bit system)} $$

$$ \text{Offset} = B + 8 \quad \text{(64-bit system)} $$

**Step 4: Required payload length.**

$$ L_{\text{payload}} = \text{Offset} + \text{sizeof}(\text{shellcode}) $$

**Step 5: Address of shellcode placement.**

The attacker places the shellcode either in the buffer (requiring a **NOP sled**) or in an environment variable and points the return address there.

### 3.3.2 Numerical Illustration

Suppose a C program declares `char buf[64];` and the target is 32-bit x86.

$$
\begin{aligned}
B &= 64 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{Offset} &= 64 + 4 = 68 \text{ bytes} \\
L_{\text{shellcode}} &= 200 \text{ bytes (assumed)} \\
L_{\text{payload}} &= 68 + 200 = 268 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

The attacker sends 64 bytes of junk + 4 bytes of fake EBP + 4 bytes of new return address + 200 bytes of shellcode.

## 3.4 Detection Performance Trade-off — ROC Analysis

A security control (IDS, antivirus) produces four outcomes. The trade-off is captured by **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC)** curves.

| Outcome | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| True Positive | $TP$ | Attack correctly blocked |
| False Positive | $FP$ | Benign file wrongly flagged |
| True Negative | $TN$ | Benign file correctly passed |
| False Negative | $FN$ | Attack missed |

**Key derived metrics:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Recall (TPR)} &= \frac{TP}{TP + FN} \\
\text{False Alarm Rate (FPR)} &= \frac{FP}{FP + TN} \\
\text{Precision} &= \frac{TP}{TP + FP} \\
\text{Accuracy} &= \frac{TP + TN}{TP + FP + TN + FN} \\
\text{F1-score} &= 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering utility:** ROC curves and F1-scores are how SOC teams evaluate new detection signatures, AV engines, and ML-based malware classifiers. They are also the basis for *AV-Comparatives* and *MITRE ATT&CK Evaluations* published every year.

## 3.5 Master Derivation Summary Table

| # | Topic | Final Formula | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ALE | $\text{ALE} = \text{AV} \cdot \text{EF} \cdot \text{ARO}$ | Risk-management problems |
| 2 | Exploit success | $1-(1-p)^n$ | Threat-probability problems |
| 3 | Buffer overflow offset | $B+4$ (32-bit) or $B+8$ (64-bit) | Code-injection questions |
| 4 | Detection metrics | $\text{TPR}, \text{FPR}, \text{F1}$ | AV/IDS comparison |
| 5 | CVSS base | Composite of 8 metrics | Severity grading |
