# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assessment Pattern Reminder (KTU 2024 Scheme, OECST721):**
> - **Part A:** 2 questions × 3 marks = 6 marks (short definitions / direct concepts).
> - **Part B:** Internal choice — answer EITHER Question A OR Question B; 14 marks total.
> - **Internal-choice sub-parts:** Typically `(a) 7 marks` and `(b) 7 marks`.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a Virus and a Worm. List two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| S.No. | Parameter | Virus | Worm |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Host file needed? | **Yes**, attaches to executable/document | **No**, self-contained |
| 2 | User action to spread | Required (run/share) | Not required (auto-propagates) |
| 3 | Primary vector | USB, email attachments | Network services |
| 4 | Detection | Signature on host file | Network traffic anomaly |
| 5 | Examples | **Melissa, ILOVEYOU** | **Morris Worm, WannaCry, Conficker** |

(1 mark for definition; 1 mark for distinction table; 1 mark for examples)

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is a Trojan Horse? Explain the role of a Remote Access Trojan (RAT).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Trojan Horse** is malicious software that *masquerades as legitimate software* but performs hidden malicious actions. Unlike a virus, it does not self-replicate. (1 mark)

A **Remote Access Trojan (RAT)** is a specific Trojan sub-type that creates a covert **C2 (Command & Control) channel**, giving the attacker full GUI-level remote control of the victim machine — including webcam, microphone, file system, and keylogging. (2 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat diagrams, explain the **classification of malware** (virus, worm, trojan, ransomware, spyware, rootkit, logic bomb). For each, mention one propagation mechanism.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A bank's online database server is valued at **₹50,00,000**. A SQL-injection attack is estimated to have an **Exposure Factor of 0.6** and is expected to occur **0.8 times/year**. A Web Application Firewall (WAF) costs **₹1,20,000/year** and is expected to reduce ARO to **0.1/year**. Compute SLE, ALE before, ALE after, and justify whether the WAF is economically justified.

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Mark distribution:** 1 mark for taxonomy, 4 marks for explaining six malware types (≈0.7 mark each), 1 mark for propagation, 1 mark for neat diagram.

1. **Virus** — attaches to host file; spreads on execution. *Propagation:* email/USB. Example: **Melissa**.
2. **Worm** — self-contained, propagates over network. *Propagation:* SMB/RPC exploits. Example: **WannaCry**.
3. **Trojan** — disguised legitimate software; no self-replication. *Propagation:* fake installers/P2P. Example: **Zeus**.
4. **Ransomware** — encrypts files, demands payment. *Propagation:* phishing + exploit kits. Example: **LockBit**.
5. **Spyware/Keylogger** — covertly records user data. *Propagation:* bundled freeware. Example: **FinFisher**.
6. **Rootkit** — hides itself and other malware. *Propagation:* drive-by downloads. Example: **Stuxnet**.
7. **Logic Bomb** — triggers payload on condition. *Propagation:* insider placement.

(Diagram of taxonomy tree earns the 7th mark — see Section 4.1.)

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Mark distribution:** 1 mark SLE, 1 mark ALE-before, 1 mark ALE-after, 1 mark NAS, 1 mark decision, 2 marks for showing full derivation.

**Step 1: Compute Single Loss Expectancy (SLE).**

$$ \text{SLE} = \text{AV} \times \text{EF} = 50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.6 = \text{₹}30{,}00{,}000 $$

**Step 2: Compute ALE before the WAF.**

$$ \text{ALE}_{\text{before}} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}_{\text{before}} = 30{,}00{,}000 \times 0.8 = \text{₹}24{,}00{,}000 $$

**Step 3: Compute ALE after the WAF.**

$$ \text{ALE}_{\text{after}} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}_{\text{after}} = 30{,}00{,}000 \times 0.1 = \text{₹}3{,}00{,}000 $$

**Step 4: Compute Net Annual Savings (NAS).**

$$ \text{NAS} = \text{ALE}_{\text{before}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{after}} - C $$

$$ \text{NAS} = 24{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}20{,}000 = \text{₹}19{,}80{,}000 $$

**Step 5: Decision.**

Since $\text{NAS} = +\text{₹}19{,}80{,}000 \gg 0$, the WAF is **strongly economically justified**. The bank should deploy it. Additionally, non-monetary benefits (regulatory compliance under RBI Cyber Security Framework, brand trust) further support the decision.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]` *(Alternative choice)*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Cyber Kill Chain** with a neat block diagram. For each stage, suggest one defensive countermeasure.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss **buffer overflow** attacks in detail. With a diagram of the stack frame, derive the **offset needed to overwrite the return address** for a 32-bit C program having a 128-byte local buffer.

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

| Stage | Description | Defensive Countermeasure |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Reconnaissance | OSINT, Shodan, social-engineering profiling | **Network segmentation, deceptive honeytokens** |
| 2. Weaponization | Pairing exploit with deliverable | **Threat-intel feeds, zero-day sandboxing** |
| 3. Delivery | Email/web/USB transmission | **Email gateways, browser isolation, USB controls** |
| 4. Exploitation | Triggering the vulnerability | **Patch management, ASLR, DEP, WAF** |
| 5. Installation | Establishing foothold | **EDR, app-whitelisting, least privilege** |
| 6. Command & Control | C2 channel opened | **DNS sinkholing, Egress filtering, NDR** |
| 7. Actions on Objectives | Exfiltration / destruction / ransom | **DLP, immutable backups, IR playbooks** |

(Marks: 1 per stage description + 1 per countermeasure, totaling 7.)

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Theory (3 marks):** A **buffer overflow** exploits the absence of bounds-checking in C/C++ functions like `strcpy`, `gets`, `sprintf`. The attacker overwrites adjacent memory — particularly the saved frame pointer and the **return address** on the stack — to redirect execution to attacker-controlled shellcode. Modern defenses include **ASLR** (randomize base addresses), **DEP/NX** (non-executable stack), **Stack Canaries** (`-fstack-protector`), and **Safe C libraries**.

**Stack-frame diagram (2 marks)** — see Mermaid Section 4.5.

**Derivation (2 marks):**

For a 32-bit C program with `char buf[128];`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
B &= 128 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{Saved EBP width} &= 4 \text{ bytes (32-bit)} \\
\text{Offset to return address} &= B + 4 = 128 + 4 = 132 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the attacker must inject **132 bytes of padding** before overwriting the 4-byte return address with the address of the shellcode.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Software-Attack Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing virus with worm** — examiners deduct 1–2 marks if you state a worm "needs a host file". A worm is *self-contained*.
> 2. **Forgetting EF in SLE** — many students write `SLE = ARO × AV` directly. The correct order is `SLE = AV × EF`, **then** `ALE = SLE × ARO`. Wrong order = **0/7** in part (b).
> 3. **Using `|` inside markdown tables** — when copying formulas with absolute values or set notation into a markdown answer sheet, replace $\vert$ with `abs()` or use LaTeX outside tables. Broken tables confuse the examiner and may not fetch full marks.
> 4. **Skipping the diagram** — a 14-mark question without a block diagram usually caps you at **10–11 marks**. Always draw a taxonomy tree or process diagram.
> 5. **Forgetting non-monetary justification** — when asked to "justify" a control, mention **regulatory compliance, brand reputation, and customer trust** alongside monetary savings. Examiners reward holistic answers.
> 6. **Mis-stating CIA triad order** — always cite in the canonical order: **Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability**.
> 7. **CVSS score confusion** — CVSS is on a **0–10 scale**, not 0–100. A CVSS of 9.8 is **Critical**, not "98%".
> 8. **Buffer-overflow bit width** — 32-bit systems use 4-byte pointers; 64-bit systems use 8-byte pointers. Mixing these gives wrong offsets.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Out Before the Exam:**

- **CIA Triad:** Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability.
- **Malware taxonomy:** Virus, Worm, Trojan, Ransomware, Spyware, Adware, Rootkit, Logic Bomb.
- **Virus vs Worm:** virus needs host + user action; worm is self-propagating over network.
- **Trojan sub-types:** Backdoor, Banking, DDoS, RAT, Dropper.
- **Ransomware evolution:** Encryption → Double-extortion (encrypt + leak) → Triple-extortion (DDoS the leak).
- **Rootkit layers:** User-mode, Kernel-mode, Bootkit, Firmware/UEFI, Hypervisor.
- **Kill Chain stages (7):** Recon → Weaponize → Deliver → Exploit → Install → C2 → Actions.
- **MITRE ATT&CK** is the matrix-based successor to the Kill Chain.
- **Key formulas:**
  - $\text{SLE} = \text{AV} \times \text{EF}$
  - $\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$
  - $P(\text{success}) = 1 - (1-p)^n$
  - $\text{Offset}_{32} = B + 4$, $\text{Offset}_{64} = B + 8$
- **CVSS** range: 0.0 → 10.0 (Critical ≥ 9.0).
- **Supply-chain attacks** (SolarWinds, Log4j, 3CX, XZ Utils) — 2020–2024.
- **Defense-in-Depth layers (7):** People, Perimeter, Endpoint, Application, Data, Identity, Monitoring.
- **Golden terms:** CVE, IOC, APT, C2, LotL, SBOM, ASLR, DEP, WAF, EDR, SIEM, DLP, MFA.
- **First Internet Worm:** Morris (1988). **First global ransomware worm:** WannaCry (2017).
- **Always draw a diagram** in 14-mark answers — it can be worth 1–2 marks.
- **End every derivation with a boxed numerical answer** for the examiner's convenience.
- **Mention real-world examples** (WannaCry, Stuxnet, SolarWinds) — they signal depth and earn higher marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hardware Attacks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST721 - CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hardware Attacks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hardware Attack:** A class of security exploit that targets the physical components, electronic circuits, electromagnetic emissions, power-consumption patterns, or micro-architectural behavior of a computing device, rather than (or in addition to) its software/firmware layer. These attacks exploit physical properties governed by the laws of physics and electronics.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OECST721 - Cyber Security, Module 1)** syllabus, hardware attacks span three classical categories:

1. **Invasive / Physical Attacks** – Direct physical tampering with the device (de-packaging, probing, micro-probing, fault injection).
2. **Non-Invasive / Side-Channel Attacks (SCA)** – Exploiting leaked physical information (power, EM, timing, sound, cache).
3. **Semi-Invasive Attacks** – Partial de-packaging followed by optical/laser probing without full electrical contact.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Broken Lock" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine a bank vault. A *software attack* is like a hacker guessing the digital PIN remotely. A *hardware attack* is like a burglar:
> - **Listening** to the clicks of the dial (timing side-channel),
> - **Stethoscoping** the motor's hum (power/EM side-channel),
> - **Drilling** a hole and sticking a fiber-optic camera inside (invasive probing),
> - **Tampering** with the gears so the lock gives a wrong combination (fault injection).

In short: the *software* is assumed to be mathematically secure, but the *physical realization* of that software on silicon leaks information. Hardware attacks exploit this gap between **mathematical security** and **physical implementation**.

## 1.3 Classification at a Glance

| Domain | Attack Vector | Skill/Equipment | Detectability |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Invasive** | Depackaging, micro-probing, FIB editing | High (clean-room, $10k–$1M) | Often destructive |
| **Semi-Invasive** | Laser fault injection, UV erasure | Medium-High | Destructive |
| **Non-Invasive SCA** | Power, EM, timing, cache, acoustic | Low–Medium ($100–$10k) | Non-destructive |
| **Micro-architectural** | Rowhammer, Spectre, Meltdown | Low (software-based) | Stealthy |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric:** The **Side-Channel Information Leakage** is measured in bits, and for an *n*-bit secret key, an attack is considered successful when the attacker recovers all *n* bits of the secret.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Threat Model — Who is the Attacker?

Every hardware attack begins with a **threat model** describing:
- **Adversary's physical access** to the device (lab, supply-chain, remote, co-tenant in cloud).
- **Equipment budget** (smartphone-grade vs. nation-state lab).
- **Target abstraction** (gate-level, RTL, gate-equivalent, chip-package, PCB, system bus).
- **Number of permitted measurements** *q* (queries to the cryptographic oracle).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** The *number of measurements* *q* is the single most important parameter that determines whether a side-channel attack is theoretically feasible. As *q* grows, the **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** of the leaked signal increases as **√q**.

## 2.2 Operational Phases of a Hardware Attack

1. **Reconnaissance / Acquisition** — Obtain the target device (open market, return-merchandise, supply-chain insertion).
2. **Physical Preparation** — Decapsulation (chemical HNO₃, mechanical polishing, laser ablation).
3. **Signal Acquisition** — Place probes (micro-probes, EM coils, shunt resistors, microphones, cache flush+reload timing).
4. **Signal Processing** — Filtering, alignment, dimensionality reduction (PCA, LDA).
5. **Key/Model Recovery** — Statistical distinguishers (DPA, CPA, MIA, TVLA) or machine-learning classifiers.
6. **Counter-Countermeasure (if needed)** — Bypass masking, blinding, jitter, shuffling.

## 2.3 The Core Mathematical Framework

The information leaked by a hardware device about a sensitive intermediate value *L* is governed by **Shannon's Mutual Information**:

$$
I(L; O) = H(L) - H(L \vert O)
$$

Where:
- $H(L)$ = Shannon entropy of the secret (in bits).
- $H(L \vert O)$ = Conditional entropy of the secret *given* the observation *O* (power trace, EM trace, timing).
- $I(L; O)$ = **Mutual Information** = the maximum number of secret bits a single observation can leak.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** The attacker's goal is to **maximize** $I(L; O)$. A perfectly secured implementation yields $I(L; O) = 0$ (zero leakage).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Shannon Mutual Information** | $I(L; O) = H(L) - H(L \vert O)$ | Bits of secret leaked per observation | **bits** |
| 2 | **SNR (Side-channel)** | $\text{SNR} = \dfrac{\text{Var}(\text{signal})}{\text{Var}(\text{noise})}$ | Quality of the leak trace | **dimensionless** |
| 3 | **SNR scaling with measurements** | $\text{SNR}_q = q \cdot \text{SNR}_1$ | After *q* traces, leakage SNR | **dimensionless** |
| 4 | **DPA Difference-of-Means** | $\Delta(j) = \dfrac{1}{N_0}\sum_{i \in S_0} T_i(j) - \dfrac{1}{N_1}\sum_{i \in S_1} T_i(j)$ | DPA distinguisher at time *j* | **power-units** |
| 5 | **Hamming Weight Model** | $L_{\text{leak}} = \alpha \cdot HW(L) + \beta + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ | Linear leakage model with Gaussian noise | **unitless / power** |
| 6 | **Hamming Distance Model** | $L_{\text{leak}} = \alpha \cdot HD(R_{\text{old}}, R_{\text{new}}) + \mathcal{N}$ | Bus/register transition model | **unitless / power** |
| 7 | **Pearson Correlation (CPA)** | $r_{H,C} = \dfrac{\sum (H_i - \bar H)(C_i - \bar C)}{\sqrt{\sum(H_i-\bar H)^2 \sum(C_i-\bar C)^2}}$ | Correlation between hypothesis *H* and consumption *C* | **$-1 \le r \le 1$** |
| 8 | **CPA key-recovery confidence** | $\rho_{\text{correct}} - \rho_{\text{wrong}} > 4 \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{N}}$ | Threshold for unambiguous key recovery (Gaussian) | **dimensionless** |
| 9 | **Bit-flip success probability (Rowhammer)** | $P_{\text{flip}} = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | Poisson process; $\lambda$ = flip rate | **probability** |
| 10 | **Cache hit time** | $T_{\text{hit}} \approx 1\text{–}4 \text{ cycles}$ vs $T_{\text{miss}} \approx 100\text{–}300 \text{ cycles}$ | Flush+Reload timing gap | **CPU cycles** |
| 11 | **Rowhammer threshold (DDR3/4)** | $N_{\text{activations}} \geq 139{,}000$ (typical) | Activations before bit-flip | **count** |
| 12 | **EM emanation (far-field)** | $E \propto \dfrac{1}{r}$ | Field strength vs. distance *r* | **V/m** |
| 13 | **DEMA bandwidth (near-field)** | $B \approx \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{clock}}} \cdot 10$ | Sampling band for digital EM analysis | **Hz** |
| 14 | **Bit-error rate under FI** | $\text{BER} = \Phi\!\left(\dfrac{V_{\text{th}} - V_{\text{glitch}}}{\sigma_n}\right)$ | Glitch-voltage success probability | **probability** |
| 15 | **AES round energy (typical)** | $E_{\text{AES-round}} \approx 5\text{–}15 \text{ nJ}$ | One round of AES-128 on smartcard | **nanojoules** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Anti-pipe safeguard:** Absolute values in the above formulas use $\vert \cdot \vert$ rendering only inside the LaTeX blocks above. The summary table deliberately avoids embedding them so the markdown table renders correctly.

## 2.5 Why Hardware Attacks Matter in Engineering

- **Cryptographic modules in production** (smart cards, TPMs, HSMs) are routinely evaluated for hardware attacks under standards such as **FIPS 140-3**, **Common Criteria EAL4+**, and **EMVCo**.
- **Cloud and data-centre security** now considers micro-architectural attacks (Spectre, Meltdown, Rowhammer) as first-class threats.
- **IoT device design** must assume the attacker has *physical possession* of the device after deployment.
- **Supply-chain trust** assumes adversarial insertion of hardware Trojans during fabrication or testing.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below are the *exhaustive* derivations of the two most important analytical frameworks for KTU-level hardware-attack problems: **(A) Differential Power Analysis (DPA) via Difference of Means** and **(B) Correlation Power Analysis (CPA) via Pearson Coefficient**. Each algebraic step is written explicitly; no step is skipped.

## 3.1 Derivation A — Difference-of-Means DPA Distinguisher

We begin with *N* power traces $T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_N$, each of length *L* time samples. Let $T_i(j)$ denote the power consumed by trace *i* at time sample *j*. The attacker's hypothesis is the value of an intermediate bit *b = f(P_i, k)*, where $P_i$ is the known plaintext, *k* is a guessed key byte, and *f* is the target function (e.g., the S-box output of the first AES round).

**Step 1 — Partition the traces into two sets based on the hypothesis:**

$$
S_0 = \{\, i \mid f(P_i, k) \text{ has bit } b = 0 \,\}
$$

$$
S_1 = \{\, i \mid f(P_i, k) \text{ has bit } b = 1 \,\}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the average trace of each set at every time sample:**

$$
\bar T_0(j) = \frac{1}{N_0} \sum_{i \in S_0} T_i(j)
$$

$$
\bar T_1(j) = \frac{1}{N_1} \sum_{i \in S_1} T_i(j)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the difference-of-means distinguisher:**

$$
\Delta(j; k, b) = \bar T_1(j) - \bar T_0(j)
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation of the result:**
- If the guessed key *k* is **correct** and the leakage model *f* is **good**, then $\Delta(j; k, b)$ exhibits a sharp **spike (peak or trough)** at the time sample *j = j\** where the targeted S-box output is actually processed.
- If *k* is **wrong**, the partition is essentially random with respect to the true internal state, so the means converge by the **Law of Large Numbers**:

$$
\lim_{N \to \infty} \bar T_0(j) = \lim_{N \to \infty} \bar T_1(j) = \mu(j)
$$

giving $\Delta(j) \to 0$ for all *j*.

**Step 5 — Maximum DPA peak as the key-recovery metric:**

$$
\hat k = \underset{k}{\arg\max}\; \max_{j}\; \vert \Delta(j; k, b) \vert
$$

The correctly guessed key produces the largest absolute peak among all 256 candidate bytes (for AES-128).

## 3.2 Derivation B — Pearson Correlation Power Analysis (CPA)

DPA is binary; CPA uses a **real-valued leakage hypothesis** $H_i$ (typically the Hamming weight of the S-box output) and correlates it with the actual measured consumption $T_i$.

**Step 1 — Define the leakage hypothesis model:**

$$
H_i(k) = W_H\!\left( f(P_i, k) \right)
$$

where $W_H$ is the Hamming-weight function (number of 1-bits). This is justified by the CMOS short-circuit power model:

$$
P_{\text{SC}}(t) \propto \sum_{l} \alpha_l \cdot HW(\text{bit } l)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the sample mean of hypotheses and traces:**

$$
\bar H = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} H_i(k)
$$

$$
\bar T(j) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} T_i(j)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the covariance numerator:**

$$
\text{Cov}(H, T)(j; k) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( H_i(k) - \bar H \right) \left( T_i(j) - \bar T(j) \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the variance denominator:**

$$
\sigma_H^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( H_i(k) - \bar H \right)^2
$$

$$
\sigma_T^2(j) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( T_i(j) - \bar T(j) \right)^2
$$

**Step 5 — Assemble the Pearson correlation coefficient:**

$$
r(j; k) = \frac{\text{Cov}(H, T)(j; k)}{\sqrt{\sigma_H^2 \cdot \sigma_T^2(j)}}
$$

**Step 6 — Key recovery by maximum correlation:**

$$
\hat k = \underset{k}{\arg\max}\; \max_{j}\; \vert r(j; k) \vert
$$

For an unprotected AES implementation, the maximum $\vert r \vert$ for the correct key typically reaches **0.6 – 0.95** at the S-box output time, while wrong keys remain below **0.1**.

**Step 7 — Confidence threshold (Gaussian) for unambiguous recovery:**

$$
\Delta \rho \;=\; \rho_{\text{correct}}^{\max} - \rho_{\text{wrong, 2nd}}^{\max} \;>\; \frac{4}{\sqrt{N}}
$$

The factor **4** corresponds to a **4σ confidence interval**, i.e., > **99.993%** statistical confidence that the recovered key is correct.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (CPA)

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:** $N = 1000$ traces, $\rho_{\text{correct}} = 0.82$, $\rho_{\text{wrong, 2nd}} = 0.18$.
> **Find:** Does this confirm correct key recovery?

$$
\Delta \rho = 0.82 - 0.18 = 0.64
$$

$$
\text{Threshold} = \frac{4}{\sqrt{1000}} = \frac{4}{31.62} = 0.1265
$$

Since $0.64 \gg 0.1265$, the key is recovered with extremely high confidence. ✓

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Which Attack for Which Target?

| Target Device | Recommended Attack | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| **Smart card / Secure Element** | Invasive micro-probing + EM SCA | High-value target, low noise |
| **FPGA bitstream** | Bitstream reverse engineering + SCA | Bitstream is unencrypted in many FPGAs |
| **Microcontroller (unshielded)** | Power SCA (CPA) | Easy power access via shunt |
| **Multi-tenant Cloud CPU** | Flush+Reload / Prime+Probe | Logical side-channel via caches |
| **DRAM (Rowhammer)** | Rowhammer / RAMBleed | Bit-flip in adjacent rows |
| **JTAG-enabled board** | JTAG debug access | Direct register/memory R/W |
| **USB / Peripheral** | Malicious firmware (BadUSB) | Reprogrammed firmware |
| **Smartphone** | Cold-boot + EM SCA | Volatile memory remanence |
| **PCB bus** | Bus-probing / fault injection | Exposed I²C/SPI/UART |
| **GPU / AI accelerator** | Model-extraction via power/EM | IP theft of trained weights |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Side-Channel Attack Pipeline

> [!NOTE]
> Mermaid block diagram. All node IDs are alphanumeric, labels are plain uppercase, and no formatting tags are embedded in node text.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[TARGET DEVICE] --> A2[CRYPTO OPERATION]
    A2 --> A3[PHYSICAL LEAKAGE]
    A3 --> A4[ACQUISITION PROBE]
    A4 --> A5[ADC SAMPLING]
    A5 --> A6[TRACE STORAGE]
    A6 --> A7[ALIGNMENT FILTER]
    A7 --> A8[KEY HYPOTHESIS LOOP]
    A8 --> A9[LEAKAGE MODEL HW HD]
    A9 --> A10[STATISTICAL DISTINGUISHER]
    A10 --> A11[CORRELATION PEAK SEARCH]
    A11 --> A12[RECOVERED SECRET KEY]
    A12 --> A13[KEY CONFIDENCE CHECK]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Hardware Attack Taxonomy (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B0[HARDWARE ATTACKS ROOT]
    B0 --> B1[INVASIVE]
    B0 --> B2[SEMI INVASIVE]
    B0 --> B3[NON INVASIVE]
    B0 --> B4[MICRO ARCHITECTURAL]

    B1 --> B1A[DEPACKAGING]
    B1 --> B1B[MICRO PROBING]
    B1 --> B1C[FIB CIRCUIT EDIT]
    B1 --> B1D[POLISH AND ETCH]

    B2 --> B2A[LASER FAULT INJECTION]
    B2 --> B2B[OPTICAL EMISSION ANALYSIS]
    B2 --> B2C[UV ERASURE]

    B3 --> B3A[POWER ANALYSIS SPA DPA CPA]
    B3 --> B3B[ELECTROMAGNETIC DEMA]
    B3 --> B3C[TIMING ATTACKS]
    B3 --> B3D[ACOUSTIC CRYPTANALYSIS]
    B3 --> B3E[CACHE TIMING]

    B4 --> B4A[ROWHAMMER]
    B4 --> B4B[DRAMA RAMBLEED]
    B4 --> B5A[SPECTRE VARIANTS]
    B4 --> B5B[MELTDOWN VARIANTS]
    B4 --> B6A[FAULTS ON BUS]
    B4 --> B6B[JTAG EXPLOIT]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Countermeasure Layering Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    C1[ALGORITHMIC LEVEL] --> C1A[CONSTANT TIME CODE]
    C1 --> C1B[BLINDING MASKING]
    C2[CIRCUIT LEVEL] --> C2A[DUAL RAIL LOGIC]
    C2 --> C2B[SENSOR FULL CUSTOM]
    C2 --> C2C[SHIELDING MESH LAYERS]
    C3[LAYOUT LEVEL] --> C3A[FLAT FILL METAL]
    C3 --> C3B[SCRAMBLING BUS]
    C4[SYSTEM LEVEL] --> C4A[JTAG LOCK FUSE]
    C4 --> C4B[TRUSTED BOOT TPCM]
    C4 --> C4C[REMOTE ATTESTATION]
    C4 --> C4D[TAMPER DETECT ENCLOSURE]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Rowhammer Conceptual Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[ATTACKER ROW A] --> R2[REPEATED ACTIVATION]
    R2 --> R3[ELECTRICAL COUPLING]
    R3 --> R4[VICTIM ROW B]
    R4 --> R5[CHARGE LEAKAGE]
    R5 --> R6[BIT FLIP IN VICTIM CELL]
    R6 --> R7[PRIVILEGE ESCALATION OR DATA CORRUPTION]
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram — JTAG-Based Hardware Attack Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    J1[JTAG PINS TDI TDO TMS TCK] --> J2[BYPASS DEVICE AUTH]
    J2 --> J3[ENTER DEBUG MODE]
    J3 --> J4[DUMP FIRMWARE]
    J3 --> J5[PATCH FIRMWARE]
    J3 --> J6[EXTRACT KEY FROM SECURE FUSE]
    J4 --> J7[REVERSE ENGINEER]
    J5 --> J8[BYPASS BOOTLOADER]
    J6 --> J9[COMPLETE DEVICE COMPROMISE]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**

**Q1. (3 Marks)** *Differentiate between invasive, semi-invasive, and non-invasive hardware attacks with one example each.*

**Model Answer:**
- **Invasive attacks** physically modify or penetrate the device package (e.g., chemical de-capsulation with HNO₃ followed by micro-probing of internal bus lines).
- **Semi-invasive attacks** expose the die but do not make electrical contact; they induce faults optically (e.g., laser fault injection using a near-IR pulsed laser).
- **Non-invasive attacks** observe the device's externally available physical emanations without opening the package (e.g., Differential Power Analysis of a smart card using a shunt resistor on the power pin).

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner cue:** Award **1 mark per category** for the correct distinction and example.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**

**Q2. (3 Marks)** *List any three countermeasures against side-channel power analysis attacks and state the principle behind each.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Masking** – Randomize intermediate values $L$ by XOR with a uniform random mask $M$: $L' = L \oplus M$. Power becomes independent of $L$.
2. **Hiding (constant-power logic)** – Use dual-rail pre-charge logic (e.g., Sense Amplifier Based Logic, SABL) so every cycle consumes identical power regardless of data.
3. **Shuffling** – Randomize the order in which S-boxes are evaluated across multiple clock cycles, decorrelating the time-of-processing with the secret.
4. (Acceptable alternative) **On-chip voltage regulator + noise generator** – Increase the noise floor $\sigma_n$ to drown the signal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner cue:** 1 mark for each correctly named countermeasure + principle; 0.5+0.5 scheme.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Style, Module 1]**

> **Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Differential Power Analysis (DPA)** attack methodology. Define the difference-of-means distinguisher and explain how the *correct key guess* is identified from a set of 256 candidates. (7 marks)
>
> **Q. (b)** Derive the **Pearson Correlation Power Analysis (CPA)** distinguisher starting from the Hamming-weight leakage hypothesis. Show that CPA yields a confidence threshold $\Delta \rho > \frac{4}{\sqrt{N}}$ for unambiguous key recovery. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Setup (1 mark):**
Collect $N$ power traces $T_i$ while the device encrypts known plaintexts $P_i$. The attacker targets a single bit $b$ of the S-box output $L = S(P_i \oplus k)$ for a guessed key byte $k$.

**Step 2 — Partitioning (2 marks):**
For each candidate $k \in \{0,\ldots,255\}$ and a chosen target bit $b$, partition the traces into sets $S_0, S_1$:

$$
S_b(k) = \{\, i \mid \text{bit } b \text{ of } S(P_i \oplus k) = b \,\}
$$

**Step 3 — Difference-of-Means computation (2 marks):**

$$
\Delta(j; k) = \frac{1}{N_1}\sum_{i \in S_1} T_i(j) - \frac{1}{N_0}\sum_{i \in S_0} T_i(j)
$$

**Step 4 — Key-recovery rule (2 marks):**
The correct key produces a sharp peak $\vert \Delta(j^\star; k_\text{true}) \vert \gg 0$ at the S-box output time $j^\star$. The key is recovered as:

$$
\hat k = \underset{k}{\arg\max}\; \max_j\; \vert \Delta(j; k) \vert
$$

Use the block diagram from SECTION 4.1 for the full attack pipeline.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Hypothesis Model (1.5 marks):**
Assume Hamming-weight leakage:

$$
H_i(k) = HW\!\left( S(P_i \oplus k) \right)
$$

**Step 2 — Mean subtraction (1 mark):** Form $\bar H$ and $\bar T(j)$ as in §3.2 Steps 2.

**Step 3 — Pearson coefficient assembly (2 marks):**

$$
r(j; k) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N}(H_i - \bar H)(T_i(j) - \bar T(j))}{\sqrt{\sum_i (H_i - \bar H)^2 \cdot \sum_i (T_i(j) - \bar T(j))^2}}
$$

**Step 4 — Confidence threshold derivation (2.5 marks):**
By the Central Limit Theorem, for wrong keys the correlation is approximately $\mathcal{N}(0, 1/N)$. The maximum among 255 wrong keys scales as $\sqrt{2 \ln(255)/N} \approx 4/\sqrt{N}$. Hence the correct key (with mean $\rho_{\text{correct}}$) is distinguishable when:

$$
\Delta \rho = \rho_{\text{correct}}^{\max} - \rho_{\text{wrong, 2nd}}^{\max} > \frac{4}{\sqrt{N}}
$$

This corresponds to a **4σ Gaussian confidence**, i.e., > **99.993%** statistical certainty.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style, Module 1]**

> **Q. (a)** Explain **Rowhammer** and **RAMBleed** attacks. Discuss how repeated DRAM row activations cause bit flips in adjacent rows, and how RAMBleed extends this to data exfiltration. (7 marks)
>
> **Q. (b)** Describe **micro-architectural side-channel attacks** based on CPU caches. With diagrams, differentiate between **Flush+Reload**, **Prime+Probe**, and **Evict+Time** techniques. Mention one mitigation. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — DRAM physical basis (1.5 marks):**
A DRAM cell is a 1-Transistor + 1-Capacitor structure. The capacitor leaks charge through the access transistor's sub-threshold leakage. The cell must be *refreshed* every 64 ms (standard JEDEC).

**Step 2 — Rowhammer mechanism (2.5 marks):**
Repeatedly opening and closing (ACTIVATING) an *aggressor* row A in a tight loop causes **electrical coupling** to adjacent victim rows A-1 and A+1. After approximately $N_{\text{act}} \geq 139{,}000$ activations (DDR3 typical), the charge of victim cells drops below the bit-line sense-amplifier threshold, causing an *unintended bit flip*.

**Step 3 — Exploitation (1.5 marks):**
The attacker maps victim-row pages into a page table, then uses rowhammer to flip page-table bits and obtain **read/write access to arbitrary physical pages** — achieving kernel privilege escalation (e.g., Google's Project Zero exploit on Linux/Android).

**Step 4 — RAMBleed extension (1.5 marks):**
RAMBleed (2020) observes that the *direction* of the bit flip (0→1 or 1→0) carries information about the victim's *original* bit value, allowing **read** (not just write) of victim-row data — including cryptographic keys stored in adjacent rows.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Why caches leak (1 mark):**
Last-Level Caches (LLC) are **shared** across cores and **partitioned** into *sets*. Memory accesses hit or miss with a measurable time gap:

$$
T_{\text{miss}} \approx 200 \text{ cycles} \gg T_{\text{hit}} \approx 4 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 2 — Flush+Reload (2 marks):**
- **Step 1:** Attacker calls `clflush` on a shared memory line.
- **Step 2:** Victim executes (or not) a code path that loads that line.
- **Step 3:** Attacker times the reload. **Fast ⇒ victim accessed it; Slow ⇒ not.**

**Step 3 — Prime+Probe (2 marks):**
- **Step 1:** Attacker *primes* a cache set by filling it with its own lines.
- **Step 2:** Victim runs; its accesses *evict* some attacker lines.
- **Step 3:** Attacker *probes* by timing reloads of its lines; the **evicted set index** reveals the victim's accessed set.

**Step 4 — Evict+Time (1 mark):**
The attacker evicts specific sets, measures the victim's execution time, and infers which set the victim uses based on slowdowns.

**Step 5 — Mitigation (1 mark):**
- **Cache partitioning** (Intel CAT, Cache QoS).
- **Constant-time programming** (avoid data-dependent memory accesses).
- **Hardware mitigations:** Disable LLC sharing, randomized cache indexing, secure memory encryption (AMD SME, Intel TME).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Hardware Attacks**
> 1. **Confusing *side-channel* with *side-effect*:** Side-channel attacks exploit *unintended* leakage, not data corruption. Do not mix up SCA with fault injection.
> 2. **Forgetting *q* (number of traces):** Always state how many traces are *required* for your attack. DPA/CPA without a trace-count estimate loses **2 marks**.
> 3. **Wrong model:** Mixing Hamming-Weight and Hamming-Distance leakage models in the same solution. Pick **one** and justify it.
> 4. **Missing the threshold derivation:** For CPA, students often quote "$r$ is high" without deriving the **$4/\sqrt{N}$** confidence threshold — this is a **2-mark** differentiator.
> 5. **Ignoring micro-architectural context:** When asked about *cache attacks*, students frequently describe only Flush+Reload and skip Prime+Probe. Listing all three is the safe KTU answer pattern.
> 6. **No mention of countermeasures:** Any "explain attack" question without a paragraph on mitigation is considered incomplete and loses **at least 1.5 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Hardware Attacks**

- [x] **Three categories:** Invasive, Semi-Invasive, Non-Invasive.
- [x] **Five canonical side channels:** Power, EM, Timing, Acoustic, Cache.
- [x] **Two key statistical distinguishers:** DPA (Difference-of-Means) and CPA (Pearson Correlation).
- [x] **CPA confidence rule:** $\Delta \rho > \frac{4}{\sqrt{N}}$ (4σ Gaussian).
- [x] **Hamming-weight model:** $L = \alpha \cdot HW(L) + \beta + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$.
- [x] **SNR scales linearly with q:** $\text{SNR}_q = q \cdot \text{SNR}_1$.
- [x] **Rowhammer threshold:** ~$10^5$ activations in DDR3/4.
- [x] **Three cache-attack flavors:** Flush+Reload, Prime+Probe, Evict+Time.
- [x] **RAMBleed = read** variant; **Rowhammer = write** variant of DRAM disturbance.
- [x] **Micro-architectural attacks (CPU):** Spectre (speculative), Meltdown (transient), Foreshadow (L1).
- [x] **Hardware Trojan = supply-chain malicious insertion** at RTL/gate/fab stage.
- [x] **JTAG = debug interface that bypasses software security** if fuses not blown.
- [x] **Countermeasures stack:** algorithmic (masking, blinding) → circuit (dual-rail, shielding) → layout (scrambling) → system (TPM, attestation, tamper mesh).
- [x] **Standards:** FIPS 140-3, Common Criteria EAL4+/EAL5+, EMVCo, ISO/IEC 17825 (SCA testing).
- [x] **Equation to remember:** $I(L; O) = H(L) - H(L \vert O)$ — the *fundamental leakage bound*.
- [x] **Timing rule of thumb:** cache hit ≈ 1–4 cycles, miss ≈ 100–300 cycles — the **gap** is the side channel.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cyber Threats and Its Classifications: Malware
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST721 - CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cyber Threats and Its Classifications: Malware

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Malware** (short for **Malicious Software**) is any program, code, script, or instruction deliberately engineered to perform unauthorized, harmful, or disruptive actions on a computer system, network, server, or end-user device, without the informed consent of the system owner. According to the **NIST Special Publication 800-83 Revision 1** (Guide to Malware Incident Prevention and Handling), malware is defined as *a program that is covertly or unexpectedly inserted into a system with the intent of compromising the confidentiality, integrity, or availability of the victim's data, applications, or operating system.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Standardized):**
> Malware is a broad umbrella term that encompasses any hostile, intrusive, or intentionally harmful software designed to exploit, damage, disrupt, or gain illegitimate access to digital infrastructure. It is the *primary delivery vehicle* for virtually every category of cyber attack in the modern threat landscape.

### The CIA Triad — Why Malware Exists

Every piece of malware is engineered to violate at least one pillar of the **CIA Triad**, the foundational security model:

| Pillar | What It Protects | How Malware Violates It |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Confidentiality** | Data privacy and secrecy | Spyware, Keyloggers, Ransomware (data theft) |
| **Integrity** | Trustworthiness of data | Viruses, Worms (data tampering) |
| **Availability** | Access to systems and services | Ransomware, DoS Malware, Wipers |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Biological Virus Model

The clearest way to understand malware is to compare it to a **biological virus**, an analogy universally used in cybersecurity pedagogy:

| Biological Virus | Malware (Cyber Virus) |
| :--- | :--- |
| Spreads through contact or air | Spreads through networks, email, USB, downloads |
| Hijacks healthy cells to replicate | Hijacks legitimate programs or OS processes |
| Has a payload (fever, organ damage) | Has a payload (deletion, encryption, theft) |
| Can lie dormant (incubation) | Can activate on a trigger (logic bomb) |
| Mutates to evade immune system | Polymorphic/metamorphic code evades antivirus |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** Think of malware as a *digital pathogen*. Just as a flu virus needs a *host cell* to reproduce, malware needs a *host program* or *host system* to execute. A virus floating in a file is dormant — it becomes dangerous the moment the host program is executed.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Malware Analysis

- **MITRE ATT&CK ID** — A globally recognized catalog of adversary tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs).
- **CVSS Score (Common Vulnerability Scoring System)** — A **0.0 to 10.0** numerical score reflecting severity.
- **Malware Signature** — A unique cryptographic hash (typically **MD5, SHA-1, or SHA-256**) used by antivirus engines to identify known malware.
- **Indicators of Compromise (IoCs)** — Forensic artifacts (IP addresses, domain names, file hashes, registry keys) confirming a breach.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When writing definitions in Part A (3-mark) questions, always quote the *category* of malware (e.g., "ransomware is a type of malware that…"). Examiners reward keyword-rich answers aligned with the standard KTU glossary.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Master Classification of Malware

Malware is not a single entity — it is a **taxonomy of attack tools**. The KTU 2024 syllabus mandates a layered understanding of the following categories:

### 2.1.1 Virus
- A **self-replicating program** that attaches itself to a *legitimate host file* (e.g., `.exe`, `.doc`, `.vbs`).
- **Requires user action** (opening a file) to execute and spread.
- **Why?** It cannot propagate on its own — it needs a "carrier."
- **How?** Modifies the host file so that when executed, the virus code runs first, then the original program.

**Categories of Virus by Infection Target:**

| Type | Infection Mechanism |
| :--- | :--- |
| **File Virus** | Attaches to `.exe`, `.com`, `.sys` files |
| **Boot Sector Virus** | Infects the Master Boot Record (MBR) |
| **Macro Virus** | Embedded in Office documents (VBA macros) |
| **Polymorphic Virus** | Mutates its code (signature) on each infection |
| **Metamorphic Virus** | Rewrites its own code entirely |
| **Stealth Virus** | Hides from OS and antivirus by intercepting calls |
| **Multipartite Virus** | Infects both boot sector and files |

### 2.1.2 Worm
- A **self-replicating, self-propagating** program that spreads **autonomously over networks** — *no human action required*.
- **Why?** Designed for mass-scale, exponential network infection.
- **How?** Exploits network vulnerabilities (buffer overflows, open ports, weak credentials) to copy itself to other machines.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Distinction (Frequently Asked):** *Virus needs a host file and human action. Worm is self-contained and self-spreading across networks.*

### 2.1.3 Trojan Horse
- **Disguises itself as legitimate, useful software** but contains a hidden malicious payload.
- **Why?** Bypasses user suspicion by appearing trustworthy.
- **How?** The user installs it voluntarily (e.g., "Free Antivirus 2024"). Once executed, the payload opens a backdoor, steals data, or downloads more malware.

**Trojan Sub-Types:**

| Sub-Type | Payload |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Backdoor Trojan** | Creates remote access (RAT) |
| **Banking Trojan** | Steals financial credentials (e.g., Zeus) |
| **Downloader Trojan** | Pulls additional malware from C2 server |
| **Infostealer Trojan** | Exfiltrates passwords, cookies, documents |
| **Remote Access Trojan (RAT)** | Full remote control of victim's machine |

### 2.1.4 Ransomware
- **Encrypts victim's files** (or locks the entire system) and demands a ransom (typically cryptocurrency) for the decryption key.
- **Why?** Direct monetization of attack — the most financially damaging malware category in 2020–2025.
- **How?** Uses strong cryptography (RSA-2048, AES-256), then displays a ransom note.

**Famous Examples:** *WannaCry (2017), NotPetya (2017), Ryuk, LockBit, Conti.*

### 2.1.5 Spyware
- **Covertly monitors user activity** and transmits data to a third party.
- Includes **Keyloggers** (record keystrokes), **Screen Capturers**, and **Stalkerware**.

### 2.1.6 Adware
- **Displays unwanted advertisements**, often bundled with free software. Technically less harmful but degrades UX and may leak browsing data.

### 2.1.7 Rootkit
- **Malicious code that gains privileged (root/admin) access** and *hides itself* deep within the OS kernel or firmware.
- **Why?** Persistence and stealth — survives antivirus scans and OS reinstalls in some cases.

### 2.1.8 Botnet / Bot Malware
- Infects a machine to turn it into a **"zombie" or "bot"** controlled remotely by a **Command-and-Control (C2) server**.
- Used for **DDoS attacks, spam campaigns, crypto-mining, and click fraud**.

### 2.1.9 Fileless Malware
- Operates **entirely in memory (RAM)**, leaving no footprint on disk.
- **Why?** Bypasses traditional file-based antivirus.
- **How?** Lives inside legitimate system tools like PowerShell, WMI, or .NET frameworks.

### 2.1.10 Logic Bomb & Time Bomb
- **Dormant code** that triggers a malicious action when a specific condition is met (a date, a user action, file deletion).
- **Why?** Insider threat vector — often planted by disgruntled employees.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-ready consolidated reference** for Module 1. Master this and you can answer 80% of theory questions.

| Attribute | Formula / Concept | Definition | Real-World Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Infection Vector (Virus)** | $I_v = f(H, U)$ | Depends on Host file + User execution | Email attachments, USB drives |
| **Propagation Rate (Worm)** | $R_0 = \beta \cdot \frac{N}{\gamma}$ | Basic reproduction number (epidemiological model) | Network-wide worm spread modeling |
| **Worm Spread (SI Model)** | $\frac{dI}{dt} = \beta \cdot S \cdot I$ | Rate of new infections in Susceptible-Infected model | Slammer worm (2003) infected 75,000 hosts in 10 min |
| **Ransomware Encryption** | $C = E_{K_{pub}}(K_{AES}, \text{file})$ | Encrypts files using hybrid RSA+AES | WannaCry used AES-128 + RSA-2048 |
| **Malware Signature** | $H = \text{SHA-256}(\text{file})$ | Cryptographic hash for unique identification | Antivirus database matching |
| **CVSS Base Score** | $\text{CVSS} = f(AV, AC, PR, UI, S, C, I, A)$ | Function of 8 base metrics (0.0 to 10.0) | Vulnerability prioritization |
| **Botnet Size** | $B = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{bot}_i$ | Total number of infected "zombie" hosts | Mirai botnet peaked at **2.5M bots** (2016) |
| **Dwell Time** | $T_d = T_{detect} - T_{compromise}$ | Time between infection and detection | Industry average: ~204 days (IBM 2023) |
| **MITRE ATT&CK Tactic ID** | TA0001 to TA0008 | Tactic numbering (e.g., TA0002 = Execution) | Threat intelligence mapping |
| **Polymorphic Mutation Rate** | $M_r = \frac{\text{unique variants}}{\text{infections}}$ | Code variation per infection | Emotet peaked at $M_r \approx 0.97$ |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Malware classification is **not academic** — it drives industry-grade decisions:

1. **Incident Response Teams** use the classification to determine containment strategy (e.g., isolating a worm vs. negotiating with ransomware actors).
2. **SIEM/SOAR Platforms** (Splunk, IBM QRadar) use signatures ($H$) for real-time detection.
3. **Threat Intelligence Platforms** (MISP, Anomali) map malware families to **MITRE ATT&CK TTPs**.
4. **Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR)** tools use behavioral analysis to catch fileless and zero-day malware that signature-based AV misses.
5. **Insurance Underwriters** (cyber-insurance) price premiums using CVSS and dwell-time metrics.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** A malware analyst's job is fundamentally a *pattern-recognition + reverse-engineering* problem. Understanding the classification helps decide *which sandbox* (Cuckoo, Joe Sandbox) or *which disassembler* (IDA Pro, Ghidra) to deploy.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown & Propagation Models

This section provides exhaustive analytical derivations of malware infection chains and propagation dynamics — the kind of structured, step-by-step reasoning KTU examiners reward in 14-mark questions.

## 3.1 The Universal Malware Lifecycle (Analytical Model)

Every malware, regardless of category, follows a **five-stage lifecycle**. We will derive and explain each stage.

### Stage 1 — Delivery (Infection Vector)

The malware must reach the victim system. The most common delivery vectors are:

$$
\text{Delivery Vectors} = \begin{cases}
\text{Email Attachments} \\
\text{Malicious URLs (Phishing)} \\
\text{USB / Removable Media} \\
\text{Software Downloads (Trojanized)} \\
\text{Network Exploits (Worms)} \\
\text{Supply Chain Compromise} \\
\text{Drive-by Downloads}
\end{cases}
$$

**Analytical Insight:** The *delivery vector* determines the *threat actor's sophistication*. A USB drop is low-skill; a supply-chain attack (e.g., SolarWinds) is nation-state level.

### Stage 2 — Exploitation (Triggering)

The malware exploits a *vulnerability* or *user behavior* to gain initial code execution.

For a **file-based virus**, the trigger is:

$$
\text{Trigger}_{virus} = (\text{User opens file}) \land (\text{File is executed})
$$

For a **worm**, the trigger is *autonomous*:

$$
\text{Trigger}_{worm} = (\text{Target port is open}) \land (\text{Vulnerability present}) \land (\text{Exploit succeeds})
$$

### Stage 3 — Installation (Persistence)

The malware establishes itself on the system. Common persistence mechanisms:

| Mechanism | Description | OS Target |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Registry Run Keys** | `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run` | Windows |
| **Scheduled Tasks** | Cron jobs / Task Scheduler | Linux / Windows |
| **Startup Folder** | LNK files in Startup directory | Windows |
| **Services** | Installs as a Windows Service | Windows |
| **Bootkits** | Modifies MBR/EFI | All |

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-Step Logic:** *Why persistence matters* — without it, a simple reboot would wipe the malware. Persistence is what makes malware a *chronic* problem, not an *acute* one.

### Stage 4 — Command & Control (C2 Communication)

Modern malware rarely acts alone. It "phones home" to a C2 server to:

- Receive updated instructions
- Exfiltrate harvested data
- Download additional payloads (modular malware)

**C2 Channels:**

$$
C2_{channel} \in \{\text{HTTP/HTTPS}, \text{DNS tunneling}, \text{IRC}, \text{Tor}, \text{Telegram API}, \text{Social media DMs}\}
$$

### Stage 5 — Action on Objectives (Payload Execution)

The final stage is the *malicious action* the attacker actually wanted:

$$
\text{Payload Actions} = \begin{cases}
\text{Data Exfiltration} \\
\text{File Encryption (Ransomware)} \\
\text{System Destruction (Wiper)} \\
\text{Credential Theft} \\
\text{DDoS Launch} \\
\text{Cryptomining} \\
\text{Escalation to Lateral Movement}
\end{cases}
$$

## 3.2 Worm Propagation Model — SI Epidemiological Derivation

The **Susceptible-Infected (SI) Model** is borrowed from epidemiology and applied to network worms. We derive it step-by-step.

**Step 1 — Define the variables:**

$$
S(t) = \text{Number of susceptible (uninfected) hosts at time } t
$$
$$
I(t) = \text{Number of infected hosts at time } t
$$
$$
N = S(t) + I(t) = \text{Total network size (constant)}
$$
$$
\beta = \text{Infection rate (probability of transmission per contact)}
$$

**Step 2 — State the rate equation:**

The rate of *new infections* per unit time depends on:
- The number of infected hosts that can spread ($I$)
- The number of susceptible hosts that can be infected ($S$)
- The contact rate per infected host ($\beta$)

$$
\frac{dI}{dt} = \beta \cdot S \cdot I = \beta \cdot (N - I) \cdot I
$$

**Step 3 — Solve the differential equation:**

We perform separation of variables:

$$
\frac{dI}{(N - I) \cdot I} = \beta \cdot dt
$$

Using partial fractions:

$$
\frac{1}{I \cdot (N - I)} = \frac{1}{N} \left( \frac{1}{I} + \frac{1}{N - I} \right)
$$

Therefore:

$$
\int \frac{1}{N} \left( \frac{1}{I} + \frac{1}{N - I} \right) dI = \int \beta \cdot dt
$$

Integrating both sides:

$$
\frac{1}{N} \left[ \ln(I) - \ln(N - I) \right] = \beta \cdot t + C
$$

Simplifying:

$$
\ln\left( \frac{I}{N - I} \right) = \beta \cdot N \cdot t + C'
$$

Exponentiating:

$$
\frac{I}{N - I} = K \cdot e^{\beta N t}
$$

where $K$ is determined by the initial condition $I(0) = I_0$.

**Step 4 — Final solution (logistic growth):**

$$
I(t) = \frac{N}{1 + \left( \frac{N - I_0}{I_0} \right) e^{-\beta N t}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Worm propagation follows a **logistic S-curve**. Initially slow (few vectors), then *exponential explosion* in the middle phase, finally saturating as susceptible hosts run out. This is why early detection and *network segmentation* are so critical — they reduce the effective $N$ and slow the curve.

## 3.3 Ransomware Encryption — Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough

Modern ransomware uses **hybrid cryptography** (asymmetric + symmetric). We trace the exact algorithm:

**Step 1 — Generate a unique symmetric key per file:**

$$
K_{AES,i} = \text{GenerateRandomKey}() \quad \text{for each file } i
$$

**Step 2 — Encrypt the file content with the symmetric key:**

$$
C_i = \text{AES-256-Encrypt}(K_{AES,i}, \text{File}_i)
$$

**Step 3 — Encrypt the symmetric key with the attacker's public RSA key:**

$$
C_{key,i} = \text{RSA-Encrypt}(K_{pub}, K_{AES,i})
$$

**Step 4 — Delete the original plaintext file and write the ciphertext:**

$$
\text{Disk} \leftarrow \{C_i, C_{key,i}\} \quad \forall i \in \{\text{target files}\}
$$

**Step 5 — Display ransom note demanding payment for $K_{priv}$.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Why this is devastating:** Without the attacker's $K_{priv}$, decrypting $C_{key,i}$ is *computationally infeasible* (RSA-2048 = $2^{2048}$ keyspace). This is why paying the ransom is often the *only* path to recovery — a core ethical dilemma discussed in KTU seminars.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Malware Categories Compared

| Attribute | Virus | Worm | Trojan | Ransomware | Spyware |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Self-Replicating?** | Yes | Yes | No | No | No |
| **Needs User Action?** | Yes | No | Yes (install) | Yes (click/attachment) | Often Yes |
| **Network Spread?** | No | Yes | No | Limited (lateral) | Limited |
| **Primary Motive** | Damage, ego | Propagation | Backdoor access | Money | Surveillance |
| **Stealth Level** | Medium | Low (noisy) | High (disguised) | Medium | High |
| **Typical Payload** | Corruption | Backdoor, DDoS | RAT, Stealer | Encryption | Keylogging |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

The following Mermaid diagrams are **board-exam friendly block diagrams** illustrating malware taxonomy, attack lifecycle, and infection propagation. They are safe to render and contain no reserved-keyword violations.

## 4.1 Master Malware Classification Taxonomy

```mermaid
    B1 --> C1[File Virus]

    B9 --> C21[PowerShell Based]
    B9 --> C22[WMI Based]
    B9 --> C23[In Memory Reflective]
```

## 4.2 The Universal Malware Attack Chain (Kill Chain)

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1[STAGE 1 DELIVERY] --> S2[STAGE 2 EXPLOITATION]
    S2 --> S3[STAGE 3 INSTALLATION]
    S3 --> S4[STAGE 4 COMMAND AND CONTROL]
    S4 --> S5[STAGE 5 ACTION ON OBJECTIVES]

    S1 --> D1[Email Attachment]
    S1 --> D2[Phishing URL]
    S1 --> D3[USB Drop]
    S1 --> D4[Network Exploit]
    S1 --> D5[Supply Chain]

    S2 --> E1[User Clicks File]
    S2 --> E2[Buffer Overflow]
    S2 --> E3[Macro Execution]
    S2 --> E4[Privilege Escalation]

    S3 --> I1[Registry Persistence]
    S3 --> I2[Scheduled Task]
    S3 --> I3[Service Install]
    S3 --> I4[Bootkit MBR]

    S4 --> C1[HTTP C2 Channel]
    S4 --> C2[DNS Tunneling]
    S4 --> C3[Tor Hidden Service]

    S5 --> A1[Data Exfiltration]
    S5 --> A2[File Encryption]
    S5 --> A3[DDoS Launch]
    S5 --> A4[Credential Theft]
    S5 --> A5[System Destruction]
```

## 4.3 Worm Propagation — SI Model Visualization

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Time T0 Few Initial Infections] --> P2[Phase 1 Slow Start Limited Vectors]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 2 Exponential Growth Beta Times N]
    P3 --> P4[Phase 3 Saturation Few Susceptibles Remain]
    P4 --> P5[Phase 4 Plateau Network Fully Compromised]

    P1 --> M1[Countermeasure Deploy Patches Early]
    P2 --> M2[Countermeasure Network Segmentation]
    P3 --> M3[Countermeasure Isolate Infected Subnets]
    P4 --> M4[Countermeasure Rebuild From Clean Backups]
    P5 --> M5[Countermeasure Post Incident Forensics]
```

## 4.4 Ransomware Operational Flow Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    R1[Initial Access Phishing RDP Exploit] --> R2[Disable Backups and Shadow Copies]
    R2 --> R3[Enumerate Network Shares]
    R3 --> R4[Generate AES Key Per File]
    R4 --> R5[Encrypt Files With AES 256]
    R5 --> R6[Encrypt AES Keys With RSA 2048]
    R6 --> R7[Drop Ransom Note and C2 Beacon]
    R7 --> R8[Demand Cryptocurrency Payment]
    R8 --> R9{Victim Pays}
```
