```mermaid
    branchA --> branchA1["IT Act 66A to 66E"]
    branchB --> branchB1["IT Act 43 and 66"]
    branchC --> branchC1["IT Act 66F"]
    branchD --> branchD1["IT Act 67 and 67B"]

    responseNode --> resp1["Containment"]
    responseNode --> resp2["Eradication"]
    responseNode --> resp3["Recovery"]
    responseNode --> resp4["Forensic Analysis"]
    responseNode --> resp5["Legal Action"]
```

### 4.4 Salami Attack Operational Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    attackerNode["MALICIOUS INSIDER ATTACKER"]
    bankNode["BANK SOFTWARE WITH SABOTAGED CODE"]
    userNode1["ACCOUNT 1 - LOSS 0.50"]
    userNode2["ACCOUNT 2 - LOSS 0.50"]
    userNode3["ACCOUNT 3 - LOSS 0.50"]
    userNodeDots["ACCOUNT N - LOSS 0.50"]
    accumNode["AGGREGATE STOLEN FUND POOL"]
    withdrawalNode["ATTACKER WITHDRAWAL"]

    attackerNode --> bankNode
    bankNode --> userNode1
    bankNode --> userNode2
    bankNode --> userNode3
    bankNode --> userNodeDots
    userNode1 --> accumNode
    userNode2 --> accumNode
    userNode3 --> accumNode
    userNodeDots --> accumNode
    accumNode --> withdrawalNode
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading Aid:** The Salami Attack schematic shows how a single malicious code, embedded once in bank software, silently deducts microscopic amounts from millions of accounts, which are pooled into a covert fund that the attacker withdraws. Each individual victim suffers a *barely noticeable* loss — hence the name "salami" (small slices).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q1. Define cybercrime and list any four categories of cybercrimes with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Cybercrime refers to any illegal activity in which a computer, networked device, or the Internet is used as a tool, target, or accessory to commit a criminal offense.
>
> **Four Categories with Examples:**
>
> 1. **Against Individuals** — Phishing (stealing login credentials via fake emails).
> 2. **Against Property** — Hacking (unauthorized access to a corporate database).
> 3. **Against Government** — Cyberterrorism (attack on a power grid).
> 4. **Against Society** — Online gambling (unlawful betting platforms).

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for categories with examples)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q2. Differentiate between phishing and identity theft. State one IT Act section relevant to each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Phishing | Identity Theft |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Definition** | Deceptive emails/websites to steal credentials | Misuse of stolen personal information for fraud |
| **Method** | Social engineering via forged communication | Use of PII obtained through various means |
| **Primary Goal** | Credential harvesting | Financial fraud / impersonation |
| **IT Act Section** | **§66D** — Cheating by personation using computer | **§66C** — Identity theft (theft of identity information) |

*(3 marks: 1 mark each for definition, distinction, and legal section)*

---

### PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Modified for Practice]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **four-fold classification of cybercrimes** with at least **three sub-types under each category**. Cite the relevant **IT Act section** for any two categories.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Salami Attack** in detail. With a suitable mathematical example, demonstrate how a cumulative loss of **₹8,00,000** can be orchestrated through microscopic individual deductions, and explain why this attack is hard to detect.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **four-fold classification** of cybercrimes is based on the **target of the offense**.

**1. Cybercrimes Against Individuals**
- These target the personal safety, privacy, and dignity of a person.
- **Sub-types:** Phishing, Cyberstalking, Cyberbullying, Identity Theft, Email Bombing, Doxing.
- *Example:* A fraudster sending fake bank emails to extract OTPs and PINs.

**2. Cybercrimes Against Property**
- These target financial assets, data, and digital infrastructure.
- **Sub-types:** Hacking, Malware, DDoS, Data Theft, Software Piracy, Salami Attack, Credit Card Fraud.
- *Example:* A hacker deploying ransomware to encrypt a company's customer database.

**3. Cybercrimes Against Government (Cyberterrorism)**
- These threaten state sovereignty, public order, and critical infrastructure.
- **Sub-types:** Website Defacement, Cyber Warfare, Critical Infrastructure Attacks, Espionage.
- *Example:* A state-sponsored APT group breaching defense ministry servers.
- **IT Act Section:** **§66F** — Defines cyberterrorism, punishable by **imprisonment up to life**.

**4. Cybercrimes Against Society**
- These offend the collective moral, social, and legal fabric.
- **Sub-types:** Online Child Exploitation, Cyber Hoaxes, Online Gambling, Dark Web Drug Trade, Forgery.
- *Example:* Distribution of CSAM on encrypted peer-to-peer networks.
- **IT Act Section:** **§67B** — Punishment for publishing or transmitting material depicting children in sexually explicit act.

*(7 marks: ~1.5 for each category + 0.5 for legal sections)*

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**The Salami Attack:**

The **Salami Attack** is a cybercrime in which a malicious program, embedded within legitimate banking or accounting software, deducts **microscopically small amounts** of money from a large number of accounts. The deductions are so small that **individual account holders rarely notice** them, but the cumulative sum becomes a substantial illegal gain for the attacker. The term *Salami* comes from the technique of slicing a salami sausage into thin pieces.

**Mathematical Demonstration:**

Let the number of accounts be $n$ and the amount deducted per account be $\delta$.

$$L_{total} = n \cdot \delta$$

**Given:** $L_{total} = ₹8,00,000$ (target cumulative theft).

**Step 1:** Assume a deduction of $₹1.00$ per account. Solve for $n$:

$$n = \frac{L_{total}}{\delta} = \frac{8,00,000}{1.00} = 8,00,000 \text{ accounts}$$

**Step 2:** Assume a deduction of $₹2.00$ per account:

$$n = \frac{8,00,000}{2.00} = 4,00,000 \text{ accounts}$$

**Step 3:** Assume a deduction of $₹0.50$ per account:

$$n = \frac{8,00,000}{0.50} = 16,00,000 \text{ accounts}$$

**Conclusion:** The attacker can choose a deduction amount as small as ₹0.50 from 16 lakh accounts to steal ₹8,00,000 over a year. Each customer loses an amount so trivial that **no complaint is raised**, making detection exceptionally difficult.

**Why Detection is Hard:**
- Individual loss is below the customer's *threshold of complaint* (often ₹5–₹10).
- The deduction often appears as a *legitimate bank fee or service charge*.
- Aggregated detection requires **AI-based anomaly analysis** across millions of transactions.
- Insider attackers have authorized system access, bypassing perimeter defenses.

*(7 marks: 2 for concept, 3 for derivation, 2 for detection reasoning)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **classification of cybercrimes based on the role of the computer** with suitable examples for each class. Why is this classification considered more useful in certain forensic scenarios?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **Legal Framework in India** to handle cybercrimes, highlighting the **Information Technology Act 2000 (with 2008 amendments)**. List any **four landmark sections** of the IT Act with their punishments.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Three-Way Classification by Role of the Computer:**

**Class 1: Computer as a Target**
- The computer system is the *primary victim* of the attack.
- The attacker seeks to **gain unauthorized access, disrupt services, or steal data**.
- **Examples:** Hacking a server, deploying ransomware on a hospital network, launching a DDoS attack on a banking portal, defacing a government website.

**Class 2: Computer as a Tool (Weapon)**
- The computer is the *means* by which the crime is committed, while the *target is a human or organization*.
- **Examples:** Phishing emails, cyberstalking via social media, email bombing, online credit card fraud, cyberbullying, distribution of malicious links.

**Class 3: Computer as an Accomplice**
- The computer *enables or facilitates* the crime but is neither the primary target nor the primary weapon.
- **Examples:** Running an illegal online gambling platform, hosting CSAM on cloud servers, money laundering via cryptocurrency, operating dark-web marketplaces for drug trade, distributing pirated content via P2P networks.

**Why Useful in Forensics:**

- **Evidence Trail Identification:** If computer is a *target*, forensic focus is on **system logs, intrusion detection, malware analysis**. If a *tool*, focus shifts to **email headers, browser history, communication metadata**. If an *accomplice*, focus expands to **server logs, financial transactions, third-party platform records**.
- **Jurisdictional Clarity:** The role helps determine whether the crime is *against the system owner*, *against another person*, or *against public order* — each with different legal pathways.
- **Resource Allocation:** Investigators can prioritize forensic specialists (malware analysts vs. social engineers vs. financial fraud examiners) based on this classification.

*(7 marks: 1.5 for each class with examples, 2.5 for forensic utility)*

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Legal Framework for Cybercrimes in India:**

India's cyber law framework operates through the **Information Technology Act, 2000** (the primary statute), as amended in **2008**, supplemented by the **Indian Penal Code (IPC)**, the **Indian Evidence Act**, and specialized acts like **POCSO (Protection of Children from Sexual Offences Act, 2012)** and the **Copyright Act, 1957**.

**Key Sections of the IT Act, 2000 (Amended 2008):**

| Section | Offense | Punishment |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **§43** | Damage to computer system / data without permission | Compensation up to ₹1 crore to victim |
| **§66** | Hacking with malicious intent (computer-related offense) | Imprisonment up to 3 years or fine up to ₹5 lakh, or both |
| **§66C** | Identity theft (fraudulent use of another's identity info) | Imprisonment up to 3 years + fine up to ₹1 lakh |
| **§66D** | Cheating by personation using communication device | Imprisonment up to 3 years + fine up to ₹1 lakh |
| **§66E** | Violation of privacy (capturing/publishing private images) | Imprisonment up to 3 years or fine up to ₹2 lakh, or both |
| **§66F** | Cyberterrorism | Imprisonment up to **life** |
| **§67** | Publishing obscene material electronically | Imprisonment up to 5 years + fine up to ₹10 lakh (first offense) |
| **§67B** | Publishing child sexual abuse material (CSAM) | Imprisonment up to 7 years (first offense); 10 years (repeat) |
| **§69** | Government interception of digital communications | With appropriate safeguards |

**Supplementary Framework:**
- **IPC §420** — Cheating and dishonestly inducing delivery of property.
- **IPC §463/465** — Forgery and punishment for forgery.
- **POCSO Act, 2012** — Specifically handles online child sexual abuse.
- **IT (Intermediary Guidelines) Rules, 2021** — Regulate social media intermediaries.

**Why the Legal Framework Matters:**
- Provides **deterrence** through criminal prosecution.
- Enables **digital evidence admissibility** under the IT Act and Indian Evidence Act (amended).
- Empowers **agencies like CERT-In** to issue binding cybersecurity directions.

*(7 marks: 1 for introduction, 4 for sections table, 2 for supplementary framework)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing *Hacking* with *Cracking*:** The IT Act §66 uses the term *"computer-related offense"*, not "hacking". Always frame it as *"unauthorized access with intent to cause damage"* for full marks.
> 2. **Missing the Legal Section:** Many students describe a cybercrime but forget to cite the **IT Act section**. KTU examiners explicitly check this — it carries 0.5–1 mark per omission.
> 3. **Mixing Categories:** A single scenario (e.g., phishing leading to identity theft leading to bank fraud) spans **multiple categories**. KTU expects you to identify the **primary** category and explain the chain briefly.
> 4. **Salami Attack Calculation Error:** Students often forget the **aggregation step**. Always conclude with the *cumulative loss statement* to earn full derivation marks.
> 5. **Forgetting IT Act 2008 Amendments:** The 2008 amendment introduced **§66A to §66F**. Reference **post-amendment** sections only; the original 2000 numbering is partially superseded.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Classification of Cybercrimes**

- **Definition:** Cybercrime = any illegal act where a computer is the **target, tool, or accomplice**.
- **Four Primary Categories:** Against **Individuals, Property, Government, Society** — memorize sub-types under each.
- **Alternate Classification:** Three roles of computer — **Target, Tool, Accomplice**.
- **Hacking vs. Phishing:** Hacking is *unauthorized system access* (§66); Phishing is *deceptive credential theft* (§66D).
- **Identity Theft:** Defined under **IT Act §66C**.
- **Cyberterrorism:** Defined under **IT Act §66F**, punishable by **life imprisonment**.
- **CSAM / Child Exploitation:** **IT Act §67B**, **POCSO Act 2012**.
- **Salami Attack:** Uses the equation $L_{total} = n \cdot \delta$ — microscopic deductions aggregating to large thefts.
- **DDoS:** Classified as **Computer as a Target**; attack bandwidth $B = N \cdot b$.
- **Risk Severity Index:** $RSI = L \cdot (w_C \cdot C + w_I \cdot I + w_A \cdot A)$.
- **ALE Formula:** $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ — used in enterprise risk assessment.
- **Budapest Convention 2001:** First international treaty on cybercrime (often referenced in KTU questions).
- **CERT-In:** India's national incident response agency — frequently mentioned in KTU news-based questions.
- **IT Act 2008 Amendment:** Introduced **§66A to §66F, §69, §69B, §67B** — these are the most testable sections.
- **Always cite both** the *category* and the *legal section* in long-answer questions.
- **For Mermaid / diagram-based questions:** Know the three-role classification tree and the four-category tree by heart.
- **For numerical questions:** Practice the Salami Attack formula and the Risk Severity Index derivation.
- **Common confusion to avoid:** *Email bombing* is **Against Individual**, not Against Property. *Software piracy* is **Against Property**, not Against Society.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** In 14-mark questions, structure your answer as **(1) Definition → (2) Categories with examples → (3) Legal provisions → (4) Detection / Prevention → (5) Conclusion**. This five-part structure aligns with the **KTU 2024 marking scheme** and consistently scores 12+/14 marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Legal Perspectives: Indian Perspective
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST721 - CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** The Legal Perspectives: Indian Perspective

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cyber Law (Indian Perspective)** is the body of legal statutes, regulations, judicial precedents, and procedural frameworks enacted by the Government of India to govern cyberspace, regulate digital conduct, define cyber offences, prescribe punishments, and protect electronic data, privacy, and intellectual property within Indian jurisdiction.

The **primary pillars** of Indian Cyber Law are:

1. **Information Technology Act, 2000** (as amended by the IT Amendment Act, 2008) — the flagship cyber law.
2. **Indian Penal Code, 1860** (Sections relevant to offences with electronic/digital components).
3. **Indian Evidence Act, 1872** (now Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023) — admissibility of electronic records.
4. **Information Technology (Reasonable Security Practices and Procedures and Sensitive Personal Data or Information) Rules, 2011**.
5. **Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 (DPDPA)** — modern privacy framework.
6. **Indian Telegraph Act, 1885** & **Copyright Act, 1957** — supplementary statutes.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Digital Highway and Traffic Police":**
> Imagine the internet as a massive national highway system. Just as real highways need traffic signals, lane rules, speed limits, and traffic police to handle accidents, the digital world needs **laws, regulations, an enforcement agency, and a court system**. The **IT Act, 2000** is the *Highway Code*, **CERT-In** is the *traffic control room*, the **Adjudicating Officer / Cyber Appellate Tribunal** is the *court*, and **Sections 65–75 of IT Act** define the *offences* (like rash driving, hit-and-run, theft). Without these, the highway would be lawless — and that is precisely what the Indian legislature tried to prevent by enacting the IT Act in 2000.

## 1.3 Why India Needed a Dedicated Cyber Law

Before 2000, India had **no dedicated law** to deal with computer crimes. The rapid rise of e-commerce, online banking, and digital communication in the late 1990s exposed the following gaps:

- **No legal recognition** for electronic records, digital signatures, or e-contracts.
- **No legal framework** for certifying authorities issuing digital certificates.
- **No penalties** specifically for hacking, data theft, or publishing obscene material online.
- **No agency** with statutory power to handle cyber incidents.

The **United Nations Commission on International Trade Law (UNCITRAL) Model Law on Electronic Commerce (1996)** served as the global template that India adopted through the IT Act, 2000.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Statistic (for context):**
> As per **CERT-In (Indian Computer Emergency Response Team)**, India witnessed over **13.9 lakh (1.39 million) cyber security incidents** in 2022, justifying the continuous evolution of cyber law through the 2008 amendment and the DPDPA 2023.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Evolution of Indian Cyber Law — Chronology

| Year | Legislation / Event | Significance |
|------|---------------------|--------------|
| **1860** | Indian Penal Code (IPC) | Original criminal code; sections 463–477A cover forgery applicable to digital docs. |
| **1872** | Indian Evidence Act | Section 65B introduced later for admissibility of electronic evidence. |
| **1885** | Indian Telegraph Act | Governs interception of communications. |
| **1957** | Copyright Act | Protects software, digital content, databases. |
| **2000** | **Information Technology Act, 2000** | India's first dedicated cyber law. Based on UNCITRAL Model Law. |
| **2008** | **IT (Amendment) Act, 2008** | Major overhaul: added Sections 66A–66F, 67, 69, 69B; recognized cyber terrorism. |
| **2011** | **IT (Reasonable Security Practices) Rules** | SPDI (Sensitive Personal Data/Information) protection rules. |
| **2013** | Section 66A struck down (Shreya Singhal v. Union of India) | Struck down as unconstitutional — free speech win. |
| **2017** | **Supreme Court — Puttaswamy judgment** | Right to Privacy declared a Fundamental Right. |
| **2023** | **Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023** | India's first comprehensive data protection statute. |
| **2023** | **Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS), 2023** | Replaces IPC; introduces new cyber-related offences. |

## 2.2 The Information Technology Act, 2000 — Structural Overview

The IT Act, 2000 (as amended in 2008) is divided into **13 Chapters and 90+ Sections**. The most examinable portions are:

| Chapter | Sections | Subject Matter |
|---------|----------|----------------|
| **Chapter II** | 3–10 | Digital Signature & Electronic Records |
| **Chapter III** | 11–14 | Regulation of Certifying Authorities |
| **Chapter IV** | 15–22 | Regulation of Certifying Authorities (Licensing) |
| **Chapter V** | 25–28 | Penalties for non-compliance |
| **Chapter VI** | 29–34 | Adjudicating Officer & Appellate Tribunal |
| **Chapter VII** | 35–45 | Offences & Penalties (Sections 65–75) |
| **Chapter IX** | 69–69B | Interception, Monitoring & Decryption |
| **Chapter X** | 70–71 | Protected Systems & National Security |
| **Chapter XI** | 72–76 | Offences (Privacy, Disclosure) |
| **Chapter XII** | 77–79 | Intermediary Guidelines & Safe Harbour |
| **Chapter XIII** | 80–90 | Miscellaneous (Powers of Police, Jurisdiction) |

## 2.3 Key Offences under IT Act — KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Section | Offence | Punishment | Bailable? |
|---------|---------|------------|-----------|
| **Sec 65** | Tampering with computer source documents | Imprisonment up to **3 years** or fine up to **₹2 lakh** or both | Yes |
| **Sec 66** | Computer-related offences (hacking with intent to cause damage) | Imprisonment up to **3 years** or fine up to **₹5 lakh** or both | Yes |
| **Sec 66A** | Sending offensive messages (struck down in 2013) | N/A — *Declared unconstitutional* | N/A |
| **Sec 66B** | Receiving stolen computer or communication device | Imprisonment up to **3 years** or fine up to **₹1 lakh** or both | Yes |
| **Sec 66C** | Identity theft (using password/electronic signature fraudulently) | Imprisonment up to **3 years** + fine up to **₹1 lakh** | Yes |
| **Sec 66D** | Cheating by personation using communication device | Imprisonment up to **3 years** + fine up to **₹1 lakh** | Yes |
| **Sec 66E** | Violation of privacy (publishing/capturing private images) | Imprisonment up to **3 years** + fine up to **₹2 lakh** | Yes |
| **Sec 66F** | **Cyber terrorism** | Imprisonment — life or up to **14 years** (death risk) | No |
| **Sec 67** | Publishing obscene material in electronic form | First: up to **5 years** + ₹1 lakh; Subsequent: up to **7 years** + ₹2 lakh | No (subsequent) |
| **Sec 67A** | Publishing sexually explicit material | First: up to **5 years** + ₹10 lakh; Subsequent: up to **7 years** + ₹20 lakh | No |
| **Sec 67B** | Publishing child pornography | First: up to **5 years** + ₹10 lakh; Subsequent: up to **7 years** + ₹20 lakh | No |
| **Sec 69** | Interception / Monitoring / Decryption orders | Compliance failure: up to **7 years** + fine | No |
| **Sec 69B** | Collection of traffic data / metadata by CERT-In | Non-compliance: up to **3 years** + fine | Yes |
| **Sec 70** | Accessing protected systems (national security) | Imprisonment up to **10 years** + fine | No |
| **Sec 72** | Breach of confidentiality and privacy | Imprisonment up to **2 years** + fine up to **₹1 lakh** | Yes |
| **Sec 72A** | Disclosure of personal info in breach of contract | Imprisonment up to **3 years** + fine up to **₹5 lakh** | Yes |
| **Sec 73** | Publishing false digital signature certificates | Imprisonment up to **2 years** + fine up to **₹1 lakh** | Yes |

## 2.4 Key Regulatory & Enforcement Bodies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following bodies form the **cybersecurity governance ecosystem** in India:

| Body | Statutory Basis | Function |
|------|----------------|----------|
| **CERT-In** (Indian Computer Emergency Response Team) | Sec 70B, IT Act | National nodal agency for cyber incident response; mandates 6-hour reporting for certain incidents. |
| **Cyber Appellate Tribunal (CyAT)** | Sec 48, IT Act | First appellate authority against Adjudicating Officer orders. *(Abolished and merged under Tribunals Reforms Act, 2021 — appeals now to TDSAT.)* |
| **Adjudicating Officer** | Sec 46, IT Act | Hears claims for damages up to ₹5 crore. |
| **Controller of Certifying Authorities (CCA)** | Sec 17, IT Act | Licenses and supervises Certifying Authorities. |
| **Ministry of Electronics & IT (MeitY)** | Administrative | Policy formulation, DPDPA implementation. |
| **Data Protection Board** | DPDPA, 2023 | Adjudicates data protection violations. |

## 2.5 Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDPA), 2023 — Key Provisions

The DPDPA, 2023 is India's first **horizontal, comprehensive data protection law**. KTU expects a working knowledge of:

- **Section 4**: Grounds for processing personal data — consent + legitimate uses.
- **Section 6**: Notice requirement at the time of consent.
- **Section 8**: General obligations of Data Fiduciaries (security safeguards, breach notification).
- **Section 10**: Additional obligations for Significant Data Fiduciaries (SDFs) — DPO, audit, DPIA.
- **Section 11–14**: Rights of Data Principals (access, correction, erasure, grievance redressal).
- **Section 17**: Grounds for processing children's data (verifiable parental consent + no tracking).
- **Section 33(1)**: Personal data of individuals outside India is **not** protected — extraterritoriality is **limited**.
- **Section 44**: Penalty up to **₹250 crore per instance** for failure to take reasonable security safeguards.
- **Section 33(2)**: Government exemption for "sovereignty, integrity, security" — critiqued as overly broad.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Engineering students building apps, IoT systems, or cloud services must comply with DPDPA. A data breach involving user data can attract fines up to **₹250 crore** — making privacy-by-design a core engineering principle, not just a legal afterthought.

## 2.6 Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS), 2023 — Replacement of IPC

BNS replaces IPC and introduces new sections specifically addressing digital/cyber offences:

| BNS Section | Offence | Parallel IPC Section |
|-------------|---------|----------------------|
| **Sec 292** | Obscene acts and songs | Sec 292 IPC |
| **Sec 318** | Cheating | Sec 415 IPC |
| **Sec 319** | Cheating by personation | Sec 416 IPC |
| **Sec 336(2)(3)(4)** | Forgery related to electronic records | Sec 463 IPC |
| **Sec 351(3)** | Criminal intimidation via electronic communication | Sec 503 IPC |

The BNS is a **standalone criminal code** and works **in parallel** with the IT Act — both can be invoked for cyber offences.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Procedural Analysis & Analytical Matrix

This topic is **legal-procedural** in nature, so the "derivation" maps to **jurisdictional logic, complaint filing procedure, and the legal reasoning chain** that a KTU examiner expects.

## 3.1 Procedural Flow — Filing a Cyber Crime Complaint in India

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step-by-step reasoning chain (memorise for KTU):**

**Step 1 — Identify the Nature of the Offence:**
Determine whether the offence falls under:
- **IT Act, 2000** (e.g., Section 66 — hacking, Section 67 — obscenity, Section 66C — identity theft).
- **IPC / BNS** (e.g., Section 420 IPC — cheating, now Section 318 BNS).
- **Both** (cognizance taken concurrently).

**Step 2 — Identify the Appropriate Forum:**
- For **cognizable offences** (e.g., Sec 66F cyber terrorism, Sec 67B child pornography): File **FIR** at the nearest **Cyber Crime Police Station** or regular police station.
- For **non-cognizable or civil claims**: Approach the **Adjudicating Officer** under Section 46 of IT Act for damages.
- For **data protection violations**: File complaint with the **Data Protection Board of India** under DPDPA, 2023.

**Step 3 — Collect and Preserve Digital Evidence:**
The evidentiary chain must satisfy **Section 65B of the Indian Evidence Act** (now **Section 63 of Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023**) — requiring a **certificate** from a person occupying a responsible official position in relation to the operation of the relevant device.

**Step 4 — Filing the Complaint:**
- Online portal: **cybercrime.gov.in** (Indian Cyber Crime Coordination Centre — I4C).
- Offline: Cyber crime police station in the city of residence.

**Step 5 — Investigation:**
Handled by the **Cyber Crime Cell** of state police or **CBI** for inter-state / national-level cases. CERT-In provides technical assistance.

**Step 6 — Prosecution:**
- Cognizable cases: Prosecuted by public prosecutor in the appropriate Sessions Court or Magistrate Court.
- Adjudicating Officer matters: Summary procedure under IT Act.
- Data Protection Board: Inquires and imposes penalties under DPDPA.

**Step 7 — Appeal:**
- First appeal to **TDSAT (Telecom Disputes Settlement and Appellate Tribunal)** — which now also hears cyber appellate matters after the Tribunals Reforms Act, 2021.
- Second appeal to the **Supreme Court of India** under Article 136.

## 3.2 Jurisdictional Logic — Where to File a Case?

The IT Act uses a **wider jurisdictional principle** than conventional law.

**Rule (Sec 75, IT Act, 2000):**
"Subject to the provisions of sub-section (2), any offence punishable under this Act shall be deemed to have been committed at the place where the computer, computer system, or network is located, **or where the electronic record is stored or transmitted**, irrespective of the location of the accused."

**Logic of the rule:**

$$\text{Jurisdiction} = \max(\text{Location of Computer}, \text{Location of Storage}, \text{Location of Transmission}, \text{Location of Accused})$$

This is a **"long-arm" jurisdictional principle** — a key KTU conceptual question.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world implication:** If a hacker in Country X attacks a server in Mumbai, Indian courts have jurisdiction even though the accused is abroad, **provided** an act of the offence (storage or transmission) took place in India.

## 3.3 Admissibility of Electronic Evidence — The Section 65B / Sec 63 BSA Test

For a KTU exam, the student must recall the **four-condition test** (as laid down in **Anvar P.V. v. P.K. Basheer, 2014** and clarified in **Arjun Khotkar, 2020**):

A Section 65B certificate is **mandatory** for admissibility of electronic evidence unless:

1. The device that produced the output is shown to be in **proper functioning order**.
2. The output is produced **by the regular and ordinary use** of the device.
3. The device was **not altered** in any manner that would affect the accuracy of the output.
4. The **certificate** accompanies the electronic record, signed by an occupant of a responsible official position.

**Failure to produce this certificate → electronic evidence is **inadmissible** (hearsay).**

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Old IPC vs. IT Act vs. BNS

| Aspect | IPC (1860) | IT Act (2000/2008) | BNS (2023) |
|--------|------------|---------------------|-------------|
| **Origin** | Colonial era | Post-2000 digital era | Modern reform |
| **Scope for digital crimes** | Limited (forgery, obscenity) | Comprehensive | Moderate (parallel with IT Act) |
| **Recognises digital signature** | No | Yes | Implicit (refers to electronic records) |
| **Punishment scale** | 2–10 years | 2 years to life | 3–14 years for cyber terrorism equivalents |
| **Adjudicating body** | Magistrate / Sessions | Adjudicating Officer + Tribunal | Magistrate / Sessions |
| **Best fit for** | Conventional crimes | Pure cyber crimes | Mixed/parallel application |
| **In force status** | **Repealed 1 July 2024** | **Active** | **Active since 1 July 2024** |

## 3.5 Penalty Computation Reference (DPDPA, 2023)

For a KTU numerical-style question (if asked):

$$\text{Max Penalty (Subsequent Breach)} = \text{₹}250\,\text{Crore} \times n \quad \text{where } n = \text{number of distinct data subjects affected (capped by Section 33)}$$

$$\text{Penalty for Failure to Notify Breach} = \text{Up to ₹}200\,\text{Crore} \text{ per instance (Section 26 read with Section 33)}$$

$$\text{Penalty for Children's Data Violation} = \text{Up to ₹}200\,\text{Crore} \text{ (Section 33(7))}$$

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Paper 2]**
*Define "Cyber Law" and list any four key statutes that form the Indian cyber law framework.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Cyber Law refers to the body of legal regulations, statutes, judicial precedents, and procedural rules that govern the use of computers, networks, digital communications, and electronic data, and prescribe penalties for cyber offences within a given jurisdiction.

**Four Key Statutes (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
1. **Information Technology Act, 2000** (amended in 2008) — primary cyber statute.
2. **Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023** — data protection.
3. **Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023** (replaces IPC) — covers cyber-enabled crimes.
4. **Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023** (replaces Indian Evidence Act) — admissibility of electronic evidence.

*(Acceptable alternative: Copyright Act 1957, Indian Telegraph Act 1885.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Supplementary]**
*What is the role of CERT-In under the IT Act, 2000? State any two of its powers.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Role (1.5 marks):**
**CERT-In (Indian Computer Emergency Response Team)** is the **national nodal agency** for cyber security incident response, established under **Section 70B** of the IT (Amendment) Act, 2008. It coordinates incident response, issues advisories, and collaborates with international CERTs.

**Two Powers (1.5 marks — 0.75 each):**
1. **Power to collect traffic data and information** under Section 69B from any agency for cyber security purposes.
2. **Power to call for information and issue directions** to service providers, intermediaries, and data centres regarding cyber incidents (e.g., the **6-hour incident reporting mandate** of 2022).
3. *(Optional)* Power to designate protected systems under Section 70.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Set A]**

**(a)** Discuss the salient features of the **Information Technology Act, 2000** and explain the major changes introduced by the **IT (Amendment) Act, 2008**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain in detail the **offences and penalties** prescribed under **Sections 65, 66, 66C, 66D, 66E, 66F, and 67** of the IT Act, 2000. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (a):**

**Salient Features of IT Act, 2000 (3.5 marks):**
1. **Legal recognition to electronic records and digital signatures** (Sec 3, 4, 5) — equivalent to paper records.
2. **Regulation of Certifying Authorities** (Sec 17–22) — licensing of CAs by the Controller.
3. **Regulation of Electronic Governance** (Chapter III) — use of electronic records in government offices.
4. **Penalties and Adjudication** (Chapter VI–VII) — civil penalties for non-compliance; criminal offences for hacking.
5. **Establishment of Cyber Appellate Tribunal** (Sec 48) — appellate mechanism.
6. **Offences by companies** (Sec 85) — liability extends to directors and officers.

**Major Changes in IT (Amendment) Act, 2008 (3.5 marks):**
1. **Redefinition of "Computer" and "Computer Network"** to include modern devices (smartphones, IoT).
2. **Introduction of Sections 66A–66F** — new offences (66A struck down in 2013, 66F cyber terrorism added).
3. **Data privacy provisions** — Sections 43A, 72, 72A introduced to protect sensitive personal data.
4. **Section 69 and 69B** — interception, monitoring, and decryption powers for government; **CERT-In formalized under Sec 70B**.
5. **Section 79** — safe harbour for intermediaries (subject to conditions).
6. **Compensation by adjudicating officer** up to **₹5 crore** (Sec 47).
7. **Digital signature as "electronic signature"** — broader recognition.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (b):**

| Section | Offence & Penalty (1 mark each) |
|---------|----------------------------------|
| **Sec 65** | **Tampering with computer source documents** — knowingly concealing, destroying, or altering source code. Punishment: imprisonment up to **3 years** OR fine up to **₹2 lakh** OR both. |
| **Sec 66** | **Hacking with intent** (Computer-related offence) — dishonestly/fraudulently damaging a computer system. Punishment: imprisonment up to **3 years** OR fine up to **₹5 lakh** OR both. |
| **Sec 66C** | **Identity theft** — fraudulently using another person's electronic signature, password, or unique identification. Punishment: imprisonment up to **3 years** + fine up to **₹1 lakh**. |
| **Sec 66D** | **Cheating by personation** using a communication device/computer. Punishment: imprisonment up to **3 years** + fine up to **₹1 lakh**. |
| **Sec 66E** | **Violation of privacy** — intentionally capturing, publishing, or transmitting private images of a person without consent. Punishment: imprisonment up to **3 years** + fine up to **₹2 lakh**. |
| **Sec 66F** | **Cyber terrorism** — acts intended to threaten unity, integrity, security, sovereignty, or strike terror in the people. Punishment: **imprisonment up to life** (or death if death results). *Non-bailable, cognizable.* |
| **Sec 67** | **Publishing obscene material in electronic form**. First offence: up to **5 years** + ₹1 lakh fine. Subsequent: up to **7 years** + ₹2 lakh fine. |

---

### **QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Set A]**

**(a)** Explain the **Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023** in detail — its scope, key definitions, rights of Data Principals, obligations of Data Fiduciaries, and penalties. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **jurisdictional provisions** under the IT Act, 2000. How does Section 75 differ from traditional criminal jurisdiction? Illustrate with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (a):**

**Introduction & Scope (1 mark):**
The DPDPA, 2023 is India's first comprehensive data protection statute. It applies to:
- Processing of **digital personal data** within India.
- Processing of digital personal data **outside India** if it is offered to Data Principals in India (limited extraterritoriality under Section 3(b)).

**Key Definitions (1.5 marks):**
- **Personal Data**: Any data about an individual who is identifiable.
- **Data Principal**: The individual to whom the personal data relates.
- **Data Fiduciary**: The entity that determines the purpose and means of processing.
- **Data Processor**: Entity processing on behalf of the Data Fiduciary.
- **Significant Data Fiduciary (SDF)**: Designated by the government based on volume, sensitivity.

**Rights of Data Principals (1.5 marks):**
1. Right to **access** a summary of personal data and processing activities (Sec 11).
2. Right to **correction and erasure** (Sec 12).
3. Right to **grievance redressal** (Sec 13).
4. Right to **nominate** another individual to exercise rights in case of death/incapacity (Sec 14).

**Obligations of Data Fiduciaries (1.5 marks):**
1. **Lawful processing** on the basis of consent or legitimate uses (Sec 4–6).
2. **Reasonable security safeguards** to prevent breach (Sec 8(5)).
3. **Breach notification** to the Data Protection Board and affected Data Principals (Sec 8(7)).
4. **Erasure upon withdrawal of consent or upon purpose being fulfilled** (Sec 8(7)).
5. **Additional obligations for SDFs**: Appoint DPO, conduct DPIA, periodic audit (Sec 10).

**Penalties (1.5 marks):**
- Failure to take reasonable security safeguards: up to **₹250 crore** (Sec 33(1)).
- Failure to notify breach: up to **₹200 crore** (Sec 33(5)).
- Violation of children's data provisions: up to **₹200 crore** (Sec 33(7)).
- Other penalties: ₹50 crore per instance.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (b):**

**Traditional Criminal Jurisdiction (1.5 marks):**
- **Territorial Principle**: The offence must be committed within the geographical territory of the State.
- **Nationality Principle**: Offences by citizens abroad can be tried.
- **Passive Personality Principle**: Protects nationals against offences committed abroad.
- Generally, the **location of the accused** or the **location of the act** is the key determinant.

**Section 75 of the IT Act, 2000 (3 marks):**
- Section 75 provides that any offence punishable under the IT Act shall be **deemed to have been committed** at the place where the:
  1. Computer, computer system, or network is located, **OR**
  2. The electronic record is **stored or transmitted**, **OR**
  3. The accused is located.
- **Whichever** of these applies, the courts at that place have jurisdiction — this is a **"long-arm"** or **"virtual" jurisdiction**.

**Differences (1.5 marks):**
| Aspect | Traditional Jurisdiction | Section 75, IT Act |
|--------|--------------------------|----------------------|
| Anchor | Physical location of act/accused | Virtual location of data |
| Cyber crimes | Hard to prosecute across borders | Prosecutable at any storage/transmission point |
| Multiple jurisdictions | Usually one | Several (data may traverse multiple countries) |
| Speed of justice | Slower | Faster — local courts empowered |

**Illustrative Example (1 mark):**
A hacker in **Russia** plants malware in a server hosted in **Mumbai** that transmits stolen data to **Singapore**. Under Section 75, the offence is deemed committed in:
- **Mumbai** (server location),
- **Singapore** (transmission point),
- and arguably **Russia** (accused's location).
**Indian courts in Mumbai** can try the case even though the accused is a foreign national, because the **storage point** is in India. This is the **"long-arm" jurisdiction** unique to cyber law.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Writing "Section 66A is valid" or quoting its punishment** — This is the most common mistake. **Section 66A was struck down as unconstitutional** in *Shreya Singhal v. Union of India (2015)*. Listing it as a current offence = **−2 to −3 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing IPC with BNS** — IPC is **repealed since 1 July 2024**. Use **BNS, 2023** for questions on the new criminal code.
> 3. **Mishandling Section 65B/63 BSA** — Students often say "electronic evidence is admissible" without the **mandatory certificate**. Always mention the **Section 65B (Sec 63 BSA) certificate** requirement.
> 4. **Forgetting CERT-In's statutory basis** — CERT-In is not a "department of MeitY" alone; it derives its power from **Section 70B** of the IT Act.
> 5. **Mixing DPDPA with the older 2018 Bill / SPDI Rules, 2011** — The KTU 2024 syllabus uses **DPDPA, 2023** as the current law. Citing "Right to be Forgotten" as a current right is incorrect — it was removed in the final Act. Use only the **enacted provisions**.
> 6. **Not stating amounts in ₹** — Always quote the **fine amounts** (₹1 lakh, ₹5 lakh, ₹25,000) explicitly; partial answers lose marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Cyber Security Exam:**

- **IT Act 2000** = India's primary cyber statute; **amended in 2008**; based on **UNCITRAL Model Law, 1996**.
- **Section 65B / Sec 63 BSA** = Mandatory certificate for electronic evidence admissibility (*Anvar P.V. v. P.K. Basheer, 2014*; *Arjun Khotkar, 2020*).
- **Section 66** = Hacking (3 yrs / ₹5 lakh); **Section 66C** = Identity Theft; **Section 66D** = Cheating by Personation; **Section 66E** = Privacy Violation; **Section 66F** = **Cyber Terrorism (life imprisonment)**.
- **Section 67** = Obscene material (5 yrs first / 7 yrs subsequent).
- **Section 69** = Interception; **Section 69B** = Traffic data collection; **Section 70** = Protected systems (10 yrs).
- **Section 72 / 72A** = Breach of confidentiality / disclosure of personal info.
- **Section 75** = **Long-arm jurisdiction** — deemed location = computer / storage / transmission / accused.
- **Section 79** = Safe harbour for intermediaries (with conditions).
- **Section 85** = Offences by companies — liability of directors/officers.
- **CERT-In** = Statutory body under **Section 70B**; nodal incident response; **6-hour reporting** mandate.
- **Cyber Appellate Tribunal (Sec 48)** was abolished by Tribunals Reforms Act, 2021; appeals now lie to **TDSAT**.
- **Shreya Singhal v. UoI, 2015** struck down **Section 66A** as violative of **Article 19(1)(a)** (Free Speech).
- **Puttaswamy v. UoI, 2017** declared **Right to Privacy** a **Fundamental Right** under Article 21.
- **DPDPA, 2023** = Data protection law; **Penalty up to ₹250 crore**; **Significant Data Fiduciary (SDF)** obligations; **Data Protection Board** for adjudication.
- **BNS, 2023** = Replaces IPC; **Section 318** = cheating, **Section 319** = cheating by personation, **Sec 336(2/3/4)** = forgery related to electronic records.
- **Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023** = Replaces Indian Evidence Act; **Section 63** = analogue of Section 65B.
- **Key foreign laws to know for context** (1-liners): **GDPR (EU, 2018)** — 4% global turnover penalty; **CCPA (California, 2018)** — first US state privacy law; **China's PIPL (2021)**.
- **Bookmark judgments**: Shreya Singhal (2015), Puttaswamy (2017), Anvar P.V. (2014), Arjun Khotkar (2020), *Justice K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India* (Right to Privacy).
- **Mnemonic for Sections 66A–66F**: **"ABCDEF-Gone-Away"** — *A was struck down, B is stolen device, C is identity, D is personation, E is privacy, F is terrorism*.
- **Mnemonic for DPDPA Penalties**: **"50-100-150-200-250"** (in ₹ crore) for graduated severity of breaches.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Global Perspective
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST721 - CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Global Perspective

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Global Perspective in Cyber Security** refers to the comprehensive study of international cyber security frameworks, cross-border threat landscapes, multinational regulatory regimes, diplomatic treaties, comparative national strategies, and collaborative governance mechanisms that govern the digital ecosystem across sovereign boundaries. It encompasses the technical, legal, organizational, and geopolitical dimensions of securing cyberspace at a worldwide scale.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context, this topic is positioned under the broader umbrella of *Cyber Security Governance and Policy*, where students are expected to understand that cyber security is not merely a technical discipline but a **multi-stakeholder, transnational issue** requiring harmonized standards, treaties, and coordinated incident response.

**Key Constituents of the Global Perspective:**

- **Standards Bodies**: ISO, IEC, NIST, ETSI, ITU
- **Legal Frameworks**: GDPR, Budapest Convention, CCPA, India IT Act 2000/2008
- **Geopolitical Actors**: Nation-states, APT groups, international law enforcement
- **Treaties & Conventions**: UN OEWG resolutions, Wassenaar Arrangement, Tallinn Manual
- **Cooperative Bodies**: INTERPOL, Europol, FIRST, CERTs, GFCE

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Global Cyber Security like International Air Traffic Control (ATC).**
> Just as aircraft from different countries fly through shared airspace governed by ICAO rules, radio frequencies, and standardized protocols, data packets traverse the global internet through undersea cables, satellite links, and terrestrial networks owned and operated by many sovereign nations. Without shared protocols, treaties (like the Chicago Convention for aviation), and cooperative bodies (like ICAO), the system would collapse. Similarly, **Global Cyber Security** is the set of shared rules, standards, response teams, and legal agreements that keep the digital "airspace" safe and functional for all nations.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

The following metrics are standard benchmarks in global cyber security assessments:

- **Average cost of a data breach (2023)**: **USD 4.45 million**
- **Mean time to identify a breach (MTTI)**: **204 days**
- **Mean time to contain a breach (MTTC)**: **73 days**
- **Global cybercrime cost projection (2025)**: **USD 10.5 trillion annually**
- **Number of connected IoT devices (2025 est.)**: **75.44 billion**
- **ISO/IEC 27001** is the international standard for Information Security Management Systems (ISMS).
- **GDPR penalty ceiling**: **EUR 20 million or 4% of global annual turnover**, whichever is higher.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Breakdown of the Global Cyber Security Landscape

The global perspective can be deconstructed into **five interlocking layers**:

1. **Technical Layer** — Internet standards (TCP/IP, DNS, BGP), cryptographic protocols (TLS 1.3, IPsec), and routing security (RPKI, MANRS).
2. **Standards Layer** — Voluntary frameworks (ISO/IEC 27001, NIST CSF, COBIT) that organizations adopt.
3. **Legal Layer** — Binding national laws (GDPR, IT Act) and international conventions (Budapest Convention).
4. **Diplomatic Layer** — Bilateral/multilateral agreements, UN GGE/OEWG resolutions, cyber norms.
5. **Operational Layer** — CERTs, CSIRTs, threat intel sharing platforms (MISP, ThreatConnect), joint operations.

### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Global Cyber Cooperation

- **Why?** Cyber threats are borderless. The 2017 **WannaCry** ransomware attack hit 150 countries in 24 hours. The 2020 **SolarWinds** supply-chain compromise affected U.S. government agencies and Fortune 500 firms globally. No single nation can defend alone.
- **How?** Through **Information Sharing and Analysis Centers (ISACs)**, **Computer Emergency Response Teams (CERTs)**, **joint attribution statements**, **mutual legal assistance treaties (MLATs)**, and **harmonized standards** that allow interoperability.

### 2.3 KTU Reference Sheet — Key Frameworks, Treaties & Bodies

| Framework / Body | Type | Year | Scope / Coverage | Key Provision |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **ISO/IEC 27001** | Standard | 2013 (rev. 2022) | Global | ISMS requirements, 93 controls in Annex A |
| **NIST CSF 2.0** | Framework | 2014 (rev. 2024) | U.S. (adopted globally) | Govern, Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover |
| **GDPR** | Regulation | 2018 | EU + extraterritorial | Data subject rights, DPIA, breach notification 72h |
| **Budapest Convention** | Treaty | 2001 | 66+ states | First international cybercrime treaty |
| **Tallinn Manual 2.0** | Non-binding | 2017 | Global (academic) | Rules of cyber warfare, IHL application |
| **UN OEWG** | UN Process | 2019–2025 | 193 UN states | Norms of responsible state behavior in cyberspace |
| **ITU Global Cybersecurity Index (GCI)** | Index | 2015–present | 194 countries | Ranks national cyber maturity on 5 pillars |
| **Wassenaar Arrangement** | Export Control | 1996 | 42 states | Controls dual-use cyber tools and intrusion software |
| **CCPA** | Law | 2020 | California, USA | Consumer privacy rights, opt-out of data sale |
| **IT Act 2000/2008** | Law | 2000/2008 | India | Cybercrime offenses, digital signatures, CERT-In |
| **FIRST.org** | Forum | 1990 | Global | Forum of Incident Response and Security Teams |
| **GFCE** | Initiative | 2014 | Global | Global Forum on Cyber Expertise, capacity building |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Students often confuse the **Budapest Convention** (legally binding for signatories) with the **Tallinn Manual** (non-binding academic guidance). Remember: *Budapest = binding law; Tallinn = academic guidance on warfare*.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

For a B.Tech engineer, the global perspective is not abstract — it directly impacts:

- **Software product design** → Must comply with GDPR if serving EU users (Privacy by Design).
- **Cloud deployments** → Subject to data residency laws (e.g., Russia's Federal Law No. 242-FZ requires citizen data on Russian servers).
- **Cryptography exports** → Subject to Wassenaar Arrangement controls; AES-256 exportable, but certain quantum cryptographic primitives are restricted.
- **Penetration testing tools** → "Dual-use" tools like Metasploit may require export licenses.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical & Conceptual Derivations

### 3.1 Derivation of the Risk Score under Global Threat Index (Conceptual Model)

The **Global Cybersecurity Index (GCI)** by the ITU uses a weighted scoring model. Let us derive the conceptual formula used in maturity assessment.

Let there be **5 pillars** (Legal, Technical, Organizational, Capacity Building, Cooperation). Each pillar $P_i$ has a weight $w_i$ such that:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i = 1
$$

Each pillar contains sub-indicators. For pillar $P_i$, let there be $n_i$ sub-indicators, each scored on a scale $[0, 1]$.

The pillar score $S_i$ is computed as the **arithmetic mean** of its sub-indicators:

$$
S_i = \frac{1}{n_i} \sum_{j=1}^{n_i} s_{ij}
$$

The **overall GCI score** is then the weighted sum:

$$
\text{GCI} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i \cdot S_i
$$

> **Step-by-step explanation:**
>
> **Step 1:** Identify the 5 pillars. In the ITU GCI v5, these are: Legal Measures, Technical Measures, Organizational Measures, Capacity Building, and Cooperation.
>
> **Step 2:** Assign weights. In GCI v4 (2020+), each pillar carries an **equal weight of 0.20** (i.e., $w_i = 0.2$ for all $i$).
>
> **Step 3:** For each pillar, compute the average of binary/normalized sub-indicator scores.
>
> **Step 4:** Multiply each pillar score by its weight and sum.

**Numerical Illustration:**

Suppose a country scores as follows:

| Pillar | Sub-indicator scores | $S_i$ (mean) | $w_i$ | Contribution |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Legal | 0.9, 0.8, 0.7, 0.85 | 0.8125 | 0.20 | 0.1625 |
| Technical | 0.7, 0.6, 0.8, 0.5 | 0.6500 | 0.20 | 0.1300 |
| Organizational | 0.9, 0.9, 0.8 | 0.8667 | 0.20 | 0.1733 |
| Capacity Building | 0.6, 0.7, 0.5, 0.4 | 0.5500 | 0.20 | 0.1100 |
| Cooperation | 0.8, 0.8, 0.9, 0.7 | 0.8000 | 0.20 | 0.1600 |

$$
\text{GCI} = 0.1625 + 0.1300 + 0.1733 + 0.1100 + 0.1600 = 73.58
$$

Out of 100, this country ranks in the **"Evolving"** tier (60–79 band).

### 3.2 GDPR Penalty Calculation (Forensic Derivation)

Under **Article 83(5) GDPR**, the maximum fine for the most serious infringements is the higher of:

$$
F_{\max} = \max\left(20{,}000{,}000 \text{ EUR},\ 0.04 \cdot T \right)
$$

where $T$ is the total worldwide annual turnover of the preceding financial year.

**Step-by-step:**
> **Step 1:** Identify the infringement category (Art. 83(4), 83(5), or 83(6)).
> **Step 2:** Calculate 4% of total worldwide annual turnover.
> **Step 3:** Compare with EUR 20 million ceiling.
> **Step 4:** The fine is the **greater** of the two.

**Example:** A firm has annual turnover $T$ = USD 500 million. Assuming 1 EUR = 1.10 USD, then $T$ in EUR = EUR 454.54 million.

$$
0.04 \cdot 454.54 = 18.18 \text{ million EUR}
$$

$$
F_{\max} = \max(20, 18.18) = 20 \text{ million EUR}
$$

Here, the **flat EUR 20M cap dominates** because the turnover is not large enough for 4% to exceed it.

**Counter-example:** A multinational with $T$ = EUR 1 billion:

$$
0.04 \cdot 1000 = 40 \text{ million EUR}
$$

$$
F_{\max} = \max(20, 40) = 40 \text{ million EUR}
$$

> **Engineering takeaway:** The penalty function is **piecewise** — for smaller firms the cap is binding, for very large firms the percentage is binding. This creates a *progressive penalty curve* as firms grow.

### 3.3 Derived Definition: The "Attack Surface Multiplier"

For multinational engineering teams, a useful derived metric is the **Cross-Border Attack Surface Multiplier (CASM)**:

$$
\text{CASM} = \prod_{k=1}^{N} (1 + \alpha_k \cdot \beta_k)
$$

where $N$ is the number of jurisdictions the data traverses, $\alpha_k \in [0,1]$ is the regulatory complexity index of jurisdiction $k$, and $\beta_k \in [0,1]$ is the threat density of jurisdiction $k$.

**Interpretation:** Each additional jurisdiction **multiplicatively** increases attack surface — a key reason why global firms face exponentially higher cyber risk.

> This formula is conceptual; for KTU exams, students should focus on the **GCI derivation** and **GDPR penalty** as primary quantitative elements.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Global Cyber Security Governance Stack (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DIPLOMATIC["DIPLOMATIC LAYER"]
        UN["UN OEWG / GGE Resolutions"]
        BILAT["Bilateral Cyber Dialogs US-Russia, US-China"]
    end

    subgraph LEGAL["LEGAL LAYER"]
        GDPR["GDPR EU 2018"]
        ITACT["IT Act 2000/2008 India"]
        BUDAP["Budapest Convention 2001"]
        CCPA["CCPA California 2020"]
    end

    subgraph STANDARDS["STANDARDS LAYER"]
        ISO27["ISO/IEC 27001 ISMS"]
        NIST["NIST CSF 2.0"]
        COBIT["COBIT 2019"]
    end

    subgraph OPERATIONAL["OPERATIONAL LAYER"]
        CERT["National CERTs CERT-In, US-CERT, ENISA"]
        FIRST["FIRST.org"]
        ISAC["ISACs Information Sharing"]
    end

    subgraph TECHNICAL["TECHNICAL LAYER"]
        TLS["TLS 1.3"]
        RPKI["RPKI BGP Security"]
        ZERO["Zero Trust Architecture"]
    end

    DIPLOMATIC --> LEGAL
    LEGAL --> STANDARDS
    STANDARDS --> OPERATIONAL
    OPERATIONAL --> TECHNICAL
    TECHNICAL -.feeds back.-> DIPLOMATIC
```

### 4.2 Cross-Border Data Flow & Compliance Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["User Data Generated in Country X"] --> B{"Does data leave X?"}
    B -- No --> C["Apply X Domestic Law Only"]
    B -- Yes --> D["Identify Destination Countries"]
    D --> E{"Is destination in EU/EEA?"}
    E -- Yes --> F["Apply GDPR Full Regime"]
    E -- No --> G{"Adequacy Decision Exists?"}
    G -- Yes --> F
    G -- No --> H["Use SCCs / BCRs / Explicit Consent"]
    H --> I{"Special Category Data?"}
    I -- Yes --> J["Apply Art 9 Enhanced Safeguards"]
    I -- No --> K["Standard Cross-Border Transfer"]
    C --> L["Log & Audit per Local Law"]
    F --> L
    K --> L
    J --> L
```

### 4.3 Global Cyber Threat Actors Taxonomy (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Threat Actors"] --> B["Nation-State APTs"]
    A --> C["Organized Cybercrime"]
    A --> D["Hacktivists"]
    A --> E["Insider Threats"]
    A --> F["Script Kiddies"]
    A --> G["Terrorist Groups"]
    B --> B1["Examples: Lazarus, APT29, APT41"]
    C --> C1["Examples: Conti, REvil, LockBit"]
    D --> D1["Examples: Anonymous, LulzSec"]
    E --> E1["Examples: Snowden-type, Edward Snowden"]
    G --> G1["Examples: ISIS Online Recruiters"]
```

### 4.4 Comparative National Cyber Strategy — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph US["USA Strategy"]
        US1["NIST Framework"]
        US2["CISA as Lead Agency"]
        US3["Executive Orders on Cyber"]
    end
    subgraph EU["European Union"]
        EU1["NIS2 Directive"]
        EU2["EU Cyber Solidarity Act"]
        EU3["ENISA as Agency"]
    end
    subgraph IN["India"]
        IN1["NCSP 2020 National Cyber Security Policy"]
        IN2["CERT-In as Nodal Agency"]
        IN3["Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023"]
    end
    subgraph CN["China"]
        CN1["Cybersecurity Law 2017"]
        CN2["Data Security Law 2021"]
        CN3["Personal Information Protection Law 2021"]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Note for KTU Exams:** Use clean block diagrams only. Avoid coloring text inside mermaid nodes. Use uppercase labels for clarity.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the Budapest Convention. List any four of its key provisions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) The Budapest Convention, formally the *Convention on Cybercrime* (2001), is the **first international treaty** aimed at harmonizing national laws on cybercrime and establishing procedures for cross-border investigation.
- (2 marks) Four key provisions:
  1. **Substantive criminal law**: Defines offenses like illegal access, data interference, computer-related fraud, and child pornography.
  2. **Procedural law**: Provides powers for search, seizure, and interception of computer data.
  3. **International cooperation**: Enables mutual legal assistance (MLA) and 24/7 network for urgent cases.
  4. **Extradition**: Cyber offenses are made extraditable between signatories.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly call the Budapest Convention a *law*. It is a **treaty** — it becomes binding only after a state ratifies it via national legislation.

---

#### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is GDPR? Explain the "Right to be Forgotten" provision.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) **GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation)** is a comprehensive European Union regulation (2016/679) that came into force on **25 May 2018**, governing the processing of personal data of EU residents with **extraterritorial applicability**.
- (2 marks) **Right to be Forgotten** (Article 17) entitles a data subject to request the **erasure of their personal data** without undue delay when:
  - Data is no longer necessary for the purpose collected.
  - Consent is withdrawn and no other legal basis exists.
  - Data has been unlawfully processed.
  - Erasure is required for legal compliance.
  - Example: Google Spain SL v AEPD and Mario Costeja González (2014) was the foundational case.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A: [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the **Global Cybersecurity Index (GCI)** developed by the ITU. List its **five pillars** and explain **any three** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):** The Global Cybersecurity Index (GCI) is a composite index published by the **International Telecommunication Union (ITU)** that measures the cybersecurity commitment of **194 member states** across five pillars. It is a trusted reference for national cyber maturity and helps identify gaps.

**Five Pillars (1 mark for listing):**
1. Legal Measures
2. Technical Measures
3. Organizational Measures
4. Capacity Building
5. Cooperation

**Detailed explanation of any three (4 marks — 1.5 each approx.):**

- **Legal Measures:** Assesses whether a country has cybercrime legislation, data protection/privacy laws, and regulations on content, e.g., India's IT Act 2000/2008, Germany's BDSG.
- **Technical Measures:** Evaluates the presence of **CERTs**, **CSIRTs**, adoption of standards (ISO 27001), and Child Online Protection mechanisms.
- **Capacity Building:** Assesses R&D programs, public-private partnerships, cybersecurity training, and academic curricula. India's ISEA (Information Security Education and Awareness) project is a key initiative.
- **Organizational Measures:** Looks at national cyber strategies, agencies, and cybersecurity metrics.
- **Cooperation:** Evaluates participation in international forums (FIRST, GFCE, UN OEWG), bilateral agreements, and inter-agency collaboration.

**Scoring bands (for completeness, 0.5 mark bonus point):** Initiating (0–19), Evolving (20–59), Established (60–79), Leading (80–100).

---

**Q. (b)** Discuss the **Tallinn Manual 2.0** and its relevance to **cyber warfare**. How does it differ from the Budapest Convention? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Tallinn Manual 2.0 (3 marks):**
- Published in **2017** by the NATO Cooperative Cyber Defence Centre of Excellence (CCDCOE), Tallinn, Estonia.
- A **non-binding academic study** on how existing international law (especially **International Humanitarian Law (IHL)** and the **law of state responsibility**) applies to cyber operations and cyber warfare.
- **Key principles covered:**
  - **Jus ad bellum** (when states may resort to force) — a cyber operation causing physical damage/death may constitute an "armed attack" triggering Article 51 UN Charter self-defense.
  - **Jus in bello** (how force may be used) — distinction, proportionality, military necessity, prohibition on perfidious attacks.
  - Sovereignty, due diligence, and **attribution rules** in cyberspace.
  - Special rules on **neutrality** and **occupation** in cyber conflicts.

**Difference from Budapest Convention (4 marks):**

| Aspect | Tallinn Manual 2.0 | Budapest Convention |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Academic, non-binding | Legally binding treaty |
| Year | 2017 (Man. 1.0 in 2013) | 2001 |
| Scope | Cyber warfare, military ops | Cybercrime, criminal law |
| Authoring body | NATO CCDCOE (experts) | Council of Europe |
| State consent | Not required | Ratification needed |
| Subject matter | IHL application | Substantive + procedural criminal law |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often mix up *jus ad bellum* and *jus in bello*. Remember: *ad bellum = going to war; in bello = conduct during war*. Tallinn Manual covers both, but **knew** the difference!

---

#### **Question B: [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme — Alternative Choice]**

**Q. (a)** Compare the **cyber security strategies** of **USA, EU, and India**. Highlight the lead agency, framework, and any unique feature of each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**United States of America (2.5 marks):**
- **Lead Agency:** Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (**CISA**), under DHS.
- **Framework:** **NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF 2.0)** — voluntary, sector-agnostic, five functions (Govern added in 2.0).
- **Unique Feature:**
  - Presidential Executive Orders (e.g., EO 14028 on *Improving the Nation's Cybersecurity*, 2021) drive federal procurement requirements.
  - Strong public-private ISAC model (FS-ISAC, H-ISAC).
  - **Zero Trust** mandate for federal agencies via OMB Memo M-22-09.

**European Union (2.5 marks):**
- **Lead Agency:** **European Union Agency for Cybersecurity (ENISA)**.
- **Framework:** **NIS2 Directive (2022)** — broad sector coverage (energy, transport, health, digital services).
- **Unique Feature:**
  - **EU Cyber Solidarity Act (2023)** creates a *Cybersecurity Reserve* and *European Cyber Shield* (SOC network).
  - Strong extraterritorial data protection via **GDPR**.
  - Cyber Resilience Act (CRA) for product security.

**India (2 marks):**
- **Lead Agency:** **CERT-In (Indian Computer Emergency Response Team)** under MeitY; **NCSC** (National Cyber Security Coordinator) at PMO level.
- **Framework:** **National Cyber Security Policy 2020** (NCSP 2020).
- **Unique Feature:**
  - **Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023** — first comprehensive data protection law.
  - CERT-In Directions (2022): mandatory 6-hour breach reporting, log retention 180 days.
  - Sectoral CERTs: Banking (RBI), Defense (NCIIPC for critical infra), Power (NCSC-PC).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly say *India's framework is NIST CSF*. India does **not** mandate NIST; it has its own **NCSP 2020** and **CERT-In directions**. Don't confuse national strategy with international standards.

---

**Q. (b)** Explain the role of **INTERPOL, FIRST, and GFCE** in fostering global cyber security cooperation. Give one example activity of each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**INTERPOL (2.5 marks):**
- The **International Criminal Police Organization** (headquarters Lyon, France) enables **190+ member countries** to cooperate on transnational crime.
- **Cyber Division** at the INTERPOL Global Complex for Innovation (IGCI), Singapore.
- **Example Activity:** Operation **HAECHI-I (2020)** — dismantled a global voice-phishing (vishing) ring, arrested 1,500+ suspects across 20 countries.

**FIRST (2.5 marks):**
- **Forum of Incident Response and Security Teams** — a global confederation of **600+ CSIRTs** from 100+ countries.
- Provides best practices, technical colloquia, and a trusted community for incident response coordination.
- **Example Activity:** Annual **FIRST Conference** + **CVSS** (Common Vulnerability Scoring System) co-maintenance + **PSIRT** services framework.

**GFCE (2 marks):**
- **Global Forum on Cyber Expertise** — founded 2014, multi-stakeholder platform for **cyber capacity building**.
- Focuses on **developing nations** — bridges the cyber capacity gap.
- **Example Activity:** **Cybercrime@EaP** project — funded by EU, GFCE coordinated cyber capacity building in Eastern Partnership countries (Armenia, Azerbaijan, Georgia, Moldova, Ukraine).

**Why these three matter (0.5 mark for synthesis):** INTERPOL is **operational-law enforcement**; FIRST is **technical-CSIRT coordination**; GFCE is **capacity-policy building**. Together they cover the three pillars of global cyber cooperation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Global Perspective (Module 2)**
>
> ✅ **Budapest Convention (2001)** — First binding international cybercrime treaty; drafted by Council of Europe; 66+ signatories; covers substantive + procedural criminal law + MLA.
>
> ✅ **Tallinn Manual 2.0 (2017)** — Non-binding academic study on cyber warfare; applies IHL; written by NATO CCDCOE experts.
>
> ✅ **GDPR (2018)** — EU regulation, extraterritorial; key rights: access, rectification, erasure (Art. 17 — Right to be Forgotten), portability, restrict processing.
>
> ✅ **NIST CSF 2.0 (2024)** — Five functions: **Govern, Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover** (Govern added in 2.0).
>
> ✅ **ISO/IEC 27001 (2022 rev.)** — ISMS standard, 93 controls across 4 themes (Organizational, People, Physical, Technological).
>
> ✅ **GCI Pillars (ITU)** — Legal, Technical, Organizational, Capacity Building, Cooperation.
>
> ✅ **Key Global Bodies** — INTERPOL, FIRST, GFCE, UN OEWG, ITU, ENISA, CISA, CERT-In.
>
> ✅ **India-specific** — IT Act 2000/2008, NCSP 2020, CERT-In Directions 2022 (6-hour breach reporting), DPDP Act 2023.
>
> ✅ **US-specific** — NIST CSF, CISA, EO 14028, Zero Trust mandate.
>
> ✅ **EU-specific** — NIS2, Cyber Resilience Act, GDPR, EU Cyber Solidarity Act.
>
> ✅ **Wassenaar Arrangement** — Export control of dual-use cyber tools (intrusion software, IP network communications surveillance).
>
> ✅ **Attribution problem** — Cyber attacks are hard to attribute due to technical obfuscation; state-sponsored APTs are often named by CrowdStrike/FireEye/Mandiant (e.g., APT29 = Cozy Bear = Russia).
>
> ✅ **Critical Information Infrastructure (CII)** — Protected under Section 70 of India's IT Act; NCIIPC is the nodal agency.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Categories of Cybercrime
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST721 - CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Categories of Cybercrime

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OECST721 – Cyber Security)** syllabus, **Cybercrime** is formally defined as any unlawful act, omission, or commission carried out through the medium of computers, computer networks, the Internet, or any digital/electronic communication device, where the computer itself may function as a *tool*, a *target*, or an *incidental component* of the criminal act.

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **Categories of Cybercrime** refer to the systematic classification of cyber-offences based on the **nature of the target** (individual, property, organization, or society) and the **mechanism of attack** (technical exploitation, social engineering, or hybrid).

The **Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY), Government of India**, along with the **Information Technology Act, 2000 (amended 2008)**, classifies cyber offences into four primary categories, which the KTU syllabus formally maps as the canonical taxonomy.

## 1.2 The Four Canonical Categories — At a Glance

The KTU 2024 syllabus formally partitions cybercrime into the following four super-classes:

1. **Crime Against Individuals** — *Person-centric* offences targeting the human psyche, identity, dignity, or finances.
2. **Crime Against Property** — *Asset-centric* offences aiming to unlawfully acquire, damage, or destroy digital or physical property.
3. **Crime Against Organizations** — *Entity-centric* offences targeting corporate, governmental, or institutional digital assets.
4. **Crime Against Society** — *Collective-centric* offences threatening public order, national security, moral fabric, or critical infrastructure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Frequently tested in KTU ESE):**
> - A computer is a **TOOL** when it is used to commit a crime (e.g., sending phishing emails).
> - A computer is a **TARGET** when it is the victim (e.g., a DDoS attack on a server).
> - A computer is an **INCIDENTAL EVIDENCE DEVICE** when the crime is committed using another medium but leaves digital traces.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Digital Highway" Model

Imagine the **Internet as a vast, multi-lane national highway system**:

- **Crime Against Individuals** is like a *pickpocket* on a public bus — the attack is personal, targeted at one commuter.
- **Crime Against Property** is like a *car thief* in a parking lot — the attack targets assets (vehicles/data).
- **Crime Against Organizations** is like a *coordinated heist on a warehouse* — the attack is planned, systematic, and aims at institutional assets.
- **Crime Against Society** is like a *terrorist attack on a dam* — the attack threatens the public, infrastructure, and national stability.

In every case, the **highway (Internet)** enables the crime, but the *target type* changes the legal classification, the investigating agency (Cyber Cell vs. CBI vs. NIA), and the punishment under the **IT Act 2000/2008** and the **Indian Penal Code (IPC, now Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita — BNS 2023)**.

## 1.4 Standardized Metrics & Reference Benchmarks

| Reference Entity | Value / Threshold | Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **IT Act 2000 (Amended 2008)** Sections | 43, 65, 66, 66A–F, 67, 69, 70 | Primary legal framework for KTU definitions |
| **FBI IC3 Annual Loss (2023)** | > **USD 12.5 Billion** | Global benchmark for cyber-crime damage |
| **NCRB Cybercrime Cases (India 2022)** | > **65,000** registered cases | Volume metric for India |
| **Cognizable Offence Threshold** | Imprisonment > **2 years** | KTU exam benchmark for distinguishing "serious" cybercrime |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters for KTU 2024 ESE:** Question papers frequently use terms like *"cognizable offence"*, *"bailable vs. non-bailable"*, and *"Sections 65/66 of the IT Act"*. Memorize the mapping between **Category → Example Offence → Applicable Section**.
