# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification — The Master Taxonomy

The KTU 2024 syllabus mandates a **4-tier hierarchical understanding** of cybercrime categories. The breakdown is shown in logical, exam-friendly bullet form:

### **A. Crime Against Individuals (Person-as-Victim)**
These offences target a specific, identifiable human being. Sub-categories include:

- **Cyberstalking** — Repeated, unsolicited use of digital communication to harass, intimidate, or frighten a specific individual.
  - *Legal hook:* IT Act §66E (violation of privacy) + IPC §509 (word, gesture intended to insult modesty).
- **Cyberbullying** — Use of digital platforms to demean, threaten, or humiliate a person, particularly minors.
  - *Legal hook:* IT Act §66A (struck down in *Shreya Singhal v. Union of India*, 2015 — but recodified under BNS).
- **Identity Theft** — Unauthorized acquisition and use of another person's Personally Identifiable Information (PII) such as Aadhaar, PAN, or credit card data.
  - *Legal hook:* IT Act §66C (identity theft — imprisonment up to **3 years** + fine up to **₹1 lakh**).
- **Phishing / Vishing / Smishing** — Deceptive digital communication impersonating a trusted entity to extract credentials.
  - *Legal hook:* IT Act §66D (cheating by personation using computer resource — up to **3 years** + fine).
- **Email Bombing / Spamming** — Flooding a target's inbox to cause denial of service to legitimate email.
- **Defamation & Morphing** — Digital alteration of images/videos to defame or harass.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Focus:** The phrase *"Crime against individuals"* is incomplete without naming *at least 3 sub-types*. Marks are awarded for **enumeration**, not just definition.

### **B. Crime Against Property (Asset-as-Victim)**
These offences target data, intellectual property, or financial assets.

- **Hacking / Unauthorised Access** — Intrusion into a computer system without permission.
  - *Legal hook:* IT Act §66 (up to **3 years** or fine up to **₹5 lakh**); §43 (civil liability for damages).
- **Data Theft / Data Leakage** — Exfiltration of confidential databases (e.g., Aadhaar leaks).
- **Software Piracy / Intellectual Property Theft** — Illegal reproduction, distribution, or use of copyrighted software.
  - *Legal hook:* IT Act §65 (tampering with computer source documents — up to **3 years** + fine up to **₹2 lakh**).
- **Credit Card Fraud / Banking Fraud** — Unauthorized electronic financial transactions.
  - *Legal hook:* IT Act §66C + IPC §420 (cheating).
- **Salami Attack** — Siphoning off microscopic monetary amounts from millions of accounts (cumulative theft remains undetected for years).
  - *Famous theoretical example:* *The Salami Slicing Attack* — a **0.0001 USD** deduction per transaction.

### **C. Crime Against Organizations (Entity-as-Victim)**
These are *systematic, planned, often financially or politically motivated* attacks against institutions.

- **Denial of Service (DoS) / Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) Attack** — Flooding a server with requests to render it unavailable.
  - *Legal hook:* IT Act §66F (cyber terrorism — up to **life imprisonment** if it threatens sovereignty).
- **Malware Injection (Virus, Worm, Trojan, Ransomware, Spyware)** — Code designed to damage, disrupt, or exfiltrate.
  - *Legal hook:* IT Act §43 + §66.
- **Corporate Espionage / Industrial Espionage** — Theft of trade secrets, R&D data, or strategic plans.
- **Email Bombing of Corporate Servers** — Denial of service targeted at enterprise mail infrastructure.
- **Ransomware Attacks** — Encryption of organizational data demanding crypto-ransom (e.g., WannaCry 2017, Petya 2017, LockBit 2023).
  - *Real-world benchmark:* *WannaCry* infected **230,000+ computers across 150 countries** in a single day, causing **USD 4–8 billion** in damages.

### **D. Crime Against Society (Collective-as-Victim)**
The broadest category — offences that threaten public welfare, morality, or national security.

- **Cyber Terrorism** — Use of cyberspace to intimidate a government or populace, disrupt critical infrastructure (power grids, banking, defense).
  - *Legal hook:* IT Act §66F — punishment extends to **imprisonment which may extend to life**.
- **Online Child Pornography / CSAM** — Distribution, possession, or creation of child sexual abuse material.
  - *Legal hook:* IT Act §67 (up to **7 years** + fine up to **₹10 lakh** on first conviction; **10 years** + fine on second).
- **Online Gambling / Money Laundering** — Use of crypto-currencies and dark-web mixers for money laundering.
- **Forgery** — Digital fabrication of documents, certificates, or currency.
- **Cyber Warfare** — State-sponsored attacks on another nation's digital infrastructure.

## 2.2 The "Tool–Target–Incidental" Framework (KTU High-Yield)

This is a **board-favourite classification** — often asked as a 7-mark question.

$$ \text{Cybercrime Role of Computer} = \begin{cases} \textbf{TOOL} & \text{if computer is the medium of attack} \\ \textbf{TARGET} & \text{if computer is the victim of attack} \\ \textbf{INCIDENTAL} & \text{if digital evidence exists but attack is non-digital} \end{cases} $$

**Application examples:**

| Offence | Role of Computer | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Sending a threatening email | **TOOL** | Email server is the medium |
| DDoS attack on a website | **TARGET** | Server is the victim |
| Online banking fraud | **TOOL** (browser used to commit) + **TARGET** (account database) | Dual role |
| Smuggling with GPS-tracked parcel | **INCIDENTAL** | Crime is physical, not digital |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Category | Sub-Type | Legal Section (IT Act 2000/2008) | Punishment (Years) | Bailable? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Individual | Identity Theft | §66C | ≤ 3 | Yes |
| 2 | Individual | Cheating by Personation | §66D | ≤ 3 | Yes |
| 3 | Individual | Violation of Privacy | §66E | ≤ 3 | Yes |

| 5 | Property | Source Code Tampering | §65 | ≤ 3 | Yes |
| 6 | Property | Damage to Computer | §43 (Civil) | Compensation only | N/A |
| 7 | Organization | Ransomware / DoS | §66 + §43 | ≤ 3 / Compensation | Conditional |
| 8 | Society | Publishing Obscene Material | §67 | ≤ 7 (1st), ≤ 10 (2nd) | **No** |
| 9 | Society | Cyber Terrorism | §66F | Up to **Life** | **No** |
| 10 | Society | Child Pornography (CSAM) | §67B | ≤ 5 (1st), ≤ 7 (2nd) | **No** |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

This classification is not academic only — it directly drives:

- **Incident Response Protocols (NIST SP 800-61):** First responders triage by *Category → Severity → Section*.
- **SOC (Security Operations Centre) Triage:** Alerts are tagged as Individual/Property/Organization/Society for SLA routing.
- **Insurance Underwriting (Cyber Insurance):** Premiums vary by category (Society-class attacks attract 3–5× premium).
- **Digital Forensics Tool Selection:** Tool-tar crimes use email-header analysis; Target-tar crimes use disk-forensics (EnCase, FTK).

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Derivations

## 3.1 The "Decision Tree" for Classifying a Cybercrime

Since this topic is conceptual (not a numerical derivation), the KTU board examiners reward a **structured, stepwise reasoning path**. Use the following **7-step analytical matrix** for any classification problem:

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this section is essential:** A 14-mark KTU question often gives a *scenario* and asks the student to *classify, justify, and cite the relevant IT Act section*. This matrix is your **evaluation rubric**.

### Step 1 — Identify the **Actor (Perpetrator)**

$$
\text{Actor} = \text{script kiddie} \;\vert\; \text{insider} \;\vert\; \text{organized group} \;\vert\; \text{state-sponsored}
$$

- *Script kiddie* → suggests low-impact, opportunistic crimes (Property class).
- *Insider* → suggests Crime Against Organization.
- *State-sponsored* → suggests Crime Against Society (Cyber Warfare / Cyber Terrorism).

### Step 2 — Identify the **Target (Victim)**

$$
\text{Target} = \text{person} \;\vert\; \text{data/asset} \;\vert\; \text{organization} \;\vert\; \text{public}
$$

This single decision determines the **super-category** (A, B, C, or D from §2.1).

### Step 3 — Identify the **Mechanism (Attack Vector)**

$$
\text{Mechanism} = \text{technical exploit} \;\vert\; \text{social engineering} \;\vert\; \text{hybrid}
$$

- *Technical exploit* → IT Act §43/65/66 (hacking, malware).
- *Social engineering* → IT Act §66C/66D (phishing, identity theft).
- *Hybrid* → APT (Advanced Persistent Threat) — e.g., Stuxnet, Pegasus.

### Step 4 — Map to the **IT Act Section**

$$
\text{Section} = f(\text{Behaviour}, \text{Intent}, \text{Damage Magnitude})
$$

For example:
- Behaviour = *Unauthorised access with intent to damage* → §66 + §43.
- Behaviour = *Publish obscene material* → §67.
- Behaviour = *Threaten sovereignty/integrity* → §66F (Cyber Terrorism).

### Step 5 — Determine **Punishment Severity**

$$
\text{Punishment} = g(\text{Section}, \text{First/Repeat Conviction}, \text{Aggregate Damage})
$$

$$
\text{Severity} = \begin{cases} \text{Low} & \text{if} \;\; \text{Punishment} \le 3 \text{ years} \\ \text{Medium} & \text{if} \;\; 3 < \text{Punishment} \le 7 \text{ years} \\ \text{High} & \text{if} \;\; \text{Punishment} > 7 \text{ years or Life} \end{cases}
$$

### Step 6 — Identify the **Investigating Agency**

$$
\text{Agency} = \begin{cases} \text{Local Cyber Cell} & \text{for Class A (Individual)} \\ \text{State CID / Cyber Crime Branch} & \text{for Class B (Property)} \\ \text{CBI / Interpol} & \text{for Class C (Organization)} \\ \text{NIA / Defence Cyber Agency} & \text{for Class D (Society)} \end{cases}
$$

### Step 7 — Compile the **Examiner's Answer**

A complete 7-mark answer must contain:

1. **Category** (A/B/C/D) — 1 mark.
2. **Sub-type** (e.g., Phishing) — 1 mark.
3. **Mechanism** (e.g., Social Engineering) — 1 mark.
4. **IT Act Section** — 2 marks.
5. **Punishment Range** — 1 mark.
6. **Investigating Agency** — 1 mark.

## 3.2 Worked-Out Analytical Problem (Model Solution Style)

> **Question (KTU-style scenario):** *A fraudster sends a mass email that appears to originate from "customercare@sbi.co.in" but is actually from "customercare@sb1.co.in" (note the digit '1' replacing the letter 'i'). The email requests users to verify their ATM PIN and OTP. 12,000 users comply. The fraudster transfers ₹4.7 crores to offshore accounts.*

**Step 1 — Actor:** Organized financial fraud ring.
**Step 2 — Target:** Individual bank customers (persons), but **aggregate damage affects public banking trust** → **dual categorization: A + D (Society)**.
**Step 3 — Mechanism:** Social Engineering (Email Phishing + Domain Spoofing).
**Step 4 — Section:** IT Act §66D (cheating by personation) + §66C (identity theft) + §67 (if obscene).
**Step 5 — Punishment:** Up to 3 years (each count, possibly consecutive) + fine.
**Step 6 — Agency:** State Cyber Cell → CBI (due to cross-border dimension).
**Step 7 — Complete Answer:** This is a **Crime Against Individuals (Class A)** with a **Crime Against Society (Class D)** overlay, executed via **Email Phishing (a social engineering attack)**, punishable under **IT Act §66D + §66C**, investigated by the **CBI** due to the offshore money-trail.

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix — All Four Categories

| Dimension | A: Individual | B: Property | C: Organization | D: Society |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Victim Count** | 1–few | 1–few | 1 entity | Mass (thousands+) |
| **Motivation** | Personal vendetta, revenge, harassment | Financial gain, thrill | Financial, espionage, hacktivism | Ideology, terror, money laundering |
| **Typical Offence** | Stalking, Phishing | Hacking, Piracy | DDoS, Ransomware | Cyber Terrorism, CSAM |
| **IT Act Anchor** | §66C, §66D, §66E | §43, §65, §66 | §66, §66F (if severe) | §66F, §67, §67B |
| **Punishment** | ≤ 3 years | ≤ 3 years | 3–7 years | Up to life |
| **Cognizable?** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes (cognizable + non-bailable) |
| **Bailable?** | Yes | Yes | Conditional | **No** |
| **Investigator** | Local Cyber Cell | State Cyber Branch | CBI, Interpol | NIA, DCyA, IB |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between the role of a computer as a "tool", a "target", and an "incidental evidence device" in cybercrime. Give one example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Role | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Tool** | Computer is the medium used to commit the crime | Sending a phishing email from a laptop |
| **Target** | Computer itself is the victim of the attack | A DDoS attack crashing a web server |
| **Incidental** | Digital evidence exists, but the core crime is non-digital | GPS logs in a smuggling case |

> **Mark split:** 1 mark for each role + example (½ + ½ each).

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**List any four categories of cybercrime as per the IT Act 2000 and give one example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Crime Against Individuals** — Cyberstalking (e.g., harassing a woman on Instagram).
2. **Crime Against Property** — Software piracy (e.g., distributing pirated MS Windows copies).
3. **Crime Against Organizations** — Ransomware (e.g., WannaCry attack on NHS, 2017).
4. **Crime Against Society** — Cyber terrorism (e.g., Stuxnet attack on Iran's nuclear program, 2010).

> **Mark split:** 1 mark for naming categories + ½ mark each for examples.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Classify cybercrime into four major categories. For each category, explain **any two sub-types** with a real-world example. State the **relevant IT Act section** and **maximum punishment** for each sub-type. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bank employee copies the entire customer database (10 million records) onto a USB drive and sells it to a marketing firm. The marketing firm then sends unsolicited calls to all customers.
   - (i) Identify the **category** of cybercrime committed. **(1 mark)**
   - (ii) Name the **IT Act section(s)** applicable. **(2 marks)**
   - (iii) State the **punishment** prescribed. **(2 marks)**
   - (iv) Which **investigating agency** should handle this? **(1 mark)**
   - (v) Suggest **two preventive controls** the bank should implement. **(1 mark)**

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The four major categories are:

**1. Crime Against Individuals (2 marks for category + sub-types):**
- *Cyberstalking* — IT Act §66E (violation of privacy) — up to **3 years** imprisonment.
- *Identity Theft* — IT Act §66C — up to **3 years** + fine up to ₹1 lakh.
- *Real-world example:* Delhi 2018 case where a man created fake social-media profiles of his ex-colleague using her photos.

**2. Crime Against Property (1.5 marks):**
- *Hacking* — IT Act §66 — up to **3 years** + fine up to ₹5 lakh.
- *Software Piracy* — IT Act §65 — up to **3 years** + fine up to ₹2 lakh.
- *Real-world example:* Microsoft India piracy raids (₹50 crore seized in 2022).

**3. Crime Against Organizations (1.5 marks):**
- *Ransomware* — IT Act §66 + §43 — up to **3 years** + civil compensation.
- *DDoS Attack* — IT Act §66 (extended interpretation under §66F if severe) — up to **3 years**.
- *Real-world example:* All India Institute of Medical Sciences (AIIMS) Delhi ransomware attack, Nov 2022 — servers down for 2 weeks.

**4. Crime Against Society (2 marks):**
- *Cyber Terrorism* — IT Act §66F — **imprisonment up to life**.
- *Child Pornography (CSAM)* — IT Act §67B — up to **5 years** first conviction; **7 years** second.
- *Real-world example:* Telegram CSAM rings busted by CBI in 2023 (1,200+ arrests nationwide).

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Category:** **Crime Against Property** (the database is the asset) with an overlay of **Crime Against Individuals** (the 10 million customers have their PII exposed). **(1 mark)**

**(ii) IT Act Sections:** §66 (hacking/disHonest intent to damage), §66B (receiving stolen computer resource), §43 (civil damage to data), and §72 (breach of confidentiality — applies to the bank employee). **(2 marks)**

**(iii) Punishment:** §66 → up to 3 years + fine up to ₹5 lakh. §72 → up to 2 years + fine up to ₹1 lakh. The marketing firm additionally faces civil liability under the **Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDPA) 2023** for unauthorized data processing. **(2 marks)**

**(iv) Investigating Agency:** **State Cyber Cell** (initial FIR) → **CID Cyber Branch** (since it crosses state boundaries) → possible **CBI** involvement if foreign marketing firm is involved. **(1 mark)**

**(v) Two preventive controls:** (1 mark total — ½ each)
1. **Data Loss Prevention (DLP) tools** on employee endpoints to block USB mass-storage devices.
2. **Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) + database activity monitoring** with real-time alerts on bulk exfiltration.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **"Tool–Target–Incidental" framework** for classifying cybercrime. For each role, give **two Indian case examples** and identify the **IT Act section** that applies. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 17-year-old receives repeated threatening messages on WhatsApp from an unknown number. The messages contain morphed obscene images of the victim.
   - (i) Identify the **category and sub-type** of cybercrime. **(1 mark)**
   - (ii) Which **IT Act sections** are applicable? **(2 marks)**
   - (iii) Discuss the **legal position** post the *Shreya Singhal v. Union of India* (2015) judgment with respect to online speech. **(3 marks)**
   - (iv) Recommend **three** remedial actions the victim can take. **(1 mark)**

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Tool–Target–Incidental Framework:** It classifies the **functional role of the computer** in any cybercrime.

**1. Computer as TOOL (3 marks):**
The computer is the medium used to commit the crime.
- *Case 1:* **Bank NRI phishing scam, 2021** — fraudsters used Telegram bots to send SMS pretending to be SBI. Applicable: **IT Act §66D** (cheating by personation).
- *Case 2:* **AIIMS Delhi ransomware, 2022** — hackers used email attachments to deliver ransomware. Applicable: **IT Act §66 + §43**.

**2. Computer as TARGET (2.5 marks):**
The computer itself is the victim.
- *Case 1:* **Yahoo data breach, 2013-14** — 3 billion accounts compromised. Applicable: **IT Act §66 + §43 + §72A** (punishment for intermediary for data failure).
- *Case 2:* **Kudankulam Nuclear Power Plant malware, 2019** — DTrack RAT targeting SCADA systems. Applicable: **IT Act §66F** (cyber terrorism, if sovereignty threatened).

**3. Computer as INCIDENTAL (1.5 marks):**
Digital evidence exists, but the core crime is non-digital.
- *Case 1:* **2018 Abhishek Manu Singhvi morphed-photo case** — court admitted WhatsApp logs as evidence; core offence is defamation under IPC §499.
- *Case 2:* **Sushant Singh Rajput case, 2020** — drug procurement via WhatsApp; core crime is narcotics (NDPS Act), but digital chats are the evidence trail.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Category and Sub-type (1 mark):**
- **Category:** Crime Against Individuals
- **Sub-type:** Cyberstalking + Cyber Defamation + Morphing (multiple sub-types)

**(ii) Applicable IT Act Sections (2 marks):**
- **§66E** — Violation of privacy (punishment up to 3 years + fine).
- **§67** — Publishing obscene material (up to 7 years + fine up to ₹10 lakh).
- **§67A** — Publishing sexually explicit material (up to 7 years).
- **§66D** — Cheating by personation (if the unknown number impersonates a known person).
- Additionally, **BNS 2023 §77** (criminal intimidation), §79 (word/gesture to insult modesty of a woman).

**(iii) Legal Position Post *Shreya Singhal* (3 marks):**

In **Shreya Singhal v. Union of India (2015)**, the Supreme Court of India **struck down Section 66A of the IT Act** as unconstitutional, holding it violated **Article 19(1)(a)** (freedom of speech) and was **vague and overbroad**. Key implications:

1. Mere *offensive or annoying* online speech is **NOT** a crime.
2. Only speech that directly incites **violence, public disorder, defamation, or obscenity** can be prosecuted.
3. Sections **66C, 66D, 66E, 66F, 67, 67A, 67B** are still valid — they target **specific, narrowly defined harms** (identity theft, privacy violation, obscenity, terrorism) and are not struck down.
4. In the current scenario, the **morphed obscene images** clearly fall under **§67/§67A** (which pass constitutional muster as they protect dignity and prevent obscenity), and **not** the struck-down §66A.

**(iv) Three Remedial Actions (1 mark — ½ mark each adjusted; pick any 3):**
1. File a complaint at **cybercrime.gov.in** (Indian Cyber Crime Coordination Centre — I4C portal).
2. Report the WhatsApp number directly to WhatsApp (use the in-app "Report" feature).
3. File an **FIR at the nearest police station** or approach the **State Cyber Cell** (cybercrime is cognizable under IT Act).
4. Preserve digital evidence — take **screen-recorded videos** with timestamp for chain of custody.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students LOSE MARKS on this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing the Tool–Target–Incidental framework** with the Individual/Property/Organization/Society framework — these are **two different classification schemes**, not one. Examiners may use either; mislabelling loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Citing wrong IT Act sections** — the most common error is citing §66A (which is *struck down*) instead of §66C/§66D/§66E for individual-targeted crimes. Examiners deduct full marks if the section is non-existent or invalid.
> 3. **Writing "punishment is 3 years" without specifying + fine.** Always write "up to 3 years imprisonment AND/OR fine up to ₹X lakh".
> 4. **Forgetting the FIRST vs. SECOND conviction distinction** for §67 and §67B — second conviction has higher punishment.
> 5. **Not mentioning the BNS 2023 (formerly IPC)** alongside the IT Act — many cases are tried jointly under both statutes.
> 6. **Mermaid diagrams in answer sheets** — Examiners do NOT evaluate mermaid code. Convert any diagram to a hand-drawn **hierarchical tree** with clear arrowheads in the answer booklet.
> 7. **Using the symbol `|` (pipe) inside table cells** for "absolute value" or "OR" — this breaks the markdown table parser. Use the word "OR" or `\vert` in LaTeX form.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this checklist the night before the exam for a 5-minute rapid revision.**

- ☐ **4 Categories of Cybercrime:** Individual / Property / Organization / Society. (Mnemonic: **I-P-O-S**)
- ☐ **Role of Computer (3 roles):** Tool / Target / Incidental. (Mnemonic: **T-T-I**)
- ☐ **IT Act §66C** = Identity Theft; **§66D** = Cheating by Personation; **§66E** = Privacy Violation; **§66F** = Cyber Terrorism (LIFE); **§67** = Obscene Material; **§67B** = CSAM.
- ☐ **§66A is DEAD** (struck down in *Shreya Singhal*, 2015). Do not cite it in answers.
- ☐ **§66F (Cyber Terrorism)** is the **only section with punishment up to life imprisonment** under the IT Act.
- ☐ **Salami Attack** is a sub-type of Property crime — deduction of microscopic amounts.
- ☐ **Ransomware = Crime Against Organization**; **WannaCry 2017** is the canonical example.
- ☐ **DPDPA 2023** (Digital Personal Data Protection Act) is the **latest Indian data protection law** — cite it for any data-leak scenario.
- ☐ **BNS 2023** has **replaced IPC 1860** — use BNS section numbers for defamation, cheating, intimidation in the exam.
- ☐ **NCRB (National Crime Records Bureau)** publishes annual cybercrime statistics — remember the >65,000 cases per year benchmark.
- ☐ **Investigating Agencies (in order of escalation):** Local Cyber Cell → State CID → CBI → NIA → DCyA (Defence Cyber Agency).
- ☐ **Most-tested sub-types for KTU 2024:** Phishing, Identity Theft, Ransomware, Cyber Terrorism, Morphing.
- ☐ Always write **"up to X years imprisonment AND/OR fine up to ₹Y lakh"** — never just "X years".
- ☐ For 14-mark questions, the **7-step analytical matrix** (Actor → Target → Mechanism → Section → Punishment → Agency → Answer) guarantees a structured, mark-rich answer.

---

**End of Module 2 — Categories of Cybercrime | OECST721 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fundamentals of Cyber Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST721 - CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fundamentals of Cyber Law

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Cyber Law

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cyber Law** is the body of legal rules, regulations, statutes, case law, and judicial precedents that govern the use of computers, the internet, digital networks, electronic communications, and the protection of data, software, hardware, and intellectual property in cyberspace. In the Indian context, it is primarily codified under the **Information Technology Act, 2000** (as amended by the **IT Act, 2008**), read with the **Indian Penal Code (IPC)**, the **Indian Evidence Act**, and the **Indian Contract Act**.

For KTU 2024 Scheme purposes, the scope of *Cyber Law* is restricted to the following three pillars:

1. **E-Governance & Digital Contracts** — legal recognition of electronic records and digital signatures.
2. **Cybercrime Offences & Penalties** — hacking, phishing, identity theft, obscenity, and data breaches.
3. **Regulatory Bodies & Adjudication** — powers of the **Controller of Certifying Authorities (CCA)**, Cyber Appellate Tribunal, and designated courts.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Digital Highway" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Think of the internet as a giant, multi-lane *highway* where every user is a vehicle. The data packets are *cargo*, the servers are *fuel stations*, and the routers are *traffic signals*. **Cyber Law is the Motor Vehicles Act + Traffic Police + Highway Court System** for this digital road. Without it, there would be *no speed limit (no data protection rules)*, *no accident liability (no cybercrime prosecution)*, and *no licensing authority (no digital signature certification)*.

Just as a citizen expects:
- A registered vehicle (→ **Digital Signature Certificate**),
- A traffic signal system (→ **Network Protocols + Cyber Regulations**),
- A police force to catch offenders (→ **Cyber Cells / CBI Cyber Wing**),

…a digital citizen expects *legal infrastructure* in cyberspace. Cyber Law is that infrastructure.

## 1.3 Why Cyber Law is Needed — The Three Core Drivers

| Driver | Real-World Manifestation | Legal Response |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Anonymity of Cyberspace** | Offenders hide behind fake IPs, VPNs, dark-web. | Section 69 IT Act (interception powers) |
| **Jurisdictional Ambiguity** | A crime committed in Kerala can target a server in Singapore. | Section 75 IT Act (extraterritorial jurisdiction) |
| **Speed of Digital Transactions** | Crimes happen in milliseconds, but courts take years. | Adjudication tribunals under Section 46 |

## 1.4 Key Entities & Terminology (Foundation Block)

> [!NOTE]
> **Must-Know Acronyms for KTU Examinations:**
> - **IT Act, 2000** — Information Technology Act, 2000 (the parent statute).
> - **CCA** — Controller of Certifying Authorities (apex body for digital certificates under Section 17).
> - **DSC** — Digital Signature Certificate (electronic equivalent of a physical signature under Section 3).
> - **CSP** — Certifying Service Provider (licensed agency issuing DSCs).
> - **CAT** — Cyber Appellate Tribunal (now merged with ITAT post Finance Act 2017).
> - **CIPA** — Computer Incident Response Team (handled by CERT-In under Section 70B).

## 1.5 Scope Boundaries in the KTU 2024 Syllabus

The KTU Module 2 expects students to master **four** specific sub-domains:

1. **The IT Act, 2000 & 2008 Amendment** — structure, salient sections, offences.
2. **Digital Signature & E-Governance Framework.**
3. **Types of Cybercrimes & Penalties.**
4. **Jurisdictional Issues & Adjudication Mechanism.**

All subsequent sections in these notes are mapped to these four sub-domains.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Information Technology Act, 2000 — Anatomy

The IT Act, 2000 is the **primary statute** and has **13 chapters, 94 sections** (original) and **120+ sections** (post-2008 amendment). For the KTU exam, the high-yield structural map is:

### Chapter-Wise High-Yield Map

| Chapter | Sections | Topic | KTU Weightage |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Chapter I** | 1–2 | Preliminary & Definitions | Medium |
| **Chapter II** | 3–10 | Digital Signature & Electronic Records | **High** |
| **Chapter III** | 11–15 | Regulation of Certifying Authorities | Low |
| **Chapter IV** | 16–21 | Licensing of Certifying Authorities (CCA, CSP) | Medium |
| **Chapter V** | 22–24 | Penalties for Non-Disclosure of Digital Signatures | Low |
| **Chapter VI** | 25–28 | Regulation of Examinations & Electronic Governance | Medium |
| **Chapter VII** | 29–34 | Digital Signature Certificates (DSC) | **High** |
| **Chapter VIII** | 35–40 | Duties of Subscribers | Medium |
| **Chapter IX** | 41–44 | Penalties & Adjudication | **High** |
| **Chapter X** | 45–64 | Cyber Regulation Appellate Tribunal | Medium |
| **Chapter XI** | 65–79 | **Offences & Penalties (Cybercrime Core)** | **Highest** |
| **Chapter XII** | 80–82 | Interception, Monitoring, Blocking | **High** |
| **Chapter XIII** | 84–90 | Miscellaneous (jurisdiction, power of police) | **High** |

## 2.2 The 2008 Amendment — Salient Features

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **IT (Amendment) Act, 2008** was a watershed event because it shifted the IT Act from a *pure e-commerce enabling law* to a *comprehensive cyber-crime law*. The amendment was triggered by India's ratification of the **Budapest Convention on Cybercrime (though India is not a signatory, the principles influenced the amendment)** and the rising incidents of terror-mail, phishing, and data theft.

### Top 5 High-Yield Changes in 2008

1. **Section 66A (struck down 2015)** — Originally criminalized "offensive messages"; struck down by the Supreme Court in *Shreya Singhal v. Union of India (2015)* for violating **Article 19(1)(a)**.
2. **Section 66B** — Punishment for dishonestly receiving stolen computer resource: **3 years imprisonment or fine up to ₹1 lakh or both**.
3. **Section 66C** — Identity theft (punishment: **3 years + fine up to ₹1 lakh**).
4. **Section 66D** — Cheating by personation using communication device (punishment: **3 years + fine up to ₹1 lakh**).
5. **Section 66E** — Violation of privacy (punishment: **3 years + fine up to ₹2 lakh**).

6. **Section 66F** — **Cyber Terrorism** (punishment: **life imprisonment**).
7. **Section 67** — Publishing obscene material electronically.
8. **Section 69** — Government's power to intercept, monitor, decrypt.
9. **Section 69B** — Power to monitor and collect traffic data / metadata; designates **CERT-In** as the national nodal agency.
10. **Section 79** — Safe-harbour provisions for **Intermediaries** (notice-and-takedown regime).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Cyber Law Essentials

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table before any Part A or Part B question. It contains the most examiner-favoured definitions.**

| Term | Section | Definition | Penalty / Effect |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Computer** | Sec 2(1)(i) | Any electronic, magnetic, optical or other high-speed data processing device. | — |
| **Computer Network** | Sec 2(1)(j) | Interconnection of computers via satellites, microwaves, etc. | — |
| **Computer Resource** | Sec 2(1)(k) | Computer, computer network, software, database, or data. | — |
| **Electronic Record** | Sec 2(1)(t) | Data, record, image stored in any computer resource. | Legally admissible (Sec 4) |
| **Digital Signature** | Sec 2(1)(p) | Authentication of electronic record by asymmetric crypto. | Legal recognition (Sec 3) |
| **Hacking** | Sec 66 | Dishonestly/fraudulently accessing/damaging a computer. | 3 yrs or ₹5 lakh or both |
| **Identity Theft** | Sec 66C | Fraudulently using unique identification of another person. | 3 yrs + ₹1 lakh |
| **Cyber Terrorism** | Sec 66F | Threatening integrity, sovereignty, security of India via computer. | **Life imprisonment** |
| **Obscene Material** | Sec 67 | Publishing/transmitting obscene material electronically. | 1st offence: 3 yrs + ₹5 lakh |
| **Child Pornography** | Sec 67B | Publishing/possessing sexually explicit material involving minors. | 1st offence: 5 yrs + ₹10 lakh |
| **Intermediary** | Sec 2(1)(w) | Person who receives/transmits/hosts electronic records. | Safe harbour under Sec 79 |
| **CERT-In** | Sec 70B | Indian Computer Emergency Response Team. | Nodal agency for cyber incidents |
| **Adjudicating Officer** | Sec 46 | Officer appointed by Central Govt to adjudicate claims ≤ ₹5 crore. | Award damages up to ₹5 crore |
| **Cyber Appellate Tribunal (CAT)** | Sec 48 (pre-2017) | Appeals from Adjudicating Officer. | Now merged with ITAT |

## 2.4 The Logic of "Why Each Section Exists" — A Causality Map

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Chain — Connect the Problem to the Law**

- **Problem:** Impersonation on social media → **Law:** Section 66D (cheating by personation via communication device) → **Penalty:** 3 years + ₹1 lakh.
- **Problem:** Aadhaar number leaked → **Law:** Section 66C (identity theft) + Section 72 (breach of confidentiality by govt servant) + Section 43A (compensation for data breach by body corporate).
- **Problem:** WhatsApp rumours triggering mob lynching → **Law:** Section 505 IPC (statements conducing to public mischief) + Section 67 IT Act (obscene material) + Section 69 IT Act (interception).
- **Problem:** WannaCry ransomware attack on hospitals → **Law:** Section 66 (damage to computer) + Section 43 (civil liability for damage) + Section 69B (CERT-In direction).

## 2.5 Jurisdictional Architecture — The Three-Layer Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Cyberspace has no physical borders. The IT Act solves this via a three-layer model:**

1. **Territorial Jurisdiction (Sec 75):** Applies to any offence committed *outside India* if the act involves a *computer, computer network, or computer resource located in India*.
2. **Subject-Matter Jurisdiction:** Adjudicating Officer handles claims up to **₹5 crore**. Claims above this go directly to the **High Court**.
3. **Appellate Jurisdiction:** Orders of Adjudicating Officer → Cyber Appellate Tribunal (now ITAT) → **High Court** (under Article 226/227) → **Supreme Court** (under Article 136).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

For a B.Tech student, this is **not just legal theory** — it directly impacts:
- **Software Development Lifecycle (SDLC):** The **CERT-In Directions of 28 April 2022** mandate *6-hour log retention*, *vulnerability reporting within 6 hours*, and *synchronization with NTP servers* for all network timestamps. This affects every backend developer.
- **Data Engineering:** Section 43A of the IT Act (read with the **SPDI Rules, 2011**) requires *compensation for negligence in implementing reasonable security practices* — directly relevant to DB design.
- **IoT & Critical Infrastructure:** Section 70 establishes a *Protected System* framework — relevant to SCADA/ICS security.
- **Cloud Computing Contracts:** Section 79 safe-harbour requires *intermediaries* to act *expeditiously* on takedown notices — relevant to cloud SLAs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Logical Walk-Throughs

## 3.1 Logical Walk-Through #1 — How a Digital Signature Works Under the IT Act

> [!NOTE]
> The IT Act, 2000 defines a digital signature using **asymmetric cryptography** (public-private key pair). The following step-by-step analysis shows the *complete legal + technical flow* — a favourite KTU 14-mark question.

### Step 1: Hash Generation
The sender's software applies a **hash function** (e.g., SHA-256 as per IT Act Schedule II) to the electronic record.

$$
H = \text{SHA-256}(M)
$$

Where $M$ is the original message and $H$ is a fixed-length 256-bit hash. Any change in $M$ will produce a different $H$.

### Step 2: Hash Encryption (Signing)
The hash $H$ is encrypted with the sender's **private key** ($K_{priv}$). The output is the **Digital Signature**.

$$
S = E_{K_{priv}}(H)
$$

The pair $(M, S)$ is transmitted. Note: The *entire* message is **not** encrypted — only the hash is. This makes digital signatures *efficient* and *authentication-only*, unlike full encryption.

### Step 3: Hash Computation at Receiver
The receiver independently computes the hash of the received message $M'$ using the same algorithm.

$$
H' = \text{SHA-256}(M')
$$

### Step 4: Signature Decryption (Verification)
The receiver decrypts $S$ using the sender's **public key** ($K_{pub}$), which retrieves the original hash $H$.

$$
H = D_{K_{pub}}(S)
$$

### Step 5: Comparison & Legal Effect
- **If $H = H'$:** Message is *authentic* and *integrity preserved*. **Legally valid** under Section 3 IT Act.
- **If $H \neq H'$:** Message is *tampered* or *signature is forged*. **Legally invalid**; admissible as evidence only after Section 65B Indian Evidence Act compliance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The role of the Certifying Authority (CA):** The CA binds the public key $K_{pub}$ to a person's identity by issuing a **Digital Signature Certificate (DSC)**. The CA itself is licensed by the **Controller of Certifying Authorities (CCA)** under Section 21. The CA's own private key is used to *sign* the DSC — forming a **chain of trust**.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Cybercrime Prosecution Flow

> [!NOTE]
> The following step-by-step matrix shows how a cybercrime moves from *incident* to *judgment* — a must-know for KTU Part B questions.

### Step 1: Incident Detection
A victim (individual or organization) detects a cybercrime. The first responders are typically:
- The company's **CISO** (Chief Information Security Officer).
- The bank's **fraud cell** (for financial fraud).
- **CERT-In** (for national-level incidents — under Section 70B).

### Step 2: First Information Report (FIR)
The victim files an **FIR** at the nearest police station. The police officer **cannot refuse** to register an FIR for a cognizable offence. Cybercrime offences under the IT Act are **cognizable** (police can arrest without warrant) and **non-bailable** (only court can grant bail).

### Step 3: Investigation by Cyber Cell
The case is transferred to a **Cyber Crime Investigation Cell (CCIC)**. The investigating officer, being a police officer of rank **Inspector or above**, has powers under Section 80 IT Act to:
- Enter any premises where computer resources are suspected.
- Search, seize, and arrest.
- Require production of records.

### Step 4: Evidence Collection — The 65B Mandate
Under **Section 65B of the Indian Evidence Act, 1872** (read with Section 3 IT Act), any electronic record is admissible only if accompanied by a **Section 65B(4) certificate** from a person who *legally controls the operation* of the device.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (lost-every-year mistake):** Students often write that "electronic evidence is admissible without the 65B certificate". This is **wrong** post the *Anvar P.V. v. P.K. Basheer (2014)* and *Arjun Panditrao Khotkar (2020)* Supreme Court rulings. The certificate is **mandatory**.

### Step 5: Filing of Chargesheet
Within **60–90 days** (depending on offence), the police file a chargesheet under:
- Section 41 IT Act (no offence compounded).
- Section 42 IT Act (compensation + adjudication route).
- Or IPC sections (read with the 2008 amendment's saving clause in Section 91).

### Step 6: Adjudication (Optional Pre-Trial Route)
The victim may also file a **claim application** before the **Adjudicating Officer** (appointed under Section 46) for damages up to **₹5 crore**. This is a *quasi-judicial* proceeding. If dissatisfied, appeal lies to the **Cyber Appellate Tribunal** (now ITAT).

### Step 7: Trial
The designated **Sessions Court / Special Court** tries the case. Conviction under Section 66/66C/66D attracts **3 years imprisonment** which is **non-compoundable** and **non-bailable**.

## 3.3 Conceptual Derivation — The "Damage Quantification Formula" for Section 43

Section 43 of the IT Act provides a *civil liability* framework. The compensation payable is **actual damage + consequential loss + statutory cap**. While the IT Act does not provide a single formula, courts (notably in *Shri. Chander Bhan v. Pawan Kumar* and *Aventis CropScience v. Cilag AG*) have adopted the following structure:

$$
\text{Total Compensation} = D_{actual} + L_{consequential} + C_{restitutionary}
$$

Where:
- $D_{actual}$ = Direct measurable damage (e.g., data destroyed, hardware damaged).
- $L_{consequential}$ = Indirect loss (e.g., business downtime, lost revenue).
- $C_{restitutionary}$ = Court-determined compensation for mental agony, loss of reputation, etc., subject to a cap of **₹1 crore** per incident (under Section 43A read with the SPDI Rules).

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Walk-Through — Identifying the Correct Section (Exam Heuristic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this heuristic to identify the correct IT Act section in any exam question — examiner's favourite analytical tool.**

**Step 1:** Identify the *actor* — is the accused a *hacker* (unauthorized access), *discloser* (privacy breach), *forger* (document tampering), or *observer* (stalking/monitoring)?

**Step 2:** Identify the *victim* — is it a *computer*, *data*, *individual's identity*, or *the sovereignty of India*?

**Step 3:** Identify the *intent* — *dishonest/fraudulent intent* (Sec 66), *negligence* (Sec 43A), *intent to threaten unity* (Sec 66F), or *sexual intent involving minors* (Sec 67B)?

**Step 4:** Apply the **mapping table**:

$$
\text{Actor} \times \text{Victim} \times \text{Intent} \longrightarrow \text{Section}
$$

**Examples:**
- Hacker + Data + Fraudulent intent → **Section 66**
- Impersonator + Individual + Dishonest intent → **Section 66D**
- Discloser + Individual's private photos + With consent violation → **Section 66E**
- Threat-maker + Nation's sovereignty + Intimidation → **Section 66F** (Cyber Terrorism)
- Publisher + Minors' images + Sexual intent → **Section 67B**

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Table — IT Act vs. IPC for Cyber Offences

| Offence Scenario | IPC Section (pre-2008) | IT Act Section (post-2008) | Recommended Charge |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Email phishing for bank password | Sec 420 IPC (cheating) | Sec 66D IT Act | **Sec 66D IT Act** (specific) |
| Publishing morphed nude images | Sec 292 IPC (obscenity) | Sec 66E IT Act (privacy) + Sec 67 IT Act (obscenity) | **66E + 67 IT Act** |
| Threatening email to extort money | Sec 384 IPC (extortion) | Sec 66D IT Act + Sec 383 IPC | **Both (concurrent)** |
| Hacking government server | Sec 451 IPC (house-trespass) | Sec 66 + Sec 43 IT Act | **Sec 66 IT Act + Sec 451 IPC** |
| Defamatory social media post | Sec 499/500 IPC (defamation) | Sec 66A IT Act (struck down) | **Sec 499/500 IPC only** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU point:** After *Shreya Singhal v. Union of India (2015)*, **Section 66A IT Act is DEAD**. Any exam answer that cites 66A as a valid section will receive **zero marks** for that sub-part.

## 3.6 Analytical Derivation — The Digital Evidence Admissibility Test

For electronic evidence to be admissible in an Indian court, it must pass **three tests** (developed in *Arjun Panditrao Khotkar v. Kailash Kushanrao Gorantyal, 2020*):

$$
\text{Admissibility} = T_{65B} \land T_{authentic} \land T_{relevant}
$$

Where:
- $T_{65B}$ = Section 65B(4) certificate present.
- $T_{authentic}$ = Evidence originates from the alleged source (proved by *oral evidence* or *device logs*).
- $T_{relevant}$ = Evidence has *logical nexus* to the fact in issue (Sections 5–55 Indian Evidence Act).

If all three are satisfied → admissible. If **any** one fails → court may still admit it under the *residuary discretion* (Section 65 proviso), but **only in the interest of justice**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram #1 — The IT Act, 2000 (with 2008 Amendment) Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[IT Act 2000 as Parent Statute] --> B[Ch 1: Preliminary Definitions]
    A --> C[Ch 2: Digital Signature and Electronic Records]
    A --> D[Ch 11: Offences and Penalties]
    A --> E[Ch 12: Interception and Monitoring]
    A --> F[Ch 13: Jurisdiction and Miscellaneous]

    C --> C1[Sec 3: Legal Recognition of Electronic Records]
    C --> C2[Sec 10A: Legal Recognition of Contracts in Electronic Form]

    D --> D1[Sec 66: Hacking with Dishonest Intent]
    D --> D2[Sec 66C: Identity Theft]
    D --> D3[Sec 66D: Cheating by Personation]
    D --> D4[Sec 66E: Privacy Violation]
    D --> D5[Sec 66F: Cyber Terrorism]
    D --> D6[Sec 67: Obscene Material]
    D --> D7[Sec 67B: Child Pornography]

    E --> E1[Sec 69: Interception Orders]
    E --> E2[Sec 69B: CERT-In Powers]

    F --> F1[Sec 75: Extra-Territorial Jurisdiction]
    F --> F2[Sec 80: Powers of Police Officer]
    F --> F3[Sec 84: Protection of Action Taken in Good Faith]

    D5 --> T1[Life Imprisonment]
    D1 --> T2[3 Years or Rs 5 Lakh]
    D2 --> T3[3 Years plus Rs 1 Lakh]
    D3 --> T4[3 Years plus Rs 1 Lakh]
    D4 --> T5[3 Years plus Rs 2 Lakh]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram #2 — Digital Signature Workflow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant SENDER
    participant HASHER
    participant SIGNER
    participant NETWORK
    participant VERIFIER
    participant CA as Certifying Authority

    SENDER ->> HASHER: Provide Original Document M
    HASHER ->> HASHER: Compute SHA-256 Hash H
    HASHER ->> SIGNER: Pass H
    SIGNER ->> SIGNER: Encrypt H with Private Key Kpriv
    SIGNER ->> NETWORK: Send M and Signature S
    NETWORK ->> VERIFIER: Deliver M and S
    VERIFIER ->> VERIFIER: Compute New Hash Hprime from M
    VERIFIER ->> CA: Request Public Key of Sender
    CA ->> VERIFIER: Return Kpub via DSC
    VERIFIER ->> VERIFIER: Decrypt S with Kpub to retrieve H
    VERIFIER ->> VERIFIER: Compare H and Hprime
    alt H equals Hprime
        VERIFIER ->> SENDER: Accept as Authentic
    else H not equal Hprime
        VERIFIER ->> SENDER: Reject as Tampered
    end
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram #3 — Cybercrime Prosecution Flow (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Cybercrime Incident] --> B[Victim Detection]
    B --> C[FIR Filing at Police Station]
    C --> D[Transfer to Cyber Cell]
    D --> E[Evidence Collection and Sec 65B Certificate]
    E --> F[Chargesheet Filing]
    F --> G{Claim Value?}
    G -->|Less than Rs 5 Crore| H[Adjudicating Officer Adjudication]
    G -->|More than Rs 5 Crore| I[Sessions Court Direct Trial]
    H --> J[Appeal to Cyber Appellate Tribunal ITAT]
    J --> K[High Court Article 226]
    I --> K
    K --> L[Supreme Court Article 136]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram #4 — The Three Pillars of Cyber Law (Taxonomy)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CYBER LAW FUNDAMENTALS] --> B[E-Governance and Digital Contracts]
    A --> C[Cybercrime Offences and Penalties]
    A --> D[Regulatory and Adjudicatory Bodies]

    B --> B1[Electronic Records Sec 3 to 10]
    B --> B2[Digital Signatures Sec 3 and 35]
    B --> B3[E-Governance Sec 4 to 10A]

    C --> C1[Against Persons Sec 66C 66D 66E 67B]
    C --> C2[Against Property Sec 66 43]
    C --> C3[Against State Sec 66F Cyber Terrorism]
    C --> C4[Against Public Decency Sec 67 67A]

    D --> D1[Controller of Certifying Authorities]
    D --> D2[Adjudicating Officer Sec 46]
    D --> D3[Cyber Appellate Tribunal Sec 48]
    D --> D4[CERT-In Sec 70B]
    D --> D5[Courts Sessions High Court Supreme Court]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram #5 — Jurisdictional Reach of the IT Act

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CYBERCRIME LOCATION] --> B{Where was the act committed?}

    B -->|Entirely within India| C[Territorial Jurisdiction Sec 75 first limb]
    B -->|Outside India| D{Does the act involve Indian Computer Resource?}

    D -->|Yes| E[Extra-Territorial Jurisdiction Sec 75 second limb]
    D -->|No| F[No Indian Jurisdiction]

    C --> G[Nearest Police Station with Cyber Cell]
    E --> G

    G --> H[Adjudicating Officer or Sessions Court]
    H --> I[Cyber Appellate Tribunal ITAT]
    I --> J[High Court]
    J --> K[Supreme Court]
```

## 4.6 Mermaid Diagram #6 — Section 79 Safe-Harbour Mechanism for Intermediaries

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Posts Content on Intermediary Platform] --> B[Intermediary Receives Court Order or Government Notice]
    B --> C{Is Notice Valid?}

    C -->|Yes| D[Intermediary Acts Expeditiously within 72 Hours]
    C -->|No| E[Intermediary Refuses to Act]

    D --> F[Content Removed within 72 Hours]
    D --> G[Intermediary Granted Safe Harbour Protection under Sec 79]

    E --> H[Intermediary may face prosecution]

    F --> I[User May Appeal to Adjudicating Officer]
    I --> J[Cyber Appellate Tribunal]
    J --> K[High Court]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define the term "Digital Signature" as per the Information Technology Act, 2000. Explain the role of the Certifying Authority in issuing a Digital Signature Certificate.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (1.5 Marks):** Under **Section 2(1)(p) of the IT Act, 2000**, a *digital signature* means the authentication of any electronic record by a subscriber by means of an electronic method or procedure in accordance with the provisions of Section 3.

> [!NOTE]
> **Role of Certifying Authority (1.5 Marks):** A **Certifying Authority (CA)** is a person who has been granted a licence under **Section 21** of the IT Act to issue **Digital Signature Certificates (DSCs)**. The CA verifies the identity of the applicant, binds the public key to that identity, and digitally signs the DSC using its own private key, thereby creating a **chain of trust**. The apex body, the **Controller of Certifying Authorities (CCA)**, regulates all CAs under Section 18 of the Act. The CCA also maintains a *Repository of Digital Certificates* accessible to the public.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**Distinguish between "hacking" under Section 66 of the IT Act and "identity theft" under Section 66C. State the punishment for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Section 66 — Hacking | Section 66C — Identity Theft |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Essence** | Dishonest/fraudulent access to or damage of a computer/computer resource. | Fraudulently or dishonestly using the *unique identification feature* (signature, password, biometric) of another person. |
| **Victim** | Computer system or data | A natural person whose identity is stolen |
| **Punishment** | Imprisonment up to **3 years** OR fine up to **₹5 lakh** OR both | Imprisonment up to **3 years** + fine up to **₹1 lakh** (mandatory) |
| **Bail** | Bailable | Non-bailable |
| **Example** | Cracking a bank server and altering data | Using someone's Aadhaar to open a bank account |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write "hacking" and "identity theft" as *synonyms*. They are **distinct** — Section 66 requires *damage to computer resource*, while Section 66C requires *fraudulent use of a person's identity feature*. Do not interchange them.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) with Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) Explain the salient features of the Information Technology (Amendment) Act, 2008. Discuss how it transformed the IT Act, 2000 from an e-commerce enabling law to a comprehensive cybercrime law. (7 Marks)**

**(b) With a neat diagram, explain the digital signature workflow as recognized under the IT Act, 2000. State the role of the Certifying Authority. (7 Marks)**

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2024 - Alternative]

**(a) Discuss the jurisdictional challenges of prosecuting cybercrimes in India. How does Section 75 of the IT Act address the issue of extra-territorial jurisdiction? (7 Marks)**

**(b) Explain the offences of cyber terrorism (Sec 66F), obscenity (Sec 67), and child pornography (Sec 67B). Compare the penalties for each. (7 Marks)**

---

### MODEL SOLUTIONS

#### Solution to Question A — Part (a)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Salient Features of the IT (Amendment) Act, 2008:**

**Feature 1: Shift in Terminology** (1 Mark)
- The phrase *"digital signature"* was retained, but *"electronic signature"* (Section 3A) was introduced for technology neutrality.

**Feature 2: New Offences Introduced** (2 Marks)
- Section 66B (receiving stolen computer resource).
- Section 66C (identity theft).
- Section 66D (cheating by personation using communication device).
- Section 66E (privacy violation).
- Section 66F (**cyber terrorism** — life imprisonment).
- Section 67B (child pornography).

**Feature 3: Strengthened Regulatory Architecture** (1 Mark)
- Section 69B: Government empowered to intercept, monitor, and collect traffic data; **CERT-In** designated as the national nodal agency under Section 70B.
- Section 43A: *Compensation for data breach* by body corporates — the first time Indian law imposed civil liability for data breaches.

**Feature 4: Intermediary Liability (Sec 79)** (1 Mark)
- The concept of *intermediary* was introduced along with the **safe-harbour** (notice-and-takedown) mechanism. This enabled the rise of Indian internet platforms.

**Feature 5: Data Protection Provisions** (1 Mark)
- Sections 72 and 72A introduced criminal penalties for breach of confidentiality by government servants and intermediaries.

**Feature 6: Update to Schedule** (1 Mark)
- Schedule II was updated to include modern **hash algorithms (SHA-2 family)** as recognized cryptographic standards.

---

#### Solution to Question A — Part (b)

**Step 1: Hash Generation (2 Marks)**
The sender's software generates a **hash** $H$ of the original message $M$ using SHA-256:
$$
H = \text{SHA-256}(M)
$$

**Step 2: Hash Encryption (2 Marks)**
$H$ is encrypted using the sender's **private key** $K_{priv}$ to produce the **Digital Signature** $S$:
$$
S = E_{K_{priv}}(H)
$$
The pair $(M, S)$ is transmitted over the network.

**Step 3: Verification at Receiver (2 Marks)**
The receiver independently computes $H' = \text{SHA-256}(M')$ and decrypts $S$ using the sender's **public key** $K_{pub}$:
$$
H = D_{K_{pub}}(S)
$$

**Step 4: Comparison and Legal Effect (1 Mark)**
- If $H = H'$: signature is *authentic* and message is *untampered*. **Legally valid** under Section 3 IT Act.
- If $H \neq H'$: signature is rejected; admissible only after Section 65B compliance.

**Role of Certifying Authority (refer to Section 4.1 diagram above) (Bonus Marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> The CA is a *trusted third party* licensed under Section 21 IT Act. It (i) verifies the identity of the applicant (KYC), (ii) issues the DSC binding the public key to the subscriber's identity, and (iii) maintains a public Repository. The CA's own signature on the DSC creates a **chain of trust** — this is the foundation of the **Public Key Infrastructure (PKI)** recognized by the IT Act.

---

#### Solution to Question B — Part (a)

**Three Core Jurisdictional Challenges (3 Marks):**

1. **Anonymity and Borderlessness:** A single act may originate from Country A, transit through Country B's server, and target a victim in Country C. Traditional IPC jurisdiction requires *territorial presence* of the accused.
2. **Difficulty in Locating the Accused:** IP addresses are not *persons*; they are *resources* and can be spoofed or shared.
3. **Conflict of Laws:** Country A may permit the act (e.g., whistleblower leaks), while Country C may criminalize it.

**Section 75 — Extra-Territorial Jurisdiction (3 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Section 75 IT Act** states that the provisions of the Act apply to **any offence or contravention committed outside India** by *any person*, irrespective of nationality, **if** the act involves a *computer, computer network, or computer resource located in India*.

**Example:** A hacker in Russia launches an attack on a server in Mumbai. The Mumbai Police has jurisdiction even though the hacker is in Russia. The Indian court will try the case under Indian law, but *extradition* depends on the **India-Russia Extradition Treaty, 1999**.

**Practical Limitation (1 Mark):**
Section 75 is a *paper tiger* without MLAT (Mutual Legal Assistance Treaty) cooperation. In practice, the CBI's Interpol wing coordinates with foreign agencies.

---

#### Solution to Question B — Part (b)

| Section | Offence | Ingredients | Maximum Punishment |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **66F Cyber Terrorism** | Act of terrorism through computer | (a) Denies access, (b) attempts to penetrate, (c) introduces malware, (d) threatens integrity/sovereignty/security of India | **Life imprisonment** |
| **67 Obscenity** | Publishing/transmitting obscene material electronically | (a) Publishes or transmits, (b) material is lascivious, (c) in electronic form | 1st offence: **3 yrs + ₹5 lakh**; 2nd/subsequent: **5 yrs + ₹10 lakh** |
| **67B Child Pornography** | Publishing/possessing/collecting/browsing sexually explicit material involving minors | (a) Child is under 18, (b) material is sexually explicit | 1st offence: **5 yrs + ₹10 lakh**; subsequent: **7 yrs + ₹10 lakh** (mandatory minimum 3 yrs) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake — 67B Punishment:** Students often write the same punishment for 67 and 67B. They are **different**. Section 67B is the *strictest* obscenity law because it deals with children — the punishment is **higher** and the **minimum sentence is mandatory**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bonus Point (1 Mark):** Section 67B also criminalizes *browsing* (Section 67B(b)) — even *viewing* child porn on a public computer is an offence. This is unique globally and is based on the POCSO Act, 2012.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before the KTU Exam:**

- **IT Act, 2000** is the **primary cyber law of India**; the **2008 amendment** is the **most important amendment** because it added the cybercrime chapter.
- **Digital Signature** is governed by **Section 3**; **Electronic Signature** by **Section 3A**; **Electronic Record** is legally recognized under **Section 4**.
- **Section 65B certificate** is **mandatory** for admissibility of electronic evidence (post *Arjun Panditrao Khotkar, 2020*).
- **Section 66** = hacking with dishonest intent; **66C** = identity theft; **66D** = personation cheating; **66E** = privacy violation; **66F** = cyber terrorism (life imprisonment).
- **Section 66A** is **DEAD** — struck down in *Shreya Singhal v. UoI (2015)*. Do **not** cite it in answers.
- **CERT-In** is the **national nodal agency** under **Section 70B** for cybersecurity incident response.
- **Section 75** grants **extra-territorial jurisdiction** to the IT Act.
- **Section 79** provides **safe harbour** to intermediaries following due diligence.
- **Adjudicating Officer** awards damages up to **₹5 crore** under **Section 46**.
- **Cyber Appellate Tribunal** was established under **Section 48** but merged with **ITAT** post the **Finance Act, 2017**.
- **Digital Signature Certificate (DSC)** is issued by a **Certifying Authority (CA)** licensed by the **Controller of Certifying Authorities (CCA)**.
- **Asymmetric cryptography** is the technical foundation of digital signatures: a **hash (SHA-256)** of the message is encrypted with the **private key** and verified using the **public key**.
- **Section 43** provides **civil liability** (compensation up to **₹1 crore** under Section 43A read with SPDI Rules) — distinct from the **criminal liability** under Section 66.
- **CERT-In Directions of 28 April 2022** are **operationally critical** for all software companies in India: 6-hour incident reporting, 180-day log retention, NTP synchronization, etc.
- **The Personal Data Protection Bill, 2019** (replaced by the **Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023**) is the **future** of Indian cyber law — beyond the IT Act, 2000.
- The **Budapest Convention on Cybercrime** is the only binding international treaty on cyber law; India is **not a signatory** but the IT Act, 2008 is largely aligned with it.
- **Key Supreme Court Cases:** *Shreya Singhal (2015)*, *Anvar P.V. (2014)*, *Arjun Panditrao Khotkar (2020)*, *K.S. Puttaswamy (2017)* (right to privacy as fundamental right).

> [!WARNING]
> **Final Examiner's Warning:** A common cause of lost marks is **citing IPC sections without the corresponding IT Act section**, or **citing both Section 66 and Section 66A in the same answer**. Section 66A is constitutionally void. Use it only to *discuss* why it was struck down — never as a *current law* provision.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Outline of Legislative Framework for Cyber Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST721 - CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Outline of Legislative Framework for Cyber Law

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Legislative Framework for Cyber Law** refers to the structured body of national statutes, amendments, subordinate legislation, and judicial precedents enacted by sovereign governments to regulate conduct in cyberspace, define cyber offences, establish digital rights, and prescribe civil and criminal liabilities for computer-mediated acts.

In the **Indian context**, this framework is principally anchored by the **Information Technology Act, 2000** (as amended in **2008**), supplemented by the **Indian Penal Code (IPC)**, **Indian Evidence Act, 1872**, and several domain-specific statutes governing data protection, intellectual property, telecommunications, and financial transactions in the digital ecosystem.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 2):** "Outline of Legislative Framework for Cyber Law – IT Act 2000, IT Amendment Act 2008, IPC, Other relevant Acts."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cyber Highway Traffic Rules"

Imagine the internet as a vast, multi-lane digital highway where billions of users travel, transact, communicate, and store valuables (data).

- **Without Cyber Law** → The highway is anarchy. No speed limits, no licensing, no accident accountability. Anyone can crash into your "vehicle" (data) with no consequence.
- **With Cyber Law** → The government posts:
  - **Traffic Police** (IT Act) — patrols for hackers, fraudsters, identity thieves.
  - **Speed Limit Signboards** (Data Protection Rules) — define how fast and how much personal data a company can collect.
  - **Accident Insurance Tribunals** (Cyber Appellate Tribunal / Adjudicating Officers) — settle disputes and award damages.
  - **Court of Appeals** (High Court / Supreme Court) — hear challenges to lower rulings.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** Just as the *Motor Vehicles Act* governs physical transport, the *IT Act 2000* governs **digital transport** — emails, e-contracts, digital signatures, and electronic records.

## 1.3 Why the Framework Exists — The Core "Why"

The legislative framework addresses **four fundamental policy objectives**:

| Objective | Plain English Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Legal Recognition of E-Records** | Make electronic documents as valid as paper ones in court. |
| **Prevention of Cyber Crime** | Define and punish hacking, phishing, identity theft. |
| **Data Protection & Privacy** | Govern how personal data is collected, stored, and processed. |
| **E-Governance & E-Commerce Enablement** | Allow government services and business to legally transact online. |

## 1.4 Core Components of the Indian Cyber Law Framework (At a Glance)

The framework operates on **three concentric layers**:

1. **Primary Layer (Cyber-Specific Statutes)** — IT Act 2000 + 2008 Amendment.
2. **Secondary Layer (Penal & Evidential Support)** — IPC 1860, Indian Evidence Act 1872.
3. **Tertiary Layer (Sectoral & Adjacent Statutes)** — Telegraph Act, Copyright Act, DPDP Act 2023, RBI/SEBI cyber guidelines.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked to "outline the framework," always structure your answer in this **three-layer pyramid**. Examiners love hierarchical organization.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Primary Layer — The Information Technology Act, 2000

The **IT Act, 2000** is India's flagship cyber law. It was enacted on **9 June 2000** (received Presidential assent on 9 June 2000) and came into effect on **17 October 2000**. It was modeled substantially on the **UNCITRAL Model Law on Electronic Commerce (1996)**.

### 2.1.1 Genesis & Objectives
- **Genesis:** Following the **United Nations Commission on International Trade Law (UNCITRAL)** Model Law, India recognized the need to legalize e-commerce and e-governance.
- **Original Objectives (Pre-2008):**
  1. Grant legal recognition to **electronic records** and **digital signatures**.
  2. Facilitate **e-filing** of documents with government agencies.
  3. Establish the **Controller of Certifying Authorities (CCA)**.
  4. Create **cyber appellate tribunals** for dispute resolution.

### 2.1.2 Salient Features of IT Act 2000
- **Chapter II** (Sections 3–10): Digital signature & electronic authentication framework.
- **Chapter III** (Sections 11–24): Regulation of Certifying Authorities.
- **Chapter V, VI, VII**: Penalties, offences, and adjudication.
- **Section 79**: Landmark "safe harbour" for intermediaries.

### 2.1.3 IT Amendment Act, 2008 — The Major Overhaul

The **Information Technology (Amendment) Act, 2008** was a watershed moment. It was enacted in response to the rising tide of cyber crime, the **Mumbai 26/11 attacks** (2008), and the growing misuse of cyberspace.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 2008 Amendment is what most KTU questions test.** Memorize the new sections added.

| New Section | Offence | Punishment |
|---|---|---|
| **Sec 66A** | Sending offensive messages via communication service | Up to **3 years** imprisonment + fine (struck down by SC, 2015) |
| **Sec 66B** | Receiving stolen computer resource or communication device | Up to **3 years** or fine or both |
| **Sec 66C** | **Identity Theft** (using someone else's password/electronic signature fraudulently) | Up to **3 years** + fine up to **₹1 lakh** |
| **Sec 66D** | **Cheating by Personation** using computer resource | Up to **3 years** + fine up to **₹1 lakh** |
| **Sec 66E** | **Voyeurism** (capturing, publishing private images without consent) | Up to **3 years** + fine |
| **Sec 66F** | **Cyber Terrorism** | Imprisonment up to **life imprisonment** |
| **Sec 67A** | Publishing sexually explicit material | Up to **7 years** + fine |
| **Sec 67B** | Publishing child pornography | Up to **7 years** + fine (first offence), **10 years** (subsequent) |
| **Sec 69** | Government interception of communications | With safeguards |
| **Sec 69A** | Blocking of websites | On grounds of sovereignty, security, public order |
| **Sec 72** | Breach of confidentiality & privacy | Up to **2 years** or fine up to **₹1 lakh** |
| **Sec 72A** | Disclosure of information by intermediary | Up to **2 years** or fine up to **₹1 lakh** |
| **Sec 79** | Intermediary safe harbour (with conditions) | Conditional immunity |
| **Sec 80** | Power of police officer to search and seize | Above Inspector rank |

## 2.2 Secondary Layer — Indian Penal Code (IPC), 1860 (Now Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita — BNS 2023)

> [!NOTE]
> **2024 Update for KTU Students:** The IPC has been **replaced** by the **Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS), 2023**, which came into effect on **1 July 2024**. Many cyber-relevant offences have been migrated or expanded.

### 2.2.1 IPC / BNS Sections Still Relevant to Cyber Crime

| IPC (Old) | BNS 2023 (New) | Offence Description | Relevance to Cyber Space |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sec 292 | Sec 294 | Sale of obscene books | Obscene content online |
| Sec 354A-D | Sec 75-79 | Sexual harassment, stalking | Cyberstalking |
| Sec 378, 379 | Sec 303, 305 | Theft | Data theft, identity theft |
| Sec 420 | Sec 318(4) | Cheating & dishonestly inducing delivery of property | Phishing, online fraud |
| Sec 463-477A | Sec 335-338 | Forgery, counterfeit documents | Digital forgery |
| Sec 499-500 | Sec 356-360 | Defamation | Online defamation, trolling |
| Sec 503-507 | Sec 308-310 | Criminal intimidation | Cyber bullying |
| Sec 507 | Sec 351(2) | Criminal intimidation by anonymous communication | Cyber threats |
| Sec 509 | Sec 79 | Word, gesture or act intended to insult modesty of a woman | Online harassment |
| **— (NEW)** | **Sec 111** | **Organised Crime** (online organized fraud rackets) | Cyber fraud syndicates |
| **— (NEW)** | **Sec 112** | **Terrorist Act** | Cyber terrorism coordination |
| **— (NEW)** | **Sec 113** | **Snatching** (redefined for digital theft) | Mobile/digital theft |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often quote *only* the IT Act and forget IPC/BNS. The question may ask for "legislative framework" — meaning **both** IT Act + IPC/BNS are required.

### 2.2.2 Indian Evidence Act, 1872 (Now Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam — BSA 2023)

The **Indian Evidence Act** was replaced by the **Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam (BSA), 2023** effective **1 July 2024**.

**Cyber-Specific Provisions:**

| Old Section (IEA 1872) | New Section (BSA 2023) | Provision |
|---|---|---|
| **Sec 3** | **Sec 2(1)(d)** | "Evidence" includes electronic records |
| **Sec 17** | **Sec 15** | Admission defined to include electronic admissions |
| **Sec 22A** | **Sec 17** | Oral admission as to contents of electronic records |
| **Sec 34** | **Sec 29** | Entries in public records made by public servants in electronic form |
| **Sec 35** | **Sec 30** | Relevance of public documents in electronic form |
| **Sec 39** | **Sec 33** | Evidence as to meaning of illegible electronic documents |
| **Sec 65A** | **Sec 58** | Admissibility of electronic records |
| **Sec 65B** | **Sec 63** | **Conditions for admissibility of secondary evidence in electronic form** |
| **Sec 73A** | **Sec 79** | Comparison of signatures, including electronic signatures |
| **Sec 81A** | **Sec 85** | Presumption as to electronic messages |
| **Sec 85A** | **Sec 88** | Presumption as to electronic records and digital signatures |
| **Sec 85B** | **Sec 89** | Presumption as to digital signatures |
| **Sec 85C** | **Sec 90** | Presumption as to electronic signature certificates |
| **Sec 88A** | **Sec 93** | Presumption as to electronic agreement |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Most Tested Section: Sec 65B (IEA) / Sec 63 (BSA) — "Certificate under Section 65B"** — This is the **procedure** by which a person authenticates electronic evidence (e.g., a CD, email printout, CCTV footage) to be admissible in court. **Without this certificate, the electronic evidence is inadmissible.**

## 2.3 Tertiary Layer — Other Relevant Statutes

### 2.3.1 Indian Telegraph Act, 1885
- **Section 5(2):** Government power to intercept telegraph messages on specified grounds (sovereignty, security).
- Often invoked in tandem with **IT Act Sec 69** for lawful interception.

### 2.3.2 The Copyright Act, 1957 (Amended 2012)
- Covers **software**, **digital content**, and **online piracy**.
- **Section 14**: Defines owner's rights including reproduction in any medium.
- **Section 51**: Infringement provisions.
- **Section 65**: Tampering with software information.

### 2.3.3 The Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 (DPDP Act)
- India's first comprehensive data protection law.
- **Key Concepts:**
  - **Data Principal** — the individual whose data is collected.
  - **Data Fiduciary** — entity that determines purpose and means of processing.
  - **Consent** — must be free, specific, informed, unconditional, unambiguous.
  - **Data Protection Board of India (DPBI)** — adjudicatory body.
  - **Significant Data Fiduciaries (SDFs)** — additional obligations (like a DPO in India).
- **Penalties:** Up to **₹250 crore** per instance for failure to take reasonable security safeguards.

### 2.3.4 Other Acts Worth Knowing

| Statute | Cyber Relevance |
|---|---|
| **Bankers' Books Evidence Act, 1891** (now Part of BSA 2023, Sec 57) | Admissibility of digital bank records |
| **Companies Act, 2013** | Section 447 — fraud; corporate e-governance |
| **Right to Information Act, 2005** | E-RTI, transparency in e-governance |
| **Indian Contract Act, 1872** | Validity of e-contracts (Sec 10A of IT Act) |
| **RBI Master Directions on Digital Lending (2022)** | Regulates digital lending apps, KYC |
| **CERT-In Directions of 28 April 2022** | Mandatory cyber incident reporting within **6 hours** |
| **IT (Intermediary Guidelines and Digital Media Ethics Code) Rules, 2021** | Social media, OTT, news digital media regulation |
| **National Cyber Security Policy, 2013** | Vision document for cyberspace security |
| **Personal Data Protection Bill, 2019** (withdrawn) | The predecessor of DPDP Act 2023 |

## 2.4 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!TIP]
> **This is the "ready-reckoner" for KTU exams. Memorize this table.**

| Legal Instrument | Year | Key Focus | Test Marker |
|---|---|---|---|
| **IT Act** | **2000** | E-commerce, digital signature, e-records | Original cyber law |
| **IT Amendment Act** | **2008** | Cyber crime, data privacy, cyber terrorism | "Most tested topic" |
| **IPC** | **1860** | General penal code | Cyber fraud, defamation |
| **BNS** | **2023** | Replaced IPC | New cyber sections (111, 112, 113) |
| **Indian Evidence Act** | **1872** | Admissibility of evidence | Sec 65B (most cited) |
| **BSA** | **2023** | Replaced Evidence Act | Sec 63 — new 65B equivalent |
| **DPDP Act** | **2023** | Personal data protection | Consent, fiduciary, penalty |
| **Telegraph Act** | **1885** | Communication interception | Sec 5(2) |
| **Copyright Act** | **1957** | Software/digital content | Sec 51, 65 |
| **CERT-In Directions** | **2022** | Incident reporting | 6-hour reporting rule |
| **IT Intermediary Rules** | **2021** | Social media, OTT | Grievance officers, traceability |
| **NCSP 2013** | **2013** | National cyber security policy | Vision, mission |

## 2.5 The "Why" Behind the Framework

The **legislative architecture** exists because:
1. **Jurisdictional Ambiguity** — A crime committed in **Mumbai** can harm a victim in **Mangalore** via a server in **Missouri**. Cyber law defines jurisdiction (Sec 75 IT Act — extra-territorial application).
2. **Technological Neutrality** — New tech (AI, blockchain, IoT) emerges faster than laws. The framework uses **broad definitions** (e.g., "computer resource," "electronic record") to remain future-proof.
3. **Balance of Interests** — Privacy vs. National Security, Free Speech vs. Hate Speech, Innovation vs. Regulation.
