## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Q1, 3 marks]**
> **Q.** Define a *computer virus*. How is it different from a worm?

**Model Answer:**
A *computer virus* is a self-replicating malicious program that attaches itself to a legitimate host file or system area and spreads only when the host is executed. The key difference is the *host-dependency*: a virus **requires a host program and a triggering human action** to propagate, whereas a **worm** is a standalone program that propagates **autonomously over a network** by exploiting vulnerabilities, requiring no host file and no human intervention. (3 marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Q2, 3 marks]**
> **Q.** What is a *polymorphic virus*? Why does it defeat signature-based antivirus?

**Model Answer:**
A polymorphic virus is a self-encrypting malicious program that **changes its byte-level signature on every replication** by using a fresh random encryption key, while preserving its functionality. Because each infected copy is bytewise unique, the *static signature database* maintained by a first-generation antivirus engine cannot match it. Detection requires a *generic decryption emulator* or *behavioural analysis*. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module-Internal Choice (ESE Style)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper Pattern, 14 marks]**
> **Module 3, Question 7A.** *With a neat diagram, describe the four phases of a virus life cycle. Explain the three structural components of a virus: infection mechanism, trigger, and payload.* **(14 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part A — 7 marks):**

**(a) The Four Phases of a Virus Life Cycle (7 marks)**

A virus progresses through four canonical phases:

1. **Dormant Phase** *(2 marks):* The virus is *idle*. After infecting a host, it waits silently for a triggering condition. During this phase it does not damage or replicate. Many modern viruses skip this phase entirely and begin propagation immediately.

2. **Propagation Phase** *(2 marks):* The virus locates and infects *new eligible hosts*. It scans file systems, emails, removable media, or network shares, and copies itself into them while preserving host executability. This phase is the *replication engine* of the virus.

3. **Triggering Phase** *(1 mark):* The virus evaluates a *trigger predicate* — a date, a count, a user action, or an external event. If the predicate is true, it transitions to the execution phase; otherwise, it returns to dormancy or continues propagation.

4. **Execution Phase** *(2 marks):* The *payload* is delivered. Damage can include file corruption, data exfiltration, screen display, encryption (ransomware), or installation of a backdoor. The payload is the *visible symptom* — the reason the user notices the infection.

**Model Solution (Part B — 7 marks):**

**(b) Three Structural Components of a Virus (7 marks)**

1. **Infection Mechanism** *(2 marks):* The *search-and-copy engine*. It uses OS API calls (e.g., `FindFirstFile`, `CreateFile` on Windows) to locate candidate host files. It injects viral code into the host (prepending, appending, or cavity insertion) and patches entry points so the virus runs first.

2. **Trigger** *(2 marks):* A *predicate* such as a calendar date (*Friday 13th*), a counter (*after 1000 file writes*), or a user event (*double-click on a document*). The trigger is the virus's *activation gate*.

3. **Payload** *(3 marks):* The *malicious effect* — file destruction, screen messages, data theft, encryption, or backdoor installation. Payloads are categorized as **non-destructive** (e.g., a message), **data-destructive** (e.g., zero-fill MBR), or **ransomware** (encryption for extortion). A virus may carry multiple payloads.

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagrammatic requirement:** A labelled block diagram showing the three components in relation to the host program is essential. (See *Section 4.1* above for the reference architecture.)

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper Pattern, 14 marks, Alternative Choice]**
> **Module 3, Question 7B.** *Explain the classification of computer viruses. With examples, distinguish between polymorphic and metamorphic viruses.* **(14 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part A — 7 marks):**

**(a) Classification of Computer Viruses (7 marks)**

Viruses are classified by **target location**, **concealment strategy**, and **infection strategy**.

1. **By target location (3 marks):**
   - **Boot sector virus** — infects the MBR; e.g., *Brain*, *Michelangelo*.
   - **File/program virus** — attaches to .exe/.com; e.g., *CIH/Chernobyl*.
   - **Macro virus** — infects Office documents via VBA; e.g., *Melissa*, *Concept*.
   - **Email virus** — propagates via attachments; e.g., *ILOVEYOU*, *Mydoom*.

2. **By concealment strategy (2 marks):**
   - **Stealth virus** — hides file-size modifications via OS hooks.
   - **Polymorphic virus** — encrypts body with rotating key; signature changes.
   - **Metamorphic virus** — rewrites own code; structure changes per copy.

3. **By infection strategy (2 marks):**
   - **Multipartite virus** — infects both boot sector and files.
   - **Companion virus** — creates a COM shadow next to an EXE.
   - **Armored virus** — uses anti-debug, anti-VM techniques.

**Model Solution (Part B — 7 marks):**

**(b) Polymorphic vs. Metamorphic Virus (7 marks)**

| Criterion | Polymorphic Virus | Metamorphic Virus |
|---|---|---|
| **Mutation level** | Data (encrypted body) | Code (instruction layout) |
| **Encryption** | Yes, fresh key per copy | Not required |
| **Signature change** | Yes, but decryption stub static | Yes, all bytes change |
| **Code structure** | Constant logic, variable bytes | New logic per copy |
| **Detection difficulty** | Defeated by signature; caught by emulators | Defeats signature *and* emulators |
| **Mathematical model** | $V_i = E_{K_i}(M) \,\vert\vert\, D_{K_i}$ | $V_{i+1} = \mathcal{T}(V_i)$ |
| **Example** | Storm Worm | Simile, MetaPHOR |

**Key engineering insight (2 marks):** Polymorphism is *data-level* randomness; metamorphism is *code-level* randomness. The former requires an emulator to unpack; the latter requires AI/ML behavioural baselining to detect.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Misclassifying virus vs. worm** — single biggest deduction. Always state the *host-dependency* property.
> 2. **Drawing the life-cycle as a circle without labelling triggers** — examiners expect explicit *phase names and transitions*.
> 3. **Confusing polymorphic and metamorphic** — must explicitly mention *data-level* vs. *code-level* mutation.
> 4. **Forgetting to give real examples** — at least one named virus family is mandatory for full marks.
> 5. **Omitting the confusion-matrix derivation** in AV-evaluation questions — examiners allocate 4 of 14 marks to the matrix.
> 6. **Hand-waving the exponential replication formula** — the closed-form $R_c(n) = R_0 (1+p)^{n}$ *must* be derived, not just quoted.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- [x] A **virus** is a *self-replicating, host-dependent* malicious program. A **worm** is *autonomous, network-aware*. **Always distinguish.**
- [x] Three structural components: **Infection Mechanism + Trigger + Payload** (Stallings framework).
- [x] Four life-cycle phases: **Dormant → Propagation → Triggering → Execution** (dormant is optional).
- [x] Boot sector viruses infect the **MBR (Sector 0)**; file viruses infect **.exe/.com**; macro viruses infect **Office docs**; email viruses spread via **attachments**.
- [x] **Stealth** virus hides file-size changes; **polymorphic** encrypts body; **metamorphic** rewrites code.
- [x] **Multipartite** = boot + file infection. **Companion** = COM shadow. **Armored** = anti-analysis.
- [x] First-generation AV uses **signatures**; second-generation uses **heuristics**; third-generation uses **behaviour/HIPS**; fourth-generation uses **cloud + sandbox + ML**; fifth-generation is **AI-driven threat hunting**.
- [x] Confusion-matrix metrics: $D$, $TPR$, $FPR$, $PPV$, $F_1$ — all derivable from $\{TP, FP, TN, FN\}$.
- [x] Replication model: $R_c(n) = R_0 (1+p)^{n}$; time-to-saturate: $n = \ln(N/R_0) / \ln(1+p)$.
- [x] Polymorphism = **data-level** randomness; Metamorphism = **code-level** randomness.
- [x] **Examiner's mantra:** *Name the virus. Cite a real-world example. State the phase. Draw the diagram.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Worms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST721 - CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Worms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Computer Worm** is a standalone, self-replicating, malicious software program (malware) that propagates itself across computer networks without any human intervention and without the need to attach itself to an existing host file or program. Unlike viruses, worms do not require a host file to infect; they are **self-contained executable programs** capable of autonomous distribution through network vulnerabilities, email systems, or removable media.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 3 Highlight):**
> *"A worm is a self-replicating program that spreads across networks by exploiting security vulnerabilities in operating systems and application software, consuming bandwidth and system resources, often leading to denial-of-service conditions."*

Formally, a worm $W$ can be modeled as a tuple:
$$W = \{ P, V, T, C, L \}$$

Where:
- $P$ = Propagation mechanism (network protocol, email, P2P)
- $V$ = Vulnerability target (buffer overflow, weak credentials)
- $T$ = Trigger/payload execution
- $C$ = Carrier or transport medium
- $L$ = Lifecycle state (dormant, propagation, activation, extinction)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Biological Worm vs. The Computer Worm**
>
> Think of a **biological worm** burrowing through soil — it moves from one patch of earth to the next, finding nutrients, and reproduces on its own. Similarly, a **computer worm** "burrows" through a network from one computer to the next, finding vulnerable systems, and replicates itself automatically.
>
> **Simpler Analogy for a KTU Student:**
> Imagine you are in a classroom of 100 students. A **virus** is like a rumor that only spreads when someone *deliberately* tells another student (requires a human action — the "host file"). A **worm** is like a **chain letter** that photocopies itself and slides under every closed door in the school on its own — no human needs to carry it. It just keeps multiplying until the whole school is flooded.

## 1.3 Key Distinguishing Characteristics

| Property | Virus | Worm |
|----------|-------|------|
| Host file required? | **Yes** (attaches to .exe, .doc) | **No** (standalone) |
| Human action needed? | **Yes** (open file) | **No** (autonomous) |
| Primary medium | Local files, USB | **Network** (TCP/IP, email) |
| Speed of spread | Slow | **Exponentially fast** |
| Primary damage | File corruption | **Bandwidth exhaustion, DoS** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Metric to Memorize:** A worm's spread follows an **epidemiological S-curve** with peak infection velocity measured in **hosts per second (hps)**. Famous benchmarks: **Code Red** infected ~359,000 hosts in **24 hours** (≈ **4.15 hps**); **Conficker** infected ~1.1 million hosts in **~2 hours** during its initial burst.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in Bold

- **TCP Port 445** — Primary exploitation vector for **SMB (Server Message Block)** vulnerabilities used by worms like Blaster and Conficker.
- **TCP Port 80** — HTTP traffic channel exploited by worms like **Code Red** and **Nimda**.
- **Default scan rate** of Morris Worm: **~100 hosts per second** (later reduced by code bug to non-lethal rates).
- **Time-to-infection (TTI)** — Time taken for a worm to infect a target percentage of vulnerable population.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Phases of a Worm (Lifecycle)

A worm's operational lifecycle consists of four discrete phases that examiners frequently test in KTU descriptive questions:

### Phase 1: **Reconnaissance / Scanning Phase**
The worm identifies potential victim hosts. Three primary scanning strategies exist:

1. **Random Scanning:** Target IP selected uniformly at random from the entire IPv4 address space.
2. **Hit-list Scanning:** Worm carries a pre-compiled list of vulnerable IPs (eliminates early wastage).
3. **Permutation Scanning:** Worm variants share a partitioned IP-space list to avoid duplicate scanning.

### Phase 2: **Exploitation / Infection Phase**
The worm attempts to compromise the victim by:
- Buffer overflow attacks (stack smashing, heap spraying)
- Dictionary-based credential brute force
- Exploiting unpatched CVEs (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures)

### Phase 3: **Replication / Payload Transfer**
The worm copies itself to the compromised host via:
- **TFTP (Trivial File Transfer Protocol)**
- **HTTP GET requests**
- **SMB file shares**
- **Embedded shell-code injection**

### Phase 4: **Execution & Lateral Movement**
The new instance activates, then resumes scanning — creating a **recursive propagation loop**.

## 2.2 Worm Propagation Mathematical Models

### 2.2.1 Simple Epidemic Model (SEM)

The simplest model, assuming infinite susceptible population:
$$\frac{dI(t)}{dt} = \beta \cdot I(t) \cdot [N - I(t)]$$

Where:
- $I(t)$ = Number of infected hosts at time $t$
- $N$ = Total vulnerable population size
- $\beta$ = **Pairwise infection rate** (units: $\text{infections per infected host per unit time}$)

This is a **logistic differential equation** with analytical solution:
$$I(t) = \frac{N}{1 + \left(\frac{N - I_0}{I_0}\right) e^{-\beta N t}}$$

Where $I_0$ is the initial number of infected hosts at $t = 0$.

### 2.2.2 Kermack-McKendrick (SIR) Model

Divides population into three compartments:
$$\begin{aligned}
S(t) + I(t) + R(t) &= N \\[4pt]
\frac{dS}{dt} &= -\beta \cdot S \cdot I \\[4pt]
\frac{dI}{dt} &= \beta \cdot S \cdot I - \gamma \cdot I \\[4pt]
\frac{dR}{dt} &= \gamma \cdot I
\end{aligned}$$

Where:
- $S(t)$ = **Susceptible** hosts
- $I(t)$ = **Infected** hosts  
- $R(t)$ = **Removed/Recovered** hosts
- $\gamma$ = **Recovery rate** (patches applied, hosts isolated)
- $\beta$ = **Infection rate constant**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** The **Basic Reproduction Number** $R_0$ determines whether a worm outbreak becomes an epidemic:
> $$R_0 = \frac{\beta \cdot N}{\gamma}$$
> - If $R_0 > 1$ → **Epidemic occurs** (worm spreads)
> - If $R_0 \leq 1$ → **Outbreak dies out**

### 2.3 Two-Factor Worm Propagation Model

The advanced model accounts for:
- **Network congestion** slowing the worm
- **Human countermeasures** (patches, blacklisting, ISP filtering)

$$\frac{dI(t)}{dt} = \beta(t) \cdot I(t) \cdot [N - R(t) - I(t)]$$

Here $\beta(t)$ becomes a **time-decreasing function** rather than a constant, capturing real-world dynamics.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Worm propagation models are not merely academic — they are deployed in:

| Application Domain | Use Case |
|--------------------|----------|
| **Network Operations Centers (NOCs)** | Predicting bandwidth saturation during outbreaks |
| **Cybersecurity Insurance** | Estimating risk premiums using $R_0$ |
| **ISP Traffic Engineering** | Pre-allocating scrubbing capacity for DDoS |
| **Epidemiology** | Modeling real biological disease spread |
| **Critical Infrastructure** | Modeling Stuxnet-style APT lateral movement |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Variables / Units |
|---------|----------------|-------------------|
| Logistic Growth | $I(t) = \frac{N}{1 + \left(\frac{N - I_0}{I_0}\right) e^{-\beta N t}}$ | $I(t)$: infected hosts; $N$: total vulnerable |
| Simple Epidemic | $\frac{dI}{dt} = \beta I (N - I)$ | $\beta$: pairwise rate |
| Basic Reproduction | $R_0 = \frac{\beta N}{\gamma}$ | Dimensionless |
| SIR Recovery | $\frac{dR}{dt} = \gamma I$ | $\gamma$: rate (1/time) |
| Two-Factor | $\frac{dI}{dt} = \beta(t) I [N - R - I]$ | Time-varying $\beta$ |
| Doubling Time | $T_d = \frac{\ln 2}{\beta N}$ | Seconds |
| Epidemic Threshold | $R_0 = 1$ | Critical boundary |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** In Part B answers, always state the **assumptions** of the model you are using (homogeneous network, infinite population, constant $\beta$). Examiners award **2-3 extra marks** for explicitly listing assumptions.

## 2.6 Why and How — The Core Logic

- **Why** random scanning fails in IPv4 scarcity: 4 billion addresses = too many wasted probes.
- **Why** hit-lists work: Pre-loading 10,000–50,000 IPs ensures rapid initial spread before the worm's IP-discovery becomes the bottleneck.
- **How** worms self-limit: Network congestion causes the effective $\beta$ to drop, naturally capping spread (the "two-factor" effect).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Solution of the Simple Epidemic Model

We begin with the Simple Epidemic Model (SEM) differential equation:

$$\frac{dI(t)}{dt} = \beta \cdot I(t) \cdot [N - I(t)]$$

**Step 1 — Identify the equation type.**
This is a first-order, non-linear, **separable ordinary differential equation** in $I(t)$ with parameter $N$ and $\beta$.

**Step 2 — Separate variables.**
We move all $I$ terms to the left and all $t$ terms to the right:

$$\frac{dI}{I(N - I)} = \beta \, dt$$

**Step 3 — Apply partial fraction decomposition.**
The left-hand side rational function decomposes as:

$$\frac{1}{I(N - I)} = \frac{A}{I} + \frac{B}{N - I}$$

Multiplying through by $I(N - I)$:

$$1 = A(N - I) + B \cdot I$$

Setting $I = 0$: $\quad 1 = AN \implies A = \frac{1}{N}$

Setting $I = N$: $\quad 1 = BN \implies B = \frac{1}{N}$

Therefore:

$$\frac{1}{I(N - I)} = \frac{1}{N}\left[\frac{1}{I} + \frac{1}{N - I}\right]$$

**Step 4 — Integrate both sides.**

$$\int \frac{1}{I(N - I)} \, dI = \int \beta \, dt$$

$$\frac{1}{N}\int\left[\frac{1}{I} + \frac{1}{N - I}\right] dI = \beta t + C$$

$$\frac{1}{N}\left[\ln I - \ln(N - I)\right] = \beta t + C$$

**Step 5 — Simplify using log rules.**

$$\frac{1}{N}\ln\left(\frac{I}{N - I}\right) = \beta t + C$$

Exponentiating both sides:

$$\frac{I}{N - I} = e^{N(\beta t + C)} = A' \cdot e^{\beta N t}$$

where $A' = e^{NC}$ is the constant of integration.

**Step 6 — Apply initial condition.**
At $t = 0$, $I(0) = I_0$:

$$\frac{I_0}{N - I_0} = A' \cdot e^{0} = A'$$

Therefore:

$$A' = \frac{I_0}{N - I_0}$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $I(t)$.**

$$\frac{I}{N - I} = \frac{I_0}{N - I_0} \cdot e^{\beta N t}$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$I(N - I_0) = I_0 (N - I) \cdot e^{\beta N t}$$

$$I \cdot N - I \cdot I_0 = I_0 N e^{\beta N t} - I_0 I e^{\beta N t}$$

$$I\left[N - I_0 + I_0 e^{\beta N t}\right] = I_0 N e^{\beta N t}$$

$$I(t) = \frac{I_0 N e^{\beta N t}}{N - I_0 + I_0 e^{\beta N t}}$$

**Step 8 — Rearrange into standard logistic form.**
Dividing numerator and denominator by $I_0 e^{\beta N t}$:

$$I(t) = \frac{N}{\frac{N - I_0}{I_0} e^{-\beta N t} + 1}$$

$$\boxed{I(t) = \frac{N}{1 + \left(\frac{N - I_0}{I_0}\right) e^{-\beta N t}}}$$

This is the **standard logistic S-curve** solution for worm infection growth.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A worm begins with $I_0 = 10$ initial infections in a vulnerable population of $N = 100{,}000$ hosts. The pairwise infection rate is $\beta = 2.5 \times 10^{-7}$ per host per second. Find (a) the number of infected hosts at $t = 1$ hour, and (b) the doubling time during early exponential growth.

### Part (a) — Infections at $t = 3600$ seconds

**Step 1:** Compute the exponent.

$$\beta N t = (2.5 \times 10^{-7})(100{,}000)(3600)$$

$$\beta N t = (0.025)(3600) = 90$$

**Step 2:** Compute the term $\left(\frac{N - I_0}{I_0}\right)$.

$$\frac{N - I_0}{I_0} = \frac{99{,}990}{10} = 9999$$

**Step 3:** Plug into the logistic formula.

$$I(3600) = \frac{100{,}000}{1 + 9999 \cdot e^{-90}}$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate $e^{-90}$.

$$e^{-90} \approx 1.22 \times 10^{-39}$$

This is essentially zero — the infection has saturated.

**Step 5:** Final answer.

$$I(3600) \approx \frac{100{,}000}{1 + 0} = 100{,}000 \text{ hosts}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The entire vulnerable population is infected within one hour. This is a **catastrophic spread rate** — illustrating why worms like Code Red were devastating.

### Part (b) — Doubling Time

During **early growth** (small $I$), the model approximates exponential growth:
$$\frac{dI}{dt} \approx \beta N I \implies I(t) \approx I_0 e^{\beta N t}$$

Doubling time satisfies $I(T_d) = 2 I_0$:

$$2 I_0 = I_0 e^{\beta N T_d} \implies \ln 2 = \beta N T_d$$

$$T_d = \frac{\ln 2}{\beta N} = \frac{0.6931}{0.025}$$

$$\boxed{T_d \approx 27.7 \text{ seconds}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** The worm doubles its infection count every **~27.7 seconds** in the early phase — meaning a single infected host becomes **2 → 4 → 8 → 1024** in just **5 minutes** of initial spread.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Worm Behaviors vs. Detection Methods

| Worm Behavior | Mathematical Indicator | Detection Mechanism |
|---------------|------------------------|---------------------|
| Rapid scan rate | High $dI/dt$ | NetFlow anomaly detection |
| Port concentration | Single-port traffic spike | IDS signature (Snort) |
| Self-replication | Bandwidth asymmetry (out > in) | Threshold-based alerts |
| Payload delivery | Outbound connections spike | DLP systems |
| Lateral movement | Internal-east-west traffic anomaly | Microsegmentation monitors |

## 3.4 Morris Worm Case Study (1988)

The first major Internet worm, released by Robert Tappan Morris:

- **Exploited:** `sendmail` (debug mode), `fingerd` (buffer overflow), weak passwords (`rsh`)
- **Initial rate:** Designed for stealth — reduced by a code bug to a fraction of intended speed
- **Affected:** ~6,000 of ~60,000 Internet hosts (≈ **10% of the early Internet**)
- **Damage:** Load averages up to 99 on infected machines — effectively unusable
- **Legal impact:** First conviction under the **Computer Fraud and Abuse Act (1986)**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Worm Lifecycle — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid

```

## 4.4 Layered Defense Architecture (Defense-in-Depth)
