<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Adware: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Adware (Advertising-Supported Software)** is a category of software that automatically delivers, renders, or downloads advertising material to a user's endpoint device (desktop, mobile, browser) — typically without obtaining informed, explicit, or revocable consent. From a **KTU 2024 Cyber Security** perspective, Adware is classified as a subclass of **Potentially Unwanted Programs (PUPs)** and **Malware**, occupying the grey zone between legitimate marketing utilities and outright malicious software, depending on its consent model, persistence mechanism, and data-harvesting behaviour.

In the **STRIDE threat model** and the **MITRE ATT&CK Enterprise Matrix (Tactic TA0007 — Discovery / TA0011 — Command and Control)**, Adware is treated as a **reconnaissance and monetization vehicle**. It typically:

- Injects advertising payloads into web browsers, Operating System (OS) dialogs, or third-party applications.
- Performs **User Behavior Tracking (UBT)** by collecting browsing history, search queries, geolocation, and device fingerprinting data.
- Establishes a covert **Command-and-Control (C2)** channel to fetch dynamic ad payloads, click-fraud scripts, or pay-per-install (PPI) redirects.

The **Adware Revenue Chain** can be formally expressed as:

$$
\text{Adware Operator} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Bundling / Exploit}}\; \text{Infected Endpoint} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Ad Impression / Click}}\; \text{Advertiser Network} \;\xrightarrow{\text{CPC / CPM / PPI Payout}}\; \text{Adware Operator}
$$

The unit of monetization, as per the **Interactive Advertising Bureau (IAB) standards**, is **CPM (Cost Per Mille — cost per 1,000 impressions)**, typically ranging between **USD 0.10 to USD 5.00** for desktop and **USD 0.50 to USD 10.00** for mobile.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Free Newspaper with a Tracking Bracelet"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Imagine you walk into a shopping mall and a friendly vendor hands you a *free* newspaper. You accept. The next day, the same vendor is at your door — they know your name, your coffee preference, the route you took to work, and they keep slipping flyers into your mailbox, your car, and even your children's school bags. That is **Adware**. The newspaper was *free* only because your attention, behaviour, and personal data became the product being sold to advertisers.

A second analogy: think of Adware as a **digital mosquito**. It does not destroy your system (like a virus or ransomware), but it persistently buzzes around — slowing things, draining resources, and transmitting your private data back to a swarm of remote servers.

---

## 1.3 Where Adware Sits in the Malware Taxonomy

| Category          | Consent Model | Resource Abuse | Data Exfiltration | KTU Classification          |
|-------------------|---------------|----------------|-------------------|------------------------------|
| **Virus**         | None          | High           | Optional          | **Malware — Destructive**    |
| **Worm**          | None          | High           | Optional          | **Malware — Propagating**    |
| **Ransomware**    | None          | Critical       | Yes               | **Malware — Extortion**      |
| **Spyware**       | None          | Medium         | Heavy             | **Malware — Surveillance**   |
| **Adware**        | Grey / Deceptive | Low–Medium  | Medium            | **PUP / Greyware**           |
| **Legitimate Ad-Supported App** | Explicit EULA | Low | Minimal, anonymized | **Benign Software**   |

---

## 1.4 Core Characteristics of Adware (KTU Board-Exam Checklist)

- **Automatic Ad Display** — Pop-ups, pop-unders, in-text ads, banner overlays.
- **Browser/DNS Hijacking** — Redirects the default search engine and homepage.
- **Tracking Cookies & Fingerprinting** — Harvests User-Agent, IP, MAC, geolocation.
- **Bundled Distribution** — Hidden inside "free" installers (Pay-Per-Install model).
- **Persistence** — Registry Run keys, Scheduled Tasks, Browser Helper Objects (BHOs), LaunchAgents.
- **Difficult Uninstallation** — Often refuses removal or reinstalls itself via watchdog processes.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Module 3 — Adware: Deep Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Lifecycle of an Adware Program

The life of an Adware payload, from a **Cyber Security** standpoint, follows a deterministic 7-stage pipeline. Each stage must be analysed at the **CO (Course Outcome) level — CO3: Apply security mechanisms to mitigate malware threats**.

### Stage 1 — Distribution (Vector)

Adware propagates through the following vectors, listed in decreasing prevalence according to the **2024 Verizon DBIR (Data Breach Investigations Report)**:

1. **Software Bundling (Pay-Per-Install / PPI)** — ~**62%** of all Adware infections.
2. **Malvertising** — Malicious ads served on legitimate websites (e.g., the 2016 MSN.com malvertising campaign).
3. **Drive-by Downloads** — Exploit kits (Angler, RIG) drop Adware via unpatched browser/plugin vulnerabilities.
4. **Trojanized Apps** — Repackaged legitimate apps on third-party app stores.
5. **Email Attachments / Macros** — Less common but rising.

### Stage 2 — Installation & Persistence

Once executed, the Adware installer (often a **dropper**) drops multiple components:

- `adware32.dll` — The core payload.
- `watchdog.exe` — A process monitor that re-installs the payload if removed.
- `browser_helper.js` — A browser extension for injection.
- `config.dat` — Encrypted C2 endpoint and campaign ID.

Persistence is achieved through:
- **Windows Registry:** `HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run`
- **Scheduled Tasks** (`schtasks /create`).
- **WMI Event Subscriptions** (fileless persistence).
- **macOS LaunchAgents:** `~/Library/LaunchAgents/com.adware.plist`.

### Stage 3 — Hooking & Injection

The Adware injects its DLL/JS into target processes via:
- **AppInit_DLLs** (legacy).
- **DLL Search Order Hijacking.**
- **Process Hollowing** (in advanced variants).
- **Browser Extension API Abuse** (e.g., `chrome.contentScript`).

### Stage 4 — Ad Fetching (C2 Communication)

The Adware phones home using:
- HTTP/HTTPS GET to a hard-coded or DGA-generated (Domain Generation Algorithm) domain.
- DNS-over-HTTPS (DoH) to bypass DNS-based blocking.
- Steganographic payload delivery inside image files.

### Stage 5 — User Tracking & Data Exfiltration

Collected telemetry typically includes:

$$
\text{Data}_{\text{exfil}} = \{\text{URL}_{\text{visited}}, \text{IP}, \text{UA}, \text{Geo}, \text{DeviceID}, \text{SearchQueries}\}
$$

### Stage 6 — Monetization

Revenue is generated through:
- **PPC (Pay-Per-Click)** fraud.
- **PPI (Pay-Per-Install)** of additional PUPs.
- **Affiliate redirects** (e.g., fake software updates).
- **Data resale** to third-party data brokers.

### Stage 7 — Self-Defense & Update

Watchdog processes, anti-VM checks, and signed-binary abuse (e.g., stealing valid code-signing certificates) make Adware resilient to naive removal.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas, metrics, and heuristic indices are essential for answering 14-mark KTU ESE questions and for solving the numerical/computational sub-parts that may appear in **Part B (a)** of Module 3.

### Table 2.2.A — Adware Detection & Risk Metrics

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Typical Value / Range | Unit |
|------------------|---------|------------------------|------|
| $N_{\text{ads}} = f \cdot t$ | Number of ad impressions in time $t$ at frequency $f$ | 5–60 ads/min | impressions |
| $R_{\text{infection}} = \dfrac{I_{\text{confirmed}}}{N_{\text{endpoints}}} \times 100\%$ | Infection rate across a fleet | 0.5%–18% | percent |
| $C_{\text{rev}} = (N_{\text{clicks}} \times \text{CPC}) + (N_{\text{imp}} \times \text{CPM}/1000) + (N_{\text{PPI}} \times \text{PPI}_{\text{rate}})$ | Daily revenue of an adware operator | USD 0.05 – USD 50.00 / endpoint / day | USD |
| $D_{\text{score}} = w_1 P + w_2 T + w_3 N + w_4 A$ | Heuristic detection score (P=persistence, T=tracking, N=network, A=anti-analysis) | 0 – 10 | dimensionless |
| $T_{\text{dwell}} = T_{\text{infection}} - T_{\text{detection}}$ | Dwell time inside the system | 7 – 240 | days |
| $\eta_{\text{consent}} = \dfrac{U_{\text{consent\_users}}}{U_{\text{total}}}$ | Legitimacy ratio of an ad-supported app | > 0.95 = legitimate; < 0.3 = malicious | ratio |
| $P_{\text{click}} = 1 - (1 - p)^n$ | Probability of at least one click in $n$ impressions | 0.01 – 0.30 | probability |
| $\text{MITRE}_{\text{score}} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} s_i \cdot w_i$ | Aggregated MITRE ATT&CK technique score | 0 – 100 | score |

> **Notation Convention:** $\cdot$ denotes multiplication, $\sum$ denotes summation, $w_i$ are normalized weights with $\sum w_i = 1$.

### Table 2.2.B — Pay-Per-Install Pricing Tiers (2024 Underground Market Estimates)

| Tier | Geo-Target          | Price Per Install (USD) | Bundle Type           |
|------|---------------------|--------------------------|------------------------|
| T1   | US, UK, CA, AU      | **USD 1.50 – 4.00**      | Silent install         |
| T2   | EU                  | **USD 0.80 – 1.80**      | Silent install         |
| T3   | IN, BR, PH          | **USD 0.10 – 0.40**      | Bundle + EULA          |
| T4   | Africa / LatAm      | **USD 0.02 – 0.10**      | Bundle + EULA          |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding Adware mechanics is not merely academic. The same **process injection**, **persistence**, and **C2 beaconing** techniques taught here are foundational to:

- **Blue Team / SOC Analyst** workflows (detection engineering, YARA rule writing).
- **EDR (Endpoint Detection & Response)** signature development (CrowdStrike, SentinelOne, Microsoft Defender).
- **Digital Forensics & Incident Response (DFIR)** — triage, root-cause analysis, and IOC (Indicator of Compromise) extraction.
- **Privacy Engineering** — GDPR / DPDP Act (India 2023) / CCPA compliance audits where Adware is treated as unlawful profiling.
- **Browser Security** — Chromium's Manifest V3 was specifically designed to limit Adware-style extension abuse.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU CO Mapping:** This topic directly satisfies **CO3** (*Identify and analyze malware threats and apply appropriate mitigation strategies*) and partially **CO4** (*Demonstrate the use of tools and best practices to secure systems*).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Module 3 — Adware: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivations are exhaustive. Every algebraic step is shown in full, in strict accordance with the **KTU 2024 Scheme step-marking scheme**, where each transition in a numerical problem typically carries **1 mark**.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Estimating Daily Revenue of an Adware Campaign

**Problem (KTU-Style 7-mark sub-part):**
An Adware operator infects **5,000** endpoints in Tier-2 geography. Each endpoint, on average, displays **40 ad impressions per day** with a **CPM of USD 1.20** and generates **0.6 clicks per day** with a **CPC of USD 0.35**. Additionally, the bundle triggers a secondary Pay-Per-Install of a browser hijacker on **8%** of endpoints at a **PPI rate of USD 0.90** per install. Compute the daily gross revenue $C_{\text{rev}}$ using the consolidated formula:

$$
C_{\text{rev}} = (N_{\text{clicks}} \times \text{CPC}) + (N_{\text{imp}} \times \frac{\text{CPM}}{1000}) + (N_{\text{PPI}} \times \text{PPI}_{\text{rate}})
$$

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the given parameters and write them in symbolic form.**

$$
N_{\text{endpoints}} = 5000
$$

$$
\text{Imp}_{\text{per\_ep\_per\_day}} = 40
$$

$$
\text{CPM} = 1.20 \;\text{USD per 1,000 impressions}
$$

$$
\text{Click}_{\text{per\_ep\_per\_day}} = 0.6
$$

$$
\text{CPC} = 0.35 \;\text{USD per click}
$$

$$
\text{PPI}_{\text{rate}} = 0.90 \;\text{USD per install}
$$

$$
P_{\text{secondary\_install}} = 0.08 \;\text{(8\%)}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the total daily impressions $N_{\text{imp}}$.**

$$
N_{\text{imp}} = N_{\text{endpoints}} \times \text{Imp}_{\text{per\_ep\_per\_day}}
$$

$$
N_{\text{imp}} = 5000 \times 40
$$

$$
N_{\text{imp}} = 200{,}000 \;\text{impressions per day}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the impression-based revenue component $R_{\text{imp}}$.**

$$
R_{\text{imp}} = N_{\text{imp}} \times \frac{\text{CPM}}{1000}
$$

$$
R_{\text{imp}} = 200{,}000 \times \frac{1.20}{1000}
$$

$$
R_{\text{imp}} = 200{,}000 \times 0.0012
$$

$$
R_{\text{imp}} = 240 \;\text{USD per day}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the total daily clicks $N_{\text{clicks}}$.**

$$
N_{\text{clicks}} = N_{\text{endpoints}} \times \text{Click}_{\text{per\_ep\_per\_day}}
$$

$$
N_{\text{clicks}} = 5000 \times 0.6
$$

$$
N_{\text{clicks}} = 3000 \;\text{clicks per day}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the click-based revenue component $R_{\text{click}}$.**

$$
R_{\text{click}} = N_{\text{clicks}} \times \text{CPC}
$$

$$
R_{\text{click}} = 3000 \times 0.35
$$

$$
R_{\text{click}} = 1050 \;\text{USD per day}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the number of secondary Pay-Per-Installs $N_{\text{PPI}}$.**

$$
N_{\text{PPI}} = N_{\text{endpoints}} \times P_{\text{secondary\_install}}
$$

$$
N_{\text{PPI}} = 5000 \times 0.08
$$

$$
N_{\text{PPI}} = 400 \;\text{secondary installs}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute the PPI-based revenue component $R_{\text{PPI}}$.**

$$
R_{\text{PPI}} = N_{\text{PPI}} \times \text{PPI}_{\text{rate}}
$$

$$
R_{\text{PPI}} = 400 \times 0.90
$$

$$
R_{\text{PPI}} = 360 \;\text{USD per day}
$$

**Step 8 — Sum all three revenue components to obtain the gross daily revenue $C_{\text{rev}}$.**

$$
C_{\text{rev}} = R_{\text{click}} + R_{\text{imp}} + R_{\text{PPI}}
$$

$$
C_{\text{rev}} = 1050 + 240 + 360
$$

$$
C_{\text{rev}} = 1650 \;\text{USD per day}
$$

**Final Answer (boxed):**

$$
\boxed{C_{\text{rev}} = 1650 \;\text{USD per day}}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** At a sustained infection base of 5,000 endpoints, the campaign grosses **USD 49,500 per month** and **USD 6,02,250 per year** — a stark reminder of why Adware remains one of the most economically motivated threat categories in the wild.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Heuristic Detection Score for a Suspicious Adware Sample

**Problem (KTU-Style 7-mark sub-part):**
A security analyst is scoring a suspected Adware sample using the heuristic formula:

$$
D_{\text{score}} = w_1 P + w_2 T + w_3 N + w_4 A
$$

The observed indicators and the assigned normalized weights are:

| Component | Observation                                                  | Weight $w_i$ | Raw Score (0–10) |
|-----------|--------------------------------------------------------------|--------------|-------------------|
| $P$ — Persistence | Drops a Run key + Scheduled Task                | 0.30         | 9                 |
| $T$ — Tracking    | Harvests browsing history + Geo + DeviceID      | 0.30         | 8                 |
| $N$ — Network     | C2 beacon every 60 s, DGA fallback              | 0.20         | 7                 |
| $A$ — Anti-Analysis | Anti-VM, anti-debugger, packed payload         | 0.20         | 6                 |

Compute the final $D_{\text{score}}$ and classify the sample.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Verify that weights sum to 1 (sanity check).**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{4} w_i = 0.30 + 0.30 + 0.20 + 0.20 = 1.00 \;\checkmark
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the raw scores and weights into the formula.**

$$
D_{\text{score}} = (0.30 \times 9) + (0.30 \times 8) + (0.20 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 6)
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate each multiplication.**

$$
D_{\text{score}} = 2.70 + 2.40 + 1.40 + 1.20
$$

**Step 4 — Sum the four terms.**

$$
D_{\text{score}} = 7.70
$$

**Step 5 — Classify using the standard KTU/industry threshold table.**

| $D_{\text{score}}$ Range | Classification      | Recommended Action           |
|---------------------------|---------------------|-------------------------------|
| 0.0 – 2.5                 | **Benign**          | Allowlist                     |
| 2.5 – 5.0                 | **Suspicious / PUP**| Sandbox + user prompt         |
| 5.0 – 7.5                 | **Malicious PUP**   | Quarantine + alert SOC        |
| 7.5 – 10.0                | **Confirmed Malware**| Block + forensic capture     |

Since $7.70 \in [7.5, 10.0]$:

$$
\boxed{D_{\text{score}} = 7.70 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Confirmed Malware — Block and Forensically Capture}}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** This same scoring framework is the conceptual ancestor of modern EDR **composite threat scores** (e.g., CrowdStrike's "Severity" or Defender's "Alert level"). KTU examiners expect students to **derive, not just state**, such scores.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Module 3 — Adware: Structural Diagrams & Infection-Chain Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — The End-to-End Adware Infection Chain (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[User Visits Compromised or Bundled Site] --> A2[Malvertising or Bundled Installer Detected]
    A2 --> A3{Dropper Executed}
    A3 -- Yes --> A4[Payload Dropped to Temp Folder]
    A3 -- No --> A5[User Cancels — Infection Aborted]
    A4 --> A6[Persistence Established: Registry, Scheduled Task, LaunchAgent]
    A6 --> A7[Browser or Process Hooking Initiated]
    A7 --> A8[C2 Beacon: Fetch Ad Configuration]
    A8 --> A9[Telemetry Collection: URL, IP, UA, Geo, DeviceID]
    A9 --> A10{User Clicks Ad}
    A10 -- Yes --> A11[CPC Revenue Credited to Operator]
    A10 -- No --> A12[CPM Impression Revenue Credited]
    A9 --> A13[Secondary PUP Push: Pay Per Install]
    A13 --> A14[Watchdog Respawns Payload if Removed]
    A14 --> A15[Self-Update Channel Receives New Variant]
    A15 --> A16[EDR or AV Triggers — IOC Captured]
    A16 --> A17[SOC Analyst Initiates DFIR Triage]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The right-side loop (A14 → A15) is the *self-healing* property that distinguishes Adware from a one-shot trojan. The bottom branch (A16 → A17) is where defensive Cyber Security operations actually begin.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Adware Detection & Defense Architecture (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Endpoint Layer: Windows macOS Android] --> B2[Network Layer: DNS Firewall IDS IPS]
    B2 --> B3[Cloud Sandbox: Detonate and Score]
    B3 --> B4[Heuristic Engine: Dscore Formula]
    B4 --> B5{Classification}
    B5 -- Benign --> B6[Allowlist]
    B5 -- PUP --> B7[Sandbox and User Prompt]
    B5 -- Malicious PUP --> B8[Quarantine and SOC Alert]
    B5 -- Malware --> B9[Block and Forensic Image]
    B8 --> B10[SIEM Correlation]
    B9 --> B10[SIEM Correlation]
    B10 --> B11[Threat Intel Platform: IOC Sharing]
    B11 --> B12[EDR Policy Update and Patch Rollout]
```

> **Engineering Note:** This block-level map directly corresponds to the **NIST SP 800-83 Rev. 2** *Guide to Malware Incident Prevention and Handling* layered defence model. KTU examiners frequently expect students to map a given threat (e.g., Adware) to the correct **NIST layer**.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — MITRE ATT&CK Mapping for Adware (Compact)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Initial Access: T1189 Drive by Compromise] --> C2[Execution: T1059 Command Interpreter]
    C2 --> C3[Persistence: T1547 Registry Run Keys]
    C3 --> C4[Privilege Escalation: T1068 Exploitation for Privilege Escalation]
    C4 --> C5[Defense Evasion: T1027 Obfuscated Files]
    C5 --> C6[Credential Access: T1552 Unsecured Credentials]
    C6 --> C7[Discovery: T1082 System Information Discovery]
    C7 --> C8[Collection: T1005 Data from Local System]
    C8 --> C9[Command and Control: T1071 Application Layer Protocol]
    C9 --> C10[Impact: T1492 Stored Data Manipulation]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Although Adware is rarely seen at the T1068 (Privilege Escalation) or T1492 (Data Manipulation) stages, *advanced* Adware variants (e.g., **Fireball**, **DollarRevenue**) have demonstrated exactly this kill-chain progression. KTU 2024 questions often test this awareness.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Module 3 — Adware: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Do **not** classify Adware as a "virus" — Adware is a **PUP / Greyware** subclass, not a self-replicating virus.
> 2. Do **not** confuse **CPC** (cost per click) with **CPM** (cost per mille). Board examiners *specifically* deduct marks for unit errors.
> 3. Always specify **which consent model** (explicit EULA vs. silent install) makes a given Adware "legitimate" or "malicious".
> 4. In numerical problems, **write the formula first, substitute second, evaluate third** — this is the official KTU mark-allocation order.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define Adware. Differentiate between legitimate ad-supported software and malicious Adware.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Adware is *advertising-supported software* that automatically displays or downloads advertising content on a user's device, often bundled with free applications. The key differentiator between **legitimate** and **malicious** Adware is the **consent model**. Legitimate ad-supported software (e.g., the free tier of Spotify) presents an **explicit End-User License Agreement (EULA)**, offers a clear opt-out, and limits data collection to anonymized telemetry. Malicious Adware employs **silent or deceptive installation**, **browser hijacking**, **data exfiltration without informed consent**, and **anti-removal persistence mechanisms**. The boundary is governed by privacy regulations such as **GDPR (EU)**, **CCPA (California)**, and India's **DPDP Act 2023**, which mandate *explicit, granular, and revocable* consent for ad personalization.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**List any four distribution vectors of Adware. Why is software bundling the most prevalent vector?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Four major distribution vectors of Adware are:
1. **Software Bundling (Pay-Per-Install)** — Hidden inside freeware/shareware installers.
2. **Malvertising** — Malicious advertisements injected into legitimate ad networks.
3. **Drive-by Downloads** — Exploit kits that silently drop Adware via browser/plugin flaws.
4. **Trojanized Mobile Apps** — Repackaged APKs on unofficial app stores.

Software bundling is the most prevalent vector because it is **economically scalable**: the Adware operator pays a small fee (USD 0.10 – 4.00 per install, depending on geography) to the bundler, and the user's trust in a "familiar installer UI" lowers their guard. The **Pay-Per-Install (PPI) affiliate model** creates a self-reinforcing supply chain of distributors.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

---

### ✅ Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

#### (a) Explain in detail the operational lifecycle of an Adware program, from distribution to monetization. Highlight at least three distinct persistence mechanisms. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

The operational lifecycle of an Adware program is a 7-stage pipeline. Each stage plays a critical role in the attack chain.

**Stage 1 — Distribution (1 mark).** The Adware operator partners with **Pay-Per-Install (PPI) affiliate networks**. A free utility (e.g., a PDF converter, media player, or browser toolbar) is repackaged with the Adware payload. The user downloads the "free" software, and during installation, the Adware is silently side-loaded via *pre-checked* or *deceptively labelled* checkboxes ("Install recommended offers").

**Stage 2 — Installation & Persistence (2 marks).** Once executed, the Adware dropper installs itself to a hidden directory (e.g., `%AppData%`, `/Library/Application Support/`). Persistence is achieved through at least three of the following mechanisms:

- **Windows Registry Run Keys:** `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run` and the `HKLM` equivalent.
- **Scheduled Tasks:** Created via `schtasks /create /sc minute /mo 30 /tn "Updater" /tr payload.exe`.
- **WMI Event Subscriptions:** Fileless persistence using `__EventFilter`, `CommandLineEventConsumer`, and `FilterToConsumerBinding`.
- **macOS LaunchAgents:** `~/Library/LaunchAgents/com.adware.plist`.
- **Browser Extension Persistence:** Re-installs the malicious extension via group policy or local storage poisoning.

**Stage 3 — Hooking & Injection (1 mark).** The Adware injects a DLL/JS payload into the browser process (`chrome.exe`, `firefox.exe`) using techniques like **AppInit_DLLs**, **process hollowing**, or **Chromium content script injection**.

**Stage 4 — C2 Communication (1 mark).** The infected host beacons to a **Command-and-Control server** over HTTP/HTTPS, often using **Domain Generation Algorithms (DGA)** or **DNS-over-HTTPS (DoH)** to evade detection. The C2 returns an ad manifest — a JSON/XML payload specifying which ad networks to load, which search engines to redirect to, and which tracking pixels to fire.

**Stage 5 — User Tracking (1 mark).** The injected scripts harvest `document.cookie`, `navigator.userAgent`, geolocation via the HTML5 API, and a hashed **DeviceID**. This data is exfiltrated back to the C2 in a compact, base64-encoded blob.

**Stage 6 — Monetization (1 mark).** Revenue is generated through three streams: **CPC (Pay-Per-Click)** on injected ad links, **CPM (Pay-Per-Mille)** on forced ad impressions, and **PPI** for the secondary drop of browser hijackers or fake AVs.

**Stage 7 — Self-Defense & Update (additional credit).** A **watchdog process** monitors the Adware binary; if removed, it re-installs the payload within minutes. Anti-VM and anti-debugger checks prevent sandbox analysis. Some variants abuse **stolen code-signing certificates** to bypass OS trust prompts.

#### (b) With a suitable block diagram, describe a layered defence architecture against Adware for an enterprise. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

A robust defence architecture against Adware is built on the **defence-in-depth** principle, with five layers:

**Layer 1 — Endpoint Hardening (1.5 marks).** Deploy a reputable **EDR** (Endpoint Detection and Response) such as Microsoft Defender for Endpoint, CrowdStrike Falcon, or SentinelOne. Enforce **Application Control** (e.g., Windows Defender Application Control / WDAC, or AppLocker) to whitelist known-good executables. Disable browser plugin auto-install via Group Policy.

**Layer 2 — Network Filtering (1.5 marks).** Deploy **DNS firewalls** (Cisco Umbrella, Quad9) to sinkhole known Adware C2 domains. Use **IDS/IPS** (Suricata, Snort) with signatures for Adware C2 traffic. Block outbound DoH to non-enterprise resolvers to force DNS visibility.

**Layer 3 — Email & Web Gateway (1 mark).** Scan all incoming mail for malicious attachments. Use a **Secure Web Gateway (SWG)** with sandboxed ad-network inspection to block malvertising.

**Layer 4 — Cloud Sandbox (1 mark).** Detonate all executables and browser extensions in a sandbox (e.g., ANY.RUN, Joe Sandbox, Hybrid Analysis). Compute the **$D_{\text{score}}$ heuristic** (see Section 3.2) and block samples scoring above **5.0**.

**Layer 5 — SIEM & Threat Intelligence (2 marks).** Forward all EDR, DNS, and proxy logs to a **SIEM** (Splunk, Elastic, QRadar). Correlate Adware IOCs (hashes, domains, IPs) with **MITRE ATT&CK** technique IDs. Subscribe to feeds from **AlienVault OTX**, **Spamhaus**, and **Malware-Traffic-Analysis.net**. Conduct monthly **purple-team exercises** simulating Adware droppers (e.g., the *Fireball* or *DollarRevenue* TTP set).

The block diagram is provided in **Section 4.2** of these notes.

---

### ✅ Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

#### (a) Compare Adware, Spyware, and Ransomware along the following dimensions: (i) Primary objective, (ii) Consent model, (iii) Data exfiltration behaviour, (iv) Monetization mechanism, (v) User-visible impact, and (vi) Typical KTU/MITRE classification. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

A side-by-side comparison:

| Dimension              | Adware                                           | Spyware                                                | Ransomware                                      |
|------------------------|---------------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------|
| **(i) Primary objective** | Display ads; monetize impressions/clicks | Covert surveillance; data theft                   | Encrypt files; demand ransom                    |
| **(ii) Consent model**  | Deceptive / grey (silent install or hidden EULA) | None — fully covert                                    | None — destructive                              |
| **(iii) Data exfiltration** | Medium: browsing history, geo, deviceID   | Heavy: keystrokes, screenshots, mic/cam, credentials   | Low (pre-encryption) but increasing ("double extortion") |
| **(iv) Monetization**   | CPC, CPM, PPI                                    | Data resale on darknet markets                         | Ransom payment (crypto)                         |
| **(v) User-visible impact** | Pop-ups, browser slowdown, homepage hijack | Mostly invisible — performance drop only              | Files encrypted, ransom note displayed          |
| **(vi) MITRE / KTU class** | PUP / Greyware (TA0007 Discovery)            | Malware — Surveillance (TA0009 Collection)              | Malware — Impact (TA0040 Impact)                |

**Explanation (each cell carries partial credit):** Adware is the *least destructive* of the three — its goal is monetization through attention, not data theft or destruction. Spyware is *stealthy and surveillance-focused*, often invisible to the user. Ransomware is the *most destructive*, employing cryptographic extortion. The **MITRE ATT&CK tactic IDs** (TA0007, TA0009, TA0040) directly map to the **Discovery, Collection, and Impact** stages of the kill chain.

#### (b) An enterprise of 8,000 endpoints has detected Adware on 720 endpoints. The EDR has flagged a sample with persistence weight 0.25 (raw score 8), tracking weight 0.35 (raw score 9), network weight 0.20 (raw score 6), and anti-analysis weight 0.20 (raw score 5). Compute the heuristic $D_{\text{score}}$ and the infection rate $R_{\text{infection}}$. Comment on the recommended action. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the infection rate $R_{\text{infection}}$.**

$$
R_{\text{infection}} = \frac{I_{\text{confirmed}}}{N_{\text{endpoints}}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
R_{\text{infection}} = \frac{720}{8000} \times 100\%
$$

$$
R_{\text{infection}} = 9.0\%
$$

**Step 2 — Verify weight normalization.**

$$
\sum w_i = 0.25 + 0.35 + 0.20 + 0.20 = 1.00 \;\checkmark
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the heuristic $D_{\text{score}}$.**

$$
D_{\text{score}} = (0.25 \times 8) + (0.35 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 6) + (0.20 \times 5)
$$

$$
D_{\text{score}} = 2.00 + 3.15 + 1.20 + 1.00
$$

$$
D_{\text{score}} = 7.35
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the infection rate.**

An infection rate of **9.0%** is significantly above the **MITRE/CIS baseline acceptable rate of < 1%**. This indicates a **lateral bundling event** — likely a popular free utility was rolled out enterprise-wide and dragged the Adware with it.

**Step 5 — Classify the sample using the threshold table.**

Since $D_{\text{score}} = 7.35 \in [5.0, 7.5]$, the sample is classified as **Malicious PUP**.

**Step 6 — Recommend action.**

$$
\boxed{D_{\text{score}} = 7.35 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Malicious PUP — Quarantine, SOC Alert, Forensically Image, Roll Back Bundled Installer}}
$$

Specific actions:
1. **Quarantine** all 720 infected endpoints via EDR.
2. **Block** the C2 domains at the DNS firewall.
3. **Roll back** the bundling source (likely a recent freeware push) via SCCM/Intune.
4. **Conduct a post-incident review** within 72 hours, per **NIST SP 800-61 Rev. 2** Incident Response guidelines.
5. **Update user awareness training** — this infection pattern is human-factor driven, not zero-day driven.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Revision Punchlist — KTU Module 3: Adware**

- **Adware = Advertising-Supported Software**, classified as **PUP / Greyware** under KTU taxonomy.
- **Primary vectors:** Software bundling (PPI), malvertising, drive-by downloads, trojanized apps.
- **Monetization streams:** **CPC** (per click), **CPM** (per 1,000 impressions), **PPI** (per install). Always remember: **M = Mille = 1,000**.
- **Top 3 persistence mechanisms:** Registry Run keys, Scheduled Tasks, Browser Extensions.
- **Telemetry collected:** `URL, IP, User-Agent, Geo, DeviceID, SearchQueries`.
- **Heuristic formula (must memorize):**

$$
D_{\text{score}} = w_1 P + w_2 T + w_3 N + w_4 A
$$

- **Revenue formula (must memorize):**

$$
C_{\text{rev}} = (N_{\text{clicks}} \times \text{CPC}) + \left(N_{\text{imp}} \times \frac{\text{CPM}}{1000}\right) + (N_{\text{PPI}} \times \text{PPI}_{\text{rate}})
$$

- **Infection rate formula (must memorize):**

$$
R_{\text{infection}} = \frac{I_{\text{confirmed}}}{N_{\text{endpoints}}} \times 100\%
$$

- **Legitimacy test:** Is there *explicit, informed, revocable* consent? If yes → legitimate. If no → malicious.
- **MITRE ATT&CK link:** TA0007 (Discovery), TA0011 (C2), TA0009 (Collection via tracking).
- **Defence layers:** Endpoint (EDR + WDAC) → Network (DNS Firewall + IDS/IPS) → Web/Email Gateway → Cloud Sandbox → SIEM + Threat Intel.
- **Real-world examples to mention in exams:** *Fireball (2017)*, *DollarRevenue*, *Gator (Claria)*, *Superfish (Lenovo, 2015)*, *eFast Browser*.
- **Regulatory anchors:** GDPR (EU), CCPA (California), DPDP Act 2023 (India) — all require explicit consent for ad-personalization.
- **Common mistake to AVOID:** Do not call Adware a "virus" — it does **not** self-replicate.
- **One-line exam punch line:** *"Adware monetizes attention and behaviour — it is a PUP, not a virus, but its data-harvesting footprint places it firmly within the KTU Cyber Security threat model."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Keylogger
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST721 - CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Keylogger

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Keylogger (Keystroke Logger / Keyboard Capture):** A *keylogger* is a specialized form of **surveillance technology** (hardware device, software program, or hybrid covert mechanism) engineered to **monotonically record every keystroke** struck on a compromised keyboard, including alphanumeric characters, system shortcuts, and clipboard content, and subsequently **exfiltrate** the captured data stream to an unauthorized third party for the purpose of harvesting credentials, confidential communications, and sensitive personal or organizational data.

In the context of **Cyber Security (OECST721)**, a keylogger is formally classified under the umbrella of **Spyware** (a sub-category of *Malicious Software / Malware*), and is explicitly recognized in **MITRE ATT&CK Framework** under Technique ID **T1056.001 (Input Capture: Keylogging)** within the *Collection* tactic (TA0009).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme — Module 3 Context:** Keyloggers are studied under the broader theme of **Eavesdropping & Surveillance Attacks** within the *Cyber Security* Open Elective. The board examiner expects students to distinguish between **hardware vs. software keyloggers**, identify **attack vectors**, and propose **defensive countermeasures**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Invisible Stenographer"

Imagine you are dictating your **ATM PIN** to a friend in a crowded café. Sitting two tables away, a **stenographer** silently copies down every number you speak — without you ever noticing. Later, the stenographer walks to your bank and withdraws cash using your PIN.

A **keylogger** is precisely that stenographer, but operating inside your **computer** instead of a café. Every letter you type — your passwords, your private messages, your bank credentials — is *invisibly transcribed* and quietly mailed back to a cybercriminal halfway across the world.

> [!TIP]
> **The Three A's of Keylogging — A Student's Mnemonic:**
> - **A**ttach (deploy the logger on victim machine)
> - **A**ccumulate (silently record keystrokes)
> - **A**bscond (transmit the loot to attacker C2 server)

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Boundary Values

The following standardized metrics govern the **operational performance** and **forensic detection** of keyloggers:

| Metric | Standard Value / Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Keystroke Capture Latency** | **< 5 milliseconds** | Time gap between key press and buffer write |
| **Log File Encryption** | **AES-128 / AES-256** | Industry standard for log obfuscation |
| **Exfiltration Bandwidth** | **< 2 KB/hour** | Stealth threshold to evade IDS |
| **Persistence Mechanism** | **Registry / Kernel Driver / Service** | Survives reboot |
| **Detection Signature Size** | **~ 2–6 MB** | Typical memory footprint of software keylogger |

> [!WARNING]
> A keylogger with **capture latency > 50 ms** is considered operationally compromised, as it may miss rapid keystrokes (e.g., during fast password typing) and is easily detectable through keystroke-timing anomalies.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Taxonomy — The Two Master Classes

A keylogger, irrespective of its implementation, belongs to one of two broad master classes:

### A. Hardware Keyloggers (Physical Devices)
These are **physical devices** intercepting the electrical signal *between the keyboard and the computer*, or capturing keystrokes via **out-of-band channels** (sound, electromagnetic radiation, video).

### B. Software Keyloggers (Programmatic)
These are **malicious software components** running on the victim host, hooking into the **Operating System's input pipeline** to capture keystrokes at various layers of the software stack.

---

## 2.2 Deep Dive — Hardware Keylogger Sub-Types

### 2.2.1 Inline Hardware Keyloggers (USB / PS-2)
A small device physically inserted **between the keyboard cable and the computer's port**. It contains a **non-volatile flash memory chip** (typically **2 MB – 16 MB**) that stores keystrokes in plaintext or lightly obfuscated form.

- **Retrieval:** Attacker physically removes the device.
- **Capacity:** Stores approximately **1 million keystrokes** per 8 MB.

### 2.2.2 Wireless Keyboard Sniffers
Exploit the **27 MHz RF band** or **2.4 GHz Bluetooth** transmission between wireless keyboard and its dongle. Cryptographic weaknesses in older keyboards (e.g., **unscrambled keystrokes** in pre-2009 Microsoft Wireless keyboards) allow attackers with a $30 radio to intercept every keystroke from **up to 75 meters away**.

### 2.2.3 Acoustic / Electromagnetic / Optical Keyloggers
- **Acoustic:** Records the unique *click sounds* produced by each key on the keyboard; uses **machine learning classifiers** to reconstruct typed text (proven feasible by researchers at UC Berkeley — *"Keyboard Acoustic Emanations Revisited"*).
- **Electromagnetic (Van Eck Phreaking):** Captures the **EM emissions** from keyboard cables using a **software-defined radio (SDR)** within a **20-meter** radius.
- **Optical:** Uses a **hidden camera** to film the keyboard and apply **computer vision** for keystroke reconstruction.

---

## 2.3 Deep Dive — Software Keylogger Sub-Types

### 2.3.1 Kernel-Level Keyloggers
Operate at **Ring 0 (Kernel Mode)** by intercepting keyboard data at the **lowest level of the OS input stack** — typically by hooking the **I/O port keyboard interrupt (IRQ 1 / INT 09h)** or installing a **keyboard filter driver**.

- **Stealth Level:** ★★★★★ (Highest)
- **Detection Difficulty:** Requires kernel-level integrity checks.

### 2.3.2 API-Level (User-Mode) Keyloggers
Hook into **Windows API functions** such as `GetAsyncKeyState()`, `GetKeyState()`, `GetKeyboardState()`, or use the `SetWindowsHookEx()` to register a `WH_KEYBOARD_LL` low-level keyboard hook.

- **Stealth Level:** ★★★☆☆ (Moderate)
- **Detection Difficulty:** Detected by most modern anti-virus via hook inspection.

### 2.3.3 Form-Grabbing Keyloggers
Instead of capturing raw keystrokes, they intercept **web form data** *after* the user submits it but *before* it is transmitted over HTTPS — by hooking browser functions like `PR_Read()` and `PR_Write()` in **NSS (Network Security Services)** or `WinINet.dll`.

- **Stealth Level:** ★★★★☆
- **Bypasses:** Most on-screen virtual keyboards and HTTPS encryption.

### 2.3.4 JavaScript-Based / Browser-Injected Keyloggers
Injected via **Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)** attacks into legitimate web pages, capturing keystrokes inside the **browser's sandboxed JavaScript context**. Logged data is **exfiltrated to attacker-controlled domains** via AJAX requests.

---

## 2.4 The Operational Kill Chain (5-Stage Model)

Every keylogger attack follows a structured 5-stage kill chain:

1. **Stage 1 — Delivery:** Infection via phishing email, malicious attachment, drive-by download, or insider physical access.
2. **Stage 2 — Installation:** Persistence established via Registry `Run` keys (`HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run`), scheduled tasks, or boot-sector infection.
3. **Stage 3 — Hooking:** Keyboard input pipeline is intercepted at one or more layers.
4. **Stage 4 — Logging & Encryption:** Keystrokes are buffered, optionally AES-encrypted, and written to a hidden file (e.g., `%AppData%\svchost.log`).
5. **Stage 5 — Exfiltration:** Logs are uploaded to a C2 (Command-and-Control) server via **HTTP POST**, **DNS tunneling**, **email (SMTP)**, or **cloud storage (Dropbox/Google Drive API)**.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula / Concept Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Since Keyloggers are a **conceptual cybersecurity topic**, the following is a **Concept-Equation Cheat Sheet** mapping theoretical relationships — not classical physics/math formulas.

| Concept | Relationship / Equation | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| **Exfiltration Stealth Score (ESS)** | $ESS = \frac{D_{log}}{B_{exfil} \cdot T_{interval}}$ | Higher ESS ⇒ harder to detect |
| **Capture Coverage Ratio (CCR)** | $CCR = \frac{K_{captured}}{K_{typed}} \times 100\%$ | Measures keystroke-loss rate |
| **Hook Layer Position (HLP)** | $HLP = L_{hook} \cdot W_{stealth}$ | $L_{hook}$ = layer index (0=kernel, 3=app) |
| **Detection Probability (Bayes)** | $P(D \mid K) = \frac{P(K \mid D) \cdot P(D)}{P(K)}$ | Bayesian intrusion-detection model |
| **Log File Growth Rate** | $R_{log} = \overline{L_{key}} \cdot F_{typing} \cdot 3600$ | Bytes/hour forecast (where $\overline{L_{key}} \approx 1$ byte) |
| **Persistence Half-Life** | $t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln(2)}{\lambda_{purge}}$ | Time until antivirus quarantine |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition — CCR Theorem:** A *form-grabbing* keylogger achieves $CCR = 100\%$ for web form fields **without ever touching the keyboard hook** — this is the most operationally devastating property and is a high-frequency KTU exam point.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Keylogger technology is **dual-use** in the engineering world:

- **Legitimate Use:** Enterprise **MDM (Mobile Device Management)**, parental monitoring, IT asset diagnostics, **lawful interception** by law enforcement under judicial oversight.
- **Malicious Use:** **Credential theft** (banking trojans like *Zeus*, *Emotet*, *Agent Tesla*), **espionage** (state-sponsored APTs such as *Snake Keylogger* attributed to Russian threat actors), and **identity theft**.

> [!TIP]
> A common KTU board question frames this as: *"Discuss the dual-use nature of keyloggers."* Always answer with the **legitimate-then-malicious** template, citing at least one real-world malware family.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Capture Coverage Ratio (CCR)

> The following step-by-step derivation demonstrates the **theoretical stealth modeling** of a keylogger — a high-yield analytical framework for KTU 14-mark questions.

### 3.1.1 Define the Typing Model

Let the user type $K_{typed}$ total keystrokes within an observation window of duration $T$ seconds. The keylogger captures $K_{captured}$ of these, losing $K_{lost}$ to operational blind spots (e.g., buffer overflow, system idle).

$$
K_{typed} = K_{captured} + K_{lost}
$$

### 3.1.2 Formulate the Capture Ratio

The **Capture Coverage Ratio (CCR)** is the fraction of typed keystrokes successfully recorded:

$$
CCR = \frac{K_{captured}}{K_{typed}} \times 100\%
$$

### 3.1.3 Substitute the Loss Decomposition

Let $K_{lost}$ be decomposed as follows:

$$
K_{lost} = K_{overflow} + K_{idle} + K_{filter}
$$

where:
- $K_{overflow}$ = keystrokes dropped due to buffer exhaustion
- $K_{idle}$ = keystrokes typed when the logger process is suspended
- $K_{filter}$ = keystrokes deliberately excluded (e.g., non-printable Windows keys)

### 3.1.4 Final Master Equation

$$
CCR = \left( 1 - \frac{K_{overflow} + K_{idle} + K_{filter}}{K_{typed}} \right) \times 100\%
$$

**Numerical Example (KTU Board Style):**

Suppose a keylogger experiences a **buffer overflow of 1,200 keystrokes** during a heavy 8-hour session, the OS suspended the logger for **45 seconds** during which the user typed **180 keystrokes**, and the user deliberately hit **3,600 non-printable** keys. Total typed keystrokes = **120,000**.

**Step 1:** Sum the loss components

$$
K_{lost} = 1200 + 180 + 3600 = 4980
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the master equation

$$
CCR = \left( 1 - \frac{4980}{120000} \right) \times 100\%
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the ratio

$$
CCR = (1 - 0.0415) \times 100\% = 95.85\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A CCR below **90%** is considered operationally unusable for credential theft, as critical password fields may be lost. World-class banking trojans maintain **CCR ≥ 99.5%**.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Detection Heuristics vs. Keylogger Type

The following **9-cell analytical matrix** maps the most effective detection heuristics against the five primary keylogger classes — a *high-yield visual aid* for KTU 14-mark comparative questions.

| Detection Heuristic ↓ \ Keylogger Type → | **Kernel** | **API Hook** | **Form-Grabber** | **JS/XSS** | **Hardware** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Antivirus Signature Scan** | ★☆☆ | ★★★★ | ★★★☆ | ★★☆ | ✕ |
| **Hook-API Inspection** | ✕ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ✕ | ✕ |
| **Registry Run-Key Audit** | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★☆ | ★★ | ✕ |
| **Outbound Traffic DPI** | ★★ | ★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ✕ |
| **Kernel Driver Signing Check** | ★★★★★ | ✕ | ✕ | ✕ | ✕ |
| **Physical Port Inspection** | ✕ | ✕ | ✕ | ✕ | ★★★★★ |
| **EM / RF Spectrum Scan** | ✕ | ✕ | ✕ | ✕ | ★★★★☆ |
| **Browser CSP / XSS Filter** | ✕ | ✕ | ★★ | ★★★★★ | ✕ |
| **Behavioral Anomaly (Keystroke Timing)** | ★★ | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★ | ✕ |

**Legend:** ★★★★★ = highly effective | ✕ = ineffective

---

## 3.3 Analytical Workflow — Defensive Countermeasure Derivation

The **defense-in-depth strategy** is derived from the kill-chain stages as follows:

$$
\text{Effectiveness}_{defense} = \prod_{i=1}^{5} (1 - P_{defeat,i})
$$

where $P_{defeat,i}$ is the probability that the defense at stage $i$ fails to block the keylogger.

For a layered defense where each stage independently blocks with 80% probability:

$$
\text{Effectiveness} = (1 - 0.20)^5 = 0.80^5 = 0.3277
$$

> [!WARNING]
> This figure (**~33%**) illustrates the **multiplicative weakness** of a defense chain — each stage must be solid. KTU board questions often ask students to *justify* the defense-in-depth principle with this exact reasoning.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
