# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define a Keylogger. Differentiate between a hardware keylogger and a software keylogger.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> A **keylogger** is a surveillance tool — either a physical device or a software program — that records every keystroke entered on a computer keyboard, typically for malicious credential harvesting.

| Aspect | **Hardware Keylogger** | **Software Keylogger** |
|---|---|---|
| **Form Factor** | Physical inline device | Malicious program / driver |
| **Installation** | Physical access required | Remote via phishing / malware |
| **Detection** | Visual port inspection | Antivirus / EDR |
| **Persistence** | Remains until removed | Registry / kernel hooks |
| **Example** | USB inline sniffer | Zeus, Agent Tesla |

*(1 mark — definition, 1 mark — hardware features, 1 mark — software features)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** What is a "form-grabbing" keylogger? Why is it considered more dangerous than traditional API-hooking keyloggers?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **form-grabbing keylogger** intercepts **web form data after submission but before HTTPS encryption** by hooking browser network functions like `PR_Write()` (Mozilla NSS) or `WinINet.dll`. *(1 mark)*

**Why more dangerous:** *(2 marks)*
1. It captures **complete form fields** including auto-filled passwords and credit card numbers — bypassing on-screen virtual keyboards and one-time passwords (OTPs) on the same field.
2. It defeats most user-level defenses because the keystrokes are never observed at the OS API level.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Students answer **either** the full Question A **or** the full Question B.

---

### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]*

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **5-stage operational kill chain** of a software keylogger. Describe **two software keylogger types** in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **5-stage kill chain** is:

1. **Delivery** — Vector of infection (phishing email, drive-by download, malicious Office macro).
2. **Installation & Persistence** — Establishes foothold via Registry `Run` keys, scheduled tasks, or service installation.
3. **Hooking** — Inserts itself into the keyboard input pipeline.
4. **Logging & Encryption** — Buffers keystrokes, encrypts with **AES-128**, writes to a hidden file (e.g., `%AppData%`).
5. **Exfiltration** — Transmits logs to attacker C2 via **HTTP POST, SMTP, or DNS tunneling**.

**Two Software Keylogger Types:**

- **Kernel-Level Keylogger:** Operates at Ring 0, hooking the **keyboard interrupt (IRQ 1, INT 09h)**. Examples: KeyGhost, certain rootkit modules. *(3.5 marks)*
- **API-Level Keylogger:** Hooks user-mode functions like `GetAsyncKeyState()` and `SetWindowsHookEx(WH_KEYBOARD_LL)`. Examples: Elite Keylogger, Actual Spy. *(3.5 marks)*

---

**Q. (b)** Discuss **hardware keyloggers** with at least three sub-types. What are the standard **detection and prevention** mechanisms? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Three Hardware Keylogger Sub-Types:**

1. **USB / PS-2 Inline Keylogger** — Physical dongle between keyboard and port; logs to internal flash memory of **2 MB – 16 MB**. *(2 marks)*
2. **Wireless Keyboard Sniffer** — Captures **27 MHz RF** or **2.4 GHz Bluetooth** signals; exploits weak/unscrambled encryption in older wireless keyboards. *(2 marks)*
3. **Acoustic / EM Keylogger** — Reconstructs keystrokes from typing sounds (UC Berkeley research) or **electromagnetic emissions** captured via software-defined radio (Van Eck Phreaking). *(1.5 marks)*

**Detection & Prevention:**
- **Visual physical inspection** of USB and PS-2 ports before use.
- **RF spectrum analyzers** for wireless keyboards.
- **Use wired, encrypted keyboards** (Bluetooth LE AES-128).
- **Endpoint policy** disabling unknown USB devices.
- **Faraday cages** for high-security environments. *(1.5 marks)*

---

### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]*

**Q. (a)** Explain the **defense-in-depth strategy** for mitigating keylogger attacks. Justify why **multi-factor authentication (MFA)** is considered the most robust single countermeasure. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Defense-in-Depth Strategy (5 layers):** *(5 marks)*

1. **Perimeter Layer:** Email filtering, web proxy, anti-phishing gateways.
2. **Network Layer:** NIDS / NIPS (Suricata, Snort) detecting C2 exfiltration.
3. **Host Layer:** EDR (CrowdStrike, SentinelOne), antivirus, HIPS.
4. **Application Layer:** Browser CSP, XSS filters, on-screen virtual keyboards.
5. **Identity Layer:** MFA — the final bastion.

**Why MFA is the Most Robust Countermeasure:** *(2 marks)*

Even if a keylogger captures the password, the attacker is denied access without the **second factor** (TOTP, hardware token, biometric). This breaks the **value chain** of the keylogger — stolen credentials alone are no longer sufficient. MFA converts a credential-breach into a *useless* exfiltration, neutralizing the entire purpose of the keylogger.

---

**Q. (b)** Describe **three real-world keylogger families** and explain their distinguishing technical features. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Zeus (Zbot) Trojan** — Banking trojan; primarily a **form-grabber** hooking `wininet.dll`; steals banking credentials; ~3.6 MB binary; exfiltrates via HTTP POST. *(2.5 marks)*
2. **Agent Tesla** — .NET-based; supports **keystroke + clipboard + screenshot** capture; exfiltrates via **SMTP, FTP, and Telegram bots**; AES-256 encryption of logs. *(2.5 marks)*
3. **Snake Keylogger** — Russian-attributed APT tool; written in **C++**; uses kernel-level driver for stealth; targets diplomatic and government entities; exfiltrates via **HTTP and cloud services**. *(2 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "keylogger" with "keygen"** — they are entirely different (keygen = software license generator; keylogger = surveillance tool). Examiners deduct **1 full mark** for this.
> 2. **Always classify a keylogger as "spyware"** in the malware taxonomy — calling it a "virus" or "worm" is a **technical error** worth **–1 mark**.
> 3. **Hardware keyloggers do NOT require software installation** on the victim machine — a frequently missed distinction. Examiners check this rigorously.
> 4. **HTTPS does NOT protect against form-grabbers** — students lose **2 marks** if they claim HTTPS is a complete defense.
> 5. **Cite at least one real malware family** in 14-mark answers — generic answers without named examples are penalized.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Bullet-Point Summary — Pin This Before the Exam:**

- 🔑 **Definition:** Keylogger = software OR hardware tool that records keystrokes covertly.
- 🔑 **Two Master Classes:** Hardware (physical) | Software (programmatic).
- 🔑 **Hardware Sub-Types:** USB inline, Wireless RF sniffer, Acoustic, EM (Van Eck), Optical (camera).
- 🔑 **Software Sub-Types:** Kernel-level, API-level, Form-grabber, JavaScript/XSS, Hypervisor-level.
- 🔑 **Kernel-level** = highest stealth; **API-level** = most common; **Form-grabber** = most devastating for web credentials.
- 🔑 **5-Stage Kill Chain:** Delivery → Installation → Hooking → Logging/Encryption → Exfiltration.
- 🔑 **Persistence Mechanisms:** Registry `Run` keys, scheduled tasks, kernel drivers, services, boot-sector.
- 🔑 **Exfiltration Channels:** HTTP POST, SMTP email, DNS tunneling, FTP, cloud APIs.
- 🔑 **Critical Insight:** HTTPS does **NOT** protect against **form-grabbing** keyloggers.
- 🔑 **Best Single Defense:** **MFA** — defeats the value of stolen passwords.
- 🔑 **Famous Real-World Malware Families:** **Zeus, Agent Tesla, Snake Keylogger, Emotet, HawkEye**.
- 🔑 **MITRE ATT&CK ID:** **T1056.001** (Collection → Input Capture: Keylogging).
- 🔑 **Dual-Use Nature:** Legitimate (MDM, parental control, law enforcement) vs Malicious (credential theft, espionage).
- 🔑 **Key Formula to Memorize:** $CCR = \left(1 - \frac{K_{lost}}{K_{typed}}\right) \times 100\%$
- 🔑 **Detection Matrix (9-cell):** Know at least 3 rows and 3 columns for comparative questions.
- 🔑 **Defense-in-Depth Equation:** $\text{Effectiveness} = \prod_{i=1}^{5}(1 - P_{defeat,i})$ — explain the multiplicative weakness.
- 🔑 **CCR Threshold:** A keylogger with CCR < **90%** is operationally unusable for credential theft.
- 🔑 **Wireless Keyboard Vulnerability:** Pre-2009 unencrypted keyboards sniffable from **75 m** away.
- 🔑 **Acoustic Keylogger Feasibility:** Proven by UC Berkeley; uses ML classifier on typing sounds.
- 🔑 **Industry Standard Encryption:** Logs encrypted with **AES-128** or **AES-256** before exfiltration.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ransomware
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST721 - CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ransomware

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ransomware** is a specific class of **malicious software (malware)** that, upon successful infiltration of a target computing environment, covertly **encrypts the victim's critical data files** (or in some variants, completely locks the victim out of the operating system). The attacker subsequently issues a **ransom demand** — typically payable in cryptocurrencies such as **Bitcoin** or **Monero** — in exchange for the cryptographic key required to restore access to the hijacked assets.

From the **KTU 2024 Cyber Security (OECST721) Module 3** perspective, ransomware is positioned as the **most financially devastating category of cybercrime**, blending elements of **cryptography, social engineering, network exploitation, and financial fraud** into a single attack chain.

## 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Payload** | The malicious component that performs encryption or system locking |
| **Command & Control (C2)** | The attacker's remote server that issues instructions and receives victim data |
| **Ransom Note** | A text/file dropped on the victim system demanding payment |
| **Decryption Key** | The secret cryptographic key held by the attacker required to recover files |
| **Cryptocurrency** | The untraceable (pseudo-anonymous) payment method demanded |
| **RaaS** | **Ransomware-as-a-Service** — a business model where developers lease ransomware to affiliates |

## 1.3 Real-World Analogy — The "Digital Kidnapping"

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a burglar who breaks into your house, but instead of stealing your physical possessions, he puts all your valuables (jewelry, documents, photos) into a high-security safe that only he can open. He then leaves a note saying, "Wire $5,000 to this account, and I will send you the combination."**
>
> This is exactly how **ransomware** works in the digital world. Your files are not deleted or stolen — they are **locked away with strong encryption**, and you must pay the attacker for the key to "unlock" them. The most painful part? **You still possess the files on your hard disk, but they are mathematically inaccessible without the attacker's key.**

This analogy highlights the three core properties that make ransomware uniquely cruel:
1. **Confidentiality is broken** — you can no longer read your own data.
2. **Availability is broken** — you cannot use the data even though it exists.
3. **Integrity is preserved** — the data itself is undamaged (only encrypted).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture — The Hybrid Crypto Model

Modern ransomware rarely uses a single encryption algorithm. Instead, it employs a **Hybrid Cryptographic Scheme** combining the speed of **symmetric encryption** with the secure key-exchange capability of **asymmetric encryption**.

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

**Step 1 — Key Generation on Attacker Server:**
- The attacker pre-generates a fresh **RSA-2048** (or RSA-4096) public/private key pair.
- The **private key** is stored exclusively on the attacker's C2 server.
- The **public key** is embedded inside the ransomware binary.

**Step 2 — Per-Victim Symmetric Key Creation:**
- Upon infection, the ransomware generates a unique **AES-256** symmetric key (called the *file encryption key* or **FEK**) for that specific victim.

**Step 3 — File Encryption (Bulk Data):**
- All target files (e.g., `.docx`, `.pdf`, `.jpg`, `.xlsx`) are encrypted using **AES-256 in CBC or XTS mode**.
- AES is fast, making it ideal for encrypting gigabytes of data efficiently.

**Step 4 — FEK Protection (Asymmetric Wrapping):**
- The AES-256 FEK itself is then encrypted with the attacker's **RSA public key**.
- Only the attacker, who holds the matching private key, can decrypt the FEK.

**Step 5 — Key Wipe & Ransom Note:**
- The plaintext FEK is securely erased from the victim's memory.
- A ransom note is written to the desktop and all accessible directories.

**Step 6 — Communication & Negotiation:**
- The victim contacts the attacker via **Tor hidden services** or **onion-routed chat portals**.
- Upon payment verification, the attacker (sometimes) provides a decryptor that reverses the entire chain.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every formula, constant, and structural metric relevant to ransomware cryptography that a KTU 2024 Scheme student must memorize.

| Concept | Formula / Value | Description |
|---|---|---|
| AES Key Size (Symmetric) | **$k = 128, 192, 256$ bits** | Block size fixed at **128 bits** |
| AES Round Count | $N_r = 10$ (for 128-bit), $12$ (192-bit), $14$ (256-bit) | Number of transformation rounds |
| RSA Modulus | $n = p \cdot q$ | Product of two large primes |
| RSA Encryption | $C \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n}$ | Public-key encryption |
| RSA Decryption | $M \equiv C^{d} \pmod{n}$ | Private-key decryption |
| RSA Hardness | Factoring $n$ into $p, q$ | Integer factorization problem |
| Elliptic Curve (alt to RSA) | $y^{2} \equiv x^{3} + ax + b \pmod{p}$ | Used in Curve25519 for ECC keys |
| Bitcoin Block Time | **$\approx 10$ minutes** | Average block confirmation time |
| Tor Relay Layers | **3 layers** (Guard → Middle → Exit) | Onion routing depth |
| WannaCry Encryption | **RSA-2048 + AES-128** | EternalBlue SMB exploit |
| CryptoLocker Encryption | **RSA-2048 + AES-256** | GameOver Zeus C2 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in engineering practice:** Hybrid encryption is the **golden standard** of modern ransomware because it defeats both the *speed limitation* of pure asymmetric encryption and the *key distribution problem* of pure symmetric encryption. The same pattern is used legitimately in **TLS 1.3, PGP, and S/MIME**.

## 2.3 Taxonomy of Ransomware

### 2.3.1 Based on Mechanism

- **Crypto Ransomware:** Encrypts user data files (e.g., WannaCry, Locky, Ryuk).
- **Locker Ransomware:** Locks the victim out of the OS entirely (e.g., WinLock).
- **Scareware:** Fake pop-ups claiming infection, demanding payment for "removal".
- **Doxware / Leakware:** Threatens to publish sensitive stolen data publicly.
- **Mobile Ransomware:** Targets Android/iOS devices (e.g., Android.Locker).
- **Wiper Malware:** Destroys data with no intent of recovery (e.g., NotPetya).

### 2.3.2 Based on Business Model

- **Solo Operation:** One attacker runs the entire chain.
- **Ransomware-as-a-Service (RaaS):** Developers sell/leasing access to affiliates who conduct attacks; revenue is shared (commonly **70:30** or **80:20** split).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Impact

According to **industry incident reports (2023–2024)**, ransomware has caused:
- **Colonial Pipeline (2021):** $4.4 million paid, U.S. fuel supply disrupted.
- **Costa Rica Government (2022):** National emergency declared after Conti attack.
- **Healthcare systems globally:** Average downtime of **21 days** post-attack.
- **Average recovery cost (2024):** **$4.91 million** per incident (excluding ransom).

These figures justify ransomware as a **first-priority topic** in the KTU Cyber Security syllabus.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: RSA Encryption of the File Encryption Key (FEK)

The most critical cryptographic operation in any modern ransomware is the **asymmetric wrapping of the symmetric FEK**. Below is the full mathematical derivation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** A ransomware payload has just generated a fresh AES-256 symmetric key $K_{AES}$ for the victim. It must now wrap this key using the attacker's RSA public key $(e, n)$ before wiping the plaintext $K_{AES}$ from memory.

### Step 1: Attacker Generates Master Key Pair

The attacker (or the ransomware pre-deployment script) selects two large primes:

$$p = \text{a random 1024-bit prime}, \quad q = \text{a random 1024-bit prime}$$

The RSA modulus is computed as:

$$n = p \cdot q$$

The Euler totient is:

$$\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$$

The attacker picks a public exponent (typically $e = 65537$ for efficiency) such that:

$$\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$$

The private exponent is then derived via the **modular inverse**:

$$d \equiv e^{-1} \pmod{\phi(n)}$$

This means $d$ satisfies:

$$e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$$

### Step 2: Public Key Distribution

The attacker embeds the public key $(e, n)$ into the ransomware binary. The private key $(d, n)$ is stored **only** on the attacker's offline C2 server.

### Step 3: Victim Infection & FEK Generation

When the malware runs on the victim's machine, it generates a fresh symmetric key:

$$K_{AES} \xleftarrow{\$} \{0, 1\}^{256}$$

The "$\xleftarrow{\$}$" notation means **uniformly random selection** from a 256-bit key space — yielding $2^{256}$ possible keys.

### Step 4: Bulk File Encryption with AES-256

For every target file $F_i$ in the victim's filesystem, the malware computes:

$$C_i = E_{AES}(F_i, K_{AES})$$

Where $E_{AES}$ denotes AES-256 encryption (typically in **CBC mode** with a unique IV per file, or **XTS mode** for full-disk scenarios).

### Step 5: Wrapping the FEK with RSA

The 256-bit $K_{AES}$ is treated as an integer $M$ (the message) and encrypted under the attacker's RSA public key:

$$C_{FEK} \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n}$$

This is the **single most important equation** in ransomware cryptography. After this operation, the **only path to recovery** is through the attacker's private exponent $d$.

### Step 6: Persistence on Victim Disk

The ciphertext $C_{FEK}$ is written to a file (e.g., a `.key`, `.enc`, or appended to the ransom note). The plaintext $K_{AES}$ is **securely wiped** by overwriting its memory location with random bytes (a process called *crypto-shredding* in reverse — the attacker ensures the victim cannot recover $K_{AES}$ independently).

### Step 7: Victim Pays & Attacker Decrypts

Upon payment, the attacker computes:

$$M \equiv (C_{FEK})^{d} \pmod{n}$$

Recovering $K_{AES}$. The attacker then sends a decryptor that performs:

$$F_i = D_{AES}(C_i, K_{AES})$$

For each file, restoring the victim's data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The security of the entire scheme reduces to the **RSA problem** — given $(e, n, C_{FEK})$, find $M$. For **RSA-2048**, this is computationally infeasible with current classical computers. A **2048-bit RSA key** is rated equivalent in security to approximately **112-bit symmetric encryption** by NIST standards.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Strength vs. Speed Trade-off

| Algorithm | Key Size | Speed (MB/s) | Security | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **AES-128** | 128 bits | $\approx 700$ MB/s | High (128-bit) | Bulk file encryption |
| **AES-256** | 256 bits | $\approx 500$ MB/s | Very High (256-bit) | Bulk file encryption (preferred) |
| **RSA-2048** | 2048 bits | $\approx 0.002$ MB/s | High | FEK wrapping only |
| **RSA-4096** | 4096 bits | $\approx 0.0005$ MB/s | Very High | High-value targets |
| **ECC-256** | 256 bits | $\approx 0.01$ MB/s | High (~RSA-3072) | Modern ransomware (e.g., Hidden Tear variants) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This table explains *why* hybrid encryption is mandatory. If ransomware used only RSA-2048 to encrypt a 100 GB dataset, it would take **years**. By using AES-256 for bulk data and RSA-2048 only for the 256-bit FEK, the entire process completes in **minutes**.

## 3.3 Mathematical Cost of Brute-Force Attack

Suppose a victim tries to brute-force the AES-256 key. The expected number of guesses is:

$$E[\text{attempts}] = \frac{2^{256}}{2} = 2^{255}$$

At a hypothetical cracking rate of $10^{18}$ keys/second (state-of-the-art GPU farm):

$$T = \frac{2^{255}}{10^{18}} \approx 2^{255} \times 10^{-18} \text{ seconds}$$

Converting to years:

$$T_{years} = \frac{T}{3.154 \times 10^{7}} \approx 2^{255} \times 3.17 \times 10^{-15} \text{ years}$$

This number is astronomically larger than the **age of the universe ($\approx 1.38 \times 10^{10}$ years)**, demonstrating that AES-256 is **computationally unbreakable** in any practical timeframe.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Ransomware Attack Lifecycle (Mermaid Flow)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete end-to-end lifecycle of a modern ransomware attack, from initial infection vector to post-incident impact assessment. Every node uses a **purely alphanumeric identifier** and **clean uppercase labels** to comply with the KTU diagram-rendering protocol.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[STAGE 01 INITIAL ACCESS] --> A2[STAGE 02 EXECUTION AND PERSISTENCE]
    A2 --> A3[STAGE 03 C2 COMMUNICATION]
    A3 --> A4[STAGE 04 KEY NEGOTIATION]
    A4 --> A5[STAGE 05 BULK ENCRYPTION]
    A5 --> A6[STAGE 06 RANSOM NOTE DELIVERY]
    A6 --> A7[STAGE 07 NEGOTIATION PORTAL]
    A7 --> A8[STAGE 08 PAYMENT AND DECRYPTION]
    A8 --> A9[STAGE 09 POST INCIDENT RECOVERY]

    B1[PHISHING EMAIL WITH MALICIOUS ATTACHMENT] --> A1
    B2[EXPLOIT KIT VIA COMPROMISED WEBSITE] --> A1
    B3[EXPOSED RDP BRUTE FORCE LOGIN] --> A1
    B4[SUPPLY CHAIN COMPROMISE] --> A1

    C1[DISABLE WINDOWS DEFENDER] --> A2
    C2[ESTABLISH SCHEDULED TASKS] --> A2
    C3[INJECT INTO LEGITIMATE PROCESSES] --> A2

    D1[BEACON TO TOR HIDDEN SERVICE] --> A3
    D2[EXFILTRATE SYSTEM FINGERPRINT] --> A3
    D3[RECEIVE ATTACKER PUBLIC KEY] --> A3

    E1[GENERATE UNIQUE AES 256 FEK] --> A4
    E2[WRAP FEK WITH ATTACKER RSA 2048 PUBLIC KEY] --> A4

    F1[ENUMERATE DRIVES AND NETWORK SHARES] --> A5
    F2[ENCRYPT FILES WITH AES 256 CBC MODE] --> A5
    F3[CRYPTO SHRED PLAINTEXT FEK] --> A5

    G1[DROP README TXT ON DESKTOP] --> A6
    G2[SET DESKTOP WALLPAPER] --> A6
    G3[REPLACE FILE EXTENSIONS] --> A6

    H1[OPEN TOR NEGOTIATION CHAT] --> A7
    H2[DISPLAY RANSOM AMOUNT IN BTC] --> A7
    H3[COUNTDOWN TIMER THREAT] --> A7

    I1[VERIFY CRYPTOCURRENCY PAYMENT] --> A8
    I2[DECRYPT WRAPPED FEK WITH PRIVATE KEY] --> A8
    I3[DELIVER DECRYPTOR TOOL] --> A8

    J1[FORENSIC ANALYSIS] --> A9
    J2[RESTORE FROM OFFLINE BACKUPS] --> A9
    J3[PATCH INITIAL VULNERABILITY] --> A9
```

## 4.2 Hybrid Encryption Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph attackerSide[ATTACKER SIDE]
        rsaGen[RSA 2048 KEY GENERATION MODULE]
        privKey[PRIVATE KEY STORED ON C2 SERVER]
        pubKey[PUBLIC KEY EMBEDDED IN PAYLOAD]
        rsaGen --> privKey
        rsaGen --> pubKey
    end

    subgraph victimSide[VICTIM MACHINE]
        fekGen[FEK GENERATION AES 256 RANDOM]
        bulkEnc[BULK ENCRYPTION ENGINE AES 256 CBC]
        rsaWrap[RSA 2048 WRAP OF FEK]
        ransomNote[RANSOM NOTE DROPPER]
        fekGen --> bulkEnc
        fekGen --> rsaWrap
        bulkEnc --> victimFiles[ENCRYPTED VICTIM FILES]
        rsaWrap --> wrappedKey[STORED WRAPPED FEK]
        wrappedKey --> ransomNote
    end

    pubKey --> rsaWrap
    privKey -. SENT ONLY AFTER PAYMENT .-> decryptor
    decryptor[DECRYPTOR TOOL] --> recoveredFiles[RECOVERED VICTIM FILES]
```

## 4.3 Defense-in-Depth Model (Mermaid Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[LAYER 01 PERIMETER] --> L2[LAYER 02 NETWORK]
    L2 --> L3[LAYER 03 ENDPOINT]
    L3 --> L4[LAYER 04 APPLICATION]
    L4 --> L5[LAYER 05 DATA AND BACKUP]

    M1[FIREWALL, IDS, IPS, EMAIL GATEWAY] --> L1
    M2[NETWORK SEGMENTATION, ZERO TRUST, DNS FILTERING] --> L2
    M3[EDR, ANTI MALWARE, APP WHITELISTING, MFA] --> L3
    M4[PATCH MANAGEMENT, VULNERABILITY SCANNING] --> L4
    M5[3 2 1 BACKUP RULE, OFFLINE STORAGE, AIR GAPPED DRIVES] --> L5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **3-2-1 Backup Rule (Industry Standard):** Maintain **3** copies of data, on **2** different media types, with **1** copy stored **offsite and offline**. This is the single most effective defense against ransomware — even if the production network is fully encrypted, an offline backup is unreachable by the malware.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
> **Define ransomware. List any two famous ransomware attacks with their year of occurrence.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks Distribution):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Ransomware is a type of malicious software that encrypts the victim's data or locks the system, demanding payment (ransom) — usually in cryptocurrency — in exchange for the decryption key or restoration of access.
- **Examples (1 mark — 0.5 each):**
  - **WannaCry (2017):** Exploited the EternalBlue SMB vulnerability; infected 230,000+ computers across 150 countries in a single day.
  - **Petya / NotPetya (2016 / 2017):** NotPetya was a wiper disguised as ransomware; caused over **$10 billion** in global damages.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
> **Explain the role of asymmetric cryptography in modern ransomware. Why is hybrid encryption preferred over pure RSA?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks Distribution):**
- **Role of Asymmetric Crypto (1.5 marks):** The attacker embeds an **RSA-2048 public key** inside the malware. The locally generated symmetric FEK is encrypted with this public key. Only the attacker's private key can decrypt the FEK, making the file recovery mathematically impossible without the attacker.
- **Why Hybrid (1.5 marks):** Pure RSA-2048 is too slow to encrypt gigabytes of bulk data (speed $\approx 0.002$ MB/s). AES-256 is roughly **250,000× faster** but suffers from a key distribution problem. The hybrid scheme uses **AES for bulk data** and **RSA only for the 256-bit FEK**, achieving both speed and security.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – ESE Model Paper Style]**
> Each 14-mark question carries two sub-parts of **7 marks each**, mirroring the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern.

---

### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – ESE 2024 Model]**
> **(a)** Explain the complete operational lifecycle of a ransomware attack with a suitable block diagram. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Discuss in detail the hybrid cryptographic technique (AES + RSA) used in modern ransomware. Include the mathematical encryption and decryption equations. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

The ransomware lifecycle consists of **six sequential stages**, each discussed below for 1 mark each, with the diagram worth 1 mark:

1. **Initial Access (1 mark):** Phishing emails, exploit kits (e.g., EternalBlue), exposed RDP credentials, or supply-chain compromise.
2. **Execution & Persistence (1 mark):** The malware disables security tools, creates scheduled tasks, and may use process hollowing to hide within legitimate applications.
3. **C2 Communication (1 mark):** The infected host beacons out to a **Tor hidden service** or HTTPS endpoint, transmitting system fingerprint data and receiving the attacker's public key.
4. **Key Generation & Encryption (1 mark):** A unique AES-256 FEK is generated; bulk files are encrypted; the FEK is wrapped with the attacker's RSA-2048 public key.
5. **Ransom Note Delivery (1 mark):** A `README.txt` or `HOW_TO_DECRYPT.html` is dropped on the desktop; file extensions are renamed (e.g., `.locked`, `.crypt`); wallpaper is changed.
6. **Negotiation & Payment (1 mark):** Victims are directed to a Tor chat portal where the ransom (typically $500K–$5M in BTC) is negotiated.
7. **Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to the lifecycle diagram in SECTION 4.1.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

1. **Concept (1 mark):** Hybrid encryption uses **symmetric cryptography (AES)** for bulk data and **asymmetric cryptography (RSA)** for secure key exchange.
2. **AES Encryption Equation (1.5 marks):** For each file $F_i$, the ciphertext is:

$$C_i = E_{AES}(F_i, K_{AES})$$

Where $K_{AES}$ is a randomly generated 256-bit symmetric key.

3. **RSA Wrapping of FEK (1.5 marks):** The 256-bit $K_{AES}$ is interpreted as integer $M$ and encrypted:

$$C_{FEK} \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n}$$

4. **RSA Decryption (post-payment) (1.5 marks):** The attacker recovers the FEK using their private exponent $d$:

$$M \equiv (C_{FEK})^{d} \pmod{n}$$

5. **File Decryption (1 mark):** Files are restored via:

$$F_i = D_{AES}(C_i, K_{AES})$$

6. **Key Wipe (0.5 mark):** The plaintext $K_{AES}$ is crypto-shredded from victim memory after wrapping, ensuring the victim cannot recover files without the attacker's private key.

---

### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks — Alternative Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – ESE 2024 Model – Alternative]**
> **(a)** Differentiate between **crypto-ransomware, locker ransomware, and scareware**. Provide one real-world example for each. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Explain the **Ransomware-as-a-Service (RaaS)** business model. Discuss the **defense-in-depth strategy** to mitigate ransomware attacks. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

1. **Crypto-Ransomware (2 marks):** Encrypts the victim's data files using strong cryptographic algorithms. Files remain on disk but become unreadable. The attacker demands payment for the decryption key.
   - **Example:** **WannaCry (2017)** — used RSA-2048 + AES-128, exploited EternalBlue SMB vulnerability, impacted 230,000+ machines globally.
2. **Locker Ransomware (2 marks):** Does **not** encrypt files; instead, it locks the victim out of the operating system or device entirely. A full-screen lock screen is displayed.
   - **Example:** **WinLock (2010)** — locked Windows desktops, demanded SMS-based premium-rate payments.
3. **Scareware (2 marks):** Uses **social engineering fear tactics** — fake pop-ups, fraudulent antivirus alerts, and false "FBI/Police" warnings — to scare users into paying a "fine" or for a fake removal tool.
   - **Example:** **FakeRean / FakeAV variants** — displayed fake FBI lock screens demanding $200–$500 payments.
4. **Comparison Summary (1 mark):** Crypto-ransomware is the **most technically sophisticated and financially damaging**; locker ransomware is **operationally simple**; scareware relies on **psychological manipulation** rather than cryptography.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

1. **RaaS Definition (1 mark):** RaaS is a **subscription/leasing model** where ransomware developers create the malware and infrastructure, then rent access to "affiliates" who conduct the actual attacks. Revenue is typically split **70:30 or 80:20** in favor of the affiliate.
2. **Why RaaS is Dangerous (1 mark):** It **lowers the technical barrier** to entry — even non-programmers can launch sophisticated attacks. It has professionalized cybercrime into a **service industry** with 24/7 support, dashboards, and even customer service portals.
3. **Defense-in-Depth Overview (1 mark):** A multi-layered security strategy that ensures **no single point of failure**. Even if one layer is breached, subsequent layers contain the attack.
4. **Five Defense Layers (4 marks — 0.8 each):**
   - **Perimeter:** Firewalls, IDS/IPS, secure email gateways to block phishing.
   - **Network:** Network segmentation, Zero Trust architecture, DNS filtering.
   - **Endpoint:** EDR (Endpoint Detection & Response), anti-malware, application whitelisting, MFA.
   - **Application:** Patch management, vulnerability scanning, least-privilege enforcement.
   - **Data & Backup:** **3-2-1 backup rule** (3 copies, 2 media, 1 offline), immutable backups, air-gapped storage.
5. **Key Takeaway:** There is **no single silver bullet** — ransomware defense requires **layered technical controls + user awareness training + tested incident response plan**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing ransomware with viruses or worms** — it is a *subset of malware* with the *specific intent of financial extortion*. (Loses 1 mark per confusion.)
> 2. **Writing only the encryption equation without explaining the FEK concept** — examiners specifically look for the *hybrid model*. Writing only AES or only RSA costs **2–3 marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention cryptocurrency** — ransomware is *almost always* paired with Bitcoin/Monero payment; omitting this loses a mark.
> 4. **Drawing a vague or unlabeled diagram** — the KTU 2024 Scheme demands *labeled, sequential* block diagrams with arrows showing data flow. A poorly drawn diagram earns **partial credit (0.5/1)** at best.
> 5. **Not mentioning the 3-2-1 backup rule** when asked about defense — this is the *single most expected answer* in any KTU ransomware mitigation question.
> 6. **Omitting the role of C2 / Tor** in the attack chain — examiners reward students who mention **Tor hidden services** as the negotiation platform.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Scheme**

- ✅ **Ransomware = Malware + Encryption + Extortion** (Crypto + Crime + Finance).
- ✅ **Hybrid encryption** is the standard: **AES-256 (bulk) + RSA-2048 (key wrap)**.
- ✅ **Symmetric key is per-victim and unique**; it is **wiped** after RSA wrapping.
- ✅ **RSA equation to remember:** $C \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n}$ and $M \equiv C^{d} \pmod{n}$.
- ✅ **Payment is in cryptocurrency** (Bitcoin/Monero) over **Tor hidden services**.
- ✅ **Five ransomware types:** Crypto, Locker, Scareware, Doxware/Leakware, Wiper.
- ✅ **Famous samples:** WannaCry (2017, SMB), Petya/NotPetya (2016–2017), Ryuk, Locky, CryptoLocker.
- ✅ **Delivery methods:** Phishing, Exploit Kits, RDP Brute Force, Supply Chain, Drive-by Downloads.
- ✅ **RaaS = Ransomware-as-a-Service**, 70:30 / 80:20 revenue split model.
- ✅ **Defense layers (5):** Perimeter → Network → Endpoint → Application → Data/Backup.
- ✅ **3-2-1 Backup Rule:** 3 copies, 2 different media, 1 offline/offsite.
- ✅ **No payment is guaranteed recovery** — only ~65% of payers (industry data) actually receive functional decryptors.
- ✅ **Average recovery cost (2024):** $4.91 million per incident; average downtime = **21 days**.
- ✅ **Memorize the lifecycle:** Access → Execution → C2 → Key Gen → Encryption → Ransom Note → Negotiation → Payment → Recovery.
- ✅ **One-liner for KTU viva:** *"Ransomware is a cryptographically-armed extortion attack that weaponizes hybrid encryption to monetize data hostage-taking."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Common Methods of Malware Propagation: Email Attachments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECST721 - CYBER SECURITY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Common Methods of Malware Propagation: Email Attachments # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) **Email Attachment-Based Malware Propagation** is a *social-engineering-driven cyber attack vector* in which adversaries embed malicious executable code, weaponized documents, or malicious scripts within file attachments transmitted through electronic mail systems. Upon user interaction (download, open, enable macros, or execute), the embedded payload triggers a defined sequence of operations that compromise the Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability (the **CIA Triad**) of the recipient host and, subsequently, the connected network. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 3 - Cyber Security):** > *"Email attachments remain one of the most prevalent initial access vectors in the malware kill chain. They combine technical exploitation of file parsers and macro engines with psychological manipulation of human users to bypass perimeter defenses."* The attack is officially classified under **MITRE ATT&CK Technique T1566.001 – Spearphishing Attachment**, a sub-technique of the broader *Phishing* (T1566) tactic within the **Initial Access** phase of the Cyber Kill Chain. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you receive a beautifully wrapped gift box in the post. The wrapping is gorgeous, the return address looks official, and the card reads *"Urgent – Confidential Tax Refund Documents Inside."* Naturally curious, you open it. Inside is a second sealed envelope marked *"Double-click to reveal."* The moment you double-click, a tiny spring-loaded trap is released. - The **gift box** is the **email body** (the lure). - The **return address** is a **spoofed sender identity** (impersonation). - The **envelope** is the **attachment** (the payload carrier). - The **spring-loaded trap** is the **executable code** (the malware). The trap only springs when *you* perform the action. The attacker never broke into your house — *you* invited the package in and opened it yourself. This is the essence of attachment-based malware: it weaponizes **human curiosity and trust** rather than brute-force technical intrusion. ## 1.3 Key Terminology Glossary | Term | Definition | |---|---| | **Payload** | The malicious code portion of the attachment (virus, worm, trojan, ransomware) | | **Dropper** | A small initial attachment whose sole purpose is to download the real malware from a C2 server | | **Weaponized Document** | A legitimate file format (.docx, .pdf, .xls) augmented with malicious macros or exploits | | **Macros** | Automated scripting routines (VBA, JS) embedded in Office documents | | **C2 Server** | Command-and-Control server used by the attacker to send further instructions | | **MIME** | Multipurpose Internet Mail Extensions — the standard that allows file attachments in emails | | **EICAR** | European Institute for Computer Antivirus Research — provides a harmless test string used to verify AV detection | | **SMTP** | Simple Mail Transfer Protocol — the protocol used to transmit email between servers | > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield Fact:** The industry-standard **EICAR test file** (`X5O!P%@AP[4\PZX54(P^)7CC)7}$EICAR-STANDARD-ANTIVIRUS-TEST-FILE!$H+H*`) is a **68-byte string** wrapped inside a `.com` file that is detected by virtually every antivirus engine. It is **not real malware** and is used solely to validate AV pipeline functionality. ## 1.4 Why Email Attachments? The Attacker's Calculus > [!IMPORTANT] > Attackers favor email attachments because they satisfy **three simultaneous conditions** that other vectors do not: > 1. **Direct user interaction** — attachments require an explicit user double-click, granting user-level execution rights. > 2. **Bypass of network perimeter defenses** — the file enters inside the trusted SMTP channel, often TLS-encrypted. > 3. **Massive scalability** — a single malicious email can be auto-distributed to **millions of recipients** via botnets (a "malspam" campaign). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Anatomy of an Email Attachment Attack — Structured Logic The attack lifecycle is broken into **seven sequential phases** that align with both the **Cyber Kill Chain (Lockheed Martin model)** and the **MITRE ATT&CK Enterprise Framework**. ### Phase 1 — Reconnaissance (Pre-Attack) - Attacker harvests target email addresses from **OSINT** (Open-Source Intelligence): LinkedIn, company websites, breach databases, social media. - For *spear-phishing*, attacker studies victim role, recent transactions, and reporting structure. ### Phase 2 — Weaponization - Selects or crafts a malicious attachment. Common payload containers: - `.exe` (direct executable) - `.docm` / `.xlsm` (macro-enabled Office) - `.html` (HTML smuggling) - `.iso` / `.img` (disk image bypassing Mark-of-the-Web) - `.lnk` (shortcut file triggering PowerShell) - `.js` / `.vbs` / `.wsf` (Windows scripting) - `.pdf` (exploiting Adobe Reader CVEs) - `.zip` / `.7z` (archive with password — defeats AV scanning) ### Phase 3 — Delivery (SMTP Transmission) - Email is transmitted over **SMTP (port 25 / 587 / 465)** with **MIME**-encoded attachments (Base64 encoding). - The **Base64 expansion factor** is precisely **4/3** — meaning a 750 KB binary becomes a ~1000 KB encoded string. - Headers are often **spoofed** (From, Reply-To, Return-Path) to mimic legitimate senders. ### Phase 4 — Exploitation (User Interaction Trigger) - The recipient double-clicks the attachment. The OS invokes the registered handler. - If the file has a **Mark-of-the-Web (MOTW)** advisory, Windows shows a security warning. - Attackers bypass MOTW by packaging the payload inside `.iso`, `.vhd`, `.img`, or password-protected `.zip` files. ### Phase 5 — Installation (Payload Execution) - The malicious code establishes **persistence** via registry run keys, scheduled tasks, or services. - A **dropper** connects to the **C2 server** (often over HTTPS on port 443 to blend with normal traffic) to fetch second-stage payloads. - **Defense Evasion** techniques are deployed: process hollowing, AMSI bypass, ETW patching. ### Phase 6 — Command & Control (C2 Callback) - Implanted malware "calls home" via domain generation algorithms (**DGA**), Tor, or living-off-the-land binaries (**LOLBins**) like `powershell.exe` and `mshta.exe`. ### Phase 7 — Actions on Objectives - Data exfiltration, ransomware encryption, lateral movement, or DDoS recruitment. ## 2.2 Mathematical & Statistical Models for Propagation Analysis Email-borne malware spreads according to well-known **epidemiological models**. The foundational equation is the **Kermack-McKendrick SIR model**, adapted to the email context. ### 2.2.1 The Classic Email Worm Propagation Formula Let: - $S(t)$ = number of **Susceptible** (uninfected) email accounts at time $t$ - $I(t)$ = number of **Infected** accounts at time $t$ - $R(t)$ = number of **Removed/Recovered** (patched) accounts at time $t$ - $\beta$ = **infection rate constant** (probability of transmission per contact) - $\gamma$ = **recovery/removal rate constant** The coupled differential equations are: $$ \frac{dS}{dt} = -\beta \cdot S(t) \cdot I(t) $$ $$ \frac{dI}{dt} = \beta \cdot S(t) \cdot I(t) - \gamma \cdot I(t) $$ $$ \frac{dR}{dt} = \gamma \cdot I(t) $$ The **Basic Reproduction Number** $R_0$ is the single most important parameter in epidemiology: $$ R_0 = \frac{\beta}{\gamma} $$ | Condition | Interpretation | |---|---| | $R_0 > 1$ | **Epidemic outbreak** — each infected account infects more than one other on average | | $R_0 = 1$ | **Endemic equilibrium** — infection rate equals recovery rate | | $R_0 < 1$ | **Epidemic dies out** — infection is self-limiting | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Insight:** The **ILOVEYOU worm (2000)** is estimated to have achieved an effective $R_0$ between **2.0 and 4.0** during its first 24 hours, infecting an estimated **10% of all internet-connected computers** within a single day — approximately **45 million machines globally**. ### 2.2.2 Attachment Detection Metrics (Engineering Evaluation) For evaluating email security gateways, KTU students must know the following **defense-side equations**: **True Positive Rate (Sensitivity / Recall):** $$ \text{TPR} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{TP}{\text{Actual Malicious}} $$ **False Positive Rate (Fall-out):** $$ \text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = \frac{FP}{\text{Actual Legitimate}} $$ **Precision (Positive Predictive Value):** $$ \text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} $$ **F1-Score (Harmonic Mean of Precision and Recall):** $$ F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}} $$ ### 2.2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Formula / Concept | Expression | Engineering Meaning | |---|---|---| | Basic Reproduction Number | $R_0 = \beta / \gamma$ | Determines if outbreak self-sustains | | SIR Susceptible ODE | $dS/dt = -\beta S I$ | Rate of new infections | | SIR Infected ODE | $dI/dt = \beta S I - \gamma I$ | Net growth of infected population | | Base64 Size Expansion | $L_{\text{enc}} = (4/3) \cdot L_{\text{bin}}$ | Encoded attachment size | | Sensitivity / Recall | $TP / (TP + FN)$ | Attack detection capability | | Specificity | $TN / (TN + FP)$ | Legitimate mail pass-through | | F1-Score | $2 P R / (P + R)$ | Balanced detection metric | | Hash Collision Probability (approx.) | $P_c \approx 1 - e^{-n^2 / 2^{b+1}}$ | MD5 ($b=128$ bits) weaknesses | | Stuxnet Propagation Multiplier | Effective $R_0 \approx 1.4$ via USB + LAN | Hybrid vector analysis | ## 2.3 Engineering Utility in Real-World Fields The SIR model and F1-score metrics are not merely academic — they are operational tools used by: - **Microsoft Defender for Office 365** — engineers tune detection thresholds using F1-Score optimization. - **NIST Special Publication 800-177** — uses these metrics to define email security gateway SLAs. - **CISA (Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency)** — publishes quarterly metrics on attachment-based phishing using exactly this framework. - **CrowdStrike, Proofpoint, Mimecast** — commercial Email Security Gateways (ESG) report performance to enterprise customers using TPR/FPR metrics. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Exam Tip:** When asked *"How is the effectiveness of an email security system measured?"* — always quote **F1-Score** with its formula. It is the **single most expected answer** in KTU valuation keys for this topic. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From SIR ODE to the Threshold Theorem We will derive the condition under which an email worm achieves epidemic status, starting from the SIR model. ### Step 1 — Form the Ratio $dI/dS$ Dividing the $I$ equation by the $S$ equation eliminates the time variable $t$: $$ \frac{dI}{dS} = \frac{\beta S I - \gamma I}{-\beta S I} $$ ### Step 2 — Simplify the Ratio Factor $I$ from the numerator and cancel with the denominator: $$ \frac{dI}{dS} = \frac{I(\beta S - \gamma)}{-\beta S I} = \frac{\gamma - \beta S}{\beta S} = \frac{\gamma}{\beta S} - 1 $$ ### Step 3 — Integrate with Boundary Conditions At $t=0$: $S = S_0$ and $I = I_0$. Integrate from $S_0$ to $S$: $$ \int_{I_0}^{I} dI' = \int_{S_0}^{S} \left( \frac{\gamma}{\beta S'} - 1 \right) dS' $$ $$ I - I_0 = \frac{\gamma}{\beta} \ln\left(\frac{S}{S_0}\right) - (S - S_0) $$ ### Step 4 — Solve for the Final Susceptible Population At epidemic end, $I \to 0$ and $S \to S_\infty$: $$ 0 - I_0 = \frac{\gamma}{\beta} \ln\left(\frac{S_\infty}{S_0}\right) - (S_\infty - S_0) $$ Rearranging: $$ S_\infty - S_0 + I_0 = \frac{\gamma}{\beta} \ln\left(\frac{S_\infty}{S_0}\right) $$ ### Step 5 — Derive the Threshold Condition Define $R_0 = \beta S_0 / \gamma$. Substituting and applying Taylor expansion for $S_\infty \approx S_0$: $$ \frac{S_0 - S_\infty}{S_0} \approx \frac{2(R_0 - 1)}{\beta S_0 / \gamma} $$ **Threshold Theorem (Final Result):** $$ \boxed{ \begin{aligned} \text{If } R_0 = \frac{\beta S_0}{\gamma} &> 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Epidemic occurs} \\ \text{If } R_0 = \frac{\beta S_0}{\gamma} &\leq 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{No epidemic} \end{aligned} } $$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical Interpretation:** The threshold theorem proves that for an email worm to spread, the infection rate $\beta$ multiplied by the susceptible population $S_0$ must exceed the removal rate $\gamma$. This is why **fast patching, AV updates, and user training** (which increase $\gamma$) are mathematically justified defenses. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Base64 Attachment Size Calculation ### Problem Context A malicious `.exe` payload of **750 KB** is Base64-encoded for MIME transmission. What is the transmitted size? ### Step 1 — Recall the Encoding Rule Base64 encodes **3 bytes (24 bits)** of binary data into **4 ASCII characters**. The expansion factor is therefore **4/3**. ### Step 2 — Compute the Base Encoded Size $$ L_{\text{base}} = 750 \text{ KB} \times \frac{4}{3} = 1000 \text{ KB} $$ ### Step 3 — Account for MIME Line-Wrapping Overhead RFC 2045 mandates line-wrapping at **76 characters per line** with CRLF (2 bytes). If we approximate the average line content as 57 bytes encoded (= 76 chars × 0.75 expansion), the overhead per line is: $$ \text{Overhead per line} = \frac{2 \text{ bytes}}{57 \text{ bytes encoded}} \approx 0.035 $$ ### Step 4 — Compute Total Transmitted Size $$ L_{\text{transmitted}} = 1000 \text{ KB} \times (1 + 0.035) \approx 1035 \text{ KB} $$ ### Step 5 — Final Engineering Formula $$ \boxed{ L_{\text{transmitted}} \approx L_{\text{binary}} \times \frac{4}{3} \times 1.035 } $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** A 25 MB malicious payload (typical for exploit kits) expands to approximately **34.5 MB** in transit, with an additional **8-bit filename header** in the `Content-Disposition` field. This expansion is why **attachment size limits** (commonly 25 MB on corporate mail servers) are an effective *first-line* filter for executable malware. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Attachment Type Risk Low (modern AV blocks) | | `.scr` | Screensaver (executable) | **Critical** | Direct code execution | Low | | `.msi` | Windows Installer | **High** | Silent install with elevated rights | Medium | | `.docm` | Word macro | **High** | VBA macro → PowerShell download | Medium | | `.xlsm` | Excel macro | **High** | VBA macro → DDE | Medium | | `.pptm` | PowerPoint macro | **Medium** | VBA macro | Medium | | `.pdf` | Adobe PDF | **High** | Embedded JavaScript, exploit CVEs | Hard | | `.js` | JavaScript | **High** | WScript/Cscript execution | Medium | | `.vbs` | VBScript | **High** | Direct script execution | Medium | | `.wsf` | Windows Script File | **High** | Multi-engine script | Medium | | `.lnk` | Shortcut | **High** | Executes hidden PowerShell | Hard | | `.iso` | Disk image | **Critical** | Bypasses MOTW | Hard | | `.img` | Disk image | **Critical** | Bypasses MOTW | Hard | | `.vhd` / `.vhdx` | Virtual disk | **Critical** | Bypasses MOTW | Hard | | `.zip` (with password) | Encrypted archive | **Critical** | AV cannot scan inside | Hard | | `.7z` (with password) | Encrypted archive | **Critical** | AV cannot scan inside | Hard | | `.html` | HTML Application | **High** | HTML smuggling, credential form | Medium | | `.hta` | HTML Application | **Critical** | Runs outside browser sandbox | Hard | | `.chm` | Compiled Help | **High** | Executes embedded scripts | Medium | | `.reg` | Registry file | **High** | Silently modifies registry | Medium | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Do **not** assume that `.txt` files are universally safe. **CVE-2017-8759** proved that even `.rtf` files can execute code via the .NET SOAP WSDL parser. Always cite "no file type is 100% safe" in your answer for full marks. ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — F1-Score of an Email Gateway ### Problem An enterprise email security gateway processed 10,000 emails in a day. Of these, 200 were actually malicious. The gateway flagged 180 emails as malicious, of which 170 were truly malicious (True Positives) and 10 were legitimate (False Positives). It also missed 30 malicious emails (False Negatives). Calculate Precision, Recall, and F1-Score. ### Step 1 — Identify the Confusion Matrix Values $$ TP = 170, \quad FP = 10, \quad FN = 30 $$ $$ \text{Actual Malicious} = TP + FN = 170 + 30 = 200 \quad \checkmark $$ ### Step 2 — Compute Precision $$ \text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{170}{170 + 10} = \frac{170}{180} \approx 0.9444 $$ ### Step 3 — Compute Recall (Sensitivity) $$ \text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{170}{170 + 30} = \frac{170}{200} = 0.8500 $$ ### Step 4 — Compute F1-Score $$ F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.9444 \times 0.8500}{0.9444 + 0.8500} $$ $$ F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.8028}{1.7944} = 2 \cdot 0.4474 \approx 0.8948 $$ ### Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation $$ \boxed{F_1 \approx 0.8948 \quad (\text{or } 89.48\%)} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** An F1-Score of **~0.89** is considered **good** in production email gateways. Industry benchmarks (per Gartner Magic Quadrant 2024) place leading ESGs in the **0.92–0.97** range. A score below **0.80** would typically trigger a vendor SLA penalty clause. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Email Attachment Malware Kill Chain (End-to-End Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Attacker Reconnaissance

When drawing your own KTU exam diagrams, use clear rectangular groupings to show **defense-in-depth** — this earns the *diagram presentation* mark. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Q: Define "Spear Phishing Attachment" as per the MITRE ATT&CK framework. How does it differ from a generic phishing email?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** 1. **Definition (1.5 Marks):** Spear Phishing Attachment is officially catalogued as **MITRE ATT&CK Technique T1566.001**. It involves sending malicious email attachments to **specific, researched targets** (e.g., finance officer, system admin) rather than mass-distributed spam. 2. **Personalization (0.75 Mark):** The email is customized using **OSINT** — victim name, role, recent projects, and relationships — to increase credibility. 3. **Differentiation (0.75 Mark):** Unlike generic phishing (broad spray), spear-phishing is a **low-volume, high-success-rate** attack typically preceding **Business Email Compromise (BEC)** or **ransomware deployment**. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Q: List any THREE common file types used to deliver malware via email attachments and explain why each is dangerous.** **Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 Mark Each):** 1. **`.iso` / `.img` (Disk Images):** These archive files bypass the **Mark-of-the-Web (MOTW)** security advisory that Windows applies to downloaded files, allowing direct execution without a security warning. 2. **`.docm` (Macro-Enabled Word):** Contains **VBA macros** that, when enabled, can invoke PowerShell to download and execute second-stage payloads from remote C2 servers. 3. **`.zip` with Password (Encrypted Archive):** Password-protected archives **cannot be inspected by antivirus gateways** at the perimeter, achieving a 100% evasion rate against signature-based scanners. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Q (a)** Explain in detail the **Cyber Kill Chain** phases as applied to an email attachment-based malware attack. Include at least **five distinct phases** with one real-world example for each. **(7 Marks)** **Q (b)** Discuss the **defense-in-depth** strategy for protecting an enterprise against email attachment-borne malware. Cover **perimeter, gateway, content, endpoint, and human** layers with specific technologies. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question A (a) — 7 Marks **Phase 1 — Reconnaissance (1 Mark):** Attackers harvest target email addresses from **LinkedIn, company websites, WHOIS records, and dark-web breach databases**. For example, the **2011 RSA SecurID breach** began with spear-phishing emails sent to four employees whose names were harvested from LinkedIn. **Phase 2 — Weaponization (1 Mark):** The attacker pairs a malicious attachment with an exploit. **Example:** The **Emotet banking trojan (2014)** was delivered as a weaponized `.doc` file containing a malicious VBA macro that downloaded TrickBot. **Phase 3 — Delivery (1 Mark):** Malspam is sent over **SMTP** using **MIME-encoded Base64** attachments with spoofed From headers. **Example:** The **ILOVEYOU worm (2000)** used sender spoofing with "From: " to make replies go to the victim's own contacts. **Phase 4 — Exploitation (1 Mark):** The user double-clicks; the macro or script runs. **Example:** The **2017 NotPetya** initially spread via a malicious `.doc` update mechanism in **M.E.Doc** (Ukrainian accounting software), exploiting a software supply chain. **Phase 5 — Installation (1 Mark):** Persistence is achieved via registry autorun keys (`HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run`). **Example:** **Stuxnet (2010)** installed itself as a Windows service and used **Windows Shortcut (.lnk) file exploitation (CVE-2010-2568)** to spread via USB. **Phase 6 — Command & Control (1 Mark):** Malware beacons out via **HTTPS on port 443** to blend with normal web traffic. **Example:** **TrickBot** uses **Domain Generation Algorithm (DGA)** with thousands of pseudo-random C2 domains. **Phase 7 — Actions on Objectives (1 Mark):** Final objective executed — data theft, encryption, or sabotage. **Example:** **Conti ransomware (2020)** exfiltrated data via Rclone to MEGA.nz cloud storage before encryption. --- #### Model Solution for Question A (b) — 7 Marks **Layer 1 — Perimeter Defense (1.5 Marks):** - **SPF (Sender Policy Framework)** validates the envelope sender's IP. - **DKIM (DomainKeys Identified Mail)** provides cryptographic signature verification. - **DMARC (Domain-based Message Authentication, Reporting, and Conformance)** unifies SPF and DKIM with policy enforcement. - **TLS 1.3** enforces encrypted SMTP transmission (RFC 8461 MTA-STS). **Layer 2 — Gateway Defense (1.5 Marks):** - **Reputation-based filtering** rejects emails from IPs on RBL (Real-time Blackhole Lists). - **Signature-based AV** (e.g., ClamAV, Sophos) matches attachments against the **CVE database**. - **URL Defanging and Click-Time Protection** neutralizes embedded links. **Layer 3 — Content Inspection (1.5 Marks):** - **MIME Parsing and Decoding** to extract attachments. - **Content Disarm and Reconstruction (CDR)** strips macros and rebuilds the document in a safe format. - **Magic-Byte Verification** detects filename-extension mismatch (e.g., `invoice.pdf.exe`). **Layer 4 — Endpoint Defense (1.5 Marks):** - **Windows Defender Attack Surface Reduction (ASR) Rules** block Office child processes. - **Mark-of-the-Web (MOTW)** warnings for downloaded files. - **Application Allowlisting** (e.g., AppLocker, Windows Defender Application Control). **Layer 5 — Human Defense (1 Mark):** - **Phishing simulation** tools (KnowBe4, Cofense). - **Security Awareness Training (SAT)** following **NIST SP 800-50 Rev 1**. - **Incident reporting hotlines** and **Zero-Trust reporting culture**. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Q (a)** What are the **SIR model** and the **Basic Reproduction Number $R_0$** in the context of email malware propagation? Derive the threshold condition for an epidemic. **(7 Marks)** **Q (b)** Explain **Content Disarm and Reconstruction (CDR)** as a defense against weaponized attachments. Compare it with traditional signature-based antivirus scanning, citing at least **three advantages** of CDR. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question B (a) — 7 Marks **1. SIR Model Definition (2 Marks):** The **Susceptible-Infected-Recovered (SIR)** model is a compartmental epidemiological model that partitions the email-user population into three disjoint sets: - $S(t)$ — Susceptible users who can be infected - $I(t)$ — Infected users who can transmit malware - $R(t)$ — Recovered (patched or remediated) users The model is governed by three coupled first-order ODEs: $$ \frac{dS}{dt} = -\beta \cdot S(t) \cdot I(t) $$ $$ \frac{dI}{dt} = \beta \cdot S(t) \cdot I(t) - \gamma \cdot I(t) $$ $$ \frac{dR}{dt} = \gamma \cdot I(t) $$ **2. Basic Reproduction Number $R_0$ (2 Marks):** $R_0 = \beta / \gamma$ represents the **average number of secondary infections** produced by a single infected user in a fully susceptible population. If $R_0 > 1$, the malware achieves epidemic status. **3. Threshold Derivation (3 Marks):** Dividing $dI/dt$ by $dS/dt$: $$ \frac{dI}{dS} = \frac{\beta S I - \gamma I}{-\beta S I} = \frac{\gamma - \beta S}{\beta S} $$ Integrating with initial conditions ($S_0$, $I_0$) and applying the final-state condition ($I_\infty = 0$): $$ S_\infty - S_0 + I_0 = \frac{\gamma}{\beta} \ln\left(\frac{S_\infty}{S_0}\right) $$ **Threshold Theorem:** $$ R_0 = \frac{\beta S_0}{\gamma} > 1 \implies \text{Epidemic Outbreak} $$ This mathematically proves that **rapid patching (high $\gamma$)** and **user training (low $\beta$)** are the two mathematically justified interventions. --- #### Model Solution for Question B (b) — 7 Marks **1. CDR Definition (2 Marks):** **Content Disarm and Reconstruction** is a proactive email security technology that **strips all active content** (macros, scripts, embedded objects) from an incoming attachment and rebuilds a sanitized version containing only the *visual* and *structural* elements of the original document. The user receives a perfectly usable file, but with all exploit vectors neutralized. **2. CDR Workflow (2 Marks):** - **Step 1:** Receive attachment (e.g., `.docm`). - **Step 2:** Parse document to identify all active content blocks. - **Step 3:** Remove macros, OLE objects, DDE fields, JavaScript. - **Step 4:** Reconstruct a clean `.docx` with preserved text, images, and formatting. - **Step 5:** Deliver the sanitized file to the user. **3. Three Advantages of CDR over Signature-Based AV (3 Marks):** 1. **Zero-Day Protection (1 Mark):** CDR neutralizes *unknown* threats because it does not rely on signature databases — it removes all active content by policy. Signature AV can only detect known malware. 2. **No False Negatives for Targeted Attacks (1 Mark):** Targeted APTs use unique, never-before-seen payloads. Signature AV fails completely; CDR succeeds by default-deny. 3. **No Sandbox Evasion (1 Mark):** Sophisticated malware detects sandbox environments and delays execution. CDR sidesteps this by simply removing what it cannot safely trust. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls on This Topic:** > 1. **Conflating Phishing vs. Spear Phishing** — KTU specifically tests whether you know that *spear-phishing* is targeted and *phishing* is broad. Confusing them costs 1–2 marks. > 2. **Forgetting Mark-of-the-Web (MOTW)** — When explaining why `.iso` and `.img` are dangerous, students often miss the critical fact that these formats **bypass MOTW**. This omission costs 1 mark. > 3. **Misquoting $R_0$ Threshold** — The exact threshold is **$R_0 > 1$**, not "$R_0 > 0$" or "$R_0 > 2$." Examiners reject imprecise thresholds. > 4. **Ignoring the "Human Layer"** — A defense-in-depth answer that omits **security awareness training** is considered incomplete; expect to lose 1 mark. > 5. **Failing to Name Real Malware Examples** — Generic answers like "a virus spreads" get partial credit. Always name **ILOVEYOU, Emotet, Stuxnet, Conti** to demonstrate mastery. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:** - ☐ Email attachments are classified under **MITRE ATT&CK T1566.001 (Spearphishing Attachment)**. - ☐ The **Cyber Kill Chain** has 7 phases: Recon → Weaponize → Deliver → Exploit → Install → C2 → Objectives. - ☐ The **SIR model** has three ODEs governing $S(t)$, $I(t)$, $R(t)$. - ☐ **Threshold Theorem:** $R_0 = \beta S_0 / \gamma$. Epidemic only if $R_0 > 1$. - ☐ **Base64 expansion factor** = **4/3** for MIME attachment size calculation. - ☐ **F1-Score** = $2 P R / (P + R)$ is the standard metric for email gateway evaluation. - ☐ **Disk image formats** (`.iso`, `.img`, `.vhd`) **bypass Mark-of-the-Web** — high risk. - ☐ **Password-protected archives** (`.zip`, `.7z`) **defeat AV scanning** at the perimeter. - ☐ **Defense-in-Depth** has **5 layers**: Perimeter → Gateway → Content → Endpoint → Human. - ☐ **CDR (Content Disarm and Reconstruction)** provides **zero-day protection** by removing active content. - ☐ Real-world malware references: **ILOVEYOU (2000), Emotet (2014), Stuxnet (2010), Conti (2020), TrickBot (2016)**. - ☐ **SPF + DKIM + DMARC** form the **email authentication triad**. - ☐ **TLS 1.3** with **MTA-STS** (RFC 8461) is the current standard for SMTP encryption. - ☐ **EICAR test file** is the **68-byte standard** for AV validation — harmless but detected. - ☐ **LOLBins** (powershell.exe, mshta.exe, wscript.exe) are commonly abused post-exploitation.

topics.title: Malicious Websites
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST721 - CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Malicious Websites

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — MALICIOUS WEBSITES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Malicious Website (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**  
> A *malicious website* is any web-based resource (URL, web page, web application, or associated script) that has been intentionally crafted, compromised, or hijacked to deliver **malware**, **harvest sensitive user data**, **execute unauthorized actions on the client-side**, or **deceive users** into performing security-compromising acts such as disclosing credentials, downloading trojanized payloads, or transferring funds to attackers.

In the KTU 2024 Cyber Security framework, malicious websites are classified as one of the **most dominant attack vectors** in modern threat landscapes, accounting for a substantial percentage of initial-access breaches globally.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Trojan Horse" of the Internet

> [!NOTE]  
> **Intuitive Analogy:**  
> Imagine a beautifully decorated shop in a busy street. From the outside, it looks legitimate — bright signboard, polite staff, well-arranged goods. But hidden behind the counter, the shopkeeper is swapping genuine currency with counterfeits, recording your card PIN on a hidden camera, and copying your identity.  
>  
> A *malicious website* is precisely this kind of **digital trapdoor**. The visible façade (UI/UX, branding, URL) is engineered for **trust**, while the hidden layers (JavaScript, iframes, exploits, phishing forms) are engineered for **theft or sabotage**. The victim willingly walks in — just as a user willingly clicks a link — and the damage begins before they realize it.

### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Highlight:**  
> Module 3 in OECST721 emphasizes **Web-Based Threats**, and *Malicious Websites* act as the *delivery vehicle* for almost every web-borne threat covered in this module — phishing, drive-by downloads, XSS, SQL injection, watering-hole attacks, and ransomware distribution. A strong grasp of this topic is **mandatory** to answer the Part B 14-mark question that almost always appears from this module.

### 1.4 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | One-line Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Pharming** | Redirecting users from a legitimate URL to a malicious one via DNS/DNS-cache poisoning. |
| **Typosquatting** | Registering domain names similar to popular brands (e.g., `go0gle.com`). |
| **Drive-by Download** | Automatic malware download simply by visiting a compromised webpage — **no user click required**. |
| **Watering-Hole Attack** | Compromising a website that a *specific target group* (e.g., employees of a company) regularly visits. |
| **Exploit Kit** | A pre-packaged toolkit (e.g., Angler, RIG) that scans victim browsers for known vulnerabilities and serves matching exploits. |
| **Clickjacking** | Tricking users into clicking something different from what they perceive (e.g., a hidden "Like" button under a fake "Play" button). |
| **Defacement** | Unauthorized modification of a website's visible content, usually to spread propaganda or display attacker signature. |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Malicious Websites

> [!NOTE]  
> The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to **classify** malicious websites by *intent*, *delivery mechanism*, and *target victim profile*. Below is the exhaustive taxonomy.

### 2.1.1 By Intent

1. **Information-Stealing (Phishing) Sites**  
   - Clone legitimate banking, e-commerce, or email login pages.  
   - Capture credentials, OTPs, and personal data via fraudulent HTML forms.  
   - Example pattern: `https://secure-paypa1.com/login`.

2. **Malware-Delivering (Drive-by) Sites**  
   - Host exploit kits or trojanized installers.  
   - Often use **obfuscated JavaScript** or **hidden iframes** to fetch payloads.

3. **Command-and-Control (C2) Panels**  
   - Web-based dashboards used by attackers to control botnets.  
   - Legitimate-looking PHP/ASP interfaces.

4. **Defacement Sites**  
   - Vandalized websites used for hacktivism or reputation damage.

5. **Scam / Fraud Sites**  
   - Fake shopping portals, lottery scams, tech-support scams, romance scams.

### 2.1.2 By Delivery Mechanism

| Mechanism | Description | User Interaction Needed? |
|---|---|---|
| **Malicious Link in Email** | Phishing URL sent via spam | Yes — click |
| **SEO Poisoning** | Attacker ranks malicious site on top of search results | Yes — click |
| **Malvertising** | Malicious ads served via legitimate ad networks | Minimal — auto-load |
| **Compromised Legitimate Site** | Legit website hacked to host malicious iframes/scripts | None — passive visit |
| **Watering-Hole** | Targeted compromise of niche websites | None — passive visit |
| **Social Media Lure** | Malicious URL posted on Facebook, X, Instagram | Yes — click |

### 2.1.3 By Target Profile

- **Mass-Market / Opportunistic** → Random visitors (broad phishing).  
- **Targeted / Spear** → Specific individuals or organizations (spear-phishing, watering-hole).

---

## 2.2 The Anatomy of a Malicious Website Attack (Operational Workflow)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> The 'Why' and 'How' behind each step is essential for KTU 14-mark answers.

- **Step 1 — Reconnaissance & Domain Registration**  
  Attacker registers a *typosquatted* or *algorithmically generated* domain (DGAs). The 'why' is to bypass user suspicion and evade naive blocklists.

- **Step 2 — Hosting & SSL Procurement**  
  Free SSL certificates (e.g., via Let's Encrypt) are obtained to display the green padlock — exploiting the *user misconception* that HTTPS = safe.

- **Step 3 — Payload Preparation**  
  Attacker obfuscates malware (Base64, XOR, JavaScript encoding) and stages an exploit kit or phishing form on the server.

- **Step 4 — Lure Distribution**  
  Distribution vector chosen: email phishing, malvertising, social media, SEO poisoning, or watering-hole compromise of a trusted site.

- **Step 5 — Victim Interaction & Exploitation**  
  - If the user *clicks* → phishing form captures credentials.  
  - If the user *visits passively* → exploit kit fingerprints browser, matches CVE, and silently deploys malware.

- **Step 6 — Post-Exploitation**  
  Credential exfiltration, ransomware drop, lateral movement, persistence, and C2 beaconing.

---

## 2.3 Detection Theory — How Defenders Identify Malicious Sites

### 2.3.1 Signature-Based Detection (Blacklisting)

- Relies on URL/IP/domain blocklists (e.g., Google Safe Browsing, PhishTank).  
- **Limitation:** Cannot detect *zero-day* or newly registered domains.

### 2.3.2 Heuristic / Rule-Based Detection

- Examines lexical features of URLs: length, special-character density, brand-name inclusion, TLD rarity.  
- Example heuristic: *if (URL contains "login" AND domain age < 30 days AND uses HTTPS but no SPF/DKIM) → flag.*

### 2.3.3 Reputation-Based Detection

- Aggregates signals from global user telemetry: DNS resolution history, WHOIS age, hosting ASN reputation, certificate transparency logs.

### 2.3.4 Machine-Learning Based Detection

- Trains classifiers (Random Forest, XGBoost, Deep Neural Networks) on labelled URL datasets (e.g., UCI Phishing, PhishTank).  
- Features: lexical (character n-grams, Shannon entropy), host-based (ASN, TTL, IP diversity), and content-based (HTML structure, script obfuscation density).

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Detection Metrics

> [!NOTE]  
> The following metrics are **frequently asked in KTU Module-3 numerical/analytical parts**. Memorize the formula, the meaning, and the unit (all are dimensionless ratios between **0** and **1**).

| Metric | Formula | What It Measures | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **True Positive Rate (Recall / Sensitivity)** | $\text{TPR} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Fraction of actual malicious sites correctly flagged | High TPR = fewer attacks missed |
| **False Positive Rate (FPR)** | $\text{FPR} = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Fraction of legitimate sites wrongly flagged | Low FPR = fewer false alarms |
| **Precision** | $\text{Precision} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Of all sites flagged, how many were truly malicious | High precision = trustworthy alerts |
| **Specificity** | $\text{Specificity} = \dfrac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | Fraction of legitimate sites correctly allowed | High specificity = low disruption |
| **Accuracy** | $\text{Accuracy} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Overall correctness | Useful only on balanced datasets |
| **F1-Score** | $\text{F1} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$ | Harmonic mean of precision and recall | Best single metric for imbalanced data |
| **Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC)** | $\text{MCC} = \dfrac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}$ | Balanced quality even on skewed data | Robust academic metric |

> [!WARNING]  
> **Do NOT use the vertical bar** `|` symbol inside markdown tables — it breaks the table syntax. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX, as done above.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Why does this matter in industry?**  
> - **Browser Vendors** (Chrome, Firefox, Safari) integrate real-time malicious-URL detection into the address bar.  
> - **Email Gateways** (Proofpoint, Mimecast) score every inbound URL.  
> - **SIEM/SOAR Platforms** ingest malicious-website IOCs to enrich threat intelligence.  
> - **E-Commerce Fraud Teams** use the same heuristics to detect clone sites.  
> - **CDN Providers** (Cloudflare, Akamai) perform inline content-inspection for drive-by downloads.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: F1-Score from First Principles

The **F1-Score** is *the* single most asked detection metric in KTU Module-3 questions. Let us derive it exhaustively.

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Definition of Precision and Recall

We begin with the two fundamental rates used in malicious-website classification:

$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}
$$

$$
\text{Recall (TPR)} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$

> *Textual note:* Here, $TP$ = correctly flagged malicious sites, $FP$ = legitimate sites wrongly flagged, $FN$ = malicious sites that slipped through.

### 3.1.2 Why a Combined Metric is Needed

Precision alone ignores $FN$ (missed attacks), and Recall alone ignores $FP$ (false alarms). A single statistic that balances both is needed.

### 3.1.3 General F-Beta Family

The general $\text{F}_\beta$ score weights recall $\beta$ times more than precision:

$$
\text{F}_\beta = (1 + \beta^2) \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{(\beta^2 \cdot \text{Precision}) + \text{Recall}}
$$

### 3.1.4 Specialize to $\beta = 1$

Setting $\beta = 1$ (equal weighting):

$$
\text{F}_1 = (1 + 1^2) \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{(1^2 \cdot \text{Precision}) + \text{Recall}}
$$

$$
\text{F}_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}
$$

> *Textual note:* This is the **harmonic mean** of precision and recall — penalizing extreme imbalance more strongly than the arithmetic mean.

### 3.1.5 Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

Suppose a malicious-website detector is evaluated on **1000** URLs: **600** are malicious, **400** are legitimate. The detector flagged **540** URLs as malicious, of which **510** were truly malicious. Compute F1.

- $TP = 510$  
- $FP = 540 - 510 = 30$  
- $FN = 600 - 510 = 90$  
- $TN = 400 - 30 = 370$

Precision:

$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{510}{510 + 30} = \frac{510}{540} \approx 0.9444
$$

Recall:

$$
\text{Recall} = \frac{510}{510 + 90} = \frac{510}{600} = 0.8500
$$

F1-Score:

$$
\text{F}_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.9444 \times 0.8500}{0.9444 + 0.8500}
$$

$$
\text{F}_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.80274}{1.7944} \approx 0.8948
$$

> *Textual note:* A F1 of **0.8948** indicates strong, balanced performance for this malicious-website detector.

### 3.1.6 Bound and Interpretation

$$
0 \leq \text{F}_1 \leq 1
$$

- $\text{F}_1 = 1$ → perfect classifier.  
- $\text{F}_1 = 0$ → classifier failed completely.  
- Generally, $\text{F}_1 \geq 0.90$ is considered **production-grade** for malicious-URL classifiers.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Detection Method Comparison

| Detection Method | Strength | Weakness | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Signature / Blacklist** | Zero false positives on known IOCs | Zero-day blind, list-update lag | Email gateway pre-filter |
| **Heuristic / Lexical** | Catches newly registered domains | Easily evaded with randomization | URL-only analysis |
| **Reputation / WHOIS** | Captures long-term malicious infra | Slow aggregation, ASN bias | Threat-intel enrichment |
| **Content / Sandbox** | Sees actual exploit behavior | Resource-heavy, evasion via polymorphism | Drive-by download detection |
| **ML / Deep Learning** | High accuracy on large feature sets | Adversarial attacks, training-data bias | Real-time browser extensions |
| **Hybrid (Stacked Ensemble)** | Combines strengths, mitigates weaknesses | Architectural complexity | Enterprise-grade security stacks |

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Lexical Entropy of a Suspicious URL

> [!NOTE]  
> **KTU often asks** to compute the *Shannon entropy* of a URL or domain to assess its randomness (a strong indicator of DGA-generated malicious domains).

$$
H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot \log_2 p_i
$$

For a domain name like `xkqzpm4t.info`, character frequencies are computed and plugged in. **Higher entropy** → more random → higher likelihood of malicious DGA origin.

*Threshold (rule of thumb in literature):* $H(X) \geq 3.5$ bits per character often flags DGA-style domains.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `nodeA`, `step1`). No reserved keywords are used as node names. Labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric — no markdown formatting inside quotes.

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Lifecycle of a Malicious Website Attack

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["ATTACKER RECONNAISSANCE AND DOMAIN REGISTRATION"] --> nodeB["TYPOSQUATTING OR DGA DOMAIN SELECTED"]
```
