Notice how the bulk data is never exposed to the KMS — only the small DEK is wrapped, dramatically reducing the KMS API load and improving performance. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Q: Define Full Disk Encryption (FDE) and list any two algorithms commonly used for it.** **Model Answer:** Full Disk Encryption (FDE) is a data-at-rest protection technique that encrypts every bit of data stored on a disk volume, including the operating system, system files, user data, and swap space. FDE operates transparently at the sector/block level, requiring authentication (password, TPM, smart card) at boot time before the OS can access the volume. **Two common algorithms:** 1. **AES-256 in XTS mode** — the IEEE 1619 standard for disk encryption; used by BitLocker, FileVault, and LUKS. 2. **AES-128 in CBC mode with ESSIV** — used historically by older Linux distributions (e.g., dm-crypt). **Key benefits:** Protects against theft of physical media; ensures entire disk is unrecoverable without the key; supports instant cryptographic erasure by destroying the key. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Q: What is a Hardware Security Module (HSM)? Why is it critical for encrypted storage?** **Model Answer:** A Hardware Security Module (HSM) is a dedicated, tamper-resistant hardware device designed to generate, store, and manage cryptographic keys, and to perform cryptographic operations (encryption, decryption, signing) in a physically secure environment. HSMs are certified under standards like **FIPS 140-2 Level 3 or 4**, and Common Criteria EAL4+. **Why critical for encrypted storage:** - **Key Isolation**: Master keys (KEKs) never leave the HSM in plaintext. - **Tamper Resistance**: Physical intrusion attempts zero out the key material. - **FIPS Compliance**: Mandatory for regulated industries (banking, defense, healthcare). - **Performance**: Hardware-accelerated crypto at millions of operations/second. - **Crypto-Erase**: Destroying an HSM instantly renders all data encrypted under its keys unrecoverable. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Q: Explain the architecture of Transparent Data Encryption (TDE) in databases. How does it differ from column-level encryption?** **Model Answer:** **Architecture of TDE:** Transparent Data Encryption operates at the **database storage layer**, encrypting data at the file level (datafiles, log files, backup files) without requiring any changes to application code or SQL queries. **Key components:** 1. **Master Encryption Key (MEK)** — stored in an external keystore (HSM, Oracle Wallet, AWS KMS, Azure Key Vault). The MEK never touches the database server in plaintext. 2. **Tablespace Encryption Key (TEK)** — derived from or wrapped by the MEK; encrypts individual tablespaces or the entire database. 3. **Encryption Layer** — sits between the database buffer cache and the disk I/O subsystem. 4. **Keystore Module** — interfaces with the external key management system to fetch and unwrap keys at database startup. **Workflow:** - At database startup, the DBA provides the keystore password or authenticates via HSM → MEK is unwrapped. - TEKs are unwrapped on demand and cached in memory (never on disk). - When a block is written to disk → encrypted with TEK using AES-128 or AES-256. - When a block is read from disk → decrypted on the fly by the database engine. - Backups of encrypted datafiles are automatically encrypted. **Differences from Column-Level Encryption:** | Aspect | TDE | Column-Level Encryption | |--------|-----|-------------------------| | Granularity | Entire tablespace / datafile | Specific columns (e.g., SSN, credit card) | | Application changes | None | Yes (functions to encrypt/decrypt) | | Query performance | Minimal impact | Can break index usage on encrypted columns | | Key management | Centralized in keystore | Per-column keys, more complex | | Use case | Whole database protection | Selective sensitive fields | | Compliance | Meets most "encryption at rest" mandates | Finer-grained control for specific PII | **Conclusion**: TDE is the preferred enterprise choice for at-rest database encryption due to its transparency and centralized management, while column-level encryption is chosen when only specific sensitive fields require protection and fine-grained access control is needed. --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Q: Describe the envelope encryption pattern used in cloud storage. Illustrate with AWS S3 SSE-KMS as an example.** **Model Answer:** **Envelope Encryption Concept:** Envelope encryption is a cryptographic pattern where a randomly generated **Data Encryption Key (DEK)** is used to encrypt the bulk data, and the DEK itself is encrypted (wrapped) by a separate **Key Encryption Key (KEK)** that resides in a hardened KMS. This two-tier approach combines the **performance of symmetric encryption** with the **security of centralized key management**. **Workflow with AWS S3 SSE-KMS:** 1. **Client uploads object to S3.** 2. S3 service generates a unique 256-bit AES **DEK** for this object. 3. S3 sends a request to **AWS KMS**: *"Decrypt" or "GenerateDataKey"* API call. 4. AWS KMS uses the Customer Master Key (CMK) — which is the **KEK** — to: - Return the plaintext DEK (in memory, over TLS) for S3 to encrypt the data. - Store the **ciphertext DEK** as metadata attached to the S3 object. 5. S3 encrypts the object with the plaintext DEK using **AES-256-GCM** (or AES-256 if SSE-S3). 6. The plaintext DEK is discarded from S3 memory immediately after use. 7. The encrypted object and the encrypted DEK (metadata) are stored in S3. **Decryption Flow (Authorized Read):** 1. Client requests the object. 2. S3 retrieves the encrypted DEK from object metadata. 3. S3 calls KMS to decrypt the DEK using the CMK. 4. KMS verifies the IAM policy, then returns the plaintext DEK. 5. S3 decrypts the object in memory, returns plaintext to the client. 6. Plaintext DEK is discarded from memory. **Key Advantages:** - **Performance**: Bulk data encrypted with fast symmetric AES; only the small DEK is processed by KMS. - **Scalability**: One CMK can wrap millions of DEKs — KMS never sees the actual data. - **Auditing**: Every KMS Decrypt call is logged in **AWS CloudTrail**. - **Automatic Rotation**: CMKs can be automatically rotated annually without re-encrypting data — KMS re-wraps the DEKs lazily on next use. - **Separation of Duties**: Data is in S3; keys are in KMS; access is governed by IAM policies. **Security Properties:** - DEK never persisted in plaintext on the server. - KEK (CMK) is protected by FIPS 140-2 validated HSMs inside KMS. - Compromise of S3 storage alone yields no usable data without KMS access. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024] #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Q: Compare symmetric and asymmetric encryption for storage. Why is hybrid encryption the industry standard?** **Model Answer:** **Symmetric Encryption (e.g., AES-256):** - Uses a single shared secret key for both encryption and decryption. - **Speed**: Extremely fast — modern CPUs with AES-NI achieve 5–10 GB/s. - **Key size**: 128/192/256 bits sufficient for 128-bit security. - **Storage use**: Ideal for encrypting large files, entire disks, database payloads. - **Limitation**: Key distribution is hard — how do you securely share the key with the recipient? In storage contexts, the key must be stored alongside (or near) the data, which is risky. **Asymmetric Encryption (e.g., RSA-3072, ECC-256):** - Uses a public key for encryption, private key for decryption. - **Speed**: 1000× to 10000× slower than symmetric; RSA-3072 ~ 1000 ops/sec. - **Key size**: Much larger (3072 bits RSA vs 256 bits AES) for equivalent security. - **Storage use**: Impractical for bulk data — only used to protect small payloads like keys. - **Advantage**: Solves the key distribution problem — public keys can be shared openly. **Why Hybrid Encryption is the Industry Standard:** Hybrid encryption combines the best of both worlds: 1. **Bulk data** is encrypted with a fast symmetric algorithm (AES) using a randomly generated DEK. 2. **The DEK** is then encrypted with the recipient's public key (RSA) or wrapped with a KEK in a KMS. 3. **The encrypted data + encrypted DEK** are stored together. **Why hybrid wins:** - **Performance**: Symmetric AES handles the bulk load; asymmetric only handles the small DEK. - **Scalability**: One RSA key pair can protect unlimited DEKs. - **Key Management**: Users don't need to share symmetric keys directly — only public keys. - **Forward Secrecy**: New DEK per file/session means compromising one DEK doesn't compromise other data. - **Standardization**: Used by TLS 1.3, PGP, S/MIME, AWS S3, Azure Blob, and virtually every cloud storage system. **Numerical Justification:** Suppose a 1 GB file needs to be uploaded encrypted. - Pure RSA-3072: $\frac{10^9 \text{ bytes}}{128 \text{ bytes/block}} \approx 7.8 \times 10^6$ RSA operations × 1 ms each = **~130 minutes**. - Hybrid (AES-256 + RSA wrap): AES encryption at 1 GB/s = **~1 second**; RSA wrap of 256-bit DEK = **~1 ms**. Total ≈ **1 second**. The performance difference is **~7800× in favor of hybrid encryption**. --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Q: Explain Self-Encrypting Drives (SEDs) and the OPAL standard. How do they achieve "instant secure erase"?** **Model Answer:** **Self-Encrypting Drives (SEDs):** A Self-Encrypting Drive is a storage device (HDD or SSD) that contains a **built-in hardware AES engine** which automatically encrypts **all data written to the drive** before it reaches the physical media, and decrypts it transparently on read. The encryption is **always on**, cannot be disabled by the user, and is invisible to the operating system. **Key features:** - Hardware-implemented AES-128 or AES-256. - Encryption keys are generated internally at first power-up and never leave the drive. - No software overhead — the host OS sees normal plaintext data; the drive handles encryption at the SATA/NVMe controller level. - Compliance: Meets TCG (Trusted Computing Group) standards. **OPAL Standard:** **OPAL** is the most widely adopted TCG specification for SEDs, defined by the Trusted Computing Group. It specifies: - **OPAL 2.0** (current standard) supports AES-256, multiple users, and complex admin structures. - **Shadow MBR** — a pre-boot authentication environment that unlocks the drive before the OS boots. - **Locking SP (Security Provider)** — manages who can read/write which LBA (Logical Block Addressing) ranges. - **Authentication** via passwords, smart cards, or TPM. **How Instant Secure Erase (ISE) Works:** Traditional drive sanitization requires overwriting the entire disk with zeros (multi-pass, time-consuming). With SEDs, **instant secure erase** is achieved by **destroying the encryption key** rather than the data: 1. The drive stores all data encrypted under a single **Media Encryption Key (MEK)** held in a secure area of the drive's controller. 2. The MEK never leaves the drive. 3. To "erase" the data, the drive controller either: - **Cryptographically erases** by generating a new random MEK, rendering all previous data permanently undecryptable (takes milliseconds). - Or, in some implementations, zeros out the MEK directly. 4. From the data recovery perspective, the data is **mathematically irrecoverable** because the MEK no longer exists. **Why this is a game-changer:** - **Time**: Milliseconds vs hours for full disk overwrite. - **Wear**: No write amplification on SSDs (which shortens their lifespan). - **Verification**: NIST SP 800-88 recognizes cryptographic erase as a valid sanitization method. - **Enterprise decommissioning**: Data centers can repurpose or retire drives instantly without risking data leakage. **Real-world deployment**: Major HDD/SSD vendors — **Seagate, Western Digital, Samsung, Crucial** — ship OPAL 2.0 compliant SEDs as enterprise SKUs. Microsoft **BitLocker** can leverage SEDs in "eDrive" mode where the hardware AES engine is used instead of software. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes students make in Encrypted Storage Solutions questions (and how to avoid losing marks):** > > 1. **Confusing FDE with File-Level Encryption** — FDE encrypts the **entire disk including OS**; FLE encrypts only user files. Examiners deduct 2 marks for this. > > 2. **Writing "AES-512"** — There is NO such algorithm. Valid AES keys are 128/192/256 bits. > > 3. **Confusing KMS with HSM** — KMS is a **software service** for key management; HSM is the **physical hardware** that stores the root key. KMS *uses* HSMs. > > 4. **Forgetting why XTS mode is used for disks** — Always mention **random access (sector-level independent encryption)**, which is the key advantage. > > 5. **Not mentioning TPM in BitLocker answers** — Full marks require explaining the **TPM-bound key sealing** mechanism, not just "BitLocker encrypts the disk". > > 6. **Writing vague "secure" instead of specific security properties** — Use precise terms: **confidentiality, integrity, authenticity, non-repudiation**. > > 7. **Ignoring performance numbers** — When asked about hybrid encryption, including the numerical comparison (~1000× slower RSA) earns extra marks. > > 8. **Not discussing key rotation in KMS answers** — Examiners expect the **lifecycle stages**: generate → distribute → store → use → rotate → destroy. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Encrypted Storage Solutions** > > - **Three states of data**: at rest, in transit, in use. Encrypted storage focuses on **at rest**. > - **AES** is the dominant symmetric cipher; valid key sizes: **128, 192, 256 bits** only. > - **XTS mode** is the IEEE 1619 standard for **disk encryption** — enables random sector access. > - **GCM mode** provides **authenticated encryption (AEAD)** — combines confidentiality + integrity. > - **Hybrid encryption** = symmetric AES for data + asymmetric RSA/ECC for key wrapping. Industry standard. > - **Envelope encryption**: DEK encrypts data; KEK encrypts DEK; KEK stays in HSM/KMS. > - **KMS** (Key Management Service) = software for key lifecycle; **HSM** = tamper-proof hardware for root keys. > - **Key lifecycle**: Generate → Distribute → Store → Use → Rotate → Destroy. 5 stages. > - **FDE** (Full Disk Encryption) encrypts entire disk volume; examples: BitLocker, FileVault, LUKS. > - **TDE** (Transparent Data Encryption) encrypts database files transparently; no app changes needed. > - **SED** (Self-Encrypting Drive) = hardware AES in drive controller; **OPAL 2.0** is the TCG standard. > - **Instant Secure Erase** = destroy the MEK to make all data permanently undecryptable. > - **AES-256 brute force time** ≈ $3.67 \times 10^{51}$ years — computationally infeasible. > - **FIPS 140-2/140-3** is the US federal standard for cryptographic module validation. > - **DPDPA 2023, GDPR, HIPAA** all mandate encryption of personal/sensitive data at rest. > - **Storage overhead** from encryption: typically 5–15% depending on mode and authentication tag size. > - **TPM (Trusted Platform Module)** binds the encryption key to the hardware, preventing disk transplant attacks. > - **Common tools**: BitLocker (Windows), FileVault (macOS), LUKS/dm-crypt (Linux), VeraCrypt (cross-platform). > - **Cloud encryption services**: AWS SSE-S3 / SSE-KMS, Azure Storage SSE / CMK, GCP CMEK. > - **Crypto-shredding** = destroying keys to render all data under them permanently inaccessible. > - **Performance tip**: AES-NI hardware acceleration gives 5–10 GB/s throughput on modern CPUs. > - **Most common exam trap**: confusing "encryption at rest" with "encryption in transit" (TLS). --- **End of Module 4 — Encrypted Storage Solutions Notes** *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme, OECST721 Cyber Security*
